analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: By saying what “many reputable physiologists recommend,” this sentence introduces a common belief, the belief that exercise “is a mechanism for the generation of weight loss.”

S2: This sentence uses an “although X, Y,” structure. In the “although X” part, the author concedes that exercise can help a person lose weight. Then, in the “Y” part, the author asks a rhetorical question to signal that (1) he or she believes that the common belief introduced in S1 is a myth and that (2) the primary purpose of the passage will be to discuss, in some way, the answer to this rhetorical question.

S3: This sentence introduces a study undertaken by Lewis Halsey and his team along with the theory that the study supports.

Since that theory is that “our bodies are programed to “calorically compensate” on the days on which we exercise, burning on these days significantly fewer calories than the laws of thermodynamics … would predict,” we can see that Lewis’s study and the theory it supports supply an answer to the rhetorical question asked by S2. The study and the theory basically imply that the answer is “No, exercise is not as effective as these experts believe.”

Simplification: A study shows that exercise does not cause as much burning of calories as experts believe it causes.

S4: Uses the look above marker “THESE days” to begin to explain why fewer calories than expected are burned on the days when people exercise, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph.

S5: The contrast marker “rather” at the beginning of this sentence signals that this sentence probably states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points.

Indeed, S5 is an important sentence because it presents the reason fewer calories than expected are burned, which is that, on days when people exercise, the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure.

Simplification: The body burns fewer calories than expected by reducing its “basal,” meaning “basic,” use of energy.

S6, S7, and S8: These sentences present an example to illustrate the concept of “basal energy expenditure” and show how additional activity results in a person’s burning “more calories over and above this basal energy expenditure.”

S9: The contrast marker “however” at the beginning of this sentence signals that this sentence probably states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points.

Indeed, S9 does state an important fact. The fact stated by S9, “in the study, Lewis and his team observed that this expected caloric burn did not materialize,” explains how the study supports the theory mentioned in S3, which is that “our bodies are programed to ‘calorically compensate’ on the days on which we exercise, burning on these days significantly fewer calories than the laws of thermodynamics … would predict.”

S10 and S11: These sentences present details of how the “expected caloric burn did not materialize.”

S12: Presents Lewis’s hypothesis about why “this expected caloric burn did not materialize.”

Simplification: Lewis’s hypothesis about why “this expected caloric burn did not materialize,” is that “humans and animals respond to greater energy being expended on activity” by reducing the amount of energy they use.

S13: Expands on the hypothesis presented in S12.

Simplification: The researchers think this reduction of energy use is something that human ancestors evolved to do.

S14 and S15: These sentences present reasons why human ancestors evolved to reduce energy use: “food was scarce” and “time spent foraging” involved danger of getting killed by predators.

question.text: The primary purpose of the passage is to

answers.text: outline the safety-related and environmental benefits of nuclear power generation
argue that governments have the ability to overcome negative beliefs about nuclear power
present some important concerns related to accidents involving nuclear reactors
advocate that all energy be produced via the use of nuclear power plants
challenge some common concerns about the security and viability of nuclear power

explanation_parts.text: To correctly answer a Primary Purpose question, we look for clues in the passage and seek to understand how the structure and important points of the passage indicate what the primary purpose of the passage is.
The important points are often found in the leading sentences of the paragraphs.

In this passage, the important points can be found in thefirst sentence of each paragraph.Notice that the first sentences of the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs include the contrast markers, “although” and “while.” Both of these markers, “although X, Y” and “while X, Y,” are used to acknowledge a counterpoint before making a main point.
Let’s review our analysis of these three sentences.
S1: Notice that a common belief is presented: “the impression that X (belief) has beenwidely held.”
Simplification: Many people in the U.S.believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.
S4: When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’sfearsthat were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated.
Simplification: the author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that it is important to overcome this fear.

S9: Here the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economicobstacles.” In the second paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety.Now, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”
Simplification: The author acknowledges that there are indeed economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

We can see that these three leading sentences of the three paragraphs indicate that the passage first presents and then challenges the impression widely held in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and uniquely expensive.
(A) outline the safety-related and environmental benefits of nuclear power generation
Trap: Too Narrow

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the 2ndparagraph does indeed discuss safety and environmental benefits of nuclear power generation.Specifically, S5 says that it is “the safest among the eight most common methods of energy production,” and S7 says that “the potential environmental benefits are profound.”
However, a Primary Purpose correct answer must cover the main ideas of the passage as a whole, rather than just one of multiple paragraphs or a few supporting details.

This choice focuses on the supporting details in the 2ndparagraph.It does not cover the common belief in the 1stparagraph, which is then challenged by the author in S4, or the economic issues discussed in the 3rdparagraph.
(B) argue that governments have the ability to overcome negative beliefs about nuclear power
Trap: Distortion
Trap: Half-Right
From our Passage Analysis, we see that the author presents and then challenges the common belief in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and expensive.

So, if you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “overcome negative beliefs about nuclear power” is an important theme of the passage.Indeed, a main point in S4 is that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”
However, the passage does NOT primarily “argue thatgovernmentshave the ability to” overcome these negative beliefs.

“Governments” are mentioned in a few details but are not the main focus of the passage.Furthermore, where government or politics is mentioned, the context does not involve their having the “ability to overcome negative beliefs about nuclear power.”
(C) present some important concerns related to accidents involving nuclear reactors
Trap: Opposite
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “concerns related to accidents involving nuclear reactors” appears in S2.
However, does the author present these concerns because the author believes that these concerns are “important”?

No, the main point of the author is tochallengethese concerns.In S4, the author argues, “theactualperil involved in nuclear power generation is highlyoverstated, and the US would benefit in many respects fromovercoming this unjustified perception.”
Trap: Too Narrow
Furthermore, the presenting of these concerns is only one aspect of the passage, not the primary purpose of the passage.
(D) advocate that all energy be produced via the use of nuclear power plants
Trap: Stretch
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that this choice goes in the right direction: the author does indeed “advocate” in favor of nuclear power.
However, notice the extreme marker, “ALL energy,” which is a yellow flag.

The passage does not make this extreme statement.The closest the passage gets is in S7: “the adoption of nuclear power is the fastest and most realistic approach to decarbonization to avert further global warming.”
While the passage suggests that some energy should be produced via use of nuclear power, the author does not advocate that all energy be produced via the use of nuclear power plants.
So, “ALL energy” goes beyond what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(E) challenge some common concerns about the security and viability of nuclear power

From our Passage Analysis, we see that the author presents and then challenges the common belief in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and expensive.So, “challenge some common concerns” in this choice appears to match our Passage Analysis.

Now, we can notice that “concerns about the security” in this choice matches with “the impression” that nuclear power is “hazardous,” i.e., dangerous, in the passage and that “viability” connects to “uniquely expensive.” “Viable” means “practical.” So, if something is too expensive, it is sometimes said to be “not financially viable.”
So, we may quickly get the sense that this choice accurately describes the primary purpose of the passage, but let’s confirm that the wording matches the passage:

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagechallenge some common concernstheactualperil involved in nuclear power generation is highlyoverstated(S4)the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming thisunjustified perception(S4)overcoming these hurdles rapidly is not only essential but also feasible (S9)securityactualperil… is highly overstated (S4)thesafest(S5)can besafelystored (S6)viabilityovercoming these hurdles rapidly is not only essential but alsofeasible.(S9)the future of nuclear power ispromising(S11)
We can see that this choice matches the passage and is the correct answer.

When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It’s helpful to notice that the passage begins by presenting a common belief: S1 says, “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.” When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion, with the two contrasting views forming the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented by S1 involves two negative ideas about nuclear power plants: that they are “particularly hazardous” and that they are “uniquely expensive.”

S2 and S3 then add supporting details that explain why people have fears of nuclear power. S2 provides the example of nuclear disaster in Chernobyl, and S3 mentions fear of radiation exposure and the health problems that can result from it.

Notice that, in the 1st paragraph, the author presents a common opinion but doesn’t present an opinion of his own.

1st paragraph summary: Many people in the US believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive and associate nuclear power plants with scary images of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster and with radiation exposure.

In S4, notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part of S4:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s “trepidation and disgust” related to nuclear power.

In the X part of S4, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that there is “some risk” involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.” Indeed, details about both safety and environmental benefits follow.

As we might expect, the rest of the 2nd paragraph provides details to support the author’s main point in S4. S5 and S6 address safety concerns, with specific statistics about the safety benefits of nuclear power. S7 introduces environmental benefits. S8 cites an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

2nd paragraph summary: The author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that there are both safety-related and environmental benefits to overcoming negative perceptions of nuclear power. The author supports his point with statistics, facts, and examples.

At this point, we may have a sense that the primary purpose of this passage is to challenge many people’s concerns that nuclear power plants are “particularly hazardous” and “uniquely expensive.”

We have seen that, in the 2nd paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, in the 3rd paragraph, the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” So, in the 3rd paragraph, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

In S9, notice the contrast marker “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part. Here, the author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10 supports the point stated by S9, saying that recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

In S11, the additional point marker “moreover” is a useful clue indicating that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above — in this case, the point above in S9. The author concludes the passage by stating a point similar to S9’s point, “overcoming these hurdles rapidly is … feasible,” saying “the future of nuclear power is promising.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly. The author supports his point that a rapid shift to nuclear power is possible, by citing two specific developments: new economical reactors and political support.

In reviewing the passage as a whole, it is important to note that the author’s overall tone is argumentative, not just descriptive. Throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the author presents a strong opinion in support of nuclear power and provides evidence of multiple types of benefits of nuclear power: safety, environmental, and economic.

Passage summary: The author presents and then challenges the impression widely held in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and uniquely expensive.

In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: We don’t need to understand the technical word “terawatt-hour” in S5 to answer the questions correctly. However, from the context, we can infer that it is a unit of energy. If you are curious, “tera” is a unit prefix in the metric system that means multiplication by one trillion, or 1012. Another common use of “tera” is in “terabytes” (TB), in the context of memory capacity.

We also don’t need to understand the technical phrase “closed fuel cycle,” in S10.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice that a common belief is presented: “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.”

When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion. So, it is helpful to be aware of the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented in this sentence includes two negative ideas about nuclear power plants:

- “particularly hazardous”

- “uniquely expensive”

Simplification: Many people in the U.S. believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.

S2: Supports S1, with an example about why people believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous.

Simplification: These plants bring to mind scary images of the nuclear disaster in Chernobyl.

S3: Supports S1, with a detail about the fear of “radiation exposure.”

Simplification: Thinking about the dangers of radiation exposure provokes a fearful mindset.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s excessive fear of nuclear power.

In the X part, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that some risk is involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.”

Simplification: the author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that it is important to overcome this fear.

S5: Supports S4, with a statistic about the safety of nuclear power, as compared with the safety of other methods of energy production.

Simplification: Statistics show that nuclear power generation is safer than other common methods, particularly coal power.

S6: Supports S4, by addressing a concern about nuclear waste

Notice that “in reality” is an opinion/contrast marker. The author is saying that “concerns about nuclear waste” are not really a big deal, once you take into account certain facts.

Simplification: Nuclear waste isn’t a significant problem, because it can be safely stored and takes up a very small volume of space.

S7: Supports the point stated by S4 that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above.

S5 and S6 provided details about the safety benefits of nuclear power. Now, S7 introduces the environmental benefits.

Simplification: Also, nuclear power could have great environmental benefits, because it is the best way to prevent further global warming.

S8: Supports S4, with an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

Simplification: With a supportive political and regulatory situation, there can be a rapid shift to nuclear power, as we can see from the examples of France and Sweden.

S9: Here the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” In the second paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

Notice the contrast marker, “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Simplification: The author acknowledges that there are indeed economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US, but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10: Specifies an economic obstacle/problem and a potential solution:

Problem: “lack of standardization and repetition”

Solution: “Generation IV reactors that can be mass-produced”

Also, notice the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.” The final part of the sentence, “consequently, they will likely generate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation,” is a positive prediction about the future low relative cost of nuclear power.

Simplification: Recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

S11: Supports the main point in S9 that overcoming the economic obstacles rapidly is possible.

Notice the additional point marker “moreover,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above. We also see the phrase “additional economic benefits.” So, this sentence is expanding on the economic benefits discussed in S10 and playing a role similar to that of S10 in supporting S9.

Simplification: Since production of these new reactors may have many additional economic benefits, it has inspired significant political support, which is an encouraging sign for the future of nuclear power.

question.text: The author of the passage is primarily concerned with

answers.text: explaining how efforts by social media platforms and major media outlets to maximize engagement and audience size result in distortion of political press coverage
considering issues related to the freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media
demonstrating why a free press is integral to the functioning of a democratic society
contending that the degree of freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media does not make sense
exploring ways in which the functioning of the press in America could be improved

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
To answer this Primary Purpose question, we can seek to understand how the important points of the passage indicate the primary purpose.

The first important point we come across is stated by the first paragraph of the passage.That point is that there are two sides to the freedom of the press: the freedom of the press is a “cornerstone,” meaning “important feature,” of American democracy, and the freedom of the press allows the media to influence outcomes of elections.

The first sentence of the 2ndparagraph appears to state an important point as well: “such influence,” meaning the influence mentioned in the 1stparagraph, “begins with what the media chooses to convey.”

The next important point is signaled by some strong language in the 3rdparagraph, “it would be hard to argue,” and that point is that “it would be hard to argue that the user is getting unbiased political news.”
Finally, most of the 4thparagraph is made up of opinions, all of which appear to be important points.The important points expressed in the 4thparagraph are the following:
- some people have questioned whether … the freedom of the press … makes sense.
- this questioning is not completely unwarranted
- a free press is integral to the functioning of a democratic society
- a better question to ask is that of what can be done within the context of freedom of the press to reduce the distortion of electoral results by the media.

So, we can see that the author discusses both sides of the situation involving the freedom of the press and arrives at a final conclusion regarding the issues discussed, “a better question to ask is ….”
Thus, the primary purpose of the passage is to consider issues related to the freedom of the press.
YOUR CHOICE(A) explaining how efforts by social media platforms and major media outlets to maximize engagement and audience size result in distortion of political press coverage
Trap: Too Narrow

The passage does do what this choice describes, but only in the 3rdparagraph, and the passage does more than what this choice describes.The passage also discusses how the media influence elections by deciding what to convey and considers the importance of the freedom of the press.
So, this choice does not capture the primary purpose of the passage as a whole.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) considering issues related to the freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media
As we saw in our Question Analysis, the passage does consider “issues related to the freedom of the press.”
Also, by reviewing what the passage says, we can see that the issues considered by the passage are related specifically to “the American media.”
So, this choice accurately describes the primary purpose of the passage.
(C) demonstrating why a free press is integral to the functioning of a democratic society
Trap: Wrong Verb

Notice that the passage does not ever “demonstrate why” the freedom of the press is integral to the functioning of a democratic society.Rather it simply states in the 1stand 4thparagraphs that the freedom of the press is a “cornerstone” and that the freedom of the press is “integral to the effective functioning of a democratic society” without “demonstrating why” these statements are true.
(D) contending that the degree of freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media does not make sense
To avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to be sure to keep straight which of the opinions that appear in the passage are the author’s and which are those of other people.

While the first sentence of the 4thparagraph does mention questioning “whether the virtually complete freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media makes sense,” that questioning is not done by the author.It’s something that the author says “some people” have done.
So, the author himself does not contend “that the degree of freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media does not make sense.”
(E) exploring ways in which the functioning of the press in America could be improved
Trap: Half-Right
This choice starts off with a correct verb because the passage is basically “exploring” considerations or issues related to the freedom of the press.
However, this choice goes wrong after the verb “exploring” because the passage does not mention any “ways in which the functioning of the press in America could be improved.”

The passage does in the last sentence bring up the question of “what can be done … to reduce the distortion of electoral results by the media,” but it does not answer that question by discussing any ways to reduce the distortion.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In the 1st paragraph, the author introduces two sides of a situation. The two sides of the situation are that the freedom of the press is a “cornerstone,” meaning “important feature,” of American democracy and that the freedom of the press allows the media to influence outcomes of elections.

Notice that, while the author does not directly state an opinion in this paragraph, the tone clues “cornerstone” and “provide voters with information they need” indicate that the author believes that the freedom of the press is important and beneficial.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st.paragraph introduces the topic that the passage goes on to discuss, which is the two sides of the freedom of the press.

The 2nd paragraph begins with the look above marker “SUCH influence,” and the 2nd paragraph presents an example of how the media influences elections, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph. This way is that the media chooses what it conveys, i.e., covers.

2nd paragraph summary: The 2nd paragraph presents an example of how the media influences elections, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph.

The 3rd paragraph further discusses the issue of the media influencing elections by bringing up another aspect of this influence, which is that both social media platforms and major media outlets present news in a biased way. The social media platforms present news in a biased way to attract users, and the major media outlets present news in a biased way to attract the interest of as large an audience as possible.

This paragraph also shows a negative result of the biased way in which major media outlets present news: voters get funneled “to either the far right or the far left of the political spectrum.”

Notice that, so far, the author has not presented an opinion about all this media influence. He’s simply describing it, though clearly presenting a somewhat negative picture.

3rd paragraph summary: The 3rd paragraph discusses the fact that, to achieve goals, social media platforms and major media outlets present news in a biased way.

So far, the author has described the situation involving the media influencing the outcomes of elections and the electorate without presenting any opinions about the situation, but now in the 4th paragraph, the author presents some opinions

The first opinion is that of “some people,” who have “questioned whether the virtually complete freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media makes sense.” Notice that this opinion is not the author’s opinion, though the author does follow up this opinion by expressing an opinion of his own, which is that “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.”

Then, the author wraps up the passage with two important points.

The first important point is signaled by the contrast marker “however,” since contrasts are generally connected to important points, and this point is that “the free press is integral to the functioning of a democratic society.” We can see that with this sentence, the author is once again bringing up the other side of the situation that was brought up in the 1st paragraph, which is that freedom of the press is important.

Then the most important point of the passage is introduced by the conclusion marker “therefore.” Why is this last sentence the most important point? Because, in this sentence, the author finally states a strong opinion about the situation involving the freedom of the press by using the strong language “a better question to ask” and then saying that what that better question is, which is “what can be done within the context of freedom of the press to reduce the distortion of electoral results by the media.”

We can see that the author believes that the freedom of the press is important and has therefore concluded that the thing to do is to figure out what to do about media influence while leaving the freedom of the press in place.

4th paragraph summary: The 4th paragraph discusses some considerations related to addressing the issue of the distorted influence of the media on electoral outcomes and then presents a final conclusion of the author’s about what how this issue should be addressed.

Passage summary: The passage introduces a situation that has two sides, the importance of the freedom of the press and the influence that the media has on the outcomes of elections, illustrates the issue of the influence of the media on the outcomes of election with examples, and wraps up by discussing how the issue might best be addressed.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

systems, programs, sets of rules

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Introduces the first side of the situation.

The first side of the situation is that freedom of the press is a “cornerstone,” meaning “important feature,” of American democracy.

Simplification: The freedom of the press is important for American democracy.

S2: Introduces the second side of the situation. 

The second side of the situation is that the media exploits freedom of the press to influence election outcomes, not always in a good way. 

By presenting these two sides of a situation, this sentence foreshadows that the passage will discuss these two sides of this situation.

Simplification: The media uses freedom of the press to influence the outcomes of elections.

S3: Uses the look above marker “SUCH influence” to introduce one example of how the media influences elections as introduced in S2, by choosing what to convey.

Simplification: One way in which the media influences elections is by choosing what to talk about.

S4: Provides detail on the fact introduced by S3 that the media can choose what to convey.

Simplification: Reporters can choose what to cover, so sometimes candidates don’t get coverage and are “left almost invisible to voters.”

S5: Uses the look above marker “SUCH candidates” to describe what happens when the candidates mentioned in the previous sentence are “left almost invisible to voters.”

Simplification: The candidates “left almost invisible to voters” struggle to win elections even when they would have done good things, because voters don’t recognize the candidates’ names.

S6: Uses the additional point marker “in addition” to introduce another example of how the media influences elections, the use of “sophisticated algorithms” by social media platforms.

Simplification: Many people access news by using social media platforms, which use algorithms to decide what news to show users.

S7: Expands on the discussion of social media “algorithms” begun by S6 by presenting the results of the use of algorithms by social media.

Notice the strong language, “it would be hard to argue ….” This strong language is a clue indicating that the author is stating an important point in this sentence.

Also, notice that “it would be hard to argue that that user is getting unbiased political news,” is sort of a double negative that means, essentially, “it can be argued that that user is getting biased political news.”

Simplification: Social media algorithms show users stories meant to capture their attention, and as a result, users get biased political news.

S8: Introduces another reason why media influence elections: major media outlets have the “goal” of attracting the interest of as large an audience as possible.

S9: Uses the look above marker “THIS aim” to continue to discuss the “goal” mentioned in S8.

Notice that this sentence uses the causation marker “since” to show a cause and effect. The cause is “voters have the tendency to seek out news that does not challenge what they believe is true,” and the effect is that major media outlets can achieve their “aim” or “goal” by “reflecting biases.” Sentences that mention causes and effects are usually closely connected to the primary purpose of the passage, and we can see that this sentence is mentioning “biases” as S7 did. So, bias in media appears to be a key theme of this passage.

Simplification: Since people seek out news they agree with, major media outlets can achieve their goal of attracting a large audience by being biased.

S10: Uses the look above marker “THIS ‘selective exposure’” to continue the discussion of major media outlets presenting biased news to attract a large audience.

“Funnels many voters to either the far right or the far left” means, basically, “directs many voters to either the far right or the far left.” So, this sentence is showing how media outlets affect elections by pushing voters to the right or left.

Also, notice that “not-so-objective” is another way of saying “biased.” So, this sentence is once again discussing media bias.

Simplification: There are many biased news outlets that push voters to the far right or far left.

S11: Uses the causation marker “consequently” to present a result of what the passage has said in the preceding paragraphs: “Consequently, some people have questioned whether … freedom of the press … makes sense.”

Notice that this sentence presents an opinion of the author’s: “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.” Directly stated opinions of the author are good clues about the primary purpose of the passage.

Simplification: Because of the effects of media’s biased coverage, some people question whether freedom of the press makes sense, and this questioning is reasonable since “the fact that the free press has a distorted influence on electoral results is a significant issue.”

S12: Uses the additional point marker “furthermore” to present more support for the author’s opinion “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.”

Simplification: Another reason why “this questioning is not completely unwarranted” is that it would be hard to do something about the not so good influence of the media on elections.

S13: Uses the contrast marker “however” to present information that contrasts with what has just been said.

“Integral” means “essential” or “necessary.” So, since something “essential” is important, this sentence is a strong statement about freedom of the press.

Simplification: A fact that contrasts with the facts that support the questioning of the freedom of the press is the fact that freedom of the press is necessary.

S14: Uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to state a final opinion of the author’s.

When the last sentence of the passage states an opinion of the author’s, that opinion is usually either the main point of the passage or very closely connected to the main point and primary purpose of the passage. So, this sentence will be a useful clue when it comes time to answer a primary purpose question about this passage.

Simplification: Therefore, rather than question the freedom of the press, people should focus on reducing media “distortion” or “bias” without changing the freedom of the press itself.

question.text: The primary purpose of the passage is to

answers.text: demonstrate that a particular type of scientific measurement produces results that have no value
explain what must be done in order for the rate of extinction of species to decrease
outline a viewpoint that serves to call into question the validity of a common scientific approach
challenge an assumption on which methods of measuring genetic diversity commonly rely
present data that serves to support one scientific opinion over another

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To correctly answer a Primary Purpose question, we look for clues in the passage and seek to understand how the structure and important points of the passage indicate what the primary purpose of the passage is.

For the purpose of identifying important points, it’s helpful to note that passages will often involve some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions.So, put extra emphasis on contrast markers, such as “however” or “but.” In this case, we see that the passage starts off by presenting the perspective and approach of “many reputable scientists” in S1 and then, in S2, uses the contrast marker “although” to introduce the contrasting view of Teixeira and Huber.

In answering a Primary Purpose question, we also must make sure not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions described in the passage.In this case, as we discussed in our Passage Analysis, the author describes the opinions of others and does not present or support an opinion of his own.
(A) demonstrate that a particular type of scientific measurement produces results that have no value
Trap: Wrong Verb
Notice that this choice has an argumentative tone.
The verb “demonstrate” is an opinion marker, with a meaning similar to “show” or “prove.”
From our Passage Analysis, we see that the passage does not demonstrate, show, or prove anything.Rather, itdescribestwo contrasting opinions without presenting or supporting a position of the author.
Trap: Stretch (goes too far)
Furthermore, even if this choice did not use the wrong verb and said, “describe an argument that” instead of “demonstrate that,” this choice would still be a Stretch.

Teixeira and Huber do indeed “challenge” and disagree with the scientists in S1, who are using a “particular type of scientific measurement.” However, Teixeira and Huber do not make the extreme statement that the results produced by that measurement have “NO value.” Rather, as we see in our Passage Analysis, Teixeira and Huber make a more balanced and nuanced critique.
(B) explain what must be done in order for the rate of extinction of species to decrease
Trap: Wrong Verb / Mix-Up
Notice that the word “must” is an opinion marker, so “explain whatmustbe done” has an argumentative tone.
From our Passage Analysis, we see that the author isdescribingtwo contrasting opinions, but does not make anargumentof his own orexplainwhat “must be done.”

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that S5 mentions an opinion about “what must be done”; S5 says, “they argue that… mitigation measures must be taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.”
However, notice that it isTeixeira and Huberwho make this argument, not theauthor.
Furthermore, this choice does not address the main point of the passage, the disagreement between Teixeira and the “many reputable scientists.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) outline a viewpoint that serves to call into question the validity of a common scientific approach
A good place to start is to check the tone of the verb.“Outline” is a non-argumentative verb that is similar to “describe.”

From our Passage Analysis, we can see that “outline a viewpoint” matches thedescriptivetone of the passage.Other than the first sentence, the passage focuses on describing the viewpoint of Teixeira and Huber.There are many opinion markers for Teixeira and Huber’s viewpoint in these sentences, including “challenges,” “they acknowledge that,” “maintain that,” “contend that,” “should,” “argue that,” and “must.”

Also, Teixeira and Huber’s viewpoint does serve to “call into question” the usefulness or “validity” of the “common scientific approach” of using measurements of genetic diversity for assessing extinction risk.
We may quickly get the sense that this choice matches our Passage Analysis, but let’s confirm that the wording matches the passage.

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passageserves tocall intoquestionthe validity ofchallengesthe assumption (S2)acommonscientific approachmanyreputable scientists in the field of conservation genetics have been measuring the genetic diversity of species and using the results as bases for assessments of species extinction risk (S1)this type of analysis has been appliedextensively(S2)
We can see that this choice matches the passage and accurately describes the passage’s primary purpose.
(D) challenge an assumption on which methods of measuring genetic diversity commonly rely
Trap: Wrong Verb
First, notice that the word “challenge” has a strong argumentative tone.
However, from our Passage Analysis, we see that the authordescribestwo contrasting views, but does not make anargumentof his own.
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that very similar wording appears in this choice and in the passage: “challenges the assumption” does indeed appear in S2.
However, this matching wording is a trap.It is always important to distinguish whether an opinion is the author’s own opinion or someone else’s opinion.
Here, we must notice that it is Teixeira and Huber who are doing the “challenging,,” not the author.
Trap: Distortion
Furthermore, even if this choice did not use the wrong verb and said, “describe a challenge of” instead of “challenge,” this choice would still be a Distortion.
Teixeira and Huber do indeed “challenge” and disagree with the scientists in S1, who are indeed “measuring genetic diversity.”

However, Teixeira and Huber do not challenge an assumption upon which the “methods ofmeasuringgenetic diversity” rely.In other words, they don’t disagree that the methods of measuring themselves work.

Rather, they challenge theconclusionsthat can be drawn fromresultsgenerated via use of these methods.Specifically, in S2, they challenge therelationshipbetween genetic diversity andextinction risk: “challenges the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a speciesis a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”
(E) present data that serves to support one scientific opinion over another
Trap: Wrong Verb (support)
First, it is helpful to put extra emphasis on opinion markers.Here, notice the opinion marker, “support.”
Is there any evidence in the passage that the author supports one opinion over another? This choice implies that the author has a strong opinion of his own.
However, from our Passage Analysis, we see that the author isdescribingtwo contrasting opinions, but does NOT express an opinion of his own.
If you picked this choice, you may have mistaken Teixeira and Huber’s opinion for the author’s opinion.
Trap: Too Narrow

Furthermore, the wording “present DATA” doesn’t match the purpose of the passage as a whole.The definition of “data” is “statistics and specific numerical evidence.” S2 through S5 discuss many opinions of Teixeira and Huber, but very little actual data.The only data cited is in S5, regarding the area of habitat loss.

When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage has only one paragraph. Therefore, we don’t have the structural clues that come from the transitions from one paragraph to another. So, when there is only one paragraph, it is particularly helpful to notice contrast markers and opinion markers, which give us clues to the structure and important points.

As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast or opinions in the first two sentences. In this passage, the most helpful clues to the structure are markers in S2: the contrast marker, “although,” and the opinion marker, “challenges.” These markers signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.” in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship is a relation such that, when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

It’s important to notice that the tone of these sentences is descriptive: the author is not making an argument of his own. Rather, the author is describing two contrasting views: that of the scientists in S1, and that of Teixeira and Huber in S2.

So, from these first two sentences, we already have a good sense of the basic structure of the passage, and it makes sense that the sentences that follow will expand upon Teixeira and Huber’s view presented in S2.

Indeed, notice the many additional opinion markers in the final three sentences: “they acknowledge that,” “maintain that,” “contend that,” “should,” “argue that,” and “must.”

Let’s summarize these additional points of Teixeira and Huber:

S3: although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival.

S4: to prevent species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

Passage summary: In the context of preventing species’ extinction, Teixeira and Huber disagree with many scientists’ focus on genetic diversity and contend that it makes more sense to focus on habitat destruction and to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.

In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: By describing what some scientists have been doing because they are “Concerned by the fact that the rate of species extinction … is at a level unprecedented,” this sentence implies that these scientists are of the view that “measuring the genetic diversity of species” makes sense or is important in the context of species extinction.

Simplification: Because scientists are concerned about the high rate of species extinction, they have been using measures of genetic diversity to assess extinction risk.

S2: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point.

Also notice the opinion marker, “challenges the assumption that.”

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, the presence of “this type of analysis” signals that we must look above to understand what “this type of analysis” refers to. We see that “this type of analysis” refers to the scientists’ work of “measuring” and “assessments” in S1.

These markers, particularly the word “challenges,” signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists mentioned above in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk”; in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.

S3: Notice two more opinion markers, “they acknowledge that” and “they maintain that.” This sentence follows up on S2 with more details and reasoning behind Teixeira and Huber’s disagreement with the scientists in S1.

Also, notice the contrast marker, “while,” which follows the structure “while X, Y.” This structure often concedes exceptions in the X part, and then makes the main point in the Y part.

Here, the X part, “they acknowledge that, in SOME cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” represents an exception to Teixeira and Huber’s main point in the Y part, “they maintain that the MAJORITY of genetic diversity is ‘neutral,’ in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival. (Note that this sentence supports the main point of S2: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.)

S4: Notice two more opinion markers, “contend that” and “should.”

Also, notice the contrast marker “instead of.” What are the two things that are in contrast?

Genetic diversity and habitat destruction – “instead of genetic diversity, the primary concern … should be the issue of habitat destruction.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, in preventing species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: This sentence starts with the support marker “Citing the fact that X,” which is logically followed by the opinion marker “they argue that Y.” Often, both support and opinion are presented in a sentence, with a structure similar to “Since X (support), Y (opinion).”

There is another opinion marker, “must,” at the end of the sentence: “mitigation measures must be taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.” This wording signifies a proposal for what should be done about the problem. In this case, the problem cited is habitat loss and ecological disruption.

Simplification: Since large areas of wildlife habitat have been destroyed, Teixeira and Huber argue that humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

question.text: The primary purpose of the passage is to

answers.text: compare different types of acts of generosity
consider multiple explanations for a puzzling aspect of human conduct
explain the functions of certain neurotransmitters in the human brain
describe research that illustrates the virtues of unselfish behavior
discuss reasons why people provide assistance to friends or family members

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To correctly answer a Primary Purpose question, we look for clues in the passage and seek to understand how the structure and important points of the passage indicate what the primary purpose of the passage is.
In many passages, the important points are found in the leading sentences of the paragraphs.

However, in this passage, S3, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …? is our most useful clue, because a rhetorical questiongenerally signals that the primary purpose is to answer this question, with one or moreexplanationsthat will follow.Indeed, this passage follows the rhetorical question by discussing two explanations: the 2ndparagraph discusses the biological argument, and the 3rdparagraph discusses Ricard’s explanation.
(A) compare different types of acts of generosity
Trap: Too Narrow
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “different types of acts of generosity” are indeed compared, in S1, S2, and S10.
However, a Primary Purpose correct answer must cover the main ideas of the passage as a whole.

The primary purpose of the passage is explaining one type of act of generosity, the “anonymous altruistic behavior” that is introduced in S2, referenced in the rhetorical question marker in S3, and then explained in the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs.
It is also helpful to notice that this choice completely ignores the 2ndparagraph.
YOUR CHOICE(B) consider multiple explanations for a puzzling aspect of human conduct

Notice that the verb “consider” is a good match for the tone of this passage.Most of the passage has a descriptive tone, though the author also makes the judgment that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” (S9) and “persuasive” (S12).
From our Question Analysis, we may quickly get the sense that this choice matches the passage, but it’s a good habit to confirm that the exact wording is correct:

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagemultiple explanationsbiological argument (S9)a more compelling perspective (S9)puzzlingperplexing (S2)aspect of human conductanonymous altruistic behavior (S2)What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor? (S3)
We can see that this choice accurately describes the primary purpose of the passage.
(C) explain the functions of certain neurotransmitters in the human brain
Trap: Too Narrow
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the “functions of certain neurotransmitters in the human brain” are indeed explained, in S6, S7, and S8.
However, these functions are only specific details; a Primary Purpose correct answer must cover the main ideas of the passage as a whole.
This choice ignores the third paragraph and the rhetorical question in S3, which asks about the motivation for anonymous altruistic behavior.
(D) describe research that illustrates the virtues of unselfish behavior
Trap: Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the passage does “describe research,” throughout the 2ndparagraph and in S11.Also, the topic of the passage is indeed “unselfish behavior.”
However, does the research “illustrate the virtues” of unselfish behavior? (Note that “virtues” means “good qualities.”)
No, the research doesn’t illustrate good qualities of unselfish behavior.Rather, the purpose of the research is to explain why people engage in such behavior.
(E) discuss reasons why people provide assistance to friends or family members
Trap: Half-Right / Too Narrow
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the passage does “discuss reasons why people provide assistance.”
However, is “to friends or family members” also correct?

No, the passage discusses assistance to friends and family only in S1 and S10.In the bigger picture, the passage focuses on explaining “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which the passage distinguishes as a type of assistance different from assistance to friends and family.The passage defines anonymous altruistic behavior as “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting some information about “people’s benevolence”; S1 says that people’s benevolence both is directed toward their kin and extends to those outside their families.

This information presented by S1 turns out to be background information; the first paragraph makes its point in S2 and S3.

S2 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals both that it’s likely that an important point is coming and that a contrast is being made. What are the two things that are contrasted? S1 has introduced one type of generous behavior, which is often reciprocated, and now, S2 introduces a different type of generous behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is a “perplexing phenomenon to scientists.” Anonymous altruistic behavior turns out to be the main topic of the passage.

Having brought up “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2, the first paragraph sets the stage for the rest of the passage with S3. S3 has a rhetorical question marker(question mark). Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question begins with “What would motivate individuals to …,” which means, essentially, “Why?” When the author asks “Why?” we have a causation marker, indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then discussed, in the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. We must pay close attention whenever explanations are discussed, because explanations tend to be important parts of passages.

S3 also defines “anonymous altruistic behavior, saying that it is “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” In defining “anonymous altruistic behavior,” S3 gives the reader a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the generous behavior described in S1, which is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

1st paragraph summary: Both scientists and other scholars are interested in explaining anonymous altruistic behavior. Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible answer the rhetorical question in S3. The possible answer, or explanation, is that “there are neural bases for such acts.” The look above marker in S4, “SUCH acts,” refers to the “anonymous deeds” in S3. So, this marker helps us understand the relationship between S4 and S3.

Then, in S5, S6, and S7, the 2nd paragraph provides some details of how “researchers” investigated this possible explanation and what they found. These details are rather involved. So, our goal in reading these sentences is not necessarily to totally understand or seek to memorize every one of these details but rather to understand in general how the study described worked.

In S8, we see the opinion marker, “contended.” Here, the main conclusion of the researchers is presented: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author’s tone in the 2nd paragraph is descriptive, and the author does not provide her own opinion.

2nd paragraph summary: The author presents one possible explanation for anonymous altruistic behavior: having studied brain activity, researchers concluded that hormones that cause positive sensations, i.e., biological factors, play an important role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

The 3rd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

In this case, by using “while X, Y” in S9, the author connects the 2nd paragraph to the 3rd paragraph; in the “while X” part of S9, the author concedes that the argument presented in the 2nd paragraph “may … lead to some useful insights,” and then, in the Y part of S9, the author presents the topic of the 3rd paragraph, the “more compelling perspective” of Matthieu Ricard. (Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.)

S10 and S11 provide details about Ricard’s perspective and the types of evidence he uses to support his points.

Finally, in S12, the author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.” Notice that, in judging Ricard’s conclusion “persuasive,” the author is once again stating an opinion of her own.

Also, we can see that there is clear contrast because Ricard’s conclusion and that of the researchers discussed in the second paragraph. S12 says that Ricard’s “persuasive case” is that serving others can be “genuinely selfless,” whereas S8 says that the researchers discussed in the second paragraph contend that such behavior can be “seemingly selfless.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author presents a second explanation that she believes is more compelling than the “biological argument” in the 2nd paragraph: Ricard’s perspective is that the motivation for serving others can be genuinely selfless and that this behavior can be cultivated through specific practices.

Passage summary: The author poses a question about why people engage in anonymous altruistic behavior, presents a biological explanation based on a brain imaging study, and then presents a second explanation that the author prefers.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage
