Note: To answer the questions correctly, we don’t need to know the technical vocabulary: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), mesolimbic pathway, neurotransmitter, dopamine, hypothalamus, oxytocin, hormones, empathy-altruism hypothesis. We can understand the meaning of each sentence from the surrounding language and the definitions that are provided in the passage. (For example, the passage defines the mesolimbic pathway: “the mesolimbic pathway, the reward center of the brain that is responsible for releasing the neurotransmitter associated with pleasure.”)
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice causation marker “because.”

Simplification: People are often kind to not only relatives but also others, because this kindness is often returned.

S2: Notice the contrast marker “however.” This sentence introduces a different type of kind behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is “perplexing to scientists,” meaning that it is harder to explain.

Note: we may not be sure what the meaning of “anonymous altruistic behavior” is at first, but we are basically given a definition in the next sentence. In general, we can look to the surrounding context if we don’t understand something.

Simplification: A different type of kind behavior, anonymous altruistic behavior, has been puzzling for scientists to explain and an important topic for other scholars.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

The question posed, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …?” is asking about the reason/cause/explanation for these anonymous deeds. So, we can expect the rest of the passage to discuss possible explanations.

This sentence also helps the reader to understand the full meaning of S2. It defines what is meant by the “anonymous altruistic behavior” introduced in S2: “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” It also gives us a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the kind behavior described in S1 that is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

Simplification: Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

S4: Notice the look above marker “SUCH acts.” We must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to. Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” As we noted in our analysis of S3, these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible explanation to answer the rhetorical question in S3.

Note: if we don’t know what “neural” means, we can see from S5 that it’s related to brain activity: “researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brain.”

Simplification: A certain study sought to find an explanation, related to brain activity, for anonymous altruistic behavior.

S5: Provides details about the design of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: Using fMRI, the researchers analyzed the subjects’ brain activity while the subjects decided whether to keep money or make a donation.

S6: Provides details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: The reward center of the brain that releases dopamine, which is associated with pleasure, became active when a donation was made.

S7: Adds more details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” S7 is very similar to S6, but discusses a different brain region and hormone.

Simplification: The region of the brain that produces oxytocin, which is associated with cooperation and trust, also became active when a donation was made.

S8: Notice the opinion marker “contended.” Also, notice that the word “so” connects a premise and a conclusion, in the structure “Premise, so Conclusion”

This sentence presents what the researchers have concluded from the findings of their study presented in the previous sentences.

Simplification: Since dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations, the scientists argue that biological factors play a role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

S9: Introduces a “compelling,” i.e., persuasive, alternative to the “biological argument” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Notice that the wording “THIS biological argument” is a look above marker and refers to the biological argument directly above in S8: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument.

Simplification: There is another perspective, that of Matthieu Ricard, which is more compelling than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

S10: Provides details about Ricard’s perspective.

Notice that “but” is a contrast marker in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard’s perspective is that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally, but it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.

S11: Discusses a type of evidence that Ricard uses, cognitive science research.

Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.

S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.”

Notice that the author repeats her positive judgment of Ricard’s perspective: S9 says that it’s “compelling,” and S12 says that it’s “persuasive.”

Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be genuinely selfless.” It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.

question.text: The primary purpose of the passage is to

answers.text: describe the effects on telecommunications systems of the Carrington Event of 1859
ease concerns regarding the effects a large CME that reaches Earth could have on electronic systems
identify steps that humanity can take to protect itself from a future powerful CME
explain why modern communications networks are less likely to be disrupted by a CME than the primitive communications systems of the past
clarify understanding of the effects a CME could have on certain assets

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
To identify the primary purpose of this passage, we can consider the simple story of the passage.
The passage begins by presenting the concept of a CME and the example of the Carrington Event.

Then, in S3, the passage states an important point signaled by the contrast word “however” and the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating.” That point is “the fact that the 1859 CME caused only minor disruptions to communications systems cannot be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems are safe from the effects of CMEs.”
As we discussed in our Passage Analysis, what the author is implying in S3 is that today’s communication systems arenotsafe from the effects of CMEs.
Then, the rest of the passage goes on to present reasons to believe that today’s communication systems, along with data on hard drives, are not safe from CMEs.
Thus, the simple story is that today’s communications systems are not safe from CMEs.So then, the primary purpose is to present this simple story.
(A) describe the effects on telecommunications systems of the Carrington Event of 1859
Trap: Mentioned Frequently

We have to be careful not to choose this choice just because the Carrington event is mentioned multiple times in the passage.The fact that something is mentioned frequently in a passage does not necessarily mean that discussing that thing is the primary purpose of the passage.
Trap: Too Narrow
Theeffectsof the Carrington event are only briefly mentioned in S3, which says that it “caused only minor disruptions to communications systems.”
Notice that S5 does not mention “the effects of the Carrington Event of 1859” but rather says what “a Carrington Event-level storm” could cause.
So, what this choice describes is too narrow to be the primary purpose of the passage as a whole.
(B) ease concerns regarding the effects a large CME that reaches Earth could have on electronic systems
Trap: Wrong Verb
“Ease” means “calm” or “soothe.”
Clearly, the purpose of this passage is not to soothe “concerns regarding the effects a large CME that reaches Earth could have.” On the contrary, the passage presents reasons to be concerned.
(C) identify steps that humanity can take to protect itself from a future powerful CME
This choice is meant to catch us thinking about what the passage could do rather than what it does do.
The passage could have presented steps humanity can take to protect itself from a future powerful CME, but it doesn’t.
(D) explain why modern communications networks are less likely to be disrupted by a CME than the primitive communications systems of the past
Trap: Opposite

Notice that this choice describes the opposite of what the passage does, since the passage indicates that modern communications networks aremorelikely, rather thanlesslikely, to be disrupted by a CME than the primitive communications systems of the past.
YOUR CHOICE(E) clarify understanding of the effects a CME could have on certain assets
This choice may not use the wording we expected to see, since the passage does not mention “understanding” or “assets.”
However, “communications infrastructure” and “data stored on computer hard drives” are “assets.” So, “certain assets” in this choice matches the passage.

Also, the passage does “clarify understanding” of the effects of CMEs, as we can see by considering what S3 says: “the fact that the 1859 CME caused only minor disruptions … cannot be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems are safe from the effects of CMEs.”

Notice that S3, along with the rest of the passage, is “clarifying” that, while it may seem that the fact that the 1859 CME caused only minor disruptions means that today’s communications systems are safe from CMEs, today’s communications systems are not in fact safe.
So, this choice accurately describes the primary purpose of the passage.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage begins by introducing the concept of a coronal mass ejection (CME), and first thing that jumps out at us as we’re reading is that S1 says that “in most cases” a CME “never reaches Earth BUT.” Contrasts words such as “but” normally introduce important points. So, the important point to notice here is “but, when a CME does reach Earth, it induces a flurry of strong magnetic and electrical activity.”

Then, the passage presents an example of a CME, the CME that caused “the largest geomagnetic storm ever recorded” during the Carrington Event in 1859.

So far, the passage has been descriptive, simply presenting information about CMEs and the example of the Carrington Event.

Then, in S3, the contrast word “however” and the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating …” indicate that S3 is stating an important point. In fact, with S3, the author begins to present his main point.

So, what is the point the author is making?

We can see that, in saying, “the fact … CANNOT be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems ARE safe from the effects of CMEs,” the author is implying that today’s communication systems are NOT safe from CMEs.

Then, S4 begins to explain why today’s communication systems are not safe from CMEs – the systems are not “primitive”; rather society is now “highly dependent upon electrical signals” – and S5 uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to present a conclusion about the current situation, “a Carrington Event-level storm today could cause a devastating ‘internet apocalypse.’”

Then the rest of the passage simply presents another example to support the point that today’s communications systems are not safe from CMEs, the example of the “Miyake event” of 775. The implications of this part of the passage are that storms even bigger than the Carrington Event are possible and could cause “almost … complete destruction of communication infrastructure” and “near total loss of the date stored on computer hard drives.”

Passage summary: The passage introduces the concept of a CME and then proceeds to make the argument that today’s communication infrastructure is not safe from CMEs by saying that today’s communication infrastructure is not primitive like past electrical infrastructure and by describing what would occur if storms like ones that occurred in the past were to occur today.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Similar Words

coronal mass ejection

a burst of matter and magnetic fields released by the sun

induces

results in, causes, gives rise to

flurry

short period of activity, burst, surge

pummeled

hit or beat repeatedly

infrastructure

system, network, foundation

apocalypse

disaster, catastrophe, destruction

megastorm

very powerful storm

incalculably

too great to be calculated, immensely, extremely

allay

reduce, sooth, calm

We don’t need to understand the technical words “magnetosphere” and “geomagnetic.” We can understand the passage and correctly answer every question without knowing exactly what these words mean.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: Introduces the concept of a coronal mass ejection (CME) and the effect a CME can have on Earth.

Simplification: A coronal mass ejection (CME) that reaches Earth causes strong magnetic and electrical activity

S2: Introduces an example of storm caused by a CME, the Carrington Event in 1859.

Notice that this sentence says that the geomagnetic storm “induced,” meaning “caused,” by the Carrington Event was “the largest geomagnetic storm ever recorded.” An extreme fact such as this one will likely play an important role in the passage. After all, it’s almost certain that the author has mentioned such an extreme fact for the purpose of making a point.

S3: There are two signs that this sentence states an important point.

The first is the contrast marker “however,” since contrasts are often connected to important points of a passage.

The second is the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating ….”

We can see that, in this sentence, the author is making a strong assertion that will likely be supported and discussed further as the passage progresses.

Simplification: The fact that the 1859 CME didn’t cause much trouble doesn’t mean that present communications systems are safe from CMEs.

S4: Uses the comparison marker “whereas” to express a comparison.

Comparisons are usually important parts of passages, and this comparison is indeed important, because the fact that, unlike in 1859, people now live in a “globally wired society” both explains why “today’s communications systems” are not safe from the effects of CMEs, as implied by S3, and supports S5, as we will see.

S5: Uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to present the consequence of what S4 says.

Simplification: Because, as S4 says, people live in a “globally wired society,” if an event like the Carrington Event were to occur today, it would cause huge communication problems.

S6: Uses the additional point marker “furthermore” to provide addition support for the author’s strong assertion in S3 that the fact that the 1859 CME didn’t cause much trouble doesn’t mean that present communications systems are safe from a large CME.

The implication of S6 is that, since such a large storm occurred in 775, a similar one could occur today.

Simplification: The Carrington event isn’t even the biggest “geomagnetic storm” ever. A much bigger one occurred in 775.

SB: Continues to support the strong assertion in S3 by saying that a storm like the one that occurred in 775 would cause “incalculably great” damage.

S9: Continues to support the strong assertion in S3 by providing more detail on the damage that would be caused by a storm like that one that occurred in 775.

question.text: The primary purpose of the passage is to

answers.text: argue against the use of a popular method of achieving stretching goals
discuss a contention regarding approaches to stretching
outline the problems involved with a popular stretching method
explain various procedures for stretching
advocate the use of certain stretching methods

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To correctly answer a Primary Purpose question, we look for clues in the passage and seek to understand how the structure and important points of the passage indicate what the primary purpose of the passage is.
In this case, the contrast marker “HOWEVER” at the beginning of S2 is a clue that helps us to identify the primary purpose.

Notice the statements in S2 and S3 that follow “HOWEVER,” which are “leading researchers and strength and conditioning coaches … call into question multiple aspects of this method,” and “These experts make the case … that there are superior alternative stretching approaches.” These statements present the author’s main point, which is then expanded upon and further discussed by everything that follows those statements.

The other key clue we can use to identify the primary purpose of the passage is at the beginning of the 2ndparagraph, the clause “Although there is a convincing body of research to substantiate the view of these experts.” The fact that the author presents the caveats mentioned in the 2ndparagraph in the context of “the view of these experts” helps us to confirm that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss the “case” or “view” of the experts.
So, what we see in both the 1stparagraph and the 2ndparagraph indicates that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss the experts’ “case.”
(A) argue against the use of a popular method of achieving stretching goals
Trap:Half-Right / Wrong Verb
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that a “popular method of achieving stretching goals” is mentioned in S1.
However, notice the verb in this choice, “argue.” Does the author “argueagainst the use of” Static Stretching prior to vigorous activity?
From our Passage Analysis, we see that the authordescribes“the case” or “view” of experts but does not ever “argue” that Static Stretching prior to vigorous activity should not be used.
As always, we must be careful not to mix up the views of the author and others.
YOUR CHOICE(B) discuss a contention regarding approaches to stretching
It’s important to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive or opinionated.

From our Passage Analysis, we can see that, rather than present his own opinion, the authordescribesthe perspectives and opinions of others – amateur athletes, leading researchers and coaches, and Kit Laughlin – and describes some approaches to stretching.
So, “discuss” matches the descriptive tone of the passage.

Now, since the passage discusses multiple opinions on stretching, we might be tempted to eliminate this choice, which mentions only one “contention.” However, we have to remember that this question is about the “primary purpose” of the passage, which may be focused on only one of the opinions discussed.So, since the passage does discuss some opinions, i.e., contentions, we better check to see whether “discuss a contention” matches the primary purpose of the passage.

From our Question Analysis, we see that “however” at the beginning of S2 is a clue indicating that S2 and S3 state the main point of the passage, which is that “leading researchers … call into question …,” and “make the case ….” Then we see that the sentences that follow S2 expand upon and support the point stated by S2 and S3.

Then, even though the 2ndparagraph presents caveats, the 2ndparagraph starts by saying, “Although there is a convincing body of research to substantiate the view of these experts.” So, even the second paragraph is, in a way, further discussion of the experts’ “case” or “view.”

Since a “case” or “view” matches “contention,” it turns out that this choice accurately describes the primary purpose of the passage, which is to discuss the “case,” “contention,” or “view” of the experts.
(C) outline the problems involved with a popular stretching method
Trap: Too Narrow
We must be aware of singular and plural nouns in answer choices.In this case, we must notice that this choice mentions a single “stretching method.”

A Primary Purpose correct answer must cover the passage as a whole.So, this choice does not match the passage, because the passage discusses three methods, Static Stretching, PNF, and DS, not “a stretching method.”
Trap: Distortion

It’s true that S3 says about Static Stretching prior to vigorous activity, “this sequence of activities produces results inferior to those ….” At first glance, this wording may seem to match with “problems involved with a popular stretching method,” but “discuss problems” is a distortion of what the passage does.

Notice that, in saying, “this sequence of activities produces results inferior to those …,” and “there are superior alternative stretching approaches,” the passage does not “discuss problems involved” in Static Stretching prior to vigorous activity.In other words, does the passage mention anyproblemswith the approach? No.The passage is simply comparing Static Stretching prior to vigorous activity with other approaches, indicating that the other approaches are better.
(D) explain various procedures for stretching
Trap: Too Narrow

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that S4 and S5 do indeed “explain various procedures for stretching,” because these sentences explain the details of the PNF and DS stretching approaches.
However, this choice is not correct, because it describes the functions of S4 and S5 only rather than the primary purpose of the passage as a whole.
In general, we have to be careful not mistake supporting details for main ideas.
(E) advocate the use of certain stretching methods
Trap: Wrong Verb

The author does notadvocatefor the use of methods.Rather the authordescribesothers’ opinions.“Advocate” means “recommend” and is more argumentative that what the author does in this case.If, for example, the author said “athletesshoulduse the PNF approach,” that wording would match this choice.
Notice also, that PNF and DS are mentioned only as examples.The author does not advocate for the use of these “certain methods.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It is helpful to notice that the first two sentences of the 1st paragraph follow the common myth structure “Many people believe X. HOWEVER, Y.” The wording in S1, “prevalent perception,” has the same meaning as “many people believe.” Then, S2 begins with the contrast marker, “however,” which signals that an idea in contrast with the “prevalent perception” is being presented, the opinion of “leading researchers and … coaches.”

“HOWEVER” is often followed by an important point, and in this case, the two statements that follow “however,” which are “leading researchers and strength and conditioning coaches … call into question multiple aspects of this method …” and “These experts make the case …,” present important points.

Now, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity,” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers” and “coaches,” in S2. What exactly do they disagree about?

In S1, “amateur athletes” believe that the “ideal stretching protocol … involves Static Stretching … prior to vigorous activity.” S2 then says, “HOWEVER, researchers … call into question multiple aspects of this method.”

It’s important to notice that the researchers call into question the stretching method, not the author of the passage. The author is describing the view of the experts, and doesn’t convey a strong opinion of her own. In S3, the author simply says, “These experts make the case.”

Then, after S2 and S3 describe the experts’ “case” about “superior alternative stretching approaches,” S4 and S5 provide details related to the “case” by describing two “superior alternative stretching approaches.”

1st paragraph summary: The author describes a common perception of a stretching method, presents a contrasting opinion held by experts, and then provides some details related to the experts’ opinion.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S6, the author acknowledges that the experts mentioned in the 1st paragraph make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S6 foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow. Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, considerations.” Three caveats are then specified:

- “it is most effective to perform most PNF stretches, aside from spine and neck stretches, only twice per week”

- “athletes frequently counteract their progress by contracting the targeted muscle with excessive force”

- “an aggressive approach can jeopardize the safety of a stretch if the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly

2nd paragraph summary: The author says that there is a body of research that supports the view of the experts and then warns that there are certain things to be aware of regarding the PNF approach described above and presents three of those things.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear throughout the passage?

The theme of the experts’ “case” or “view” appears repeatedly in the passage.

While the first sentence is about “a prevalent perception,” most of the 1st paragraph is about the experts’ case and details related to that case.

Then, the 2nd paragraph presents some caveats. At the same time, notice that the caveats are presented in the context of the statement “there is a convincing body of research to substantiate the view of these experts.” So, even the 2nd paragraph is, in a way, further discussion of the “view” of the experts.

Passage summary: The passage begins by mentioning “a prevalent perception” and then continues by presenting a contrasting “case” or “view” and discussing some details and caveats related to that contrasting “case” or “view.”
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

We don’t need to understand the technical vocabulary: isometric, inverse myotatic reflex, Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation. We can correctly answer every question without knowing what any of these things are.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: First opinion presented: amateur athletes’ opinion.

Let’s translate this sentence’s wording into simpler similar words:

Prevalent perception = common opinion

Ideal stretching protocol = best stretching method

Prior to vigorous activity = before activity

Simplification: Amateur athletes commonly believe that the best stretching method for achieve three goals (performance, range of motion, preventing injury) is Static Stretching before activity.

S2: Contrast with S1: Experts disagree.

Notice the contrast marker“however” and the opinion marker “call into question.” “However” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, it signals that a contrasting opinion is being presented.

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers and coaches,” in S2.

Simplification: Experts disagree with multiple aspects of the amateur athletes’ stretching method described above.

S3: The author outlines the experts’ disagreement.

The look above marker “this sequence of activities” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity”

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure that’s common in SC. This structure signifies that there are 2 reasons why the experts disagree.

Simplification: The experts make a strong case that:

1 - Static Stretching before a workout is inferior to doing it after a workout

2 - There are superior alternative approaches, such as PNF and DS. (Notice that this statement implies that the Static Stretching approach is inferior.)

S4: Details the PNF approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Naturally, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of PNF and why PNF is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “calming the muscle to prevent injury and allowing the athlete to stretch further”

S5: Details the DS approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Just like S4, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of DS and why DS is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “reducing the likelihood that the athlete will suffer an injury”

S6: The author acknowledges that the experts above make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

This sentence foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow.

Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, stipulations, considerations.”

There is also a tricky but important nuance to notice here: the contrast marker “although” at the start of the sentence can be used for different kinds and degrees of contrast. Here, it is not being used to signal direct opposites; rather, the sentence is acting as more of a “qualifier” (making a statement less absolute).

Example of opposite: Although the experts like this method, it is terrible.

Example of qualifier: Although there is evidence to support this method, it’s important to take certain precautions.

S7: Details about the first caveat

Notice that “while” is a contrast marker. So, we must check: what are the two things that are in contrast? The frequency of stretching is being contrasted: “every day” vs. “twice per week.”

Simplification: Many athletes do the same stretches every day, BUT Laughlin believes that it can be more effective to do most of them only twice per week.

Note: this simplification captures the main idea of this sentence, but we may need to analyze it in more detail for a specific question.

For example, notice that the language “most” and “aside from spine and neck stretches” means that there are exceptions; the passage does not say “all.”

S8: Details about the second caveat

Notice that “moreover” is an additional point marker. This marker tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion, more than one sentence above. Here, that conclusion is in S6: “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S9: Details about the third caveat

Notice that “likewise” (which means “similarly” or “also”) is an additional point marker that tells us that this sentence will state a point similar to the one stated by the previous sentence.

This sentence presents an additional point in support of the conclusion in S6.

question.text: The passage is primarily concerned with

answers.text: contrasting retirement trends over different time periods
discussing the economic implications of retirement trends
arguing in favor of a certain explanation for a retirement trend
discussing current economic developments that have attracted significant attention
considering the value of a scholarly work concerning a retirement trend

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To correctly answer a Primary Purpose question, we look for clues in the passage and seek to understand how the structure and important points of the passage indicate what the primary purpose of the passage is.

The important points are often found in the first two sentences of the first paragraph and in the first sentences of the paragraphs that follow (when the passage has more than one paragraph).Sometimes, there will also be a conclusion in the final sentence, particularly if there is only one paragraph.

In this passage, the first sentence gives us context on why the topic of retirement is important, and the second sentence introduces a specific scholarly work, Costa’s book, which is discussed throughout the rest of the passage.

Notice that, overall, the style of the passage could be described as a “book review.” The author is clearly focused on the work of Costa, and a clue is that the keyword “Costa” appears in five of the seven sentences.
In our Passage Analysis, we noted several tone clues that signal that the author has apositive opinionof Costa’s book: “illuminating” in S2, “credibly” in S5, and “much-needed” in S7.
Let’s review our Passage Summary from our Passage Analysis.
Passage Summary: With a positive tone, the author discusses a scholarly work about retirement, specifically focusing on the causes of the decrease in the LFPRE during the twentieth century.
(A) contrasting retirement trends over different time periods
Trap: Distortion / Too Narrow

If you picked this choice, you may have thought that the passage “contrasts retirement trends over different time periods,” in S3 and S4.Although there is a contrast between different time periods, the plural noun “retirement trends” is not contrasted.Rather, S3 and S4 contrast two different primarycausesforoneretirement trend, the decline in LFPRE.

Additionally, the correct answer to a Primary Purpose question must cover the passage as a whole, but the passage contrasts what occurred during different time periods in only a few sentences.So, “contrasting retirement trends over different time periods” is too narrow to be the primary purpose of the passage.
(B) discussing the economic implications of retirement trends
Trap: Too Narrow
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S1 does indeed mention economic implications of retirement trends.

However, this choice places too much emphasis on the first sentence rather than captures the primary purpose of the entire passage.Note that “implications” means “futurepotential impact.” It’s important to be aware of time frames.Does the rest of the passage discuss “implications”?
No, the passage discusses the work of Costa, which involveshistoricalanalysis, not a discussion of thefuture.
(C) arguing in favor of a certain explanation for a retirement trend
Trap: Distortion / Wrong Verb

Notice that “a certain explanation” is singular.However, in S3 and S4, we can see that Costa’s work discusses two different primary causes, over two different time frames, of the retirement trend of a decrease in LFPRE.

Also notice that “arguing” is a strong opinion word that would require a tone more argumentative than the generally descriptive tone of this passage.Although the tone clues “illuminating analysis” (S2) and “credibly debunks” (S5) suggest that author has a positive opinion of Costa’s work, the author is not “arguing in favor” of Costa’s explanations.Rather, the author describes Costa’s analysis.
(D) discussing current economic developments that have attracted significant attention
Trap: Distortion / Half-Right / Too Broad
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that, in S1, the passage does “discuss economic developments that have attracted significant attention.”

However, notice the adjective “current” in this choice.To avoid traps,we must build the habit of noticing modifying words or phrases and NOT skimming over them.Is the passage focused on “currenteconomic developments”?

We must be aware of the time frames involved in the passage.Although S1 does mention “contemporary retirement trends,” it also mentions “longer-term historical analysis.” Notice that most of the passage is about Costa’shistoricalanalysis of events in the past, notcurrentdevelopments.

Also, while it is more common for a trap answer choice in a primary purpose question to be too narrow rather than too broad, this choice is a bit too broad.Notice that “economic developments” is plural.The passage focuses on one scholarly work, Costa’s book, which is focused on the causes of the decline in the LFPRE.So, the focus of the passage is more narrow than the very broad topic of the plural noun in this choice, “economic developments.”
YOUR CHOICE(E) considering the value of a scholarly work concerning a retirement trend

If you didn’t pick this choice, you may have wondered whether the verb “considering” is correct.On the GMAT, the verb “consider” is a good fit when the tone of the passage is “reviewing” something; in this case, the tone of the author is generally descriptive, but the author also expresses a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Notice that, overall, the style of the passage could be described as a “book review.” The author is clearly focused on the work of Costa, and a clue is that the keyword “Costa” appears in five of the seven sentences.Since Costa’s work is about the retirement trend of the decrease in the LFPRE, we can see that “a scholarly work concerning a retirement trend” matches what the passage says.
Finally, is “the value of” correct?

Yes, in our Passage Analysis, we noted several tone clues that signal that the author has apositive opinionof the value of Costa’s book: “illuminating” in S2, “credibly” in S5, and “much-needed” in S7.Furthermore, both S1 and S7 discuss the importance of research on retirement.S1 makes the broad statement that “With demographic transformations … having far-reaching economicimplications…analysis of retirement … have recentlyreceived greater consideration.” S7 states a conclusion about Costa’s work in particular: “Costa’s work exemplifies amuch-needed resurgenceof research on retirement.”

When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage; the main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author implies that that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Having discussed in S1 that retirement topics have received attention among economists, in S2, the author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa. Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

In S2, also notice the causation marker, “causes of X,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. The main point of S2 is that Costa’s book explains the causes of the decline of the LFPRE.

LFPRE, the labor force participation rate of the elderly, will continue to appear throughout the passage, so we must keep track of what it means. It is helpful to connect this term to the topic of retirement; a decline in the LFPRE would mean that the retirement rate is increasing. To understand why, note that LFPRE is defined in S2 as “the percentage of the elderly still working.” Mathematically, the percentage still working + the percentage that are retired must add to 100 percent. So, if the percentage of the elderly still working decreases, the percentage retired must increase.

Then, S3 through S6 go into the specifics of Costa’s analysis. S3 and S4 contrast the first and second halves of the twentieth century. S5 and S6 discuss Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decrease in the LFPRE.

It’s helpful to notice that causation/explanation is a repeated theme throughout the passage. A useful clue is that there are many causation markers: causes (S2), attributed to (S3), cause (S4), as a result of (S4), explanations (S5).

In S5, notice another tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Finally, S7 sums up the author’s review of Costa’s work. In saying that “Costa’s work exemplifies a much-needed resurgence of research on retirement,” the author reiterates the point in S1 that this topic of retirement is important, as well as indicates once again that he has a positive opinion of Costa’s work. After all, something that “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” would presumably be something positive.

Notice that, overall, the style of the passage could be described as a “book review.” The author is clearly focused on the work of Costa, and a clue is that the keyword “Costa” appears in five of the seven sentences of the passage. The author presents some key points of Costa’s work, and as we’ve seen in our analysis of the tone clues, the author expresses a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Passage Summary: With a positive tone, the author discusses a scholarly work about retirement, specifically focusing on the causes of the decrease in the LFPRE during the twentieth century.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: This sentence introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage.

The main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author also implies that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Simplification: Because demographic changes, such as an increase in the percentage of the population that is elderly, have a significant economic impact, topics about retirement have recently received greater attention from economists.

S2: The author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa.

Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Notice the causation marker “causes of,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. Costa is explaining the decline of the LFPRE.

Simplification: Costa provides a helpful explanation of the causes of the decline in LFPRE during the twentieth century.

S3: Provides details about Costa’s primary explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE for the first half of the twentieth century.

Notice the causation marker, “X can be attributed to Y.” In this structure, X is the result and Y is the cause. So, Costa is saying that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE is caused by “rising incomes.” In other words, Costa says that “rising incomes” are the primary cause for the decrease, but other factors account for 40 percent of the decrease.

Notice that the time frame is limited to the first half of the twentieth century, 1901 through 1950.

S4: Contrast with S3 — the primary cause of the decrease is different during a later timeframe, the second half of the twentieth century.

Notice the contrast marker, “In contrast.” As always, we must pay close attention when we notice a contrast marker — what are the two things that are in contrast?

S3: During the first half of the century, the primary cause was rising incomes.

S4: During the second half of the century, the primary cause was increased affordability of leisure activities resulting from technological improvements

Note that wording such as “increased affordability” could easily be reversed to something like “decreased cost” in an answer choice and retain the same meaning.

S5: Introduces Costa’s work in debunking alternative explanations.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” The author is providing another example of how Costa provides an “illuminating analysis of the causes of the decline … in the LFPRE.”

Note that “debunks” means “expose the falseness of a myth.” So, we can see that Costa disagrees with some alternative explanations, “including health issues and a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers.”

Notice the tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue indicating that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

S6: Expands on S5.

The phrase “perhaps most notably” indicates that this sentence presents a notable example of what was discussed in the previous sentence, Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decline in the LFPRE.

The example presented by this sentence is Costa’s “observation.”

Simplification: One notable example of Costa’s debunking is her observation that, in older people, “the rate of chronic disease was higher” when the LFPRE was higher “during the first half of the twentieth century.” In other words, she observed that, when older people had more “health issues,” more older people worked. Thus, she debunked the explanation offered by the “other historians” in S5 that “health issues” caused the LFPRE to decrease from a higher level “during the first half of the twentieth century” to a lower level “since that period.”

S7: Concludes the passage similarly to how the passage began.

The passage began by implying that the topic of retirement is important, and this sentence conveys essentially the same thing by indicating that “research on retirement” is “much needed.”

Also, the tone clue “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” indicates once again that the author has a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Simplification: While Costa’s work does not provide all the analysis needed for prediction of retirement trends, Costa’s work is an example the type of research needed on this important topic.

question.text: The passage is primarily concerned with

answers.text: presenting two competing theories of evolution
proving that a theory of evolution is correct
outlining how a theory has been refuted over time
describing how a theory of evolution has been developed
considering how modern evidence affects the perception of a theory

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To correctly answer a Primary Purpose question, we look for clues in the passage and seek to understand how the structure and important points of the passage indicate what the primary purpose of the passage is.

For the purpose of identifying important points, it’s helpful to note that most passages will involve some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions.So, put extra emphasis on contrast markers, such as “however” or “but.” These contrast markers often indicate important points that we should pay close attention to.In this case, the contrast markers “however” in S2 and S5 and “nevertheless” in S10 are clues that help us identify the primary purpose.
Additionally, conclusions often connect to the primary purpose.In this case, we can see the conclusion markers “So” and “Thus” in S9 and S12.

Most importantly, in this passage, S7, “Has Lamarck’s evolutionary theory been proven at least somewhat valid?” is our most useful clue, because a rhetorical question generally signals that the primary purpose is to answer this question.Indeed, the author goes on to answer this question, particularly with the author’s conclusions in S9 and S12.
Finally, let’s review the passage as a whole; do any consistent themes appear?
Throughout the passage, there is a lot of contrast, and the author goes back and forth on the question of whether Lamarck’s evolutionary theory is valid.
(A) presenting two competing theories of evolution
Trap: Distortion
When we analyze this choice, the first question we might ask ourselves is what exactly are the “twocompeting theories of evolution” in the passage?
The passage focuses on onlyonetheory of evolution, Lamarck’s theory.Although Darwin’s theory is mentioned in S1, Darwin’s theory is not an important topic discussed throughout the passage.

Furthermore, the passage doesn’t clearly indicate that Lamarck and Darwin’s theories are “competing.” While it’s true that, in real life, they are competing theories, we must always stick to what the passage says and not use outside knowledge.
(B) proving that a theory of evolution is correct
Trap: Stretch / Wrong Verb
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the 3rdparagraph provides some support for Lamarck’s theory.

However, notice that “proving… is correct” is a very strong positive judgment about Lamarck’s theory.Is the author’s opinion really this strong, or is it more nuanced? Does the author make points that go against Lamarck’s theory?

Notice that the support for Lamarck’s theory in S10 is limited to only one example: “Nevertheless, Lamarck’s theory has found asole refuge.” Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.”
Also, the discussion in the 2ndparagraph goes in the opposite direction, indicating that Lamarck’s theory has not “been proven at least somewhat valid.”
So, the passage as a whole does notprovethat Lamarck’s theory of evolution is correct.
(C) outlining how a theory has been refuted over time
Trap: Distortion

Presumably, since the passage focuses on one theory, “a theory” refers to “Lamarck’s evolutionary theory.” Note that“refuted”would need to match with a strong argumentagainstthis theory.It’s true that the 1stparagraph, S8, and S9 discuss reasoning that goes against this theory.
However, overall, the passage involves a lot of contrast, and the author goes back and forth on the question of whether Lamarck’s evolutionary theory is valid.

In particular, the 3rdparagraph begins by saying, “Nevertheless, Lamarck’s theory has found asole refuge.” “Refuge” means “shelter” or “safe place.” So, the third paragraph is about a situation in which Lamarck’s theory is safe, or valid, not “refuted.”
So, the purpose of the passage as a whole is not to outline how Lamarck’s theory has been “refuted.”
(D) describing how a theory of evolution has been developed
Trap: Distortion
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the two “discoveries” discussed in the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs could be considered developments, and this choice includes the word “developed.”

However, this choice talks about howa theory of evolutionhas been developed.The developments in the passage arerecentpieces of evidence that are discussed for the purpose of considering the validity of Lamarck’s theory that was proposed way back in 1809.
The passage does not discuss how Lamarck’s theory of evolution was, or has been, developed.

(E)Correct.The verb “considering” matches the tone of this passage. “Consider” means “think carefully about” or “examine,” and throughout the passage, the author is “considering” how Lamarck’s theory has been perceived at various points in time.

In the first paragraph, we learn that “with the discovery of genetics in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, it seemed thatLamarck’s theory had been completely refuted” and that “biologists...had made it appear thatevolution as Lamarck had described it is impossible.” So, at that point, Lamarck’s theory was perceived as incorrect.

In the second paragraph, we learn that “more recently, such a mechanism has been discovered in the form of modification of gene expression,” and the author poses a rhetorical question: “Has Lamarck’s evolutionary theory been proven at least somewhat valid?” This question indicates that, while Lamarck’s theory had previously been considered invalid, it was now up for debate.The paragraph concludes by stating that “this type of passing of traits from generation to generation does not result in evolution.” In other words, Lamarck’s theory was still seen as inaccurate.

Finally, the third paragraph states that “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge” and discusses the gene expression of single-celled organisms before concluding that “the changes in traits resulting from these organisms’ changes to their genestruly constitute evolution.” In other words, Lamarck’s theory is considered correct in this instance.

So, as more evidence was presented, Lamarck’s theory went from being perceived as “completely refuted” to being considered correct in a specific instance.We can see that this choice accurately describes the primary purpose of the passage.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast, opinions, and tone clues in the first two sentences.

In this passage, there is an opinion marker in S1, “Lamarck proposed that ….” S1 sets the stage for the rest of the passage by presenting the theory of evolution that Lamarck proposed, which is “evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.”

Then, in S2, the contrast marker “however” signals an important point, which is “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted.”

Note that the tone clue, “seemed” signals some uncertainty about whether Lamarck’s theory had actually been refuted and foreshadows that there may be further debate or insights on this topic.

So, from these first two sentences, we already get the sense that the main topic of the passage is likely to be discussion of the validity of Lamarck’s evolutionary theory.

Then, S3 and S4 go on to explain why it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted, the main reason being “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.” In other words, S3 and S4 communicate that what “biologists decided” indicated that evolution could not work in accordance with Lamarck’s theory.

Notice that, so far, the author’s tone is descriptive; she presents Lamarck’s theory and the biologists’ reasoning against it, but she does not make an argument of her own. In other words, she has not yet expressed her own opinion.

1st paragraph summary: Lamarck proposed a theory of evolution, “evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.” Then, with the discovery of genetics, it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been refuted.

Next, we see that the 2nd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals that what the 2nd paragraph says is in contrast with what’s said at the end of the first paragraph.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S5, we also see the look above marker “SUCH a mechanism.” What “such a mechanism” refers to is the mechanism mentioned earlier, in S4, “a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.”

What’s said in the 2nd paragraph contrasts with what’s said in the 1st paragraph in the following way. Whereas S4 says, “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring,” now S5 says, “However, more recently, such a mechanism has been discovered.” In other words, the biologists’ belief has been shown to be incorrect.

Then, S6 provides details about this recent discovery. This discovery is that new traits developed during an organism’s lifetime CAN be passed on to offspring. We know from the 1st paragraph that passing on traits is an important element of “evolution.”

Next, in S7, we want to put extra emphasis on the rhetorical question “Has Lamarck’s evolutionary theory been proven at least somewhat valid?” Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Indeed, S8 and S9 answer this question, with basically a “No” answer. Notice that S8 provides the reasoning of “most evolutionary biologists,” and then S9 goes on to provide a direct conclusion of the author, not the opinion of another group.

2nd paragraph summary: A recent discovery involving gene expression modifications shows that traits acquired during an organisms’ lifetime can be passed on to the organism’s offspring, but only for a few generations. So, this phenomenon isn’t evolution and does not support Lamarck’s theory.

Like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph begins with a contrast marker, “nevertheless.” So, what is the contrast?

Whereas S8 and S9 go against Lamarck’s theory, S10 supports Lamarck’s theory: “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge, in the world of a certain few single-celled organisms that have been discovered to be able to change their genes ….” Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.” So, S10 means essentially “There is one situation in which Lamarck’s theory does apply, the world of certain single-celled organism that have been discovered to be able to change their genes.”

As we might expect, S11 then provides additional details to explain why this discovery supports Lamarck’s theory. Notice the contrast marker structure, “Unlike X, Y.” S11 is saying that “these changes made by these organisms” are “unlike” the gene expression modifications discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

What is the key difference between “gene expression modifications” and “these changes”?

Whereas S8 said that “gene expression modifications and their associated traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for only a few generations and eventually disappear,” S11 says that “these changes” made by the single-celled organisms are “passed from generation to generation indefinitely,” which means “for an unlimited period of time.” Simply put, the difference is about traits passed on temporarily versus traits passed on indefinitely.

In S12, the author’s final conclusion is signaled by the conclusion marker “thus.”

We can see that this conclusion by the author contrasts with the author’s conclusion in S9. Whereas in S9, the “gene expression modification” type of passing of traits does NOT result in evolution, the “changes to genes” type of passing of traits in S12 DOES result in evolution.

3rd paragraph summary: However, there is one discovery that DOES support Lamarck’s evolutionary theory: single-celled organisms that change their genes and traits during their lifetimes and pass on those traits indefinitely.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear?

Throughout the passage, there is a lot of contrast, and the author goes back and forth on the question of whether Lamarck’s evolutionary theory is valid.

Passage summary: The author considers the validity of Lamarck’s theory of evolution, in light of biologists’ understanding in the past and two more recent discoveries.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Similar Words, and Definitions

evolution

change in the traits of a species over successive generations (Note: we don’t need to know the definition of “evolution” on our own, because it is defined in certain ways in the passage. The passage suggests that there are multiple different types of evolution. Note that S8 and S9 specify that “traits passed on … for only a few generations…does NOT result in evolution”)

traits

characteristics

(Note that “sole” is similar to “solo,” which means “alone.”)
