analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Defines a key term/theme, the strategy of “planned obsolescence,” and presents a negative opinion about it.

Notice the opinion marker, “conviction that X.” “Conviction” means “a strong belief.”

Notice that a key term, “planned obsolescence,” is specifically defined in between the two dashes.

Simplification: Consumer protection advocates believe that “planned obsolescence” unfairly takes advantage of consumers. Planned obsolescence is the business strategy of designing products in a way that requires more frequent replacement.

S2: The author expresses support for the negative opinion presented in S1, by citing an example.

Notice the opinion marker “indeed.” “Indeed” signals that the author supports the consumer protection advocates’ opinion above that planned obsolescence is exploitative.

Note that “would be hard-pressed to justify” means “would have trouble justifying.” Basically, when the author says, “there are certain cases that even the most pro-business advocates would be hard-pressed to justify,” he is saying that there are some examples that are almost indisputably exploitative. Notice the strong language, “even the most.”

Notice the support marker “such as,” which introduces the example of the “infamous Phoebus cartel.” We can clearly see that this case of deliberately reducing the lifespan of bulbs to 1,000 hours is an example of “planned obsolescence” as defined in S1 — “supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for the reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Simplification: Indeed, some examples of planned obsolescence are almost indisputably exploitative, such as the Phoebus cartel’s deliberate reduction of the lifespan of light bulbs.

S3: The author provides an additional example of planned obsolescence.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” S2 said that there are “certain cases” that are hard to justify, and provided one example. We can see that S3 provides a second example of planned obsolescence that is “hard to justify”: “designers explicitly discuss how to design products that will wear out as rapidly as possible while leaving customers with sufficient faith in a product to replace it immediately.”

Simplification: Annie Leonard cites evidence of the planned obsolescence strategy being discussed in industrial-design journals.

S4: The author introduces a different possible reason for frequent product replacement.

Notice the contrast marker, “at the same time,” which has a similar meaning to “meanwhile,” and can be thought of as a softer version of “nevertheless.” This marker is used here to transition to discussing a different possible reason for frequent product replacement; this reason is “a demand-side phenomenon.”

At this point, it may not be totally clear what is meant by “demand-side phenomenon,” but we can expect to gain more context as we read further. Indeed, the next sentence starts with “For example.”

Particularly once we’ve read further, we can see that “demand-side phenomenon” means “related to consumer preferences.”

Also notice that “demand-side” is in contrast to the word “supplying” in S1: “‘planned obsolescence’ — the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Notice that the author’s tone is descriptive, and the author doesn’t express a strong opinion of his own; the wording used is “an interesting argument has been made that.” The word “interesting” is quite neutral; it does not express strong support of or opposition to this argument.

S5: Provides an example to support the point made in S4.

Notice the support marker “for example.”

Notice the contrast marker “although.” This example is being cited as a “demand-side phenomenon” unlike the “planned obsolescence” described in the 1st paragraph. Although the phones are replaced very frequently, the reason for it is NOT that the phones have worn out; S5 says that “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.” The author is mentioning this example because it is different from the reason for frequent replacement discussed in the 1st paragraph — products that are intentionally designed to wear out quickly.

S6: Expands on the example in S5, by adding details about why people replace their smartphones so frequently.

Notice the contrast marker “while X, Y.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Here, the Y part says that Walker “has a strong desire to stay current with the latest impressive technological innovations.” This is an example of the “demand-side phenomenon” introduced in S4.

S7: Expands on the point in S6 about Walker’s motivation

Notice the conclusion marker “therefore,” which connects Walker’s “strong desire to stay current,” in S6, to the point in S7.

Notice the contrast marker “instead of X, Y”: “instead of resenting the cost of replacement, he looks forward to upgrading his device regularly.” This positive attitude is different from the critical attitude of the “consumer protection advocates” in S1.

S8: Quotes Walker’s general conclusion.

The quote of Walker’s conclusion summarizes the key point in S4 that “frequent product replacement has sometimes become a demand-side phenomenon.” Note that “exactly what we want” connects closely with the meaning of “demand-side.”

question.text: Which of the following best describes the structure of the passage?

answers.text: A literary theory is presented, and then evidence that helps to refute that theory is presented.
An observation is made, and then evidence in support of that observation is presented.
A critical analysis is described and then stories from Ernest Hemingway’s life are presented.
Evidence is presented, and then a conclusion is reached on the basis of that evidence.
A commonly held view about great writers is presented, and then novels written by those writers are used as examples.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

This passage has a simple structure to it.In the 1stparagraph the author sets forth the idea that great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories, and that they sometimes transform their stories into reality.” He or she goes on to say that Ernest Hemingway and his novel follow this theme.The entire rest of the passage (three paragraphs) is evidence used to support this position.
(A) A literary theory is presented, and then evidence that helps to refute that theory is presented.
Trap: Opposite

In the 1stparagraph, the author sets forth the idea that “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories, and they sometimes transform their stories into reality.” Now, is this idea a literary theory? Maybe, but we don’t need to be sure about whether it is or is not because the rest of the passage contains evidence used to support that idea, not to “refute” it.
So, what this choice says is the opposite of what we’re seeking.
CORRECT ANSWER(B) An observation is made, and then evidence in support of that observation is presented.
Notice that what this answer says correctly captures the structure of the passage.Of course, the “observation” mentioned in this choice is that:

“Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories, and they sometimes transform their stories into reality.Over time, the line between fiction and fact, the lives about which they write in their novels and the reality in which they live outside of their typewriters, often becomes less clear.Ernest Hemingway and his novels are no exception.”
Then, the following three paragraphs provide evidence in support of that point.
(C) A critical analysis is described and then stories from Ernest Hemingway’s life are presented.
Trap: Half Right
The author of this passage is not being critical at all.If the author is not being critical, he or she is certainly not doing a “critical analysis.”
Yes, stories of Hemingway’s life are presented, so the second part of this choice is correct, but remember – half right is completely wrong.
(D) Evidence is presented, and then a conclusion is reached on the basis of that evidence.
The passage begins with what could be considered a conclusion and then moves to present evidence.Thus, what this choice says clearly does not correctly capture the structure of the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(E) A commonly held view about great writers is presented, and then novels written by those writers are used as examples.
First, nowhere does the author indicate that his or her views presented in the 1stparagraph are “commonly held,” so we can’t make this assumption.
Second, the remainder of the passage focuses on the works and life of Hemingway, not, as this choice suggests, on the novels of multiple great writers.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting generalizations about “great writers of fiction,” which are that they “often transform their realities into their stories, and they sometimes transform their stories into reality,” and that “Over time, the line between fiction and fact … often becomes less clear.”

Then the final sentence of the 1st paragraph, S3, narrows the scope of the discussion from “great writers” in general to “Ernest Hemingway and his novels.”

By narrowing the scope of the discussion in this way, S3, signals that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss how Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to the generalizations presented by S1 and S2.

1st paragraph summary: Ernest Hemingway and his novels follow patterns common among great writers of fiction that involve these writers’ realities and stories being transformed into each other.

The 2nd paragraph first presents an aspect of Hemingway’s reality, that he developed an “interest in the reversal of traditional gender roles and sexuality” as a result of experiences he had when he was young.

Then, in S6, the passage shows how “this interest” from Hemingway’s “reality” shows up in Hemingway’s “stories” by presenting details of The Garden of Eden that match up with the interest and experiences mentioned in S4 and S5.

We can see that the 2nd paragraph supports the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

2nd paragraph summary: In his reality, Hemingway experienced and developed an interest in the reversal of traditional gender roles and sexuality, and these aspects of Hemingway’s reality are “apparent” in his story The Garden of Eden.

Like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph begins by presenting an aspect of Hemingway’s reality, his relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky and the “anguish” associated with that relationship.

Then, S9 through S12 show how this reality was “put to work” in Hemingway’s story, A Farewell to Arms.

So, like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph uses examples from Hemingway’s reality and his stories to support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

3rd paragraph summary: In his reality, Hemingway experienced anguish in a personal relationship, and Hemingway “put to work” this anguish in the creation of his story A Farewell to Arms.

Whereas the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs provide support for the assertion that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern of great writers transforming their realities into their stories, now the 4th paragraph presents an example of a case in which Hemingway’s story appeared in his reality: he told a story “as if it were truth” when in fact “it was merely … an extension of” his fiction.

So, like the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the 4th paragraph supports the point stated by S3, but in this case by providing an example of the pattern of writers “sometimes transform their stories into reality.”

4th paragraph summary: Hemingway frequently told a story “as if it were truth” though it was actually from his “fictional writing,” and thus, in a way, transformed one of his stories into reality, in accordance with a pattern of “great writers of fiction” discussed in the 1st paragraph.

Passage summary: The passage presents some generalizations about “great writers of fiction,” asserts that Ernest Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to these generalizations, and presents examples from Hemingway’s reality and his stories to support that assertion.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Similar Words

after the death of the author

sultry

highlights, emphasizes

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Introduces the topic of the passage by presenting a generalization about “great writers of fiction.”

S2: Expands on what S1 says by presenting the additional generalization that, for these “great writers,” the line between fiction and fact often becomes less clear over time.

S3: By saying that Ernest Hemingway and his novels “are no exception,” this sentence communicates both that Hemingway is one of the “great writers of fiction” mentioned in S1 and S2 and that the generalizations presented by S1 and S2 apply to him.

Notice that this sentence narrows the scope of what the passage is discussing from “great writers of fiction” to Ernest Hemingway specifically.

By narrowing the scope of the discussion in this way, S3, signals that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss how Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to the generalizations presented by S1 and S2.

S4: Presents an aspect of Hemingway’s “reality,” that he experienced and developed an interest in “reversal of gender roles and sexuality.”

S5: Continues to discuss the aspect of Hemingway’s reality presented by S4.

S6: S6 uses the look above marker “THIS interest” to refer to Hemingway’s “interest in reversal of gender roles and sexuality” mentioned in S4 and presents a way in which Hemingway’s “reality” described in S4 and S5 made its way into Hemingway’s “stories.”

Simplification: The experience and interest in reversal of gender roles and sexuality from Hemingway’s reality show up in Hemingway’s work The Garden of Eden.

S7: Presents details of The Garden of Eden to further support the point that Hemingway’s reality made its way into this novel.

Notice that S4 through S7 work together to support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

S8: Presents a second aspect of Hemingway’s “reality,” that he fell in love with Agnes von Kurowsky.

S9: Continues to discuss the reality of Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky and then shows how Hemingway “put to work” that reality to create a story, the novel A Farewell to Arms.

S10 and S11: These sentences present details of A Farewell to Arms to further demonstrate how it resembles the reality of Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky.

S12: By saying that the pain of Henry, a character in Hemingway’s fiction, is “palpable,” meaning “apparent” or “clear,” the passage indicates that Hemingway used the “anguish” that he experienced to create strong fiction.

Notice that, in S8 through S12, the passage once again supports the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

S13: Presents a story that Hemingway told as if it were reality.

S14: Says that “the story” mentioned in S13 was “merely theatre,” meaning “simply fiction.”

Notice that S13 and S14 support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern of great writers “sometimes transform their stories into reality.”

question.text: Which of the following best describes the structure of the passage?

answers.text: Opposing views on a topic are discussed, and then those views are reconciled.
An experiment is outlined, and then its results are shown to support a belief commonly held among physiologists.
A question is presented, and then information is explored that shows that that question does not have a definitive answer.
Multiple theories are presented in favor of a popular opinion, and then a conclusion about those theories is presented.
A commonly held view is presented, and then evidence casting reasonable doubt on that view is introduced.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

We see that purpose of the 1stparagraph is to introduce a recommendation based on a common belief among physiologists (that exercise is an effective tool for weight loss).In addition, the author uses this paragraph to establish that he or she will discuss the study done by Lewis and his team, which casts some doubt on that commonly held belief.
The purpose of the 2ntand 3rdparagraphs is to describe that study in more detail.
The purpose of the 4thparagraph is to present some possible evolutionary explanations for why the phenomenon described (downward caloric compensation) occurs.
(A) Opposing views on a topic are discussed, and then those views are reconciled.
Trap: Half-Right

If you picked this choice as correct, you may have done so because the author describes in the 1stparagraph the view commonly held by physiologists and then the “theory” that is in contrast to that commonly held.So, having seen those two things discussed in the 1stparagraph, you might have decided that two opposing views are being discussed.

However, notice that the second part of this choice is clearly wrong.There is no reconciliation of these views.Instead, the author describes the research study and then provides some explanations for why the phenomenon described in the research study might exist.
(B) An experiment is outlined, and then its results are shown to support a belief commonly held among physiologists.
Trap: Half-Right

This choice is half right, making it completely wrong.Yes, an experiment is outlined, but its results are certainly not used by the author to support a belief commonly held among physiologists.Instead, the author uses the research study as evidence against a belief commonly held by physiologists.
(C) A question is presented, and then information is explored that shows that that question does not have a definitive answer.
Trap: Half-Right

This choice is half right, making it completely wrong.Yes, a question is presented.However, the author presents evidence that shows that the question probablydoeshave a definitive answer.After all, the research by Lewis and his team certainly does appear to answer the rhetorical question presented by the author in the 1stparagraph.
(D) Multiple theories are presented in favor of a popular opinion, and then a conclusion about those theories is presented.
Trap: Opposite

Notice from the passage that it is not true that multiple theories are presented in favor of a popular opinion.In fact, the author is presenting evidence that casts doubt on the common belief held by physiologists.
YOUR CHOICE(E) A commonly held view is presented, and then evidence casting reasonable doubt on that view is introduced.
As we saw in our Question Analysis, this is exactly how the passage is structured.

When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage opens by presenting the fact that “many physiologists recommend exercise … for generation of weight loss.” Presenting what physiologists recommend is this passage’s way of presenting a common belief of physiologists. After all, the fact that physiologists recommend exercise for weight loss indicates that they believe that exercise causes weight loss.

The passage next asks a rhetorical question about this common belief, “is exercise as effective in causing weight loss as these experts believe?” Seeing this rhetorical question, we can tell that the passage is going to answer this rhetorical question and is likely going to show that the physiologists’ common belief is somehow inaccurate.

Having presented the rhetorical question, the passage next shows, in S3, how it’s going to answer the rhetorical question, which is by discussing a study that supports the theory that “our bodies are programmed to ‘calorically’ compensate” and thus burn fewer calories than expected.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents a common belief, asks a rhetorical question about that belief, and presents a study that the passage will discuss to answer that rhetorical question.

The passage next begins to answer the rhetorical question asked by S2 by presenting details of what the study found.

The contrast marker “rather” at the beginning of S5 indicates that S5 states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points. We see that S5 presents the reason fewer calories than expected are burned, which is that, on days when people exercise, the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure.”

Then, the rest of the 2nd paragraph presents an example to explain what S5 says about “basal energy expenditure” and “additional energy expenditure” and to present the idea that a certain set of activities is associated with a certain “expected caloric burn.”

2nd paragraph summary: The main point of the 2nd paragraph is that the reason people burn fewer calories than expected when they exercise is that the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure,” and the rest of the 2nd paragraph presents information to explain this point.

The contrast marker “however” at the beginning of the 3rd paragraph indicates that S9 states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points. Indeed, S9 presents the main thing that the study found, which is that the “expected caloric burn” discussed in the 2nd paragraph “did not materialize,” meaning “did not occur.”

The remainder of the 3rd paragraph then presents details of these study results, which are basically that people burned “fewer calories in total than expected.”

3rd paragraph summary: The study found that the “caloric burn” expected when people engage in a certain set of activities did not occur and that people burned “fewer calories in total than expected.”

The 4th paragraph wraps up the passage by discussing the hypotheses the research team came up with after seeing the study results.

The first of these hypotheses is that “humans and animals respond” to greater energy expenditure by “reducing the energy expended.” The second of these hypotheses is that this “downward caloric compensatory phenomenon” is an evolutionary development.

Then, S14 and S15 present reasons why human ancestors evolved to reduce energy use: “food was scarce” and “time spent foraging” involved danger of getting killed by predators.

4th paragraph summary: The research team hypothesized that humans and animals evolved to reduce energy expenditure because reducing use of calories had certain benefits.

Passage summary: Physiologists recommend exercise for losing weight, but a study found that exercise does not cause people to burn as many calories as expected.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: By saying what “many reputable physiologists recommend,” this sentence introduces a common belief, the belief that exercise “is a mechanism for the generation of weight loss.”

S2: This sentence uses an “although X, Y,” structure. In the “although X” part, the author concedes that exercise can help a person lose weight. Then, in the “Y” part, the author asks a rhetorical question to signal that (1) he or she believes that the common belief introduced in S1 is a myth and that (2) the primary purpose of the passage will be to discuss, in some way, the answer to this rhetorical question.

S3: This sentence introduces a study undertaken by Lewis Halsey and his team along with the theory that the study supports.

Since that theory is that “our bodies are programed to “calorically compensate” on the days on which we exercise, burning on these days significantly fewer calories than the laws of thermodynamics … would predict,” we can see that Lewis’s study and the theory it supports supply an answer to the rhetorical question asked by S2. The study and the theory basically imply that the answer is “No, exercise is not as effective as these experts believe.”

Simplification: A study shows that exercise does not cause as much burning of calories as experts believe it causes.

S4: Uses the look above marker “THESE days” to begin to explain why fewer calories than expected are burned on the days when people exercise, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph.

S5: The contrast marker “rather” at the beginning of this sentence signals that this sentence probably states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points.

Indeed, S5 is an important sentence because it presents the reason fewer calories than expected are burned, which is that, on days when people exercise, the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure.

Simplification: The body burns fewer calories than expected by reducing its “basal,” meaning “basic,” use of energy.

S6, S7, and S8: These sentences present an example to illustrate the concept of “basal energy expenditure” and show how additional activity results in a person’s burning “more calories over and above this basal energy expenditure.”

S9: The contrast marker “however” at the beginning of this sentence signals that this sentence probably states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points.

Indeed, S9 does state an important fact. The fact stated by S9, “in the study, Lewis and his team observed that this expected caloric burn did not materialize,” explains how the study supports the theory mentioned in S3, which is that “our bodies are programed to ‘calorically compensate’ on the days on which we exercise, burning on these days significantly fewer calories than the laws of thermodynamics … would predict.”

S10 and S11: These sentences present details of how the “expected caloric burn did not materialize.”

S12: Presents Lewis’s hypothesis about why “this expected caloric burn did not materialize.”

Simplification: Lewis’s hypothesis about why “this expected caloric burn did not materialize,” is that “humans and animals respond to greater energy being expended on activity” by reducing the amount of energy they use.

S13: Expands on the hypothesis presented in S12.

Simplification: The researchers think this reduction of energy use is something that human ancestors evolved to do.

S14 and S15: These sentences present reasons why human ancestors evolved to reduce energy use: “food was scarce” and “time spent foraging” involved danger of getting killed by predators.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the relationship between the second and third paragraphs of the passage?

answers.text: The third paragraph discusses the flaws of a study described in the second paragraph.
The second paragraph presents a viewpoint, and the third paragraph presents an alternative one.
The third paragraph refutes the conclusion presented in the second paragraph.
The third paragraph assesses the integrity of a research study discussed in the second paragraph.
The third paragraph expands on an explanation described in the second paragraph.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

The wording of the stem, “relationship between the second and third paragraphs,” tells us that this is a Structure question.We approach Structure questions similarly to the way we approach Primary Purpose questions: our goal is to understand main points, structure, purpose, and tone.
However, one difference here is that we will put more emphasis on the transition between the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs.
The transition between two paragraphs is generally accomplished by the first sentence of the second of the two paragraphs, which is S9 in this case.So, let’s review S9.

S9 says, “While this biological argument may eventually lead to some useful insights, a more compelling perspective can be found in the writings of Buddhist monk and author Matthieu Ricard, who also holds a PhD in molecular genetics.”
“This biological argument” in S9 refers to the conclusion of the scientists in S8, “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

So, S9 indicates that the 3rdparagraph will present “a perspective” that is “more compelling” than the argument presented in the 2ndparagraph, and by reviewing the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs, we see that the 3rdparagraph does present a perspective that is an alternative to the one presented by the 2ndparagraph.
(A) The third paragraph discusses the flaws of a study described in the second paragraph.
Trap: Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “a study described in the second paragraph” matches the passage.

However, the 3rdparagraph doesn’t discuss the “flaws” of the study in the second paragraph.Although the author does make the judgment that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2ndparagraph, no specific flaws of the study are discussed.The 3rdparagraph is mainly focused on presenting a separate perspective, Ricard’s perspective.
YOUR CHOICE(B) The second paragraph presents a viewpoint, and the third paragraph presents an alternative one.
As we can see from our Question Analysis, the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs do present two different “viewpoints.”
The verb “presents” is a good match for the descriptive tone of these paragraphs.
“Viewpoint” has a similar meaning to the four opinion markersbelow:
- “scientistscontendedthat” (S8)
- “biologicalargument” (S9)
- “a more compellingperspective” (S9)
- “casethat” (S12)
So, this choice accurately describes the relationship between the second and third paragraphs of the passage.
(C) The third paragraph refutes the conclusion presented in the second paragraph.
Trap: Stretch
Notice that “refutes” is a very strong opinion word that means “disproves” or “rejects.”

Although the author does make the judgment that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2ndparagraph, notice that this judgment is only a comparison statement, not an absolute or extreme judgment.
To give another example, if a passage were to say “X is better than Y,” we cannot infer the absolute judgment that “Y isbad.” We could only infer the comparison judgment that “Y is not as good as X.”
Also, notice that the author has a balanced tone and talks about some potential positives of the “biological argument”: “this biological argument may eventually lead to someusefulinsights.”
So, the author is not “refuting” the conclusion of the scientists in S8.
Furthermore, notice that the 3rdparagraph is focused mainly on presenting a separate viewpoint, Ricard’s perspective.
(D) The third paragraph assesses the integrity of a research study discussed in the second paragraph.
Trap: Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “a research study discussed in the second paragraph” matches the passage.

However, the 3rdparagraph doesn’t “assess the integrity” of the study discussed in the 2ndparagraph.Note that “integrity” means “trustworthiness.” Although the author does make the one judgment that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2ndparagraph, the author does not make any further judgments that would match with “assess the integrity” of the study.Rather, the 3rdparagraph is focused mainly on presenting a separate viewpoint, Ricard’s perspective.
(E) The third paragraph expands on an explanation described in the second paragraph.
Trap: Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “an explanation described in the second paragraph” matches the passage.

However, the 3rdparagraph doesn’t “expand on” the explanation in the 2ndparagraph.(Note that “expands on” means “adds detail to.”) Rather, the 3rdparagraph is focused mainly on presenting aseparate viewpoint, Ricard’s perspective.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting some information about “people’s benevolence”; S1 says that people’s benevolence both is directed toward their kin and extends to those outside their families.

This information presented by S1 turns out to be background information; the first paragraph makes its point in S2 and S3.

S2 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals both that it’s likely that an important point is coming and that a contrast is being made. What are the two things that are contrasted? S1 has introduced one type of generous behavior, which is often reciprocated, and now, S2 introduces a different type of generous behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is a “perplexing phenomenon to scientists.” Anonymous altruistic behavior turns out to be the main topic of the passage.

Having brought up “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2, the first paragraph sets the stage for the rest of the passage with S3. S3 has a rhetorical question marker(question mark). Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question begins with “What would motivate individuals to …,” which means, essentially, “Why?” When the author asks “Why?” we have a causation marker, indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then discussed, in the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. We must pay close attention whenever explanations are discussed, because explanations tend to be important parts of passages.

S3 also defines “anonymous altruistic behavior, saying that it is “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” In defining “anonymous altruistic behavior,” S3 gives the reader a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the generous behavior described in S1, which is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

1st paragraph summary: Both scientists and other scholars are interested in explaining anonymous altruistic behavior. Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible answer the rhetorical question in S3. The possible answer, or explanation, is that “there are neural bases for such acts.” The look above marker in S4, “SUCH acts,” refers to the “anonymous deeds” in S3. So, this marker helps us understand the relationship between S4 and S3.

Then, in S5, S6, and S7, the 2nd paragraph provides some details of how “researchers” investigated this possible explanation and what they found. These details are rather involved. So, our goal in reading these sentences is not necessarily to totally understand or seek to memorize every one of these details but rather to understand in general how the study described worked.

In S8, we see the opinion marker, “contended.” Here, the main conclusion of the researchers is presented: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author’s tone in the 2nd paragraph is descriptive, and the author does not provide her own opinion.

2nd paragraph summary: The author presents one possible explanation for anonymous altruistic behavior: having studied brain activity, researchers concluded that hormones that cause positive sensations, i.e., biological factors, play an important role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

The 3rd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

In this case, by using “while X, Y” in S9, the author connects the 2nd paragraph to the 3rd paragraph; in the “while X” part of S9, the author concedes that the argument presented in the 2nd paragraph “may … lead to some useful insights,” and then, in the Y part of S9, the author presents the topic of the 3rd paragraph, the “more compelling perspective” of Matthieu Ricard. (Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.)

S10 and S11 provide details about Ricard’s perspective and the types of evidence he uses to support his points.

Finally, in S12, the author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.” Notice that, in judging Ricard’s conclusion “persuasive,” the author is once again stating an opinion of her own.

Also, we can see that there is clear contrast because Ricard’s conclusion and that of the researchers discussed in the second paragraph. S12 says that Ricard’s “persuasive case” is that serving others can be “genuinely selfless,” whereas S8 says that the researchers discussed in the second paragraph contend that such behavior can be “seemingly selfless.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author presents a second explanation that she believes is more compelling than the “biological argument” in the 2nd paragraph: Ricard’s perspective is that the motivation for serving others can be genuinely selfless and that this behavior can be cultivated through specific practices.

Passage summary: The author poses a question about why people engage in anonymous altruistic behavior, presents a biological explanation based on a brain imaging study, and then presents a second explanation that the author prefers.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: To answer the questions correctly, we don’t need to know the technical vocabulary: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), mesolimbic pathway, neurotransmitter, dopamine, hypothalamus, oxytocin, hormones, empathy-altruism hypothesis. We can understand the meaning of each sentence from the surrounding language and the definitions that are provided in the passage. (For example, the passage defines the mesolimbic pathway: “the mesolimbic pathway, the reward center of the brain that is responsible for releasing the neurotransmitter associated with pleasure.”)
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice causation marker “because.”

Simplification: People are often kind to not only relatives but also others, because this kindness is often returned.

S2: Notice the contrast marker “however.” This sentence introduces a different type of kind behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is “perplexing to scientists,” meaning that it is harder to explain.

Note: we may not be sure what the meaning of “anonymous altruistic behavior” is at first, but we are basically given a definition in the next sentence. In general, we can look to the surrounding context if we don’t understand something.

Simplification: A different type of kind behavior, anonymous altruistic behavior, has been puzzling for scientists to explain and an important topic for other scholars.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

The question posed, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …?” is asking about the reason/cause/explanation for these anonymous deeds. So, we can expect the rest of the passage to discuss possible explanations.

This sentence also helps the reader to understand the full meaning of S2. It defines what is meant by the “anonymous altruistic behavior” introduced in S2: “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” It also gives us a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the kind behavior described in S1 that is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

Simplification: Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

S4: Notice the look above marker “SUCH acts.” We must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to. Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” As we noted in our analysis of S3, these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible explanation to answer the rhetorical question in S3.

Note: if we don’t know what “neural” means, we can see from S5 that it’s related to brain activity: “researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brain.”

Simplification: A certain study sought to find an explanation, related to brain activity, for anonymous altruistic behavior.

S5: Provides details about the design of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: Using fMRI, the researchers analyzed the subjects’ brain activity while the subjects decided whether to keep money or make a donation.

S6: Provides details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: The reward center of the brain that releases dopamine, which is associated with pleasure, became active when a donation was made.

S7: Adds more details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” S7 is very similar to S6, but discusses a different brain region and hormone.

Simplification: The region of the brain that produces oxytocin, which is associated with cooperation and trust, also became active when a donation was made.

S8: Notice the opinion marker “contended.” Also, notice that the word “so” connects a premise and a conclusion, in the structure “Premise, so Conclusion”

This sentence presents what the researchers have concluded from the findings of their study presented in the previous sentences.

Simplification: Since dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations, the scientists argue that biological factors play a role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

S9: Introduces a “compelling,” i.e., persuasive, alternative to the “biological argument” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Notice that the wording “THIS biological argument” is a look above marker and refers to the biological argument directly above in S8: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument.

Simplification: There is another perspective, that of Matthieu Ricard, which is more compelling than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

S10: Provides details about Ricard’s perspective.

Notice that “but” is a contrast marker in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard’s perspective is that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally, but it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.

S11: Discusses a type of evidence that Ricard uses, cognitive science research.

Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.

S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.”

Notice that the author repeats her positive judgment of Ricard’s perspective: S9 says that it’s “compelling,” and S12 says that it’s “persuasive.”

Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be genuinely selfless.” It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the organization of the passage?

answers.text: The author discusses a viewpoint and then explains why that viewpoint is no longer widely held.
After introducing a misconception, the author discredits those who hold this misconception.
After presenting a popular notion, the author outlines multiple counterpoints to that notion.
After describing some common concerns, the author argues that these concerns have no basis in reality.
The author outlines the problems resulting from a widespread belief and then outlines how those problems could be solved.

explanation_parts.text: We approach Structure questions similarly to the way we approach Primary Purpose questions: our goal is to understand the structure, main points, purpose, and tone.

We must carefully compare each noun and verb in the answer choices with what the passage says.In particular, we must watch out for Half-Right traps, in which part of the answer choice matches the passage, but part doesn’t.

In this case, we saw in our Passage Analysis that the passage begins by presenting a common negative belief about nuclear power (1stparagraph), and then the author challenges this belief (2ndand 3rdparagraphs).
The correct answer will accurately capture each piece of this structure.
(A) The author discusses a viewpoint and then explains why that viewpoint is no longer widely held.
Trap: Half-Right
Trap: Opposite
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “the author discusses a viewpoint,” in the 1stparagraph.
However, the author does not suggest that the viewpoint is “no longer widely held.”

Rather, the author suggests that the negative viewpoint about nuclear powercurrently IS widely held,but this negative beliefshould be overcome, in the future.The author’s point stated in S4 is “the actual peril involved in nuclear power generation IS highly overstated, and the US WOULD benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.” Notice that, in S4, the word “is” is present tense, and “would benefit” is about what is possible in the future.In general, it is important to not mix up tenses and time frames.
(B) After introducing a misconception, the author discredits those who hold this misconception.
Trap: Half-Right
Trap: Distortion
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the author is “introducing a misconception,” in the 1stparagraph, and then challenges that misconception in the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs.
However, does the authordiscredit“those who hold this misconception”?

In general, we must think carefully about the specific definitions of the verbs in answer choices in order to avoid a Wrong Verb trap.Here, “discredits” goes in a direction similar to the one “challenge” goes in but has a different meaning and is a distortion of what the passage says.Note that the definition of “discredits people” is “harms the good reputation of people.”
The author does notharm people’s reputations.Rather, the author provides a variety of evidence tochallenge the misconceptionof the people.
YOUR CHOICE(C) After presenting a popular notion, the author outlines multiple counterpoints to that notion.

We saw in our passage analysis that the passage begins by presenting a “widely held” negative “impression” of nuclear power (1stparagraph), and then the author challenges this impression, or belief (2ndand 3rdparagraphs).
Notice that “popular notion” in this choice matches with “widely held” “impression” and that “outlines multiple counterpoints to that notion” matches with “challenges this impression, or belief.”
So, we may quickly get the sense that this choice is the correct answer, but let’s confirm that the wording matches the passage:

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagepresenting apopular notiontheimpressionthat nuclear power plants are particularly hazardous, as well as uniquely expensive, has beenwidely heldoutlines multiplecounterpointsto that notiontheactualperil involved in nuclear power generation is highlyoverstated(S4)the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming thisunjustified perception(S4)thesafestamong the eight most common methods of energy production (S5)therealityis that it can besafelystored (S6)the potentialenvironmental benefits are profound(S7)there can berapid transformation, as evidenced by thesuccess storiesof France and Sweden (S8)overcoming these hurdles rapidly is not only essential but alsofeasible(S9)they will likely generate electricity at acost lowerthan that of fossil fuel generation (S10)rare bipartisan political support, a development suggesting that the future of nuclear power ispromising(S11)
We can see that this choice accurately describes the organization of the passage.
(D) After describing some common concerns, the author argues that these concerns have no basis in reality.
Trap: Half-Right
Trap: Stretch
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that author is indeed “describing some common concerns,” in the 1stparagraph, and that the author does challenge these concerns.
However, notice the extreme marker, “NO basis in reality,” which is a yellow flag.

The author does argue in favor of nuclear power, but the author is also cautious to acknowledge some basis for concerns.The author acknowledges some small counterpoints to his argument in both S4 and S9, in the “X” parts of the “Although X, Y” and “While X, Y” structures:
S4: “Although there is inevitablyat least some riskto every method of energy production,”
S9: “while it is true that thereremain economic obstaclesto widespread use of nuclear power in the United States,”
Overall, the author’s point is that the concerns are excessive and can be addressed, not that they have no basis at all in reality.So, we can see that this choice goes beyond what the passage says.
(E) The author outlines the problems resulting from a widespread belief and then outlines how those problems could be solved.
Trap: Distortion

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the passage discusses a “widely held” “impression” in the 1stparagraph and that then, in S4, the author argues that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”
However, the author never “outlines theproblemsresulting” from this belief or “outlines how those problems could be solved.”

Rather than discussproblems that resultfrom the belief, the author challenges the belief itself; the author argues that the populace in S1 is wrong in believing that nuclear power plants are dangerous and too expensive.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It’s helpful to notice that the passage begins by presenting a common belief: S1 says, “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.” When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion, with the two contrasting views forming the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented by S1 involves two negative ideas about nuclear power plants: that they are “particularly hazardous” and that they are “uniquely expensive.”

S2 and S3 then add supporting details that explain why people have fears of nuclear power. S2 provides the example of nuclear disaster in Chernobyl, and S3 mentions fear of radiation exposure and the health problems that can result from it.

Notice that, in the 1st paragraph, the author presents a common opinion but doesn’t present an opinion of his own.

1st paragraph summary: Many people in the US believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive and associate nuclear power plants with scary images of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster and with radiation exposure.

In S4, notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part of S4:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s “trepidation and disgust” related to nuclear power.

In the X part of S4, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that there is “some risk” involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.” Indeed, details about both safety and environmental benefits follow.

As we might expect, the rest of the 2nd paragraph provides details to support the author’s main point in S4. S5 and S6 address safety concerns, with specific statistics about the safety benefits of nuclear power. S7 introduces environmental benefits. S8 cites an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

2nd paragraph summary: The author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that there are both safety-related and environmental benefits to overcoming negative perceptions of nuclear power. The author supports his point with statistics, facts, and examples.

At this point, we may have a sense that the primary purpose of this passage is to challenge many people’s concerns that nuclear power plants are “particularly hazardous” and “uniquely expensive.”

We have seen that, in the 2nd paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, in the 3rd paragraph, the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” So, in the 3rd paragraph, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

In S9, notice the contrast marker “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part. Here, the author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10 supports the point stated by S9, saying that recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

In S11, the additional point marker “moreover” is a useful clue indicating that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above — in this case, the point above in S9. The author concludes the passage by stating a point similar to S9’s point, “overcoming these hurdles rapidly is … feasible,” saying “the future of nuclear power is promising.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly. The author supports his point that a rapid shift to nuclear power is possible, by citing two specific developments: new economical reactors and political support.

In reviewing the passage as a whole, it is important to note that the author’s overall tone is argumentative, not just descriptive. Throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the author presents a strong opinion in support of nuclear power and provides evidence of multiple types of benefits of nuclear power: safety, environmental, and economic.

Passage summary: The author presents and then challenges the impression widely held in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and uniquely expensive.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.
