Note: We don’t need to understand the technical word “terawatt-hour” in S5 to answer the questions correctly. However, from the context, we can infer that it is a unit of energy. If you are curious, “tera” is a unit prefix in the metric system that means multiplication by one trillion, or 1012. Another common use of “tera” is in “terabytes” (TB), in the context of memory capacity.

We also don’t need to understand the technical phrase “closed fuel cycle,” in S10.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice that a common belief is presented: “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.”

When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion. So, it is helpful to be aware of the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented in this sentence includes two negative ideas about nuclear power plants:

- “particularly hazardous”

- “uniquely expensive”

Simplification: Many people in the U.S. believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.

S2: Supports S1, with an example about why people believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous.

Simplification: These plants bring to mind scary images of the nuclear disaster in Chernobyl.

S3: Supports S1, with a detail about the fear of “radiation exposure.”

Simplification: Thinking about the dangers of radiation exposure provokes a fearful mindset.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s excessive fear of nuclear power.

In the X part, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that some risk is involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.”

Simplification: the author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that it is important to overcome this fear.

S5: Supports S4, with a statistic about the safety of nuclear power, as compared with the safety of other methods of energy production.

Simplification: Statistics show that nuclear power generation is safer than other common methods, particularly coal power.

S6: Supports S4, by addressing a concern about nuclear waste

Notice that “in reality” is an opinion/contrast marker. The author is saying that “concerns about nuclear waste” are not really a big deal, once you take into account certain facts.

Simplification: Nuclear waste isn’t a significant problem, because it can be safely stored and takes up a very small volume of space.

S7: Supports the point stated by S4 that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above.

S5 and S6 provided details about the safety benefits of nuclear power. Now, S7 introduces the environmental benefits.

Simplification: Also, nuclear power could have great environmental benefits, because it is the best way to prevent further global warming.

S8: Supports S4, with an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

Simplification: With a supportive political and regulatory situation, there can be a rapid shift to nuclear power, as we can see from the examples of France and Sweden.

S9: Here the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” In the second paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

Notice the contrast marker, “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Simplification: The author acknowledges that there are indeed economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US, but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10: Specifies an economic obstacle/problem and a potential solution:

Problem: “lack of standardization and repetition”

Solution: “Generation IV reactors that can be mass-produced”

Also, notice the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.” The final part of the sentence, “consequently, they will likely generate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation,” is a positive prediction about the future low relative cost of nuclear power.

Simplification: Recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

S11: Supports the main point in S9 that overcoming the economic obstacles rapidly is possible.

Notice the additional point marker “moreover,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above. We also see the phrase “additional economic benefits.” So, this sentence is expanding on the economic benefits discussed in S10 and playing a role similar to that of S10 in supporting S9.

Simplification: Since production of these new reactors may have many additional economic benefits, it has inspired significant political support, which is an encouraging sign for the future of nuclear power.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the organization of the passage?

answers.text: A scientific perspective is shown to have been questioned, and a proposed course of action is presented.
Two conflicting scientific opinions are presented and then reconciled.
The results of a research study are demonstrated to be suspect, and then a proposal is made.
A problem is outlined, and two potential solutions to that problem are evaluated.
A point of scientific disagreement is identified, and the argument on each side is outlined.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
We approach Structure questions similarly to how we approach Primary Purpose questions: our goal is to understand the structure, important points, purpose, and tone.
For the purpose of identifying important points, it’s helpful to note that passages often will involve some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions.

In this case, we see that the passage starts off by presenting the perspective and approach of “many reputable scientists” in S1 and then, in S2, uses the contrast marker “although” to introduce the contrasting view of Teixeira and Huber.The remainder of the passage expands on Teixeira and Huber’s view.
YOUR CHOICE(A) A scientific perspective is shown to have been questioned, and a proposed course of action is presented.
First, is a scientific perspective “shownto have been questioned”?
Notice that the word “questioned” has a similar meaning to the opinion marker, “challenges,” in S2.

We must confirm that Teixeira and Huber are questioning “a scientific perspective.” Indeed, S2 refers to a perspective (the assumption that), in “challengesthe assumption thata high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”
So, we can see that by describing Teixeira and Huber’s challenging the “assumption,” the passage shows a scientific perspective to have been questioned.
Next, is “a proposed course of action presented”?

S5 has theopinion marker, “must” in “they argue that … mitigation measuresmustbe taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.” This wording indicates that Teixeira and Huber are arguing for orproposingtaking this particular course of action.
Also notice that the verb “presented” in this choice matches the descriptive tone of the author.
So, this choice accurately describes the structure of the passage.
(B) Two conflicting scientific opinions are presented and then reconciled.
Trap: Half-Right
Trap:Wrong Verb

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that, in the passage, “two conflicting scientific opinions are presented.” Indeed, this choice matches well with the contrast between Teixeira and Huber’s opinion and the approach of “many reputable scientists.”
However, does the verb “reconciled” match?
Reconciled means “restore friendly relations with” or “show to be compatible.”
However, the two opinions are never shown to be compatible.Rather, the two opinions are only shown to be in conflict.
(C) The results of a research study are demonstrated to be suspect, and then a proposal is made.
Trap: Mix-Up / Wrong Verb
If you selected this choice, you may have noticed that S1 mentions “using theresults,” that S3 mentions Teixeira and Huber’s “study,” and that S3 says “challenges the assumption.”
Notice, however, that this choice does not match the passage because this choice mixes up the topics mentioned in the passage.

The “results” mentioned in S2 are not “a research study.” The only “research study” mentioned is Teixeira and Huber’s study.So, this choice mixes up “research study” and what’s challenged.In other words, the passage does mention a “study,” but the results of that study are not challenged, and the passage says that Teixeira and Huber challenge something, but not that they demonstrate to be suspect “the results of a research study.”

Furthermore, notice that the passage itself does not “demonstrate” that any “results of a research study” are “suspect.” The only study mentioned in the passage is Teixeira and Huber’s study, and the passage does not demonstrate anything about this study.Rather, the passage simplydescribesthis study and Teixeira and Huber’s opinions.

Finally, if you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “then a proposal is made” seems to match part of S5.In arguing that “mitigation measuresmustbe taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems,” Teixeira and Huber areproposingtaking mitigation measures.

Notice, however, that the author of the passage is not making this proposal.The author simplydescribeswhat Teixeira and Huber have proposed.So, “a proposal is made” does not accurately describe what the passage itself does.
(D) A problem is outlined, and two potential solutions to that problem are evaluated.
Trap: Half-Right
Trap: Wrong Verb
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that a “problem is presented” in S1: “therate of species extinctionon Earth is at a levelunprecedentedin modern history.”
However, we must always check the entire choice: does “two potential solutions to that problem are evaluated” match what the passage does?

No, the word “evaluated” would imply that the author is judging two solutions and presenting some sort of conclusion about their merits.However, from our Passage Analysis, we see that the author isdescribingtwo contrasting opinions, but does NOT express any conclusion about them.
Trap: Distortion

Furthermore, “two solutions” is a bit of a distortion.Notice that “two solutions” is plural; although Teixeira and Huber discuss a “solution,” the scientists do not.The scientists in S1measuregenetic diversity toassessextinction risk.This process is then referred to as “this type ofanalysis,” at the start of S2.“Analysis” is not the same as a “solution”; a solution would include more action steps to solve a problem.
(E) A point of scientific disagreement is identified, and the argument on each side is outlined.
Trap: Half-Right

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “a point of scientific disagreement is identified” by the passage.The passage indicates that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists mentioned in S1.

However, “the argument oneachside isoutlined” would mean that both arguments are discussed point-by-point, with both premises and conclusions.However, in the passage, only Teixeira and Huber’s argument is “outlined.” The approach of the scientists in S1 is only mentioned briefly, in the context of Teixeira and Huber’s challenge of it.In other words, the passage focuses on Teixeira and Huber’s argument, not on “each side.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage has only one paragraph. Therefore, we don’t have the structural clues that come from the transitions from one paragraph to another. So, when there is only one paragraph, it is particularly helpful to notice contrast markers and opinion markers, which give us clues to the structure and important points.

As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast or opinions in the first two sentences. In this passage, the most helpful clues to the structure are markers in S2: the contrast marker, “although,” and the opinion marker, “challenges.” These markers signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.” in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship is a relation such that, when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

It’s important to notice that the tone of these sentences is descriptive: the author is not making an argument of his own. Rather, the author is describing two contrasting views: that of the scientists in S1, and that of Teixeira and Huber in S2.

So, from these first two sentences, we already have a good sense of the basic structure of the passage, and it makes sense that the sentences that follow will expand upon Teixeira and Huber’s view presented in S2.

Indeed, notice the many additional opinion markers in the final three sentences: “they acknowledge that,” “maintain that,” “contend that,” “should,” “argue that,” and “must.”

Let’s summarize these additional points of Teixeira and Huber:

S3: although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival.

S4: to prevent species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

Passage summary: In the context of preventing species’ extinction, Teixeira and Huber disagree with many scientists’ focus on genetic diversity and contend that it makes more sense to focus on habitat destruction and to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Simpler Similar Words

extinction

disappearance, dying out

unprecedented

unmatched, unequaled, never experienced before

reputable

respected, highly regarded

bases (plural of “basis”)

support, foundation, justification

assessments

evaluation, estimation

genetic mutations

genetic changes or variations (can result in increased genetic diversity)

thrive

grow, prosper, expand, flourish

contend

argue, believe, assert

vast

large, immense

widespread ecological disruption

significant environmental damage

mitigation

lessening, alleviation (reducing the severity or painfulness of something)

measures

actions, steps
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: By describing what some scientists have been doing because they are “Concerned by the fact that the rate of species extinction … is at a level unprecedented,” this sentence implies that these scientists are of the view that “measuring the genetic diversity of species” makes sense or is important in the context of species extinction.

Simplification: Because scientists are concerned about the high rate of species extinction, they have been using measures of genetic diversity to assess extinction risk.

S2: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point.

Also notice the opinion marker, “challenges the assumption that.”

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, the presence of “this type of analysis” signals that we must look above to understand what “this type of analysis” refers to. We see that “this type of analysis” refers to the scientists’ work of “measuring” and “assessments” in S1.

These markers, particularly the word “challenges,” signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists mentioned above in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk”; in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.

S3: Notice two more opinion markers, “they acknowledge that” and “they maintain that.” This sentence follows up on S2 with more details and reasoning behind Teixeira and Huber’s disagreement with the scientists in S1.

Also, notice the contrast marker, “while,” which follows the structure “while X, Y.” This structure often concedes exceptions in the X part, and then makes the main point in the Y part.

Here, the X part, “they acknowledge that, in SOME cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” represents an exception to Teixeira and Huber’s main point in the Y part, “they maintain that the MAJORITY of genetic diversity is ‘neutral,’ in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival. (Note that this sentence supports the main point of S2: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.)

S4: Notice two more opinion markers, “contend that” and “should.”

Also, notice the contrast marker “instead of.” What are the two things that are in contrast?

Genetic diversity and habitat destruction – “instead of genetic diversity, the primary concern … should be the issue of habitat destruction.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, in preventing species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: This sentence starts with the support marker “Citing the fact that X,” which is logically followed by the opinion marker “they argue that Y.” Often, both support and opinion are presented in a sentence, with a structure similar to “Since X (support), Y (opinion).”

There is another opinion marker, “must,” at the end of the sentence: “mitigation measures must be taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.” This wording signifies a proposal for what should be done about the problem. In this case, the problem cited is habitat loss and ecological disruption.

Simplification: Since large areas of wildlife habitat have been destroyed, Teixeira and Huber argue that humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the organization of the passage?

answers.text: A type of event is described, a claim about that type of event is made, and then support for that claim is provided.
A theory is presented, the historical implications of that theory are discussed, and then that theory is applied in the prediction of future events.
A problem is presented, an opinion about that problem is expressed, and then a contrasting opinion is considered.
A phenomenon is explained, and then effects such a phenomenon has had during different time periods are compared.
An issue is introduced, examples of that issue are presented, and then ways of addressing that issue are explored.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
Since this passage is composed of only one paragraph, we can analyze the structure by considering the purposes of sentences in the passage.
S1 and S2 present the concept of a CME and the example of the Carrington Event.

Then, as we discussed on our Passage Analysis, in S3, the contrast word “however” and the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating …” indicate that S3 is stating an important point.In fact, with S3, the author begins to present his main point.

We can see that, in saying, “the fact … CANNOT be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems ARE safe from the effects of CMEs,” the author is implying in S3 that today’s communication systems are NOT safe from CMEs.

In S4, the author continues to present his main point about communications systems’ not being safe from CMEs by saying that things are different now, in that communications systems are no longer “primitive,” and people live in “a globally wired society.”
Then, S5 completes the presentation of the author’s main point by stating the conclusion, “therefore, a Carrington Event-level storm” would cause severe disruptions of today’s communications systems.
The rest of the passage supports the author’s point with information about another storm caused by a CME.
So, the structure of the passage is that it presents the concept of a CME, states a point or claim about CMEs, and then supports that point or claim.
YOUR CHOICE(A) A type of event is described, a claim about that type of event is made, and then support for that claim is provided.
“A type of event is described” in this choice matches the passage’s presenting the concept of a CME in the first two sentences.
“A claim about that type of event” in this choice matches the author’s presenting his main point in S3 through S5.
“Support for that claim” in this choice matches the discussion of the 775 CME toward the end of the passage.
So, this choice accurately describes the organization of the passage.
(B) A theory is presented, the historical implications of that theory are discussed, and then that theory is applied in the prediction of future events.
Trap: Distortion
This choice goes wrong mostly because it uses the noun “theory.” The passage does not discuss any theory but, rather, discusses the phenomena, or events, CMEs and the geomagnetic storms they cause.
(C) A problem is presented, an opinion about that problem is expressed, and then a contrasting opinion is considered.

We might wonder whether “a problem” in this choice matches “the effects of CMEs” in the passage, and we might wonder whether “an opinion about the problem” matches the author’s opinion expressed by S3 and S5.

However, when we compare the last part of this choice with the passage, we no longer have to wonder whether this choice is correct, because the passage simply does not consider any “contrasting opinion.”
(D) A phenomenon is explained, and then effects such a phenomenon has had during different time periods are compared.
Trap: Wrong Verb

The first indication we have that this choice is likely not the correct answer is that, in the passage, the phenomenon a CME is not really “explained.” The passage says a few things about CMEs and the “flurry of strong magnetic and electrical activity” that a CME induces without explaining them.
Trap: Distortion
Then what takes this choice completely out of contention is the wording “effects such a phenomenonhas hadduring different time periods arecompared.”

The passage mentions the effects of only one CME, the 1859 CME.It doesn’t “compare” the effects of that CME with the effects any other CME “has had” in the past.Rather, after mentioning the 1859 CME, the passage discusses what “could” happen in the future.
(E) An issue is introduced, examples of that issue are presented, and then ways of addressing that issue are explored.
We might wonder whether “an issue” in this choice matches “the effects of CMEs” in the passage, and we might wonder whether “examples of that issue” matches the storms discussed in the passage.

However, when we compare the last part of this choice with the passage, we no longer have to wonder whether this choice is correct, because the passage simply does not explore any “ways of addressing” any issue.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage begins by introducing the concept of a coronal mass ejection (CME), and first thing that jumps out at us as we’re reading is that S1 says that “in most cases” a CME “never reaches Earth BUT.” Contrasts words such as “but” normally introduce important points. So, the important point to notice here is “but, when a CME does reach Earth, it induces a flurry of strong magnetic and electrical activity.”

Then, the passage presents an example of a CME, the CME that caused “the largest geomagnetic storm ever recorded” during the Carrington Event in 1859.

So far, the passage has been descriptive, simply presenting information about CMEs and the example of the Carrington Event.

Then, in S3, the contrast word “however” and the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating …” indicate that S3 is stating an important point. In fact, with S3, the author begins to present his main point.

So, what is the point the author is making?

We can see that, in saying, “the fact … CANNOT be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems ARE safe from the effects of CMEs,” the author is implying that today’s communication systems are NOT safe from CMEs.

Then, S4 begins to explain why today’s communication systems are not safe from CMEs – the systems are not “primitive”; rather society is now “highly dependent upon electrical signals” – and S5 uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to present a conclusion about the current situation, “a Carrington Event-level storm today could cause a devastating ‘internet apocalypse.’”

Then the rest of the passage simply presents another example to support the point that today’s communications systems are not safe from CMEs, the example of the “Miyake event” of 775. The implications of this part of the passage are that storms even bigger than the Carrington Event are possible and could cause “almost … complete destruction of communication infrastructure” and “near total loss of the date stored on computer hard drives.”

Passage summary: The passage introduces the concept of a CME and then proceeds to make the argument that today’s communication infrastructure is not safe from CMEs by saying that today’s communication infrastructure is not primitive like past electrical infrastructure and by describing what would occur if storms like ones that occurred in the past were to occur today.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Similar Words

coronal mass ejection

a burst of matter and magnetic fields released by the sun

induces

results in, causes, gives rise to

flurry

short period of activity, burst, surge

pummeled

hit or beat repeatedly

infrastructure

system, network, foundation

apocalypse

disaster, catastrophe, destruction

megastorm

very powerful storm

incalculably

too great to be calculated, immensely, extremely

allay

reduce, sooth, calm

We don’t need to understand the technical words “magnetosphere” and “geomagnetic.” We can understand the passage and correctly answer every question without knowing exactly what these words mean.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: Introduces the concept of a coronal mass ejection (CME) and the effect a CME can have on Earth.

Simplification: A coronal mass ejection (CME) that reaches Earth causes strong magnetic and electrical activity

S2: Introduces an example of storm caused by a CME, the Carrington Event in 1859.

Notice that this sentence says that the geomagnetic storm “induced,” meaning “caused,” by the Carrington Event was “the largest geomagnetic storm ever recorded.” An extreme fact such as this one will likely play an important role in the passage. After all, it’s almost certain that the author has mentioned such an extreme fact for the purpose of making a point.

S3: There are two signs that this sentence states an important point.

The first is the contrast marker “however,” since contrasts are often connected to important points of a passage.

The second is the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating ….”

We can see that, in this sentence, the author is making a strong assertion that will likely be supported and discussed further as the passage progresses.

Simplification: The fact that the 1859 CME didn’t cause much trouble doesn’t mean that present communications systems are safe from CMEs.

S4: Uses the comparison marker “whereas” to express a comparison.

Comparisons are usually important parts of passages, and this comparison is indeed important, because the fact that, unlike in 1859, people now live in a “globally wired society” both explains why “today’s communications systems” are not safe from the effects of CMEs, as implied by S3, and supports S5, as we will see.

S5: Uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to present the consequence of what S4 says.

Simplification: Because, as S4 says, people live in a “globally wired society,” if an event like the Carrington Event were to occur today, it would cause huge communication problems.

S6: Uses the additional point marker “furthermore” to provide addition support for the author’s strong assertion in S3 that the fact that the 1859 CME didn’t cause much trouble doesn’t mean that present communications systems are safe from a large CME.

The implication of S6 is that, since such a large storm occurred in 775, a similar one could occur today.

Simplification: The Carrington event isn’t even the biggest “geomagnetic storm” ever. A much bigger one occurred in 775.

SB: Continues to support the strong assertion in S3 by saying that a storm like the one that occurred in 775 would cause “incalculably great” damage.

S9: Continues to support the strong assertion in S3 by providing more detail on the damage that would be caused by a storm like that one that occurred in 775.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the organization of the passage?

answers.text: A problem is introduced, causes of the problem are identified, and potential solutions are considered
Two opposing opinions are considered, and then one is shown to have more merit than the other.
An issue is presented and illustrated, and then considerations related to addressing that issue are discussed.
A policy is presented, benefits and drawbacks of the policy are examined, and the value of the policy is assessed.
A question is presented, and then potential answers to that question are considered.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
To answer this Structure question, we can consider important points and the purposes of sentences and paragraphs in the passage.

Then, we must carefully compare each noun, verb, and modifier in the answer choices with what the passage says so that we don’t choose a choice such that part of the choice matches the passage and part doesn’t.

We saw in our Passage Analysis that the passage begins by presenting the two sides of a situation involving freedom of the press: freedom of the press is important, and freedom of the press allows the media to influence outcomes of elections.

Then the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs expand on the idea that freedom of the press allows the media to influence outcomes of elections by presenting examples and explaining why the media presents news in a biased way.
Finally, the 4thparagraph wraps up the passage by discussing some considerations related to addressing the issue of the influence of the press on electoral outcomes.
The correct answer will accurately capture each piece of this structure.
(A) A problem is introduced, causes of the problem are identified, and potential solutions are considered
Trap: Half-Right

This choice starts off OK because we could say that the 1stparagraph introduces the problem that media influences outcomes of elections and that the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs describe “causes of” that problem.
However, this choice goes clearly wrong at the end because the 4thparagraph does not present any “potential solutions” to the problem.
So, this choice is a great example of why we have to make sure that all of a choice we’re considering matches the passage.
(B) Two opposing opinions are considered, and then one is shown to have more merit than the other.
Trap: Half-Right
The passage doesn’t begin by considering “two opinions.” It begins by presenting two sides of a situation involving freedom of the press.

The second part of this choice could be considered correct, because the last two sentences of the passage say, “a free press is integral ….Therefore, a better question to ask ….” In saying that, the passage is basically saying that the opinion implied by the question “what can be done within the context of freedom of the press to reduce the distortion,” is more valid than the opinion implied by the question presented earlier, “whether the virtually complete freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media makes sense.”
All the same, since this choice is only partially correct, it’s completely wrong.
YOUR CHOICE(C) An issue is presented and illustrated, and then considerations related to addressing that issue are discussed.

As we discussed in our question analysis, the 1stparagraph presents the issue of the fact that the freedom of the press is important but allows the media to influence the outcomes of elections, then illustrates the issue by presenting examples and explaining causes in the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs, and finally, in the 4thparagraph, discusses some considerations related to addressing the issue.
So, this choice accurately describes the organization of the passage.
(D) A policy is presented, benefits and drawbacks of the policy are examined, and the value of the policy is assessed.
Trap: Distortion
This choice comes close to describing the structure of the passage, but misses a bit with each part.

The first part of this choice doesn’t quite match the passage because, while the freedom of the press could be considered “a policy,” the first part of the passage doesn’t just introduce the freedom of the press; it introduces two sides of a situation involving the freedom of the press.

The second part of this choice misses because, while the passage does examine some issues that could be considered “drawbacks” of the freedom of the press, the passage does not examine any “benefits.”

Finally, the third part of this choice doesn’t quite match the passage because, while the passage does say in the 4thparagraph that the freedom of the press is “integral,” the passage doesn’t really “assess,” i.e., determine, the “value” of the freedom of the press.
(E) A question is presented, and then potential answers to that question are considered.
This choice is almost completely wrong.
The passage does present some questions, but at the end, not at the beginning.
Also, the passage does not really consider answers to those questions.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In the 1st paragraph, the author introduces two sides of a situation. The two sides of the situation are that the freedom of the press is a “cornerstone,” meaning “important feature,” of American democracy and that the freedom of the press allows the media to influence outcomes of elections.

Notice that, while the author does not directly state an opinion in this paragraph, the tone clues “cornerstone” and “provide voters with information they need” indicate that the author believes that the freedom of the press is important and beneficial.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st.paragraph introduces the topic that the passage goes on to discuss, which is the two sides of the freedom of the press.

The 2nd paragraph begins with the look above marker “SUCH influence,” and the 2nd paragraph presents an example of how the media influences elections, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph. This way is that the media chooses what it conveys, i.e., covers.

2nd paragraph summary: The 2nd paragraph presents an example of how the media influences elections, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph.

The 3rd paragraph further discusses the issue of the media influencing elections by bringing up another aspect of this influence, which is that both social media platforms and major media outlets present news in a biased way. The social media platforms present news in a biased way to attract users, and the major media outlets present news in a biased way to attract the interest of as large an audience as possible.

This paragraph also shows a negative result of the biased way in which major media outlets present news: voters get funneled “to either the far right or the far left of the political spectrum.”

Notice that, so far, the author has not presented an opinion about all this media influence. He’s simply describing it, though clearly presenting a somewhat negative picture.

3rd paragraph summary: The 3rd paragraph discusses the fact that, to achieve goals, social media platforms and major media outlets present news in a biased way.

So far, the author has described the situation involving the media influencing the outcomes of elections and the electorate without presenting any opinions about the situation, but now in the 4th paragraph, the author presents some opinions

The first opinion is that of “some people,” who have “questioned whether the virtually complete freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media makes sense.” Notice that this opinion is not the author’s opinion, though the author does follow up this opinion by expressing an opinion of his own, which is that “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.”

Then, the author wraps up the passage with two important points.

The first important point is signaled by the contrast marker “however,” since contrasts are generally connected to important points, and this point is that “the free press is integral to the functioning of a democratic society.” We can see that with this sentence, the author is once again bringing up the other side of the situation that was brought up in the 1st paragraph, which is that freedom of the press is important.

Then the most important point of the passage is introduced by the conclusion marker “therefore.” Why is this last sentence the most important point? Because, in this sentence, the author finally states a strong opinion about the situation involving the freedom of the press by using the strong language “a better question to ask” and then saying that what that better question is, which is “what can be done within the context of freedom of the press to reduce the distortion of electoral results by the media.”

We can see that the author believes that the freedom of the press is important and has therefore concluded that the thing to do is to figure out what to do about media influence while leaving the freedom of the press in place.

4th paragraph summary: The 4th paragraph discusses some considerations related to addressing the issue of the distorted influence of the media on electoral outcomes and then presents a final conclusion of the author’s about what how this issue should be addressed.

Passage summary: The passage introduces a situation that has two sides, the importance of the freedom of the press and the influence that the media has on the outcomes of elections, illustrates the issue of the influence of the media on the outcomes of election with examples, and wraps up by discussing how the issue might best be addressed.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Introduces the first side of the situation.

The first side of the situation is that freedom of the press is a “cornerstone,” meaning “important feature,” of American democracy.

Simplification: The freedom of the press is important for American democracy.

S2: Introduces the second side of the situation. 

The second side of the situation is that the media exploits freedom of the press to influence election outcomes, not always in a good way. 

By presenting these two sides of a situation, this sentence foreshadows that the passage will discuss these two sides of this situation.

Simplification: The media uses freedom of the press to influence the outcomes of elections.

S3: Uses the look above marker “SUCH influence” to introduce one example of how the media influences elections as introduced in S2, by choosing what to convey.

Simplification: One way in which the media influences elections is by choosing what to talk about.

S4: Provides detail on the fact introduced by S3 that the media can choose what to convey.

Simplification: Reporters can choose what to cover, so sometimes candidates don’t get coverage and are “left almost invisible to voters.”

S5: Uses the look above marker “SUCH candidates” to describe what happens when the candidates mentioned in the previous sentence are “left almost invisible to voters.”

Simplification: The candidates “left almost invisible to voters” struggle to win elections even when they would have done good things, because voters don’t recognize the candidates’ names.

S6: Uses the additional point marker “in addition” to introduce another example of how the media influences elections, the use of “sophisticated algorithms” by social media platforms.

Simplification: Many people access news by using social media platforms, which use algorithms to decide what news to show users.

S7: Expands on the discussion of social media “algorithms” begun by S6 by presenting the results of the use of algorithms by social media.

Notice the strong language, “it would be hard to argue ….” This strong language is a clue indicating that the author is stating an important point in this sentence.

Also, notice that “it would be hard to argue that that user is getting unbiased political news,” is sort of a double negative that means, essentially, “it can be argued that that user is getting biased political news.”

Simplification: Social media algorithms show users stories meant to capture their attention, and as a result, users get biased political news.

S8: Introduces another reason why media influence elections: major media outlets have the “goal” of attracting the interest of as large an audience as possible.

S9: Uses the look above marker “THIS aim” to continue to discuss the “goal” mentioned in S8.

Notice that this sentence uses the causation marker “since” to show a cause and effect. The cause is “voters have the tendency to seek out news that does not challenge what they believe is true,” and the effect is that major media outlets can achieve their “aim” or “goal” by “reflecting biases.” Sentences that mention causes and effects are usually closely connected to the primary purpose of the passage, and we can see that this sentence is mentioning “biases” as S7 did. So, bias in media appears to be a key theme of this passage.

Simplification: Since people seek out news they agree with, major media outlets can achieve their goal of attracting a large audience by being biased.

S10: Uses the look above marker “THIS ‘selective exposure’” to continue the discussion of major media outlets presenting biased news to attract a large audience.

“Funnels many voters to either the far right or the far left” means, basically, “directs many voters to either the far right or the far left.” So, this sentence is showing how media outlets affect elections by pushing voters to the right or left.

Also, notice that “not-so-objective” is another way of saying “biased.” So, this sentence is once again discussing media bias.

Simplification: There are many biased news outlets that push voters to the far right or far left.

S11: Uses the causation marker “consequently” to present a result of what the passage has said in the preceding paragraphs: “Consequently, some people have questioned whether … freedom of the press … makes sense.”

Notice that this sentence presents an opinion of the author’s: “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.” Directly stated opinions of the author are good clues about the primary purpose of the passage.

Simplification: Because of the effects of media’s biased coverage, some people question whether freedom of the press makes sense, and this questioning is reasonable since “the fact that the free press has a distorted influence on electoral results is a significant issue.”

S12: Uses the additional point marker “furthermore” to present more support for the author’s opinion “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.”

Simplification: Another reason why “this questioning is not completely unwarranted” is that it would be hard to do something about the not so good influence of the media on elections.

S13: Uses the contrast marker “however” to present information that contrasts with what has just been said.

“Integral” means “essential” or “necessary.” So, since something “essential” is important, this sentence is a strong statement about freedom of the press.

Simplification: A fact that contrasts with the facts that support the questioning of the freedom of the press is the fact that freedom of the press is necessary.

S14: Uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to state a final opinion of the author’s.

When the last sentence of the passage states an opinion of the author’s, that opinion is usually either the main point of the passage or very closely connected to the main point and primary purpose of the passage. So, this sentence will be a useful clue when it comes time to answer a primary purpose question about this passage.

Simplification: Therefore, rather than question the freedom of the press, people should focus on reducing media “distortion” or “bias” without changing the freedom of the press itself.

question.text: Which of the following most accurately describes the relationship between the second and third paragraphs of the passage?

answers.text: The third paragraph expands on an idea presented in the second paragraph.
The third paragraph presents an effective rebuttal of a point made in the second paragraph.
The third paragraph presents a scientific development with implications that contrast with those of one presented in the second paragraph.
The third paragraph provides an example of an exception to a statement of generality that appears in the second paragraph.
The third paragraph qualifies a claim presented in the second paragraph.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

The wording of the stem, “relationship between the second and third paragraphs,” tells us that this is a Structure question.We approach Structure questions similarly to the way we approach Primary Purpose questions: our goal is to understand main points, structure, purpose, and tone.
However, one difference here is that we will put more emphasis on the transition between the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs, and be aware of markers that help us understand the connection between them.
The transition between two paragraphs is generally accomplished by the first sentence of the second of the two paragraphs, which, in this case, is S10.
Let’s review our Sentence Analysis for S10:
S10: Introduces another discovery, which supports Lamarck’s theory.
Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.”
Notice the contrast marker “nevertheless,” which conveys, “while the statements above are true, the following is also true.”
The contrast is that, whereas S8 and S9 go against Lamarck’s theory, S10 supports Lamarck’s theory: “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge.”
Simplification: Nevertheless, one discovery does support Lamarck’s theory: single-celled organisms that can change their genes and traits during their lifetimes.
Then, S11 and S12 explain why “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge.”
So, the relationship between the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs is that the third paragraph presents a discovery that contrasts with what’s discussed in the 2ndparagraph.
(A) The third paragraph expands on an idea presented in the second paragraph.
Trap: Wrong Verb

The verb “expands” does not match thecontrastexpressed in the passage.“Expands” would be correct if the ideas in the two paragraphs were going in the same direction, but in our Question Analysis we noted thecontrastbetween what the two paragraphs say.
(B) The third paragraph presents an effective rebuttal of a point made in the second paragraph.
Trap: Distortion
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed the contrast marker “nevertheless” in S10 and the comparison marker “unlike” in S11 and thought that the word “rebuttal” fit with this contrast.
However, note that “rebuttal” means “refutation,” and it doesn’t make sense that the author would directly refute her own conclusion in S9.

In fact, the contrast between the two paragraphs does not constitute a rebuttal.Rather the 3rdparagraph uses “nevertheless” to convey the meaning, “while the claims above may be true, the following is also true.” Then the 3rdparagraph presents information on a discovery different from the one discussed in the 2ndparagraph and states a conclusion that contrasts with S9 but is not a “rebuttal” of S9.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) The third paragraph presents a scientific development with implications that contrast with those of one presented in the second paragraph.
Notice the wording “contrast with.” We may quickly get a sense that this choice captures the contrast discussed in our Question Analysis, but we must confirm that the exact wording matches.

Note those “those of one” in this choice means “the implications of a scientific development.” So, this choice means “The third paragraph presents a scientific development with implications that contrast withthe implications of a scientific developmentpresented in the second paragraph.”

In the 2ndparagraph, the “scientific development” is the discovery of gene expression modifications, and the “implications” are that Lamarck’s theory of evolution has NOT been proven at least somewhat valid because “this type of passing of traits from generation to generation does NOT result in evolution.”

In the 3rdparagraph, the “scientific development” is the discovery involving single-celled organisms, and the “implications” are that “Lamarck’s theory HAS found a sole refuge” because “the changes in traits resulting from these organisms’ changes to their genes” DO “constitute evolution.”
We can see that the implications of the scientific development presented in the 3rdparagraph contrast with those of the development presented in the 2ndparagraph.
So, this choice is correct.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The third paragraph provides an example of an exception to a statement of generality that appears in the second paragraph.
Trap: Distortion

This is a particularly tempting trap.S8 uses the qualifier marker, “typically,” which does suggest that there could be potential exceptions to the “statement of generality” in S8: “such gene expression modifications and their associated traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for only a few generations and eventually disappear.”

However, to be an “exception” to this generalization, the discovery in the 3rdparagraph would also have to involve gene expression modifications, but the passage says that this discovery is quite different.S11 says that “these changes made by these organisms during their lifetimes” are “unlike gene expression modifications.”

Additionally, it’s helpful to note that the conclusion in S9, “this type of passing of traits from generation to generation does not result in evolution,” is limited to just “this type,” which refers to gene expression modifications.In contrast, the 3rdparagraph discusses adifferent typeof passing of traits from generation to generation.
(E) The third paragraph qualifies a claim presented in the second paragraph.
Trap: Distortion / Wrong Verb
Note that “qualifies” means “make a statement or assertion less extreme or less general.”

The third paragraph does not “qualify” a claim presented in the second paragraph.Rather, from our Question Analysis, we can see that the 3rdparagraph uses the contrast marker “nevertheless” to convey, “while the statements above are true, the following is also true,” and then presents a discovery that contrasts with the discovery discussed in 2ndparagraph.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast, opinions, and tone clues in the first two sentences.

In this passage, there is an opinion marker in S1, “Lamarck proposed that ….” S1 sets the stage for the rest of the passage by presenting the theory of evolution that Lamarck proposed, which is “evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.”

Then, in S2, the contrast marker “however” signals an important point, which is “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted.”

Note that the tone clue, “seemed” signals some uncertainty about whether Lamarck’s theory had actually been refuted and foreshadows that there may be further debate or insights on this topic.

So, from these first two sentences, we already get the sense that the main topic of the passage is likely to be discussion of the validity of Lamarck’s evolutionary theory.

Then, S3 and S4 go on to explain why it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted, the main reason being “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.” In other words, S3 and S4 communicate that what “biologists decided” indicated that evolution could not work in accordance with Lamarck’s theory.

Notice that, so far, the author’s tone is descriptive; she presents Lamarck’s theory and the biologists’ reasoning against it, but she does not make an argument of her own. In other words, she has not yet expressed her own opinion.

1st paragraph summary: Lamarck proposed a theory of evolution, “evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.” Then, with the discovery of genetics, it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been refuted.

Next, we see that the 2nd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals that what the 2nd paragraph says is in contrast with what’s said at the end of the first paragraph.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S5, we also see the look above marker “SUCH a mechanism.” What “such a mechanism” refers to is the mechanism mentioned earlier, in S4, “a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.”

What’s said in the 2nd paragraph contrasts with what’s said in the 1st paragraph in the following way. Whereas S4 says, “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring,” now S5 says, “However, more recently, such a mechanism has been discovered.” In other words, the biologists’ belief has been shown to be incorrect.

Then, S6 provides details about this recent discovery. This discovery is that new traits developed during an organism’s lifetime CAN be passed on to offspring. We know from the 1st paragraph that passing on traits is an important element of “evolution.”

Next, in S7, we want to put extra emphasis on the rhetorical question “Has Lamarck’s evolutionary theory been proven at least somewhat valid?” Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Indeed, S8 and S9 answer this question, with basically a “No” answer. Notice that S8 provides the reasoning of “most evolutionary biologists,” and then S9 goes on to provide a direct conclusion of the author, not the opinion of another group.

2nd paragraph summary: A recent discovery involving gene expression modifications shows that traits acquired during an organisms’ lifetime can be passed on to the organism’s offspring, but only for a few generations. So, this phenomenon isn’t evolution and does not support Lamarck’s theory.

Like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph begins with a contrast marker, “nevertheless.” So, what is the contrast?

Whereas S8 and S9 go against Lamarck’s theory, S10 supports Lamarck’s theory: “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge, in the world of a certain few single-celled organisms that have been discovered to be able to change their genes ….” Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.” So, S10 means essentially “There is one situation in which Lamarck’s theory does apply, the world of certain single-celled organism that have been discovered to be able to change their genes.”

As we might expect, S11 then provides additional details to explain why this discovery supports Lamarck’s theory. Notice the contrast marker structure, “Unlike X, Y.” S11 is saying that “these changes made by these organisms” are “unlike” the gene expression modifications discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

What is the key difference between “gene expression modifications” and “these changes”?

Whereas S8 said that “gene expression modifications and their associated traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for only a few generations and eventually disappear,” S11 says that “these changes” made by the single-celled organisms are “passed from generation to generation indefinitely,” which means “for an unlimited period of time.” Simply put, the difference is about traits passed on temporarily versus traits passed on indefinitely.

In S12, the author’s final conclusion is signaled by the conclusion marker “thus.”

We can see that this conclusion by the author contrasts with the author’s conclusion in S9. Whereas in S9, the “gene expression modification” type of passing of traits does NOT result in evolution, the “changes to genes” type of passing of traits in S12 DOES result in evolution.

3rd paragraph summary: However, there is one discovery that DOES support Lamarck’s evolutionary theory: single-celled organisms that change their genes and traits during their lifetimes and pass on those traits indefinitely.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear?

Throughout the passage, there is a lot of contrast, and the author goes back and forth on the question of whether Lamarck’s evolutionary theory is valid.

Passage summary: The author considers the validity of Lamarck’s theory of evolution, in light of biologists’ understanding in the past and two more recent discoveries.

In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Similar Words, and Definitions

evolution

change in the traits of a species over successive generations (Note: we don’t need to know the definition of “evolution” on our own, because it is defined in certain ways in the passage. The passage suggests that there are multiple different types of evolution. Note that S8 and S9 specify that “traits passed on … for only a few generations…does NOT result in evolution”)

(Note that “sole” is similar to “solo,” which means “alone.”)
