Note: The technical terms “modification of gene expression” and “gene expression modifications” appear throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. The passage provides context and details about what this means so we don’t have to know on our own. We must not make the assumption that this term means that the genes themselves are being changed (modification); rather, “gene expression” has a different meaning from just “genes.” We can see this most clearly in the passage with the comparison made in S11: “UNLIKE gene expression modifications, these changes made by these organisms during their lifetimes involve changes to the information encoded in genetic material and are passed from generation to generation indefinitely.”
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice the opinion marker, “Lamarck proposed that ….” This sentence introduces Lamarck’s theory of evolution, which is an opinion on how evolution occurs.

Simplification: Lamarck’s theory is that evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.

S2: The contrast marker “however” signals that this sentence presents an important point, which is “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted.”

Note that the tone clue “seemed,” signals some uncertainty about whether Lamarck’s theory had actually been refuted and foreshadows that there may be further debate or insights on this topic.

Simplification: The discovery of genetics seemed to disprove Lamarck’s theory.

S3: Follows up the important point in S2 by presenting reasons why “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted.”

Notice the opinion marker “biologists decided.” So, this sentence presents biologists’ opinions.

The biologists had two opinions:

“all physical traits are passed from parents to offspring by means of information encoded in genetic material”

“such information is unaffected by an organism’s development of traits during its lifetime”

Notice the extreme marker “all” in “ALL physical traits are passed from parents to offspring” by means of genetic information that doesn’t change during an organism’s lifetime. In other words, the meaning is that NO physical traits are passed from parents to offspring in other ways, such as the way that Lamarck theorizes: “organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.”

Simplification: Biologists arrived at two conclusions about the passing of traits from parents to offspring that conflicted with Lamarck’s theory.

S4: Notice the premise and conclusion marker “SINCE premise, conclusion.”

We can see that premise part of S4 directly counters Lamarck’s theory:

S1: “Lamarck proposed that evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring”

S4: “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring”

Also notice that the conclusion part of S4 has a meaning similar to the point of S2:

S2: “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted

S4: “they had made it appear that evolution as Lamarck had described it is impossible”

Simplification: Since biologists believed that traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could NOT be passed on to its offspring, it seemed that Lamarck’s theory of evolution is impossible.

S5: Introduces a recent discovery, “modification of gene expression,” that counters the biologists’ premise in S4.

Notice the contrast marker “However.”

It’s also helpful to notice the look above marker, “SUCH a mechanism”; we must understand what is above that “such a mechanism” refers to. We see that S4 says, “Since … biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.”

So, the contrast presented by S5 is between the view of “biologists” in the past and a recent discovery about the existence of this “mechanism.” The biologists’ view (in the timeframe mentioned in S2, the “late 19th and early 20th centuries”) was that this mechanism doesn’t exist.

(Note: The technical term “modification of gene expression” is introduced here. This term and “gene expression modifications” appear throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. The passage provides context and details about what this means so we don’t have to know on our own.)

S6: Provides details about the discovery introduced in S5

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure. This structure (“found not only that X but also that Y”) signifies that there are two findings:

“an organism can modify its gene expression to develop particular physical traits during its lifetime”

“gene expression modifications made during an organism’s lifetime and the traits associated with those modifications CAN be passed on from the organism to its offspring.

What’s the key difference between this discovery and the biologists’ belief in S4? Whereas S4 says, “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring,” this discovery is that new traits developed during an organism’s lifetime CAN be passed on to offspring. We know from the 1st paragraph that passing on traits is an important element of “evolution.”

S7: The author poses a rhetorical question about the implications of the discovery presented in S5 and S6.

Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Simplification: The author is asking whether the discovery presented in S5 and S6 at least partly counters the biologists’ refutation of Lamarck’s evolutionary theory by proving Lamarck’s theory at least somewhat valid.

S8: Answers the rhetorical question in S7, with the tone of “not really.”

Notice the contrast structure, “X, BUT Y.” This structure puts emphasis on the Y part. In this case, we can see the contrast between “some people contend that it has” and “most evolutionary biologists point out that ….”

In the Y part, notice the qualifier marker “only a few.” The biologists point out a key fact about the discovery that “the traits associated with those modifications can be passed on.” That fact is that the “traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for ONLY a few generations and eventually disappear.”

Simplification: Some people argue that this discovery supports Lamarck’s theory, but most evolutionary biologists argue that Lamarck’s theory is still not valid, because the traits are passed on for only a few generations and eventually disappear.

S9: The author answers her rhetorical question, S7, by concluding that the discovery introduced in S5 does not support Lamarck’s theory of evolution.

Notice the conclusion marker, “So,” which is similar to “therefore.”

Notice also that S9 is a direct conclusion of the author’s, not the opinion of another person or group.

Notice the look above marker, “THIS type of passing of traits”; we must understand what is above that “this type of passing of traits” refers to. We see that S8 says, “such gene expression modifications and their associated traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for only a few generations and eventually disappear.”

Simplification: The “type of passing of traits” discussed in the 2nd paragraph is not evolution.

S10: Introduces another discovery, which supports Lamarck’s theory.

Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.”

Notice the contrast marker “nevertheless,” which conveys, “while the statements above are true, the following is also true.”

The contrast is that, whereas S8 and S9 go against Lamarck’s theory, S10 supports Lamarck’s theory: “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge.”

Simplification: Nevertheless, one discovery does support Lamarck’s theory: single-celled organisms that can change their genes and traits during their lifetimes.

S11: Describes a difference between these genetic changes and the “gene expression modifications” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker structure, “Unlike X, Y.” S11 is saying that “these changes made by these organisms” are “unlike” the gene expression modifications discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Simplification: Unlike the changes described in the 2nd paragraph, the changes made by these organisms during their lifetimes are passed from generation to generation indefinitely (for an unlimited period of time).

S12: The author concludes that the discovery discussed in S10 and S11 does represent evolution.

Notice the conclusion marker “Thus,” which is similar to “therefore.”

Notice also that S12 is a direct conclusion of the author, not the opinion of another group.

Simplification: The changes in traits made by these organisms represent evolution.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the organization of the passage?

answers.text: A critique of a scholarly work is first presented and then refuted.
A claim is made, and then evidence in support of that claim is presented.
A view is presented, an alternative view is introduced, and then reasons why one is more valid than the other are offered.
A seemingly paradoxical outcome is presented and then explained.
A historical work is criticized, and then some positive aspects of that work are listed.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
To answer this Structure question, we can consider important points and the purposes of sentences and paragraphs in the passage.

Then, we must carefully compare each noun, verb, and modifier in the answer choices with what the passage says so that we don’t choose a choice such that part of the choice matches the passage and part doesn’t.

We saw in our Passage Analysis that the passage begins by presenting a somewhat paradoxical situation in which Thomas Carlyle’s bookOn Heroes“has many critics” but “is still widely read and discussed” (1stparagraph), and then the author states her conclusion about what the “most likely” explanation for that situation is (2ndparagraph).
The correct answer will accurately capture each piece of this structure.
(A) A critique of a scholarly work is first presented and then refuted.
Trap: Half-Right
This choice starts off somewhat correctly, because the opinions of the “many critics” presented in the 1st paragraph could be considered a “critique” of Carlyle’s book.

However, this choice goes wrong partway through, because the 2ndparagraph does not “refute,” i.e., “dispute the validity of,” the criticism presented in the 1stparagraph.Rather the 2ndparagraph presents explanations for the fact that, despite what critics say, Carlyle’s book is popular.
(B) A claim is made, and then evidence in support of that claim is presented.
Trap: Distortion
This choice resembles the organization of the passage but doesn’t quite get it right.
The 1stparagraph doesn’t really present a “claim,” i.e., a conclusion.Rather, it states as fact that Carlyle’s book “is still widely read and discussed.”
Also, the 2ndparagraph doesn’t present evidence to support a claim.Rather, it presents explanations for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed.
(C) A view is presented, an alternative view is introduced, and then reasons why one is more valid than the other are offered.
Trap: Half-Right
This choice starts off somewhat correctly, because in a sense the 1stparagraph does present a view, that of Carlyle, and an alternative view, that of Buckle.

However, notice that the 1stparagraph does not really present Carlyle’s view.Rather, it describes Carlyle as “carried away in a virtual worship of certain “heroic” individuals.” So, the fact that the passage does not really present Carlyle’s view is a sign that this choice is not the correct answer.

Meanwhile, this choice goes clearly wrong partway through, because the 2ndparagraph does not offer reasons why Carlyle’sviewis morevalidthan Buckle’s.Rather the 2ndparagraph offers reasons why Carlyle’sbook“is stillwidely read and discussed.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) A seemingly paradoxical outcome is presented and then explained.

As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the 1stparagraph presents a somewhat paradoxical situation, and then, in the 2ndparagraph, the author states her conclusion about what the “most likely” explanation for that situation is.
So, we can see that this choice accurately describes the organization of the passage.
(E) A historical work is criticized, and then some positive aspects of that work are listed.
Trap: Wrong Verb
Notice that Carlyle’s work is not “criticized” by the author.Rather, the authordiscussesother people’s criticism of Carlyle’s work.
Trap: Distortion

Also, while the idea that “Carlyle captures something essential” could be considered a positive aspect of Carlyle’s work, the purpose of the 2ndparagraph is not to list “some positive aspects of that work.” Rather, the 2ndparagraph presents explanations for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author introduces Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History, along with the fact that the book “has many critics.”

Also, in S1, we see the opinion marker “who contend.” “Who” refers to the “many critics” of Thomas Carlyle’s book, and S1 goes on to present two of these critics’ opinions: “that Carlyle overemphasizes the role of the individual in shaping historical events and that his doing so results from his carelessness of thought and willingness to arrive at conclusions based on scant evidence.”

Since opinions are usually closely connected to important points and the primary purpose of a passage, we should note these two opinions and the role they play in the passage.

Then S2 and S3 expand on what S1 says by discussing two ways in which some critics of Carlyle have contrasted Carlyle with his contemporary Henry Thomas Buckle.

It’s important to notice that, in contrasting Carlyle and Buckle, the critics are implying that Carlyle is not as good as Buckle, both in terms of Carlyle’s and Buckle’s views and in terms of how Carlyle and Buckle arrive at their views.

It’s also important to notice that, in S1, S2, and S3, the author is descriptive; the author simply presents facts. In other words, the criticism of Carlyle’s book and the comparisons presented are not the author’s own. Rather, they are opinions and comparisons made by the critics of Carlyle’s book.

The last sentence of the 1st paragraph, S4, begins with the contrast marker “yet,” which signals that the passage is likely expressing an important point with S4. We see that S4 points out an interesting contrast: whereas the first three sentences say that critics have negative opinions of Carlyle and his work and believe that he and his work compare unfavorably with Buckle and his work, S4 says that Buckle is “largely forgotten,” while Carlyle’s book, On Heroes, is “still widely read and discussed.”

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents a somewhat paradoxical situation: Carlyle’s book On Heroes has many critics who have negative opinions of Carlyle’s book and believe that he and his book compare unfavorably with Buckle and his work, yet Buckle is “largely forgotten,” whereas Carlyle’s book is “still widely read and discussed.”

The 2nd paragraph begins, in S5, by stating something that “it would be reasonable to believe,” which is “that Carlyle’s work has endured for so long simply because his accounts of the lives and accomplishments of ‘great’ individuals are engaging.” Notice that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long” in S5 must be referring to “nearly two centuries after the publication of On Heroes, the book is still widely read nearly and discussed” in S4. So, S5 is presenting a possible explanation for the fact that “nearly two centuries after the publication of On Heroes, the book is still widely read and discussed.”

Also, it’s important to notice that, by saying, “It would be reasonable to believe,” the author has shifted from simply presenting facts in the 1st paragraph to now presenting her own opinion about what would be “reasonable to believe.”

S6 presents another opinion of the author’s, which is that an “alternative possibility,” in other words, another possible explanation for the fact that On Heroes “is still widely read and discussed,” is that “many whose worldviews resemble that of Carlyle find comfort in reading his work.”

Then, S7 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals that S7 likely states an important point of the passage. Indeed, S7 does present an important point, which is that neither of the explanations presented by S5 and S6 can fully explain “why individuals from a wide spectrum of cultures, lifestyles, occupations, and schools of thought read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.” We can see that S7 is setting up the passage to say what the author believes can fully explain why Carlyle’s book is so widely read, cited, and debated.

Indeed, S8 begins with the conclusion marker “thus” and presents the author’s final conclusion, which is that the reason why Carlyle’s book is still “widely read and discussed,” is “mostly likely” that Carlyle’s book “holds a certain attraction because … Carlyle captures something essential.”

2nd paragraph summary: The 2nd paragraph presents two possible explanations for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed nearly two centuries after it was published, explains why those two explanations cannot fully explain why Carlyle’s book is still widely read, cited, and debated about, and closes with the author’s final conclusion regarding what explains why Carlyle’s book is still widely read, cited, and debated about.

Passage summary: The passage begins by presenting a somewhat paradoxical situation in which Carlyle’s book “has many critics” yet is “still widely read and discussed,” presents two possible explanations for that situation, and concludes by stating the author’s own conclusion about what explains that situation, which is that “what seems most likely is that” Carlyle’s book “holds a certain attraction because … Carlyle captures something essential.”
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: This sentence introduces Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History, along with the fact that the book “has many critics.”

It also expands on the fact that the book has many critics by presenting what these critics “contend” about the book.

The opinion marker “contend” signals that this sentence is closely connected to an important point of the passage, since opinions that appear in passages are generally connected to important points.

Simplification: Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes has many critics, and the opinion of these critics is that Carlyle overemphasizes the role of the individual in historical events because his work is careless.

S2: This sentence expands on what S1 says about the opinion of critics of Carlyle by describing a contrast between Carlyle and Henry Thomas Buckle that some of the critics of Carlyle’s book have pointed out.

Simplification: Some critics of Carlyle have pointed out a contrast between how Carlyle virtually worships certain “heroic” individuals and the fact that Buckle wrote, “individuals count for nothing.”

S3: This sentence also expands on what S1 says about the opinion of critics of Carlyle’s book by presenting a contrast that the critics “have noted,” which is that Carlyle “relies on strings of barely substantiated hypotheses or even hunches,” whereas Buckle “based his ideas on careful scientific analyses.”

Notice that S3 is basically expanding on what S1 says about Carlyle’s “carelessness of thought and willingness to arrive at conclusions based on scant evidence.”

Simplification: Carlyle relies on guesses, whereas Buckle based his ideas on scientific analyses.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “Yet.” This sentence presents an idea that contrasts with everything that has been said so far by the passage. Whereas the first three sentences are about criticism of Carlyle’s book and how some critics contrast Carlyle in a negative way with Buckle, this sentence presents an outcome that is a bit surprising given what has been said so far: Buckle is now “largely forgotten,” while Carlyle’s book is still “widely read and discussed.

Simplification: Despite everything critics say about Carlyle and Buckle, Buckle is forgotten, while Carlyle’s book is still widely read.

S5: This sentence uses the causation marker “because” to present a possible explanation for the fact that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long.”

Notice that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long,” in S5 refers to “nearly two centuries after the publication of On Heroes, the book is still widely read and discussed,” in S4.

Simplification: One reasonable explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed is that it is engaging to both serious and casual students of history.

S6: By beginning with “an alternative possibility,” this sentence signals that, having presented in S5 one reasonable explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed, the passage is now presenting an alternative explanation.

Simplification: An alternative possible explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed is that some people find it engaging.

S7: The contrast marker “however” signals that this sentence presents a contrast and that it probably expresses an important point.

Then S7 uses a “while X, Y,” structure. In a “while X, Y,” structure, the “while X part makes a concession, and the Y part states the main point of the “while X, Y” structure. In this case, the main point stated by the Y part of the “while X, Y,” structure is that “neither” of the explanations presented in S5 and S6 can fully explain why “individuals from a wide spectrum of cultures, lifestyles, occupations, and schools of thought read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.”

Simplification: The explanations presented in S5 and S6 cannot fully explain why Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed.

S8: Notice the conclusion marker, “thus.” This sentence states the author’s final conclusion about the seemingly paradoxical situation presented by the 1st paragraph.

In addition to the fact that conclusions are important points of a passage, another clue indicating that this sentence states an important point is the strong language “what seem most likely is that.” We can see that the author considers this explanation the best one.

Simplification: The most likely explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book “is still widely read and discussed” is that “Carlyle captures something essential.”

question.text: Which of the following best describes the relationship of the second paragraph to the first?

answers.text: The second paragraph presents multiple considerations related to a stretching approach presented in the first paragraph.
The second paragraph offers evidence that serves to counter a recommendation presented in the first paragraph.
The second paragraph provides some examples of applications of a stretching method mentioned in the first paragraph.
The second paragraph discusses problems that serve to undermine the case for using a stretching approach described in the first paragraph.
The second paragraph presents an opinion in disagreement with the opinions of experts mentioned in the first paragraph.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

The wording of the stem, “relationship of the second paragraph to the first,” tells us that this is a Structure question.We approach Structure questions similarly to how we approach Primary Purpose questions: our goal is to understand main points, structure, purpose, and tone.However, one difference here is that we will put more emphasis on the paragraphs.We must understand the purpose of the second paragraph, and how it connects back to the first paragraph.

In S6, at the beginning of the 2ndparagraph, notice the contrast marker, “although,” and opinion marker, “it is necessary to.” These markers signal an important point.Also, since “THESE X” is a look above marker, the presence of “these experts” signals that we must look above to understand what “these experts” refers to.We see that “these experts” refers to the experts in S3.

These markers help us understand the author’s point in S6: the author acknowledges that the experts above make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.” Thus, S6, the opening sentence of the 2ndparagraph, indicates that details about “some potential caveats” will follow.Three caveats related to the PNF approach are then discussed.So, the purpose of the 2ndparagraph is to discuss some caveats related to the PNF approach.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The second paragraph presents multiple considerations related to a stretching approach presented in the first paragraph.

You may quickly get the sense that this answer choice matches well with what we said in the introduction to this explanation about the relationship between the two paragraphs, but it is a good habit to take the time to confirm that the exact wording is correct, so that we don’t fall into a trap.
Indeed, each word or phrase in this choice matches the author’s main point in S6:

Wording in Answer ChoiceWording in Passagepresentsit is necessary to discussmultipleSomeconsiderationscaveatsrelated toassociated witha stretching approach presented in the first paragraphthe PNF method
(B) The second paragraph offers evidence that serves to counter a recommendation presented in the first paragraph.
Trap: Distortion / Wrong Verb / Half-Right
First, notice that “counter” is a negative opinion marker that indicates disagreement.
In our Question Analysis, we saw that the author does not disagree with the experts, but only makes the qualifier statement “it is necessary to discuss some caveats.”

If you picked this choice, you may have thought that the contrast marker “although” was signaling an opposite opinion and that the author disagrees with the experts.Remember, however, we have to go beyond just noticing markers and read to understand what the passage says.
(C) The second paragraph provides some examples of applications of a stretching method mentioned in the first paragraph.
Trap: Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the second paragraph does discuss “a stretching method mentioned in the first paragraph.” This “method” is the PNF approach.
However, “provides some examples of applications” (uses of something) does not match the passage.The second paragraph discusses three “caveats” (warnings, conditions, considerations), not three uses.
(D) The second paragraph discusses problems that serve to undermine the case for using a stretching approach described in the first paragraph.
Trap: Half-Right / Distortion
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the second paragraph does discuss “a stretching method mentioned in the first paragraph.” This “method” is the PNF approach.
However, we must consider the entire choice, to avoid a trap.
Notice that “undermine the case” is a negative opinion marker that signals disagreement.“Undermine the case” misrepresents what the passage says.

In the introduction to this explanation, we saw that the author does not “undermine the case of” or disagree with the experts, but only makes the qualifier statement “it is necessary to discuss some caveats.”

If you picked this choice, you may have thought that “although” was signaling an opposite opinion and that the author disagrees with the experts, but by reading the second paragraph carefully we can see that the author merely adds information about the PNF approach without disagreeing with the experts.

Also, “problems” is a bit of a distortion.The caveats discussed are “considerations” or “recommended adjustments” to ways that athletes often use the PNF approach, but the author does not present “problems” with the PNF approach itself.
(E) The second paragraph presents an opinion in disagreement with the opinions of experts mentioned in the first paragraph.
Trap: Distortion / Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the second paragraph does discuss “the experts mentioned in the first paragraph,” in S6.
However, we must check the rest of this choice, to avoid a trap.

Notice that “disagreement” is a negative opinion marker that is a distortion of what the 2ndparagraph says.In the introduction to this explanation, we saw that the author does notdisagreewith the experts, but only makes the qualifying statement “it is necessary to discuss some caveats.”

If you picked this choice, you may have thought that “although” was signaling an opposite opinion and that the author disagrees with the experts, but by reading the second paragraph carefully we can see that the author merely adds information about the PNF approach without disagreeing with the experts.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It is helpful to notice that the first two sentences of the 1st paragraph follow the common myth structure “Many people believe X. HOWEVER, Y.” The wording in S1, “prevalent perception,” has the same meaning as “many people believe.” Then, S2 begins with the contrast marker, “however,” which signals that an idea in contrast with the “prevalent perception” is being presented, the opinion of “leading researchers and … coaches.”

“HOWEVER” is often followed by an important point, and in this case, the two statements that follow “however,” which are “leading researchers and strength and conditioning coaches … call into question multiple aspects of this method …” and “These experts make the case …,” present important points.

Now, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity,” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers” and “coaches,” in S2. What exactly do they disagree about?

In S1, “amateur athletes” believe that the “ideal stretching protocol … involves Static Stretching … prior to vigorous activity.” S2 then says, “HOWEVER, researchers … call into question multiple aspects of this method.”

It’s important to notice that the researchers call into question the stretching method, not the author of the passage. The author is describing the view of the experts, and doesn’t convey a strong opinion of her own. In S3, the author simply says, “These experts make the case.”

Then, after S2 and S3 describe the experts’ “case” about “superior alternative stretching approaches,” S4 and S5 provide details related to the “case” by describing two “superior alternative stretching approaches.”

1st paragraph summary: The author describes a common perception of a stretching method, presents a contrasting opinion held by experts, and then provides some details related to the experts’ opinion.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S6, the author acknowledges that the experts mentioned in the 1st paragraph make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S6 foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow. Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, considerations.” Three caveats are then specified:

- “it is most effective to perform most PNF stretches, aside from spine and neck stretches, only twice per week”

- “athletes frequently counteract their progress by contracting the targeted muscle with excessive force”

- “an aggressive approach can jeopardize the safety of a stretch if the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly

2nd paragraph summary: The author says that there is a body of research that supports the view of the experts and then warns that there are certain things to be aware of regarding the PNF approach described above and presents three of those things.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear throughout the passage?

The theme of the experts’ “case” or “view” appears repeatedly in the passage.

While the first sentence is about “a prevalent perception,” most of the 1st paragraph is about the experts’ case and details related to that case.

Then, the 2nd paragraph presents some caveats. At the same time, notice that the caveats are presented in the context of the statement “there is a convincing body of research to substantiate the view of these experts.” So, even the 2nd paragraph is, in a way, further discussion of the “view” of the experts.

Passage summary: The passage begins by mentioning “a prevalent perception” and then continues by presenting a contrasting “case” or “view” and discussing some details and caveats related to that contrasting “case” or “view.”

In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

We don’t need to understand the technical vocabulary: isometric, inverse myotatic reflex, Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation. We can correctly answer every question without knowing what any of these things are.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: First opinion presented: amateur athletes’ opinion.

Let’s translate this sentence’s wording into simpler similar words:

Prevalent perception = common opinion

Ideal stretching protocol = best stretching method

Prior to vigorous activity = before activity

Simplification: Amateur athletes commonly believe that the best stretching method for achieve three goals (performance, range of motion, preventing injury) is Static Stretching before activity.

S2: Contrast with S1: Experts disagree.

Notice the contrast marker“however” and the opinion marker “call into question.” “However” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, it signals that a contrasting opinion is being presented.

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers and coaches,” in S2.

Simplification: Experts disagree with multiple aspects of the amateur athletes’ stretching method described above.

S3: The author outlines the experts’ disagreement.

The look above marker “this sequence of activities” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity”

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure that’s common in SC. This structure signifies that there are 2 reasons why the experts disagree.

Simplification: The experts make a strong case that:

1 - Static Stretching before a workout is inferior to doing it after a workout

2 - There are superior alternative approaches, such as PNF and DS. (Notice that this statement implies that the Static Stretching approach is inferior.)

S4: Details the PNF approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Naturally, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of PNF and why PNF is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “calming the muscle to prevent injury and allowing the athlete to stretch further”

S5: Details the DS approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Just like S4, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of DS and why DS is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “reducing the likelihood that the athlete will suffer an injury”

S6: The author acknowledges that the experts above make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

This sentence foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow.

Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, stipulations, considerations.”

There is also a tricky but important nuance to notice here: the contrast marker “although” at the start of the sentence can be used for different kinds and degrees of contrast. Here, it is not being used to signal direct opposites; rather, the sentence is acting as more of a “qualifier” (making a statement less absolute).

Example of opposite: Although the experts like this method, it is terrible.

Example of qualifier: Although there is evidence to support this method, it’s important to take certain precautions.

S7: Details about the first caveat

Notice that “while” is a contrast marker. So, we must check: what are the two things that are in contrast? The frequency of stretching is being contrasted: “every day” vs. “twice per week.”

Simplification: Many athletes do the same stretches every day, BUT Laughlin believes that it can be more effective to do most of them only twice per week.

Note: this simplification captures the main idea of this sentence, but we may need to analyze it in more detail for a specific question.

For example, notice that the language “most” and “aside from spine and neck stretches” means that there are exceptions; the passage does not say “all.”

S8: Details about the second caveat

Notice that “moreover” is an additional point marker. This marker tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion, more than one sentence above. Here, that conclusion is in S6: “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S9: Details about the third caveat

Notice that “likewise” (which means “similarly” or “also”) is an additional point marker that tells us that this sentence will state a point similar to the one stated by the previous sentence.

This sentence presents an additional point in support of the conclusion in S6.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the relationship of the second paragraph to the first?

answers.text: The second paragraph explains in detail an observed phenomenon introduced in the first paragraph.
The second paragraph offers experimental evidence indicating that an assertion mentioned in the first paragraph is unquestionable.
The second paragraph presents a convincing challenge of the validity of evidence discussed in the first paragraph.
The second paragraph indicates that the significance of a development mentioned in the first paragraph has been misjudged.
The second paragraph presents examples of a type of process mentioned in the first paragraph.

explanation_parts.text: To correctly answer this Structure question about the relationship between the two paragraphs of the passage, we can consider what each paragraph of the passage accomplishes.In other words, we can consider the purpose of each paragraph.
So, let’s review our paragraph and passage summaries from our Passage Analysis.

1stparagraph summary: The 1stparagraph presents the common belief that Neanderthals were “less cognitively advanced” than other ancestors of modern humans, introduces “discoveries” that indicate that that common belief is inaccurate, presents an example of one those discoveries, which is “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch,” and presents many archeologists’ conclusion based on that evidence, “Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch.”

2ndparagraph summary: In contrast to what the “many archeologists” mentioned in the 1stparagraph have concluded, which is that “evidence that Neanderthals produced pitch” indicates that “Neanderthals must have been … cognitively advanced,” experiments show that Neanderthals could have developed a method of pitch production accidentally.

Passage summary: The passage introduces the fact that recently discovered evidence indicates that Neanderthals used pitch for making tools, presents many archeologists’ conclusion based on that fact, and then presents experimental results that challenge the archeologists’ conclusion.
To find the correct answer, we’ll compare each part of each choice with these summaries to eliminate the choices that don’t match the summaries and find the one that does.
(A) The second paragraph explains in detail an observed phenomenon introduced in the first paragraph.
Trap: Half-Right / Distortion

The 2ndparagraph does explain something in detail, but it doesn’t explain “an observed phenomenon introduced in the 1stparagraph.” Rather, the 2ndparagraph explains how experiments showed that Neanderthals may have accidentally discovered a method of producing pitch.

Also, notice that the passage does not even discuss an “observed phenomenon” in the 1stparagraph.A phenomenon is an occurrence, but the passage does not say that anyone “observed” an occurrence.Rather, it says that people found evidence.
(B) The second paragraph offers experimental evidence indicating that an assertion mentioned in the first paragraph is unquestionable.
Trap: Half-Right / Opposite

This choice is correct up until the last word, “unquestionable,” since the passage does offer experimental evidence that shows something about the archeologists’ assertion mentioned in the 1stparagraph.

However, the word “unquestionable” means “can’t be questioned,” but the 2ndparagraph does the opposite of show that the archeologists’ conclusion can’t be questioned.As S5 says, the experiments discussed in the 2ndparagraph “have generated evidence that serves tocall this conclusion into question.”
This choice is a great example of why we have to read each choice in its entirety.If we were to read all of this choice but the last word, we could easily decide that it’s correct.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The second paragraph presents a convincing challenge of the validity of evidence discussed in the first paragraph.
Trap: Distortion

Notice that the challenge presented by the 2ndparagraph is not a “challenge of the validity of evidence discussed in the 1stparagraph.” In other words, the 2ndparagraph does not indicate that the “evidenceindicating that Neanderthals produced pitch” is not valid.
Rather, the 2ndparagraph challenges the archeologists’conclusionbased on that evidence.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) The second paragraph indicates that the significance of a development mentioned in the first paragraph has been misjudged.

As we saw in our question analysis, the passage introduces the fact that recently discovered evidence indicates that Neanderthals produced pitch, presents the archeologists’ conclusion based on that fact, and then presents experimental results that challenge the archeologists’ conclusion.
We can see that “a development” in this choice matches the recent discovery of evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch.

Also, notice that the archeologists’ conclusion “in order to produce pitch, Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch,” implies that the archeologistsjudgedthat the “development,” in other words, the recent discovery of evidence, meant that their conclusion is correct.
So, by showing that the archeologists’ conclusion is not supported by that evidence, the experimental results indicate that the archeologists “misjudged” the significance of that evidence.
So, we can see that, as this choice says, the 2ndparagraph indicates that the archeologists “misjudged” the significance of the development mentioned in the 1stparagraph.
(E) The second paragraph presents examples of a type of process mentioned in the first paragraph.
Trap: Distortion

The first sign that this choice is incorrect is that the 2ndparagraph does not present multiple “examples” of processes of producing pitch.Rather, it presents only one example, the example of burning bark near a stone surface.

Also, this choice does not really capture the organization of the passage.If we consider what the 1stand 2ndparagraphs accomplish, we see that they don’t just mention the process of producing pitch and then present an example of that process.Rather, the 1stparagraph presents a conclusion about Neanderthals, and then the 2ndparagraph presents information that calls that conclusion into question.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting a common belief, the belief that Neanderthals were “less cognitively advanced than the other ancestors of modern humans who inhabited Eurasia along with the Neanderthals.”

Having presented that common belief, S1 continues by presenting a development related to that common belief: “recent archeological work has yielded multiple discoveries indicating that this perception of Neanderthals is inaccurate.” Since “THIS perception” is a look above marker, we can see that “this perception of Neanderthals” refers to the common belief mentioned earlier in the sentence. So, the passage is saying that recent discoveries indicate that the belief that Neanderthals were “less cognitively advanced” is inaccurate.

Then S2 presents an example of one of the recent “discoveries” mentioned in S1. That example is “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch for the purpose of making tools.”

S3 goes on to say why “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch for the purpose of making tools” is “particularly interesting.”

Then S4 pulls together everything else said by the 1st paragraph to say that, because of the evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch, many archeologists have concluded that Neanderthals were “sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch.”

So, it appears that the purpose of the 1st paragraph is to present the situation involving beliefs and evidence related to Neanderthals to lead up to the 1st paragraph’s most important point, which is the point about what the archeologists have concluded.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents the common belief that Neanderthals were “less cognitively advanced” than other ancestors of modern humans, introduces “discoveries” that indicate that that common belief is inaccurate, presents an example of one those discoveries, which is “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch,” and presents many archeologists’ conclusion based on that evidence, “Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch.”

Continuing to the 2nd paragraph, the contrast word “however” at the beginning of S5 indicates that now the passage is presenting information that contrasts with what the 1st paragraph says. “THIS conclusion is a look above marker. So, we can see that S5 is saying that “recent experiments” have served to call into question the archeologists’ conclusion discussed above.

S6 and S7 begin to describe the experiments mentioned in S5, and then, at the beginning of S8, we come to another “however,” which signals that S8 likely expresses an important point. In fact, S8 does express an important point, the point that, by simply burning birch bark “near a vertical stone surface,” the researchers were able to produce “a significant quantity of pitch.”

We now begin to see the point of these experiments discussed in the 2nd paragraph. Whereas the archeologists mentioned in the 1st paragraph concluded that Neanderthals “must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch,” the researchers produced pitch using a method that wasn’t sophisticated at all, simply burning bark near a stone.

Then, S9 and S10 describe how the researchers confirmed that the pitch produced via the method of burning bark near a stone is “suitable for the creation of tools.”

Finally, S11 uses the support marker “since” and the conclusion marker “it is clear that” to support and state a conclusion of the author’s. We can see that, in S11, the author is concluding that, given the results of the experiments and the fact that Neanderthals likely “commonly” burned birch bark near a vertical stone surface, Neanderthals may have figured out how to make pitch accidentally not, as the “many archeologists” concluded, by using “advanced cognition” to “develop a sophisticated method.”

2nd paragraph summary: In contrast to the conclusion of “many archeologists” mentioned in the 1st paragraph, which is that “evidence that Neanderthals produced pitch” indicates that “Neanderthals must have been … cognitively advanced,” experiments show that Neanderthals could have developed a method of pitch production accidentally.

Passage summary: The passage introduces the fact that recently discovered evidence indicates that Neanderthals used pitch for making tools, presents many archeologists’ conclusion based on that fact, and then presents experimental results that challenge the archeologists’ conclusion.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Wording in Passage

Similar Words

cognitively

cognitively advanced

Eurasia

related to study of material items from humanity’s past

perception

view, conclusion

pitch

a thick black substance, tar, glue

straight up and down

routine

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Opens the passage by using the contrast marker “while” to present a contrast between what has been “commonly believed” about Neanderthals and what recent discoveries indicate.

Simplification: People have commonly believed that Neanderthals were not as cognitively advanced (intelligent) as other human ancestors, but recent discoveries indicate that this common belief is not correct.

S2: Uses the look above marker “these discoveries” to introduce an example of one of the discoveries mentioned in S1, the discovery of “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch for the purpose of making tools.”

Simplification: One of the discoveries that indicate that the common belief mentioned in S1 is not correct is the discovery of evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch.

S3: Uses the look above marker “this discovery” to expand on S2 by saying why the discovery mentioned above in S2 is “particularly interesting.”

Notice that this sentence uses the strong language “particularly interesting” and the causation marker “because.” Strong language and causation markers generally appear in sentences that state important points. So, what this sentence says is likely important in the scheme of the passage.

Simplification: The evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch to make tools is particularly interesting because making pitch requires a high temperature, but pitch burns if exposed to fire.

S4: Uses the opinion marker “concluded” to present the conclusion that “many archeologists” have arrived at because of the “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch.”

Notice that, in presenting the conclusion of “many archeologists,” this sentence pulls together much of what the 1st paragraph says about Neanderthals and the recent discoveries. In other words, the 1st paragraph states some facts, and now, this sentence uses “therefore” to present a result of those facts, the result that “many archeologists have concluded that, in order to produce pitch, Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method.”

Simplification: Because of the evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch to make tools, archeologists have concluded that Neanderthals must have been smart enough to develop a sophisticated method of making pitch.

S5: Uses the contrast marker “however” to present a fact that contrasts with what the 1st paragraph says.

Notice that the look above marker “this” in “this conclusion” indicates that the conclusion mentioned in this sentence is the conclusion of the archeologists presented above in the 1st paragraph.

Simplification: In contrast with what the 1st paragraph says, experiments have generated evidence indicating that the conclusion of the archeologists may not be correct.

S6: Begins to describe the experiments mentioned in S5.

S7: Continues to describe the experiments mentioned in S5.

S8: Notice the contrast marker “however” at the beginning of this sentence. It signals that this sentence likely expresses an important point.

In fact, S8 does express an important point, the point that the researchers discovered a simple way to produce pitch, which was “birch bark was burned near a vertical stone surface.”

Simplification: The researchers discovered that, by burning birch bark near a stone, they produced pitch.

S9: Continues to describe the experiments by saying that the researchers used the pitch to make tools.

S10: Notice that the topic of this sentence, “a result indicating,” is likely important in the passage. After all, the results of an experiment discussed in the passage are likely closely connected to the main point and primary purpose of the passage.

Simplification: The experiments showed that pitch produced via the method of burning bark near a stone is good for making tools.

S11: Notice that this sentence uses the support marker “since” and the conclusion marker “it is clear that” to presents a premise, “Since Neanderthals’ routine use of fire likely commonly involved burning of birch bark near a vertical stone surface,” and a conclusion, “it is clear that development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally.”

In fact, in this sentence, the author states a conclusion that is based on almost everything said above in the 2nd paragraph.

Simplification: Since it’s likely that Neanderthals commonly burned birch bark near stones in their normal use of fire, they may have discovered the method of producing pitch by burning birch bark by near a stone by accident, rather than come up with a sophisticated method of producing pitch by using advanced thinking.

question.text: Which of the following statements best describes the function of the last sentence of the passage?

answers.text: It provides evidence to support the view of the defenders of the government policy.
It confirms that there were not any positive effects of the increase in TAs.
It elaborates on an assertion concerning why an educational result came about.
It provides an example of the kinds of challenges involved with certain students.
It compares the methods used by two different categories of educational staff.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

Since the question asks about the last sentence (S6), this sentence must connect to something above, not below it.We know from S3 that the main purpose of the 2ndparagraph is to discuss “the most plausible reason for these results.” Two explanations are then discussed, the defenders’ explanation, and the author’s own explanation.
Since the “function of” a given sentence will often involve the sentence directly above, a helpful place to start is to ask ourselves, “What is the relationship between S5 and S6?”

S5 introduces “a more convincing explanation,” and S6 adds more details to this explanation.S5 says that “teachers may have often used their TAs as convenient supervisors for the unruliest students, rather than attend to these students themselves.” S6 expands on this explanation by adding that “these difficult studentshad been better served by experienced teachers.”

Also, in S6, notice the look above marker, “thesedifficult students,” which refers to the “unruliest students” mentioned in S5.Since “these” refers to the same students mentioned in the sentence above, this look above marker is a clue that these two sentences are closely connected.
(A) It provides evidence to support the view of the defenders of the government policy.
Trap: Mix-Up
Notice the phrase “support the view of the DEFENDERS” in this answer choice.When there are multiple opinions involved, we must be extra careful to avoid a Mix-Up trap.
The last sentence of the passage adds details to the author’s opinion about “a more convincing explanation” in S5 and doesn’t support the view of the defenders in S4.
(B) It confirms that there were not any positive effects of the increase in TAs.
Trap:Stretch / Distortion
Notice the extreme marker, “any,” which is a yellow flag.

S2 says “the results were underwhelming,” and S5 and S6 provide a possible explanation why, but the author doesn’t confirm that there weren’t ANY positive effects that weren’t mentioned in the passage.We must be careful not to choose a choice that goes beyond what the passage says; always find proof in the passage.
Also, in the last sentence, the author’s point is not about theeffectsthemselves, but rather about thecauseof the disappointing results described in paragraph one.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It elaborates on an assertion concerning why an educational result came about.

In our question analysis, we saw that the function of the highlighted sentence is to expand on the author’s explanation introduced in the prior sentence, S5.We also know, from S3, that the main purpose of paragraph two is to discuss “the most plausible reason for these results.”

You may quickly get the sense that this answer choice matches well with our Analysis, but it is a good habit to take the time to confirm that the exact wording is correct, so that we don’t fall into a trap:
“Elaborates on” means “expands on, continues, adds details to,” so this wording is correct.
Next, we must confirm that “an assertion concerning why a certain result came about” is correct:
“Assertion” matches with “a more convincingexplanation” (S4)
“whya certain result came about” matches with “reasonfor these results” (S3)
Now that we’ve confirmed that the wording is correct, we can safely choose this answer.
(D) It provides an example of the kinds of challenges involved with certain students.
Trap:Distortion / Half Right
If you chose this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “challenges involved with certain students” are mentioned in S6.
However, does the highlighted sentence provide an “exampleof thekindsof challenges involved”?

The word “example” means “a specific case/instance that is mentioned to illustrate a larger point.” In S6, nospecificexample of thekindsof challenges is specified.Rather, S6 makes ageneralstatement about challenges involved.Therefore, this choice is incorrect.

Also, it is helpful to be aware that the correct answer to a Specific Purpose question will usually show the connection between the highlighted text and other language nearby in the passage, as opposed to just describing the content of the highlighted text.
(E) It compares the methods used by two different categories of educational staff.
Trap: Distortion / Half Right

While it’s true that the experience/training of teachers and TAs is compared in S6, their “methods used” are neither specified nor compared.A “method” means a “specific procedure for accomplishing something,” but S6 does not specify any procedures.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author begins by describing a change in a government policy and public education spending: “Britain had a reputation for spending frugally on public education, lagging behind most other developed countries, BUT the country’s policy was transformed under Labour government leadership.” Notice the contrast marker “but.” “But” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, the fact that “the country’s policy was transformed” is an important point.

How was the country’s policy transformed? Notice that, later in S1, details about the change are specified: “the number of teaching assistants (TAs) … surged from 60,600 to 176,000.”

The first paragraph continues by discussing the underwhelming, i.e., disappointing, results of the policy change: S2 says, “Initially optimistic about the effects of this change, proud government ministers boasted about the increase, BUT despite the substantial investment involved, the results were underwhelming.” Here again, we must pay close attention to what follows the contrast marker, “but.” The key point to see is that the results of the policy change were underwhelming.

Notice that, so far, the author is completely descriptive and is presenting historical facts; he or she does make any arguments of his or her own. In other words, he or she has not yet taken a strong position.

1st paragraph summary: The author discusses a change in Britain’s public education policy that involved an increase in the number of teaching assistants in Britain and discusses the disappointing results of the change.

Notice that the 2nd paragraph opens with a rhetorical question, S3, “What is the most plausible reason for these results?” Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question uses the causation marker “reason,” indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then mentioned. We must pay close attention whenever an author presents an explanation for a phenomenon, because explanations tend to be important parts of the passages.

In S4, the author notes a flaw in the explanation of the “defenders,” and then, in S5, the author proceeds with his or her own explanation, “a more convincing explanation is that ….” We want to pay close attention whenever an author uses strong language, and “a more convincing explanation is that” certainly qualifies as strong language. “More” is a comparison marker, and “Convincing” means “believable,” so when the author says “a more convincing” explanation, the author is making the strong statement that he or she believes that this second explanation is better than the defenders’ explanation.

2nd paragraph summary: The author discusses two possible explanations for the results described in the first paragraph, and the author favors one of these explanations over the other.

For understanding the structure of the entire passage, it’s helpful to ask ourselves, “What is the relationship between the paragraphs?” Given that, in the first paragraph, the author has presented results that were disappointing and unexpected, it makes sense that the author would be interested in explaining these results, and explaining “these results” is exactly what the author does in the second paragraph.

For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.
