analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: This sentence begins by providing some background information: For many years, Britain had a reputation for spending frugally on public education, lagging behind most other developed countries.”

Then, the contrast marker “but” signals a main point: “BUT the country’s policy was transformed.” As always, when we see a contrast marker, we must pay extra close attention and notice what two things are in contrast.

Simplification: Britain was frugal in public education spending but increased spending and the number of TAs significantly between 1997 and 2009.

S2: Contrast between expectations and results:

“Initially optimistic … ministers boasted” … “BUT…the results were underwhelming.” (Note: if we didn’t know the word “underwhelming,” we could guess that it means “disappointing” because of the context and contrast.)

Also, since “this X” is a look above marker, when we see “this change,” we must understand what is above that “this change” refers to. We see that “this change” refers to “the number of teaching assistants (TAs) … surged from 60,600 to 176,000.”

After the “:,” details are provided about how the results were underwhelming: “a consistent negative relationship between the amount of support a student received from TAs and the progress the student made.”

Simplification: Government ministers thought more TAs would help, but the results were disappointing.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Also, S3 includes the causation marker “reason.” This marker signals that possible explanations will follow.

Finally, notice the look above marker “these results.” “These results” refers to the “underwhelming results” described in the first paragraph.

Simplification: What is the most likely cause of the disappointing results described above?

S4: The contrast marker “while” in “While some defenders … argue” signals that the author is noting a flaw in the argument of the “defenders.”

“Successfully accounted for this variable” means “successfully adjusted so that the results were not biased.”

“Argue that” is an opinion marker, so we must make sure not to mix up different opinions.

Simplification: Policy defenders argue that the results were biased, but the defenders are wrong because they failed to take something into account.

S5: As we might expect, the author follows up by presenting a “more convincing explanation,” after noting the flaw of the defenders’ argument in S4.

Simplification: A better explanation is how the TAs were used.

S6: The author elaborates on the explanation in S5.

Simplification: Before more TAs were hired, the most difficult students had been better served by experienced teachers.

question.text: The author of the passage mentions Annie Leonard’s citation most probably in order to

answers.text: demonstrate that the 1950s was one of the worst periods for exploitative business practices
indicate why consumers should be wary in their choice of products
make note of the effectiveness of a certain business strategy
expand on an argument that pro-business advocates attempt to justify indefensible business strategies
provide an example to support an assessment of a criticism of a business practice

explanation_parts.text: (A) demonstrate that the 1950s was one of the worst periods for exploitative business practices
Trap: Stretch / Distortion

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that some parts of the wording, “demonstrate … exploitative business practices,” correctly match our Question Analysis.“Demonstrate” is an appropriate verb for providing an example of exploitative business practices.

However, is the author arguing that the 1950s, specifically, was one of the “WORST periods”? Notice that “worst” is an extreme marker, which is a yellow flag.“Worst” conveys that the 1950s were worse than all other periods.
Although the author does suggest that the example cited by Annie Leonard is particularly hard to justify, the author does not make the extreme statement that it is the “worst.”
(B) indicate why consumers should be wary in their choice of products
Trap: Matches but Incorrect
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that Annie Leonard’s citation may in fact “indicate why consumers should be wary in their choice of products.”

However, the correct answer to a Specific Purpose question will not simply describe the part of the passage that the question is about.The correct answer will describe the purpose that part of the passage serves in the passage.

So, notice that the author does not mention Annie Leonard’s citation for the purpose of indicating “why consumers should be wary in their choice of products.” After all, the author is not making an argument about what consumers “should” do.
(C) make note of the effectiveness of a certain business strategy
Trap: Distortion

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that some parts of the wording, “make note of” and “a certain business strategy,” seem consistent with our Question Analysis.“Make note of” is an appropriate verb for providing an example of exploitative business practices.
However, is the author focused on the “effectiveness” of the business strategy of planned obsolescence? Note that the word “effective” means “successful in producing a desired result.”

In S2, the author agrees with the consumer protection advocates that the business strategy of planned obsolescence can be “exploitative of consumers.” Notice that the wording “exploitative” in S1 and “hard-pressed to justify” express ideas about thefairness/moralityof the strategy, not itseffectiveness.The author doesn’t say anything about the strategy’seffectiveness.
(D) expand on an argument that pro-business advocates attempt to justify indefensible business strategies

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that some parts of the wording, “expand on” and “indefensible,” seem consistent with our Question Analysis; “expand on” is an appropriate verb that matches with the additional point marker in S3, “additionally,” and “indefensible” matches with “hard-pressed to justify.”
However, does the author make the argument that “pro-business advocates attempt to justify indefensible business strategies”?
No.It is important to notice the wording of S2, “there are certain cases that even the most pro-business advocateswouldbe hard-pressed to justify.”

Notice that the author does not argue that “pro-business advocates attempt to justify indefensible business strategies.” Rather the author makes the point that there are certain cases that pro-business advocates WOULD be hard-pressed to justify IF they were to try to justify them.
Furthermore, the author does not even make an argument about “pro-business advocates”; in S2 and S3, the author is making a point about “certain cases” of planned obsolescence.
YOUR CHOICE(E) provide an example to support an assessment of a criticism of a business practice

From our Question Analysis, we may quickly get the sense that this choice fits the specific purpose that Annie Leonard’s citation serves in the passage, but it’s a good habit to confirm that the exact wording of the choice is correct.

As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the author mentions Annie Leonard’s citation to provide an example to support the opinion, “Indeed, there are certain cases that even the most pro-business advocates would be hard-pressed to justify.” So, the first part of this choice, “provide an example to support,” is correct.
Now, we have just to confirm that “an assessment of a criticism of a business practice” is also correct.

Notice that, S1 presents “consumer protection advocates” criticism of planned obsolescence as “exploitative of consumers.” Then, in S2, “Indeed, there are certain cases that even the most pro-business advocates would be hard-pressed to justify,” is essentially the author’s “assessment” that, “indeed,” the consumer protection advocates’ “criticism” of planned obsolescence as exploitative of consumers is correct in “certain cases.” Thus, the second part of this choice matches the passage as well.
So, “provide an example to support an assessment of a criticism of a business practice” accurately describes the purpose Annie Leonard’s citation serves in the passage.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, notice the opinion marker, “conviction that X.” “Conviction” means “a strong belief.” Whenever we notice an opinion, we must make sure we understand both the “who” and the “what” — whose opinion is being presented and what that opinion involves.

Here, consumer protection advocates have the negative belief that “planned obsolescence” is “exploitative of consumers,” meaning that it unfairly takes advantage of consumers.

Notice that a key term, “planned obsolescence,” is specifically defined in between the two dashes in S1: planned obsolescence is the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.

In S2, notice the opinion marker, “indeed.” “Indeed” signals that the author supports the consumer protection advocates’ opinion above that planned obsolescence is exploitative.

Then, the author presents two examples to support this opinion: the “Phoebus cartel” and the citation made by Annie Leonard.

1st paragraph summary: The author introduces the concept of “planned obsolescence,” the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases, and provides two examples that support consumer protection advocates’ belief that planned obsolescence unfairly takes advantage of consumers.

In S4, notice the contrast marker “at the same time,” which has a similar meaning to “meanwhile,” and can be thought of as a softer version of “nevertheless.” This marker is being used here to transition to discussing a different possible reason for frequent product replacement; this reason is “a demand-side phenomenon.” Notice that “demand-side” is in contrast to the word “supplying” in S1: “‘planned obsolescence’ — the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

At this point, it may not be totally clear what is meant by “demand-side phenomenon,” but we can expect to gain more context as we read further. Indeed, the next sentence starts with the support marker “For example.”

In S5, the example of frequent smartphone replacement is cited as a “demand-side phenomenon” rather than the “planned obsolescence” described in the 1st paragraph. Although the phones are replaced very frequently, the reason for it is NOT that the phones have worn out; S5 says, “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.” The author is mentioning this example because it is different from the reason for frequent replacement discussed in the 1st paragraph — products that are intentionally designed to wear out quickly.

The rest of the paragraph expands on this example, and in S8, the quote of Walker’s conclusion summarizes the key point in S4 that “frequent product replacement has sometimes become a demand-side phenomenon.” Note that “the market gives us exactly what we want” connects closely with the meaning of “demand-side.”

It’s important to notice that this paragraph does NOT directly dispute the consumer protection advocates’ negative opinion about “planned obsolescence” in the 1st paragraph. Rather, the second paragraph introduces a different reason for frequent product replacement, consumer demand/preferences. This “demand-side phenomenon” is NOT cited as an example of “planned obsolescence,” because the 2nd paragraph doesn’t say that the products are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases. Rather, the second paragraph focuses on the desires of consumers.

2nd paragraph summary: The author introduces a different possible reason for frequent product replacement, consumer demand/preferences, and supports this point with the example of frequent smartphone replacement; unlike the products described in the 1st paragraph that are designed to wear out quickly, these discarded smartphones are “still in good working order.”

Passage summary: The author discusses two different reasons for frequent product replacement: the business strategy of intentionally designing products to wear out quickly and, contrastingly, consumer demand for frequent upgrades.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Defines a key term/theme, the strategy of “planned obsolescence,” and presents a negative opinion about it.

Notice the opinion marker, “conviction that X.” “Conviction” means “a strong belief.”

Notice that a key term, “planned obsolescence,” is specifically defined in between the two dashes.

Simplification: Consumer protection advocates believe that “planned obsolescence” unfairly takes advantage of consumers. Planned obsolescence is the business strategy of designing products in a way that requires more frequent replacement.

S2: The author expresses support for the negative opinion presented in S1, by citing an example.

Notice the opinion marker “indeed.” “Indeed” signals that the author supports the consumer protection advocates’ opinion above that planned obsolescence is exploitative.

Note that “would be hard-pressed to justify” means “would have trouble justifying.” Basically, when the author says, “there are certain cases that even the most pro-business advocates would be hard-pressed to justify,” he is saying that there are some examples that are almost indisputably exploitative. Notice the strong language, “even the most.”

Notice the support marker “such as,” which introduces the example of the “infamous Phoebus cartel.” We can clearly see that this case of deliberately reducing the lifespan of bulbs to 1,000 hours is an example of “planned obsolescence” as defined in S1 — “supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for the reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Simplification: Indeed, some examples of planned obsolescence are almost indisputably exploitative, such as the Phoebus cartel’s deliberate reduction of the lifespan of light bulbs.

S3: The author provides an additional example of planned obsolescence.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” S2 said that there are “certain cases” that are hard to justify, and provided one example. We can see that S3 provides a second example of planned obsolescence that is “hard to justify”: “designers explicitly discuss how to design products that will wear out as rapidly as possible while leaving customers with sufficient faith in a product to replace it immediately.”

Simplification: Annie Leonard cites evidence of the planned obsolescence strategy being discussed in industrial-design journals.

S4: The author introduces a different possible reason for frequent product replacement.

Notice the contrast marker, “at the same time,” which has a similar meaning to “meanwhile,” and can be thought of as a softer version of “nevertheless.” This marker is used here to transition to discussing a different possible reason for frequent product replacement; this reason is “a demand-side phenomenon.”

At this point, it may not be totally clear what is meant by “demand-side phenomenon,” but we can expect to gain more context as we read further. Indeed, the next sentence starts with “For example.”

Particularly once we’ve read further, we can see that “demand-side phenomenon” means “related to consumer preferences.”

Also notice that “demand-side” is in contrast to the word “supplying” in S1: “‘planned obsolescence’ — the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Notice that the author’s tone is descriptive, and the author doesn’t express a strong opinion of his own; the wording used is “an interesting argument has been made that.” The word “interesting” is quite neutral; it does not express strong support of or opposition to this argument.

S5: Provides an example to support the point made in S4.

Notice the support marker “for example.”

Notice the contrast marker “although.” This example is being cited as a “demand-side phenomenon” unlike the “planned obsolescence” described in the 1st paragraph. Although the phones are replaced very frequently, the reason for it is NOT that the phones have worn out; S5 says that “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.” The author is mentioning this example because it is different from the reason for frequent replacement discussed in the 1st paragraph — products that are intentionally designed to wear out quickly.

S6: Expands on the example in S5, by adding details about why people replace their smartphones so frequently.

Notice the contrast marker “while X, Y.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Here, the Y part says that Walker “has a strong desire to stay current with the latest impressive technological innovations.” This is an example of the “demand-side phenomenon” introduced in S4.

S7: Expands on the point in S6 about Walker’s motivation

Notice the conclusion marker “therefore,” which connects Walker’s “strong desire to stay current,” in S6, to the point in S7.

Notice the contrast marker “instead of X, Y”: “instead of resenting the cost of replacement, he looks forward to upgrading his device regularly.” This positive attitude is different from the critical attitude of the “consumer protection advocates” in S1.

S8: Quotes Walker’s general conclusion.

The quote of Walker’s conclusion summarizes the key point in S4 that “frequent product replacement has sometimes become a demand-side phenomenon.” Note that “exactly what we want” connects closely with the meaning of “demand-side.”

question.text: Whichof the following best describes the function of the highlighted sentence?

answers.text: To call into question the theory that humans burn daily fewer calories than expected
To support the assertion that foraging for food was dangerous for our hunter and gatherer ancestors
To highlight the exercise-related metabolic similarities between humans and animals
To present one explanation for why early humans would have evolved to conserve energy
To demonstrate that calorie conservation was probably not useful to humans’ early ancestors

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
To understand the role of the highlighted sentence, we can consider what’s happening above the highlighted sentence in the 4thparagraph:

“He and his fellow researchers hypothesized that this downward caloric compensatory phenomenon is an evolutionary development passed down to us from our distant hunter and gatherer ancestors who spent a significant portion of their days expending caloric energy in search of sustenance.”

We see that, so far, the 4thparagraph is presenting what Lewis and fellow researchers hypothesized about the origin of “this downward caloric compensatory phenomenon,” which is that it’s an “evolutionary development passed down to us from … ancestors.”
Then, the highlighted sentence says the following:
“Because at that time food was scarce, these ancestors would have benefited from their bodies’ protecting calories during times of activity.”

When we view the highlighted sentence in the context of what appears above it in the 4thparagraph, we can see that the purpose of the highlighted sentence is toexplain whyour hunter gatherer ancestors would have developed “this downward caloric compensatory phenomenon”: they “would have benefited from their bodies’ protecting calories.”
(A) To call into question the theory that humans burn daily fewer calories than expected

This choice is wrong for a few reasons.First, it’s pretty much the opposite of what we’re seeking.The highlighted sentence provides us with a reason why the mentioned downward caloric compensatory phenomenon occurs.Therefore, the highlighted sentence is in a wayproviding support for, notcalling into question, the idea that humans burn fewer calories than would be expected on the days they exercise.

Second, the passage is not about humans burning “fewer overall daily calories than expected.” That statement is too broad.Instead, the passage is about humans burning fewer overall daily calories than expectedon the days during which they exercise.So, this choice distorts a key point in the passage.
(B) To support the assertion that foraging for food was dangerous for our hunter and gatherer ancestors

This choice starts off OK in saying “to support the assertion.” After all, as we saw in our Question Analysis, the highlighted sentence provides support for the hypothesis that “this downward caloric compensatory phenomenon is an evolutionary development passed down to us from … ancestors” by explaining why “these ancestors would have benefited from their bodies’ protecting calories during times of activity.”

However, as always, we must carefully read the entire choice, which says “that foraging for food was dangerous for our hunter and gatherer ancestors.” By going back to the passage, we see that the highlighted sentence is not used to support the assertion that “that foraging for food was dangerous for our hunter and gatherer ancestors.” That assertion is stated in the next sentence but is not supported by the highlighted sentence.
(C) To highlight the exercise-related metabolic similarities between humans and animals
If you chose this choice as correct, you likely did so because wording similar to what this choice says can be found close to the highlighted sentence.
The author begins the 4thparagraph by saying:
“Lewis writes, “Both humans and animals may respond to greater energy being expended on activity over the long term by reducing the energy expended on the process.”

Although that sentence does discuss the metabolic similarities between humans and animals, the function of the highlighted sentence is not to highlight the exercise-related metabolic similarities between humans and animals.If we continue reading further in the 4thparagraph we see:

“He and his fellow researchers hypothesized that this downward caloric compensatory phenomenon is an evolutionary development passed down to us from our distant hunter and gatherer ancestors who spent a significant portion of their days expending caloric energy in search of sustenance.Because at that time food was scarce, these ancestors would have benefited from their bodies’ protecting calories during times of activity.”

We see that the highlighted sentence provides support for the hypothesis that “this downward caloric compensatory phenomenon is an evolutionary development passed down to us from … ancestors” by explaining why “these ancestors would have benefited from their bodies’ protecting calories during times of activity.”
YOUR CHOICE(D) To present one explanation for why early humans would have evolved to conserve energy

In our Question Analysis, we saw that the function of the highlighted sentence is to provide support for the hypothesis that “this downward caloric compensatory phenomenon is an evolutionary development passed down to us from … ancestors” by explaining why “these ancestors would have benefited from their bodies’ protecting calories during times of activity.”
In other words, the purpose of the highlighted sentence is to “present one explanation for why early humans would have evolved to conserve energy.”
(E) To demonstrate that calorie conservation was probably not useful to humans’ early ancestors

It would not make sense to choose this choice as correct because we see from the 4thparagraph that the highlighted sentence does the opposite of what this choice says; the highlighted sentence provides a reason why calorie conservation was useful to humans’ early ancestors.

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage opens by presenting the fact that “many physiologists recommend exercise … for generation of weight loss.” Presenting what physiologists recommend is this passage’s way of presenting a common belief of physiologists. After all, the fact that physiologists recommend exercise for weight loss indicates that they believe that exercise causes weight loss.

The passage next asks a rhetorical question about this common belief, “is exercise as effective in causing weight loss as these experts believe?” Seeing this rhetorical question, we can tell that the passage is going to answer this rhetorical question and is likely going to show that the physiologists’ common belief is somehow inaccurate.

Having presented the rhetorical question, the passage next shows, in S3, how it’s going to answer the rhetorical question, which is by discussing a study that supports the theory that “our bodies are programmed to ‘calorically’ compensate” and thus burn fewer calories than expected.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents a common belief, asks a rhetorical question about that belief, and presents a study that the passage will discuss to answer that rhetorical question.

The passage next begins to answer the rhetorical question asked by S2 by presenting details of what the study found.

The contrast marker “rather” at the beginning of S5 indicates that S5 states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points. We see that S5 presents the reason fewer calories than expected are burned, which is that, on days when people exercise, the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure.”

Then, the rest of the 2nd paragraph presents an example to explain what S5 says about “basal energy expenditure” and “additional energy expenditure” and to present the idea that a certain set of activities is associated with a certain “expected caloric burn.”

2nd paragraph summary: The main point of the 2nd paragraph is that the reason people burn fewer calories than expected when they exercise is that the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure,” and the rest of the 2nd paragraph presents information to explain this point.

The contrast marker “however” at the beginning of the 3rd paragraph indicates that S9 states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points. Indeed, S9 presents the main thing that the study found, which is that the “expected caloric burn” discussed in the 2nd paragraph “did not materialize,” meaning “did not occur.”

The remainder of the 3rd paragraph then presents details of these study results, which are basically that people burned “fewer calories in total than expected.”

3rd paragraph summary: The study found that the “caloric burn” expected when people engage in a certain set of activities did not occur and that people burned “fewer calories in total than expected.”

The 4th paragraph wraps up the passage by discussing the hypotheses the research team came up with after seeing the study results.

The first of these hypotheses is that “humans and animals respond” to greater energy expenditure by “reducing the energy expended.” The second of these hypotheses is that this “downward caloric compensatory phenomenon” is an evolutionary development.

Then, S14 and S15 present reasons why human ancestors evolved to reduce energy use: “food was scarce” and “time spent foraging” involved danger of getting killed by predators.

4th paragraph summary: The research team hypothesized that humans and animals evolved to reduce energy expenditure because reducing use of calories had certain benefits.

Passage summary: Physiologists recommend exercise for losing weight, but a study found that exercise does not cause people to burn as many calories as expected.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: By saying what “many reputable physiologists recommend,” this sentence introduces a common belief, the belief that exercise “is a mechanism for the generation of weight loss.”

S2: This sentence uses an “although X, Y,” structure. In the “although X” part, the author concedes that exercise can help a person lose weight. Then, in the “Y” part, the author asks a rhetorical question to signal that (1) he or she believes that the common belief introduced in S1 is a myth and that (2) the primary purpose of the passage will be to discuss, in some way, the answer to this rhetorical question.

S3: This sentence introduces a study undertaken by Lewis Halsey and his team along with the theory that the study supports.

Since that theory is that “our bodies are programed to “calorically compensate” on the days on which we exercise, burning on these days significantly fewer calories than the laws of thermodynamics … would predict,” we can see that Lewis’s study and the theory it supports supply an answer to the rhetorical question asked by S2. The study and the theory basically imply that the answer is “No, exercise is not as effective as these experts believe.”

Simplification: A study shows that exercise does not cause as much burning of calories as experts believe it causes.

S4: Uses the look above marker “THESE days” to begin to explain why fewer calories than expected are burned on the days when people exercise, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph.

S5: The contrast marker “rather” at the beginning of this sentence signals that this sentence probably states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points.

Indeed, S5 is an important sentence because it presents the reason fewer calories than expected are burned, which is that, on days when people exercise, the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure.

Simplification: The body burns fewer calories than expected by reducing its “basal,” meaning “basic,” use of energy.

S6, S7, and S8: These sentences present an example to illustrate the concept of “basal energy expenditure” and show how additional activity results in a person’s burning “more calories over and above this basal energy expenditure.”

S9: The contrast marker “however” at the beginning of this sentence signals that this sentence probably states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points.

Indeed, S9 does state an important fact. The fact stated by S9, “in the study, Lewis and his team observed that this expected caloric burn did not materialize,” explains how the study supports the theory mentioned in S3, which is that “our bodies are programed to ‘calorically compensate’ on the days on which we exercise, burning on these days significantly fewer calories than the laws of thermodynamics … would predict.”

S10 and S11: These sentences present details of how the “expected caloric burn did not materialize.”

S12: Presents Lewis’s hypothesis about why “this expected caloric burn did not materialize.”

Simplification: Lewis’s hypothesis about why “this expected caloric burn did not materialize,” is that “humans and animals respond to greater energy being expended on activity” by reducing the amount of energy they use.

S13: Expands on the hypothesis presented in S12.

Simplification: The researchers think this reduction of energy use is something that human ancestors evolved to do.

S14 and S15: These sentences present reasons why human ancestors evolved to reduce energy use: “food was scarce” and “time spent foraging” involved danger of getting killed by predators.

An easy way to establish the attitude toward Lewis’ discovery taken by the author of the passage is to consider the words used by the author.First, notice that there are no words or excitement, praise, or overt happiness.Second, notice that there are no words that would express criticism, skepticism, or disagreement.

Instead, the author has written this passage in the type of neutral way that is commonly used to convey scientific facts, discoveries, and findings.Therefore, the author’s attitude toward Lewis’s discovery can be best described as neutral.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage opens by presenting the fact that “many physiologists recommend exercise … for generation of weight loss.” Presenting what physiologists recommend is this passage’s way of presenting a common belief of physiologists. After all, the fact that physiologists recommend exercise for weight loss indicates that they believe that exercise causes weight loss.

The passage next asks a rhetorical question about this common belief, “is exercise as effective in causing weight loss as these experts believe?” Seeing this rhetorical question, we can tell that the passage is going to answer this rhetorical question and is likely going to show that the physiologists’ common belief is somehow inaccurate.

Having presented the rhetorical question, the passage next shows, in S3, how it’s going to answer the rhetorical question, which is by discussing a study that supports the theory that “our bodies are programmed to ‘calorically’ compensate” and thus burn fewer calories than expected.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents a common belief, asks a rhetorical question about that belief, and presents a study that the passage will discuss to answer that rhetorical question.

The passage next begins to answer the rhetorical question asked by S2 by presenting details of what the study found.

The contrast marker “rather” at the beginning of S5 indicates that S5 states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points. We see that S5 presents the reason fewer calories than expected are burned, which is that, on days when people exercise, the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure.”

Then, the rest of the 2nd paragraph presents an example to explain what S5 says about “basal energy expenditure” and “additional energy expenditure” and to present the idea that a certain set of activities is associated with a certain “expected caloric burn.”

2nd paragraph summary: The main point of the 2nd paragraph is that the reason people burn fewer calories than expected when they exercise is that the body reduces its “basal energy expenditure,” and the rest of the 2nd paragraph presents information to explain this point.

The contrast marker “however” at the beginning of the 3rd paragraph indicates that S9 states an important point since contrasts are usually connected to important points. Indeed, S9 presents the main thing that the study found, which is that the “expected caloric burn” discussed in the 2nd paragraph “did not materialize,” meaning “did not occur.”

The remainder of the 3rd paragraph then presents details of these study results, which are basically that people burned “fewer calories in total than expected.”

3rd paragraph summary: The study found that the “caloric burn” expected when people engage in a certain set of activities did not occur and that people burned “fewer calories in total than expected.”

The 4th paragraph wraps up the passage by discussing the hypotheses the research team came up with after seeing the study results.

The first of these hypotheses is that “humans and animals respond” to greater energy expenditure by “reducing the energy expended.” The second of these hypotheses is that this “downward caloric compensatory phenomenon” is an evolutionary development.

Then, S14 and S15 present reasons why human ancestors evolved to reduce energy use: “food was scarce” and “time spent foraging” involved danger of getting killed by predators.

4th paragraph summary: The research team hypothesized that humans and animals evolved to reduce energy expenditure because reducing use of calories had certain benefits.

Passage summary: Physiologists recommend exercise for losing weight, but a study found that exercise does not cause people to burn as many calories as expected.

question.text: Whichof the following best describes the function of the highlighted sentence?

answers.text: To provide evidence that Ernest Hemingway was a great writer of fiction.
To cast doubt on the idea that Ernest Hemingway was similar to other great writers of fiction.
To highlight the turbulent nature of Ernest Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky.
To demonstrate the differences between the personalities of Ernest Hemingway and Lieutenant Henry.
To present evidence that the events happening in the novels of Ernest Hemingway blurred with those of his personal life.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

As we know, the author of this passage presents an observation in the 1stparagraph.Then, he or she provides support for that observation in each of the three paragraphs that follow.In other words, each of the last three paragraphs uses examples from the life or novels of Hemingway to support the point set forth in the 1stparagraph, which is:

“Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories, and they sometimes transform their stories into reality.Over time, the line between fiction and fact, the lives about which they write in their novels and the reality in which they live outside of their typewriters, often becomes less clear.Ernest Hemingway and his novels are no exception.”

When we examine the highlighted sentence, we see that is sits at the heart of the evidence provided in the 2ntparagraph.Notice that the sentence provides a factual example of how Hemingway is an example of “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.” After all, what this sentence conveys is that Hemingway took the pain he felt from Agnes rejection and used it in his novel “A Farewell to Arms.”
So, when we go to the choices, we’ll be seeking one that classifies the highlighted sentence as evidence.
(A) To provide evidence that Ernest Hemingway was a great writer of fiction.
Trap: Distortion

The author of the passage has used the highlighted sentence as evidence not to support the idea that Ernest Hemingway was a great writer of fiction, but to support the idea that Hemingway is an example of “Great writers of fiction often transforming their realities into their stories.”
(B) To cast doubt on the idea that Ernest Hemingway was similar to other great writers of fiction.
Trap: Opposite

Because the final three paragraphs provide evidence supporting the idea that Hemingway is an example of “Great writers of fiction often transforming their realities into their stories,” what this choice says is the opposite of what we’re seeking in a correct answer.
(C) To highlight the turbulent nature of Ernest Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky.
Trap: Distortion.
Nowhere in the passage has the author made the claim that Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky was “turbulent.”

Instead, the author mentions Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky along with the fact that he used it as a basis for a novel as evidence to support the idea that Hemingway is an example of “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”
(D) To demonstrate the differences between the personalities of Ernest Hemingway and Lieutenant Henry.
Trap: Opposite

If anything, the highlighted sentence serves to highlight the similarities between Hemingway and Lieutenant Henry.Thus, what this answer choice says is the opposite of what we’re seeking in a correct answer.
YOUR CHOICE(E) To present evidence that the events happening in the novels of Ernest Hemingway blurred with those of his personal life.

Notice that “present evidence that the events happening in the novels of Ernest Hemingway blurred with those of his personal life” is exactly what the role of the highlighted sentence is.After all, the author is using the highlighted sentence as evidence to support the idea that Hemingway is an example of “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting generalizations about “great writers of fiction,” which are that they “often transform their realities into their stories, and they sometimes transform their stories into reality,” and that “Over time, the line between fiction and fact … often becomes less clear.”

Then the final sentence of the 1st paragraph, S3, narrows the scope of the discussion from “great writers” in general to “Ernest Hemingway and his novels.”

By narrowing the scope of the discussion in this way, S3, signals that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss how Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to the generalizations presented by S1 and S2.

1st paragraph summary: Ernest Hemingway and his novels follow patterns common among great writers of fiction that involve these writers’ realities and stories being transformed into each other.

The 2nd paragraph first presents an aspect of Hemingway’s reality, that he developed an “interest in the reversal of traditional gender roles and sexuality” as a result of experiences he had when he was young.

Then, in S6, the passage shows how “this interest” from Hemingway’s “reality” shows up in Hemingway’s “stories” by presenting details of The Garden of Eden that match up with the interest and experiences mentioned in S4 and S5.

We can see that the 2nd paragraph supports the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

2nd paragraph summary: In his reality, Hemingway experienced and developed an interest in the reversal of traditional gender roles and sexuality, and these aspects of Hemingway’s reality are “apparent” in his story The Garden of Eden.

Like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph begins by presenting an aspect of Hemingway’s reality, his relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky and the “anguish” associated with that relationship.

Then, S9 through S12 show how this reality was “put to work” in Hemingway’s story, A Farewell to Arms.

So, like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph uses examples from Hemingway’s reality and his stories to support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

3rd paragraph summary: In his reality, Hemingway experienced anguish in a personal relationship, and Hemingway “put to work” this anguish in the creation of his story A Farewell to Arms.

Whereas the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs provide support for the assertion that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern of great writers transforming their realities into their stories, now the 4th paragraph presents an example of a case in which Hemingway’s story appeared in his reality: he told a story “as if it were truth” when in fact “it was merely … an extension of” his fiction.

So, like the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the 4th paragraph supports the point stated by S3, but in this case by providing an example of the pattern of writers “sometimes transform their stories into reality.”

4th paragraph summary: Hemingway frequently told a story “as if it were truth” though it was actually from his “fictional writing,” and thus, in a way, transformed one of his stories into reality, in accordance with a pattern of “great writers of fiction” discussed in the 1st paragraph.

Passage summary: The passage presents some generalizations about “great writers of fiction,” asserts that Ernest Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to these generalizations, and presents examples from Hemingway’s reality and his stories to support that assertion.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Introduces the topic of the passage by presenting a generalization about “great writers of fiction.”

S2: Expands on what S1 says by presenting the additional generalization that, for these “great writers,” the line between fiction and fact often becomes less clear over time.

S3: By saying that Ernest Hemingway and his novels “are no exception,” this sentence communicates both that Hemingway is one of the “great writers of fiction” mentioned in S1 and S2 and that the generalizations presented by S1 and S2 apply to him.

Notice that this sentence narrows the scope of what the passage is discussing from “great writers of fiction” to Ernest Hemingway specifically.

By narrowing the scope of the discussion in this way, S3, signals that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss how Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to the generalizations presented by S1 and S2.

S4: Presents an aspect of Hemingway’s “reality,” that he experienced and developed an interest in “reversal of gender roles and sexuality.”

S5: Continues to discuss the aspect of Hemingway’s reality presented by S4.

S6: S6 uses the look above marker “THIS interest” to refer to Hemingway’s “interest in reversal of gender roles and sexuality” mentioned in S4 and presents a way in which Hemingway’s “reality” described in S4 and S5 made its way into Hemingway’s “stories.”

Simplification: The experience and interest in reversal of gender roles and sexuality from Hemingway’s reality show up in Hemingway’s work The Garden of Eden.

S7: Presents details of The Garden of Eden to further support the point that Hemingway’s reality made its way into this novel.

Notice that S4 through S7 work together to support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

S8: Presents a second aspect of Hemingway’s “reality,” that he fell in love with Agnes von Kurowsky.

S9: Continues to discuss the reality of Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky and then shows how Hemingway “put to work” that reality to create a story, the novel A Farewell to Arms.

S10 and S11: These sentences present details of A Farewell to Arms to further demonstrate how it resembles the reality of Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky.

S12: By saying that the pain of Henry, a character in Hemingway’s fiction, is “palpable,” meaning “apparent” or “clear,” the passage indicates that Hemingway used the “anguish” that he experienced to create strong fiction.

Notice that, in S8 through S12, the passage once again supports the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

S13: Presents a story that Hemingway told as if it were reality.

S14: Says that “the story” mentioned in S13 was “merely theatre,” meaning “simply fiction.”

Notice that S13 and S14 support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern of great writers “sometimes transform their stories into reality.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting generalizations about “great writers of fiction,” which are that they “often transform their realities into their stories, and they sometimes transform their stories into reality,” and that “Over time, the line between fiction and fact … often becomes less clear.”

Then the final sentence of the 1st paragraph, S3, narrows the scope of the discussion from “great writers” in general to “Ernest Hemingway and his novels.”

By narrowing the scope of the discussion in this way, S3, signals that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss how Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to the generalizations presented by S1 and S2.

1st paragraph summary: Ernest Hemingway and his novels follow patterns common among great writers of fiction that involve these writers’ realities and stories being transformed into each other.

The 2nd paragraph first presents an aspect of Hemingway’s reality, that he developed an “interest in the reversal of traditional gender roles and sexuality” as a result of experiences he had when he was young.

Then, in S6, the passage shows how “this interest” from Hemingway’s “reality” shows up in Hemingway’s “stories” by presenting details of The Garden of Eden that match up with the interest and experiences mentioned in S4 and S5.

We can see that the 2nd paragraph supports the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

2nd paragraph summary: In his reality, Hemingway experienced and developed an interest in the reversal of traditional gender roles and sexuality, and these aspects of Hemingway’s reality are “apparent” in his story The Garden of Eden.

Like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph begins by presenting an aspect of Hemingway’s reality, his relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky and the “anguish” associated with that relationship.

Then, S9 through S12 show how this reality was “put to work” in Hemingway’s story, A Farewell to Arms.

So, like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph uses examples from Hemingway’s reality and his stories to support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

3rd paragraph summary: In his reality, Hemingway experienced anguish in a personal relationship, and Hemingway “put to work” this anguish in the creation of his story A Farewell to Arms.

Whereas the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs provide support for the assertion that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern of great writers transforming their realities into their stories, now the 4th paragraph presents an example of a case in which Hemingway’s story appeared in his reality: he told a story “as if it were truth” when in fact “it was merely … an extension of” his fiction.

So, like the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the 4th paragraph supports the point stated by S3, but in this case by providing an example of the pattern of writers “sometimes transform their stories into reality.”

4th paragraph summary: Hemingway frequently told a story “as if it were truth” though it was actually from his “fictional writing,” and thus, in a way, transformed one of his stories into reality, in accordance with a pattern of “great writers of fiction” discussed in the 1st paragraph.

Passage summary: The passage presents some generalizations about “great writers of fiction,” asserts that Ernest Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to these generalizations, and presents examples from Hemingway’s reality and his stories to support that assertion.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Introduces the topic of the passage by presenting a generalization about “great writers of fiction.”

S2: Expands on what S1 says by presenting the additional generalization that, for these “great writers,” the line between fiction and fact often becomes less clear over time.

S3: By saying that Ernest Hemingway and his novels “are no exception,” this sentence communicates both that Hemingway is one of the “great writers of fiction” mentioned in S1 and S2 and that the generalizations presented by S1 and S2 apply to him.

Notice that this sentence narrows the scope of what the passage is discussing from “great writers of fiction” to Ernest Hemingway specifically.

By narrowing the scope of the discussion in this way, S3, signals that the primary purpose of the passage is to discuss how Hemingway and his novels “are no exception” to the generalizations presented by S1 and S2.

S4: Presents an aspect of Hemingway’s “reality,” that he experienced and developed an interest in “reversal of gender roles and sexuality.”

S5: Continues to discuss the aspect of Hemingway’s reality presented by S4.

S6: S6 uses the look above marker “THIS interest” to refer to Hemingway’s “interest in reversal of gender roles and sexuality” mentioned in S4 and presents a way in which Hemingway’s “reality” described in S4 and S5 made its way into Hemingway’s “stories.”

Simplification: The experience and interest in reversal of gender roles and sexuality from Hemingway’s reality show up in Hemingway’s work The Garden of Eden.

S7: Presents details of The Garden of Eden to further support the point that Hemingway’s reality made its way into this novel.

Notice that S4 through S7 work together to support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

S8: Presents a second aspect of Hemingway’s “reality,” that he fell in love with Agnes von Kurowsky.

S9: Continues to discuss the reality of Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky and then shows how Hemingway “put to work” that reality to create a story, the novel A Farewell to Arms.

S10 and S11: These sentences present details of A Farewell to Arms to further demonstrate how it resembles the reality of Hemingway’s relationship with Agnes von Kurowsky.

S12: By saying that the pain of Henry, a character in Hemingway’s fiction, is “palpable,” meaning “apparent” or “clear,” the passage indicates that Hemingway used the “anguish” that he experienced to create strong fiction.

Notice that, in S8 through S12, the passage once again supports the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern “Great writers of fiction often transform their realities into their stories.”

S13: Presents a story that Hemingway told as if it were reality.

S14: Says that “the story” mentioned in S13 was “merely theatre,” meaning “simply fiction.”

Notice that S13 and S14 support the point stated by S3 that Hemingway and his novels are no exception to the pattern of great writers “sometimes transform their stories into reality.”

question.text: The author of the passage mentions President Kennedy's declaration primarily in order to

answers.text: explain the viewpoint of President Kennedy
argue that motivation is the most important factor in achieving goals
illustrate a point about the benefits of high aspirations
provide an example of a remarkable achievement of the United States
demonstrate the power of space exploration

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
Since the stem of this Specific Purpose question asks about “President Kennedy’s declaration,” our first step in answering this question is to find these keywords, which we find in S3.

Next, read the start of the sentence, right before these keywords.We that S3 starts with “A powerfulexampleoften cited is ….” Examples are generally used to support a point made above.Therefore, we can see that the specific purpose of mentioning President Kennedy's declaration is to provide an example to support a point made above.

What is that point made above? In S2, notice the opinion marker, “argued.” We can see that what is argued is that “challenging goals” have benefits.Three benefits are mentioned in a list: “challenging goals inspire motivation, confidence, and impressive results.”
Thus, the specific purpose of mentioning President Kennedy’s declaration is to provide an example to support an opinion about the benefits of challenging goals.
(A) explain the viewpoint of President Kennedy
Trap: Matches but Incorrect
We must remember that the correct answer to a Specific Purpose question will describe thepurposeof the part of the passage, as opposed to just describing the part of the passage itself.

So, although Kennedy’s declaration in the passage does indeed “explain the viewpoint of President Kennedy,” this choice doesn’t answer the question in the stem about the purpose of mentioning Kennedy's declaration.After all, the author does not mention President Kennedy’s declaration for the purpose of explaining the viewpoint of President Kennedy.
(B) argue that motivation is the most important factor in achieving goals
Trap: Stretch
Notice the extreme marker “the MOST,” which is a yellow flag indicating that this choice may be a Stretch choice.
“Most” signifies acomparisonto other “factors in achieving goals” — “the MOST important factor” means thatall other factorsare less important.
However, the passage never compares the importance of motivation and that of other factors in achieving goals, and it does not say that “motivation” is the “most important factor.”
So, this choice goes beyond what is said in the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(C) illustrate a point about the benefits of high aspirations
In our Question Analysis, we saw that the specific purposeof the mention of President Kennedy's declaration is to provide an example to support an opinion about the benefits of challenging goals.
Now, we must confirm whether this summary of the specific purpose matches the wording in this choice:
Does “Illustrate a point” match with “provide an example”? Yes.
Does “benefits of high aspirations” match with what the passage says, “motivation, confidence, and impressive results”? Yes.
Does “high aspirations” match with “challenging goals”? Yes.
Therefore, we can safely pick this choice.
(D) provide an example of a remarkable achievement of the United States
Trap: Matches but Incorrect
We must remember that the correct answer to a Specific Purpose question will describe thepurposeof the part of the passage, as opposed to just describing the part of the passage itself.

So, although, by mentioning Kennedy’s declaration, the author does in a way “provide an example of a remarkable achievement of the United States,” this choice doesn’t answer the question in the stem about thepurposeof mentioning Kennedy's declaration, which, as we discussed in our Question Analysis, is to provide an example to support an opinion about the benefits of challenging goals.
(E) demonstrate the power of space exploration
From our auestion analysis, we see that the author is not making a point about space exploration in general.
Rather, the specific purpose of the mention of Kennedy’s declaration is to provide an example to support an opinion about the benefits of challenging goals.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In the 1st paragraph, the author presents the idea that some authors advocate employing “massive action” and “ambitious commitments” in “achieving aims.” She also presents Kennedy’s ambitious plan to send a man to the moon as an example of such massive action and ambitious commitment in pursuit of a goal. Notice that the author does not express her own opinion on the value this approach; she simply describes an idea.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents some authors’ opinion about “achieving aims” (objectives). This opinion is that people should employ the “massive action” approach.

In the 2nd paragraph, the author presents a contrasting opinion on the matter of achieving goals, that of Stanford Professor BJ Fogg, who contends that “to start small” is “more effective and sustainable.” Notice that, regarding the approach mentioned in the 1st paragraph (massive action and ambitious commitments), the author says, “In contrast, Stanford Professor BJ Fogg contends that this approach can result in adverse outcomes, including the inevitable emotions of guilt and discouragement, if a person fails to accomplish an aggressive goal.”

Although, in the 2nd paragraph, the author has presented an alternative opinion on the matter of achieving goals, it’s important to notice that the author does not express her own opinion on the value of the “start small” approach or on whether the “start small” approach is better or worse than the massive action and ambitious commitments approach mentioned in the first paragraph; she simply presents the “start small” approach as advocated by BJ Fogg.

2nd paragraph summary: “In contrast” signals that the 2nd paragraph presents a second opinion that contrasts with the one presented in the first paragraph. This paragraph presents the opinion that people should employ the “start small” approach.

So, we see that, within the first two paragraphs, we don’t have a firm position or conclusion set forth by the author. If the passage had ended here, the author’s conclusion would have been a mystery.

In the final paragraph, the author presents her own conclusion, which is that neither of the two approaches is a clear winner and that “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics of the desired result, as well as the particular personality of the individual in question.” She goes on to say that “Some people are more likely to achieve superior outcomes by thinking big and committing themselves to bold plans, whereas many others benefit from the Tiny Habits approach.” We can clearly see that the author does not believe that one of the two approaches is superior; she believes that both have a time and a place.

3rd paragraph summary: The author doesn’t take a strong stance one way or the other. The author’s main conclusion is: “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics.”

Passage summary: The author presents two contrasting approaches and does not take a strong position in favor of any approach.
