analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for Reading Comprehension, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you work though the analysis we’ve provided below.

S1: Notice that “assert” is an opinion marker. So, we know that this sentence is presenting the opinion of “the authors” of “personal development books.”

Simplification: Many personal development authors argue that people should take large actions to achieve goals.

S2: This sentence expands on the point of S1 and provides two examples of the kinds of books mentioned in S1. Notice another opinion marker, “argued.” Additional benefits of “challenging goals” are mentioned: “motivation, confidence, and impressive results.”

Simplification: In some examples of these books, it is argued that challenging goals have many benefits.

S3: Kennedy’s ambitious plan to send a man to the moon is cited as an example of “massive action” and “ambitious commitments” in pursuit of a goal.

S4: This sentence expands on the example in S3: the successful result of Kennedy’s plan is described.

S5: Notice the contrast marker “in contrast” and the opinion marker “contends.”

These markers tell us that the 2nd paragraph will discuss a viewpoint that contrasts with the viewpoint discussed in the 1st paragraph.

Also, notice the look above marker “THIS approach,” in “Fogg … contends that this approach can result in adverse outcomes.” “This approach” refers to the “massive action” approach discussed in the 1st paragraph, and Fogg’s opinion is that there are downsides (adverse outcomes) to the “this approach.”

S6: Notice another opinion marker, “he argues,” and the contrast marker “MORE effective … to start small.” We can see that this sentence continues the discussion of Fogg’s view that S5 began.

S7: The phrase “COMMON perception” acts as a myth marker that foreshadows that Fogg may believe that this common perception is a myth. This sentence expands on the author’s critique of “our culture” in S6.

S8: As we might expect from the foreshadowing in S7, this sentence begins with the opinion marker, “Fogg suggests,” which is followed by the contrast marker “in actuality.”

So, we can see that the point of this sentence is that Fogg disagrees with the “common perception” because he believes that “the problem is with the individual’s system, not the individual.”

S9: The previous four sentences all describe Fogg’s opinion on the “start small” approach to achieving goals. This sentence describes James Clear’s support for Fogg’s opinion. James Clear cites a study that supports Fogg’s opinion.

S10: Notice the contrast marker “while.” The author’s conclusion is that, although both approaches discussed the previous two paragraphs have some positive qualities, “the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics ….” We can see that the author does not believe that one of the two approaches is superior; she believes that both have a time and a place.

S11: The author expands on her main conclusion in S10 with additional details. Notice another contrast marker, “whereas.”

Simplification: Some people benefit from the “massive action” approach (presented in the 1st paragraph), whereas other people benefit from the Tiny Habits approach (presented in the 2nd paragraph).

S12: Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” This marker tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion stated by a sentence probably located more than one sentence above.

Both S12 and S11 support the conclusion in S10 that “the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics ….”

S11 said that different people can benefit from different approaches, and S12 adds that the same person can also benefit from using different approaches for different goals.

S13: Notice the support marker “for example,” which tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support the point made in the previous sentence, S12, with an example.

question.text: Which of the following best describes the function of the highlighted sentence?

answers.text: Show that choosing challenging goals is effective
Identify a reason why the Tiny Habits approach is beneficial
Explain that a variety of approaches is necessary
Prove that an assertion in the first paragraph is invalid
Support a claim made earlier in the paragraph

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

In a Specific Purpose question (stem says “in order to” or “function of”), the question asks about a specific part of the passage.The purpose of that specific part of the passage is most often to support a point madeabovethat part of the passage.However, sometimes, the purpose could involve somethingbelow.

In this case, notice the additional point marker “furthermore” at the start of the highlighted sentence.This marker is a particularly helpful clue that tells us that the purpose of this sentence is support a conclusion that is probably located more than one sentence above.

As it turns out, we need to look two sentences above to find the conclusion supported by the highlighted sentence, “while both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approachdependson the specifics ….”

Notice that the highlighted sentence is about the benefits of “usingdifferent approachesfor different goals.” This point is very similar to the conclusion stated by S10, “the most appropriate approachdependson the specifics.”
So, the specific purpose of the highlighted sentence is to provide support for the author’s main conclusion, which is stated two sentences above the highlighted sentence.
(A) Show that choosing challenging goals is effective

From our question analysis, we see that highlighted portion supports the conclusion that “the most appropriate approachdependson the specifics,” in other words the conclusion that either choosing challenging goals or starting small may, or may not, be effective depending on the specifics.
So, the purpose of the highlighted portion is not to show that “choosing challenging goals is effective.”
(B) Identify a reason why the Tiny Habits approach is beneficial
Trap: Nearby
If you picked this choice, you may have thought that the highlighted sentence was supporting the second half of the sentence directly above, “whereas many others benefit from theTiny Habitsapproach.”

However, the additional point marker “furthermore” is a helpful clue that tells us that the purpose of the highlighted part is to support a conclusion that is probably located more than one sentence above.

Also, in considering the meaning of the highlighted sentence, we see that the sentence is about the benefits of “usingdifferent approaches,” not about the single “Tiny Habits approach.” In other words, the fact that “the same individual may benefit from using different approaches for different goals” is not a reason why one approach, the Tiny Habits approach is beneficial.

We must be aware of both the structural clues and the meaning of the passage.Although the specific purpose of a highlighted part is often to support the wording in the passage immediately before the highlighted part, there are exceptions.So, we must be careful not to assume that the function is always to support the wording immediately before the highlighted part.
(C) Explain that a variety of approaches is necessary
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the highlighted sentence says “the same individual may benefit from usingdifferent approachesfor different goals.” Indeed, this quote does resemble “variety of approaches” in this answer choice.
Notice, however, the strong wording of this choice, “isnecessary.” The wording of the highlighted part of the passage is much less strong, because it uses the qualifier marker“may,” in “maybenefit.”
So, in saying “a wide variety of approaches is necessary,” this choice goes too far, saying something that goes beyond what the highlighted portion says.
(D) Prove that an assertion in the first paragraph is invalid
Trap: Stretch

Notice the strong language in this choice, “prove…invalid.” “Invalid” means “wrong” or “untrue,” but is the purpose of the highlighted portion toprove invalidthe assertion in the first paragraph ‘their readers should take "massive action" and make ambitious commitments in order to achieve their aims’? No.

In saying “the same individual may benefit from using different approaches,” the highlighted part does not “prove” the assertion “readers should take massive action” invalid.After all, if an individual may benefit from using different approaches, it also may be the case that that individual “should take massive action.”

It is true that, since the highlighted part says “the same individual may benefit from using different approaches,” the highlighted part suggests that approaches other than “massive action”maybenefit people.However, in saying that the highlighted part “provesthat an assertion isinvalid,” this choice goes too far.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Support a claim made earlier in the paragraph

The key clue that we can use in answering this question is the additional point marker “furthermore,” located at the beginning of the highlighted part, which indicates that the purpose of the highlighted part is to support a conclusion that is probably located more than one sentence above.
In our Question Analysis, we saw that the specific purpose of the highlighted part is to provide support for the author’s main conclusion, which is stated two sentences above the highlighted part.
Now, we must determine whether this choice matches the specific purpose of the highlighted part:
“Support a claim” matches “provide support for the author’s main conclusion,” and “earlier in the paragraph” means “above.”
Therefore, we can safely pick this choice.

2nd paragraph summary: “In contrast” signals that the 2nd paragraph presents a second opinion that contrasts with the one presented in the first paragraph. This paragraph presents the opinion that people should employ the “start small” approach.

So, we see that, within the first two paragraphs, we don’t have a firm position or conclusion set forth by the author. If the passage had ended here, the author’s conclusion would have been a mystery.

In the final paragraph, the author presents her own conclusion, which is that neither of the two approaches is a clear winner and that “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics of the desired result, as well as the particular personality of the individual in question.” She goes on to say that “Some people are more likely to achieve superior outcomes by thinking big and committing themselves to bold plans, whereas many others benefit from the Tiny Habits approach.” We can clearly see that the author does not believe that one of the two approaches is superior; she believes that both have a time and a place.

3rd paragraph summary: The author doesn’t take a strong stance one way or the other. The author’s main conclusion is: “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics.”

Passage summary: The author presents two contrasting approaches and does not take a strong position in favor of any approach.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for Reading Comprehension, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you work though the analysis we’ve provided below.

S1: Notice that “assert” is an opinion marker. So, we know that this sentence is presenting the opinion of “the authors” of “personal development books.”

Simplification: Many personal development authors argue that people should take large actions to achieve goals.

S2: This sentence expands on the point of S1 and provides two examples of the kinds of books mentioned in S1. Notice another opinion marker, “argued.” Additional benefits of “challenging goals” are mentioned: “motivation, confidence, and impressive results.”

Simplification: In some examples of these books, it is argued that challenging goals have many benefits.

S3: Kennedy’s ambitious plan to send a man to the moon is cited as an example of “massive action” and “ambitious commitments” in pursuit of a goal.

S4: This sentence expands on the example in S3: the successful result of Kennedy’s plan is described.

S5: Notice the contrast marker “in contrast” and the opinion marker “contends.”

These markers tell us that the 2nd paragraph will discuss a viewpoint that contrasts with the viewpoint discussed in the 1st paragraph.

Also, notice the look above marker “THIS approach,” in “Fogg … contends that this approach can result in adverse outcomes.” “This approach” refers to the “massive action” approach discussed in the 1st paragraph, and Fogg’s opinion is that there are downsides (adverse outcomes) to the “this approach.”

S6: Notice another opinion marker, “he argues,” and the contrast marker “MORE effective … to start small.” We can see that this sentence continues the discussion of Fogg’s view that S5 began.

S7: The phrase “COMMON perception” acts as a myth marker that foreshadows that Fogg may believe that this common perception is a myth. This sentence expands on the author’s critique of “our culture” in S6.

S8: As we might expect from the foreshadowing in S7, this sentence begins with the opinion marker, “Fogg suggests,” which is followed by the contrast marker “in actuality.”

So, we can see that the point of this sentence is that Fogg disagrees with the “common perception” because he believes that “the problem is with the individual’s system, not the individual.”

S9: The previous four sentences all describe Fogg’s opinion on the “start small” approach to achieving goals. This sentence describes James Clear’s support for Fogg’s opinion. James Clear cites a study that supports Fogg’s opinion.

S10: Notice the contrast marker “while.” The author’s conclusion is that, although both approaches discussed the previous two paragraphs have some positive qualities, “the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics ….” We can see that the author does not believe that one of the two approaches is superior; she believes that both have a time and a place.

S11: The author expands on her main conclusion in S10 with additional details. Notice another contrast marker, “whereas.”

Simplification: Some people benefit from the “massive action” approach (presented in the 1st paragraph), whereas other people benefit from the Tiny Habits approach (presented in the 2nd paragraph).

S12: Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” This marker tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion stated by a sentence probably located more than one sentence above.

Both S12 and S11 support the conclusion in S10 that “the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics ….”

S11 said that different people can benefit from different approaches, and S12 adds that the same person can also benefit from using different approaches for different goals.

S13: Notice the support marker “for example,” which tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support the point made in the previous sentence, S12, with an example.

question.text: The last paragraph performs which of the following functions in the passage?

answers.text: It specifies which of two approaches is the most effective approach.
It presents evidence in favor of a perspective mentioned earlier in the passage.
It summarizes the discussion of two viewpoints.
It points out that the applicability of two different methods can vary.
It suggests that both of two approaches are inappropriate.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

We can tell from the stem, “The last paragraph performs which of the following functions,” that this Specific Purpose question is asking for the purpose of the 3rdparagraph.We approach a Specific Purpose question about a paragraph similarly to the way we approach a Primary Purpose question: our goal is to understand the structure and main points of the passage.However, a difference is that we will put more emphasis on the 3rdparagraph.
It’s helpful to review the summaries from our Passage Analysis:
1stparagraph summary: The 1stparagraph presents some authors’ opinion about “achieving aims” (objectives).This opinion is that people should employ the “massive action” approach.

2ndparagraph summary: “In contrast” signals that the 2ndparagraph presents a second opinion that contrasts with the one presented in the first paragraph.This paragraph presents the opinion that people should employ the “start small” approach.

3rdparagraph summary: The author doesn’t take a strong stance one way or the other.The author’s main conclusion is: “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approachdepends on the specifics.”
Passage summary: The author presents two contrasting approaches and does not take a strong position in favor of any approach.
(A) It specifies which of two approaches is the most effective approach.

This choice could be tempting, because the wording in this choice, “specifies” and “most effective approach,” resembles wording in the first sentence of the third paragraph, “specifics” and “most appropriate approach.”

Notice, however, that since “specifies” means “explicitly names something,” “specifies which … is the MOST effective approach” is strong language that indicates that, in the third paragraph, the author states that one approach is “the most effective approach.”

From our question analysis, we see that the author does NOT take a strong position in favor of any approach.The author’s main conclusion is “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approachdependson the specifics ….”
Therefore, “specifies … the most effective approach” is incorrect, because this wording means “take a strong position in favor of one approach.”
(B) It presents evidence in favor of a perspective mentioned earlier in the passage.
Trap: Distortion
Notice that “in favor of” is a strong positive position.

In our Question Analysis, we saw that, in the third paragraph, the author does NOT take a strong position in favor of either of the perspectives mentioned earlier in the passage.Rather, the author presents the conclusion “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approachdependson the specifics ….” Then, the statements that follow that conclusion serve only to expand on and support that conclusion, not to support a perspective mentioned in an earlier paragraph.
Therefore, “presents evidencein favor of” does not match what the 3rdparagraph says.
(C) It summarizes the discussion of two viewpoints.
Trap: Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that, in the 3rdparagraph, the author is indeed commenting on “the discussion of two viewpoints.”

However, is the word “summarizes” correct? We must carefully consider the meaning of this choice: “summarize” means “review and describe the main points of something.” Thus, a paragraph that “summarizes” would match with thedescriptivetone of the rest of the passage, rather than present a new opinion or conclusion.

In our Question Analysis, we saw that author is not summarizing the two viewpoints.Rather, the author is offering her ownconclusionthat neither of the two approaches is a clear winner and that “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics…”
In general, to avoid a Half-Right trap, we must confirm that each part of the answer choice matches the passage.
YOUR CHOICE(D) It points out that the applicability of two different methods can vary.

In our Question Analysis, we saw that, in the third paragraph, the author doesn’t take a strong position in favor of any approach.The author’s main conclusion is “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach dependson the specifics ….”

You may quickly get the sense that this answer choice matches well with what we said about the third paragraph, but it is a good habit to take the time to confirm that the exact wording is correct, so that we don’t fall into a trap:
- “Points out that” is an opinion marker, and the author is expressing an opinion/conclusion in the 3rdparagraph.So, this wording is correct.
- “Applicability” matches with “appropriate” (S10).
- “Two differentmethods” matches with the twoapproachesthat are described in the 1stand 2ndparagraphs and then discussed in the 3rdparagraph.

- “Canvary” matches with the author’s conclusion presented in the third paragraph: the author believes that both methods have a time and a place.Also, in the passage, the specific wording matches: “the most appropriate approachdepends” (S10) and “differentapproaches for different goals” (S12).
Now that we’ve confirmed that the wording is correct, we can safely pick this choice.
(E) It suggests that both of two approaches are inappropriate.
Trap: Half-Right

This choice starts off resembling the third paragraph, because the third paragraph is about “both arguments,” which resembles “both of two approaches.” So, to avoid choosing this trap choice, we have to be careful toread all the way to the end of the choiceto see the word “inappropriate.”
“Both of two approaches areinappropriate,” is a strong negative position.

In our Question Analysis, we saw that the author does NOT take a strong position in favor of or against any approach.The author’s main conclusion is: “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach dependson the specifics ….”
Also, when the author says “both arguments have theirmerits,” the word “merits” is apositivejudgment, not anegativejudgment.
Therefore, the negative word “inappropriate” does not match what the 3rdparagraph says.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In the 1st paragraph, the author presents the idea that some authors advocate employing “massive action” and “ambitious commitments” in “achieving aims.” She also presents Kennedy’s ambitious plan to send a man to the moon as an example of such massive action and ambitious commitment in pursuit of a goal. Notice that the author does not express her own opinion on the value this approach; she simply describes an idea.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents some authors’ opinion about “achieving aims” (objectives). This opinion is that people should employ the “massive action” approach.

In the 2nd paragraph, the author presents a contrasting opinion on the matter of achieving goals, that of Stanford Professor BJ Fogg, who contends that “to start small” is “more effective and sustainable.” Notice that, regarding the approach mentioned in the 1st paragraph (massive action and ambitious commitments), the author says, “In contrast, Stanford Professor BJ Fogg contends that this approach can result in adverse outcomes, including the inevitable emotions of guilt and discouragement, if a person fails to accomplish an aggressive goal.”

Although, in the 2nd paragraph, the author has presented an alternative opinion on the matter of achieving goals, it’s important to notice that the author does not express her own opinion on the value of the “start small” approach or on whether the “start small” approach is better or worse than the massive action and ambitious commitments approach mentioned in the first paragraph; she simply presents the “start small” approach as advocated by BJ Fogg.

2nd paragraph summary: “In contrast” signals that the 2nd paragraph presents a second opinion that contrasts with the one presented in the first paragraph. This paragraph presents the opinion that people should employ the “start small” approach.

So, we see that, within the first two paragraphs, we don’t have a firm position or conclusion set forth by the author. If the passage had ended here, the author’s conclusion would have been a mystery.

In the final paragraph, the author presents her own conclusion, which is that neither of the two approaches is a clear winner and that “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics of the desired result, as well as the particular personality of the individual in question.” She goes on to say that “Some people are more likely to achieve superior outcomes by thinking big and committing themselves to bold plans, whereas many others benefit from the Tiny Habits approach.” We can clearly see that the author does not believe that one of the two approaches is superior; she believes that both have a time and a place.

3rd paragraph summary: The author doesn’t take a strong stance one way or the other. The author’s main conclusion is: “While both arguments have their merits, the most appropriate approach depends on the specifics.”

Passage summary: The author presents two contrasting approaches and does not take a strong position in favor of any approach.

More complex words require a bit more effort. question.text: It can be inferred that the author of the passage discusses “bipartisan political support” (see highlighted text) primarily in order to

answers.text: show that use of nuclear power could lead to an increase in jobs and the growth of trade
reinforce the point that it is attainable to bring about extensive utilization of nuclear power in a relatively short timeframe
support an argument that nuclear power generation has more economic benefits than fossil fuel power generation has
demonstrate that the use of nuclear power is typically unpopular across the political spectrum
provide an example of the progress that has been made in developing nuclear reactors

explanation_parts.text: In a Specific Purpose question (stem says “in order to” or “function of”), the question asks about a specific part of the passage.The purpose of that specific part of the passage is most often to support a point madeabovethat part of the passage.However, sometimes, the purpose could involve somethingbelow.

In S11, notice the additional point marker “moreover” at the start of the sentence with the highlight.This marker is a particularly helpful clue that tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion that is probably located more than one sentence above.

In this case, we need to look two sentences above, in S9, to find the conclusion supported by the highlight, “while it is true that there remain economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the United States,overcoming these hurdles rapidly is not only essential but also feasible.”
Simply put, S9 says that it is possible to rapidly overcome economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power.
Also, it is helpful to notice that a similar conclusion appears after the highlighted text, “a development suggesting thatthe future of nuclear power is promising.”

So, we can see that the specific purpose of mentioning the rare bipartisan political support for the production of new economical reactors is to provide support for two important conclusions, one at the start of the paragraph and one at the end of the paragraph.Both conclusions are positive predictions about the future of nuclear power.
(A) show that use of nuclear power could lead to an increase in jobs and the growth of trade
Trap: Nearby
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “an increase in jobs and the growth of trade” is discussed in S11, before the highlighted text.

However, notice what S11 says: “because of its potential to give rise to … export trade growth and job creation, the production of these reactors has recently received rare bipartisan political support.”
We can see that S11 means that “potential to give rise to … export trade growth and job creation” hascaused“political support.”
In other words, in the passage, “political support” is mentioned as aneffectof “potential” for “export trade growth and job creation.”

On the other hand, in this choice, the wording “show that” means that the highlighted “bipartisan political support”provesthat nuclear power could “lead to an increase in jobs and the growth of trade.”

So, the passage mentions “political support” as an effect of “an increase in jobs and the growth of trade,” whereas this choice mentions “political support” as proof of “an increase in jobs and the growth of trade.”
We can see that this choice does not accurately describe the purpose of the highlighted text.
YOUR CHOICE(B) reinforce the point that it is attainable to bring about extensive utilization of nuclear power in a relatively short timeframe

From our question analysis, we can see that the specific purpose of the highlighted text is to support the author’s point in S9, that it is possible to rapidly overcome economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power.
Note that “reinforce the point” has the same meaning as “support.”
We may quickly sense that this choice matches our question analysis, but let’s confirm that the exact wording matches the passage:

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passageattainablefeasible (S9)the future of nuclear power ispromising(S11)bring aboutovercoming these hurdles (S9)extensive utilizationwidespread use (S9)in a relatively short timeframerapidly (S9)
We can see that this choice accurately describes the purpose of the highlighted text.
(C) support an argument that nuclear power generation has more economic benefits than fossil fuel power generation has
Trap: Nearby
Trap: Wrong Comparison
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “economic benefits” are mentioned earlier in the sentence that includes the highlighted text “bipartisan political support.”

However, notice what S11 says.S11 says, “because of its potential to give rise to many additional economic benefits, … the production of these reactors has recently received rarebipartisan political support.”
We can see that S11 means that “potential to give rise to many economic benefits” hascaused“political support.”
In other words, in the passage, “political support” is mentioned as aneffectof “economic benefits,” not as “support for an argument” about “economic benefits.”

Also, notice that the passage does not say that nuclear power generation has “more economic benefits” than fossil fuel power generation.The passage does compare “cost” in S10, when it says, “they will likely generate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation.” However, comparing the “cost” is not the same as saying that one has “more economic benefits” than the other.
(D) demonstrate that the use of nuclear power is typically unpopular across the political spectrum
Trap: Distortion

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the word “rare” appears before the highlighted words “bipartisan political support.” So, what the passage says in a way matches with “is typically unpopular across the political spectrum.”

However, this choice is specifically about “the use of nuclear power.” On the other hand, the passage says that “bipartisan political support” is rare in general, not that political support for nuclear power is rare.We have no information about how politicians across the political spectrum feel about nuclear power in particular.
Furthermore, as we see from our Question Analysis, this choice misses the fact that the specific purpose of the highlighted text is to support the important point in S9.
(E) provide an example of the progress that has been made in developing nuclear reactors
Trap: Distortion
If you picked this choice, you may have been tempted by the fact that this choice mentions “developing,” and the passage says, “bipartisan political support, a development ….”
If we look carefully, though, we can see that this choice does not describe what the highlighted wording does.
After all, he “bipartisan political support” in the highlight is different from “the progress that has been made indeveloping nuclear reactors.”

In other words, as discussed in our Question Analysis, the fact that the reactors have “bipartisan political support” logicallysupportsthe points in S9 and at the end of S11 about thepromising futureof nuclear power in general, and is not anexampleof progress made in the specific activity ofdeveloping nuclear reactors.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It’s helpful to notice that the passage begins by presenting a common belief: S1 says, “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.” When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion, with the two contrasting views forming the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented by S1 involves two negative ideas about nuclear power plants: that they are “particularly hazardous” and that they are “uniquely expensive.”

S2 and S3 then add supporting details that explain why people have fears of nuclear power. S2 provides the example of nuclear disaster in Chernobyl, and S3 mentions fear of radiation exposure and the health problems that can result from it.

Notice that, in the 1st paragraph, the author presents a common opinion but doesn’t present an opinion of his own.

1st paragraph summary: Many people in the US believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive and associate nuclear power plants with scary images of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster and with radiation exposure.

In S4, notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part of S4:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s “trepidation and disgust” related to nuclear power.

In the X part of S4, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that there is “some risk” involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.” Indeed, details about both safety and environmental benefits follow.

As we might expect, the rest of the 2nd paragraph provides details to support the author’s main point in S4. S5 and S6 address safety concerns, with specific statistics about the safety benefits of nuclear power. S7 introduces environmental benefits. S8 cites an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

2nd paragraph summary: The author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that there are both safety-related and environmental benefits to overcoming negative perceptions of nuclear power. The author supports his point with statistics, facts, and examples.

At this point, we may have a sense that the primary purpose of this passage is to challenge many people’s concerns that nuclear power plants are “particularly hazardous” and “uniquely expensive.”

We have seen that, in the 2nd paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, in the 3rd paragraph, the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” So, in the 3rd paragraph, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

In S9, notice the contrast marker “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part. Here, the author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10 supports the point stated by S9, saying that recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

In S11, the additional point marker “moreover” is a useful clue indicating that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above — in this case, the point above in S9. The author concludes the passage by stating a point similar to S9’s point, “overcoming these hurdles rapidly is … feasible,” saying “the future of nuclear power is promising.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly. The author supports his point that a rapid shift to nuclear power is possible, by citing two specific developments: new economical reactors and political support.

In reviewing the passage as a whole, it is important to note that the author’s overall tone is argumentative, not just descriptive. Throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the author presents a strong opinion in support of nuclear power and provides evidence of multiple types of benefits of nuclear power: safety, environmental, and economic.

Passage summary: The author presents and then challenges the impression widely held in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and uniquely expensive.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: We don’t need to understand the technical word “terawatt-hour” in S5 to answer the questions correctly. However, from the context, we can infer that it is a unit of energy. If you are curious, “tera” is a unit prefix in the metric system that means multiplication by one trillion, or 1012. Another common use of “tera” is in “terabytes” (TB), in the context of memory capacity.

We also don’t need to understand the technical phrase “closed fuel cycle,” in S10.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice that a common belief is presented: “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.”

When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion. So, it is helpful to be aware of the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented in this sentence includes two negative ideas about nuclear power plants:

- “particularly hazardous”

- “uniquely expensive”

Simplification: Many people in the U.S. believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.

S2: Supports S1, with an example about why people believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous.

Simplification: These plants bring to mind scary images of the nuclear disaster in Chernobyl.

S3: Supports S1, with a detail about the fear of “radiation exposure.”

Simplification: Thinking about the dangers of radiation exposure provokes a fearful mindset.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s excessive fear of nuclear power.

In the X part, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that some risk is involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.”

Simplification: the author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that it is important to overcome this fear.

S5: Supports S4, with a statistic about the safety of nuclear power, as compared with the safety of other methods of energy production.

Simplification: Statistics show that nuclear power generation is safer than other common methods, particularly coal power.

S6: Supports S4, by addressing a concern about nuclear waste

Notice that “in reality” is an opinion/contrast marker. The author is saying that “concerns about nuclear waste” are not really a big deal, once you take into account certain facts.

Simplification: Nuclear waste isn’t a significant problem, because it can be safely stored and takes up a very small volume of space.

S7: Supports the point stated by S4 that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above.

S5 and S6 provided details about the safety benefits of nuclear power. Now, S7 introduces the environmental benefits.

Simplification: Also, nuclear power could have great environmental benefits, because it is the best way to prevent further global warming.

S8: Supports S4, with an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

Simplification: With a supportive political and regulatory situation, there can be a rapid shift to nuclear power, as we can see from the examples of France and Sweden.

S9: Here the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” In the second paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

Notice the contrast marker, “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Simplification: The author acknowledges that there are indeed economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US, but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10: Specifies an economic obstacle/problem and a potential solution:

Problem: “lack of standardization and repetition”

Solution: “Generation IV reactors that can be mass-produced”

Also, notice the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.” The final part of the sentence, “consequently, they will likely generate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation,” is a positive prediction about the future low relative cost of nuclear power.

Simplification: Recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

S11: Supports the main point in S9 that overcoming the economic obstacles rapidly is possible.

Notice the additional point marker “moreover,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above. We also see the phrase “additional economic benefits.” So, this sentence is expanding on the economic benefits discussed in S10 and playing a role similar to that of S10 in supporting S9.

Simplification: Since production of these new reactors may have many additional economic benefits, it has inspired significant political support, which is an encouraging sign for the future of nuclear power.

question.text: The author of the passage mentions Daniel Batson’s hypothesis most probably in order to

answers.text: show that Ricard is an expert in the field of psychology
argue that people are not motivated by the anticipation of personal benefits
illustrate a point about the kinds of evidence that Ricard uses in making his argument
support Ricard’s assertion that friends and family are often recipients of instinctive kindness from others
suggest that Ricard is correct that anonymous altruism can be cultivated through meditation

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

In a Specific Purpose question (stem says “in order to” or “function of”), the question asks about a specific part of the passage.The purpose of that specific part of the passage is most often to support a point madeabovethat part of the passage.However, sometimes, the purpose could involve somethingbelow.
Here, since the stem asks about “Daniel Batson’s hypothesis,” our first step is to find these keywords, which we find in S11.

Our next step is to read what comes before “Daniel Batson’s … hypothesis” in the sentence because, often, the purpose of the text mentioned in the stem is to support something earlier in the same sentence.
S11 says, “Ricard enlists the support of cognitive science; for instance, he cites psychologist Daniel Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.”
Since “for instance” means “for example,” we can see that the passage mentions “Daniel Batson’s … hypothesis” to provide an example of the “cognitive science” that Ricard “enlists” for support.
(A) show that Ricard is an expert in the field of psychology
Trap: Distortion

S11 doesn’t show that Ricard is an “expert” in psychology.S11 says that Ricard “cites psychologist Daniel Batson’s … hypothesis.” However, the purpose of mentioning that he cites that hypothesis it not to show that he is an expert in psychology.
Notice that the passage does not argue that Ricard is an expert in psychology.
(B) argue that people are not motivated by the anticipation of personal benefits
Trap: Mix-Up

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S11 says that Batson’s hypothesis is that “benefits to self are not the ultimate goal of helping.” The meaning of this hypothesis is similar to what this choice says, “people are not motivated by the anticipation of personal benefits.”

However, this choice mixes up the author’s opinion and Batson’s opinion.It’s important to remember that, when this choice says that the passage mentions Daniel Batson’s hypothesis in order to “argue that X,” the X would have to represent theauthor’sopinion in order for the choice to be correct.
YOUR CHOICE(C) illustrate a point about the kinds of evidence that Ricard uses in making his argument
As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the passage mentions “Daniel Batson’s … hypothesis” to provide an example of the “cognitive science” that Ricard “enlists” for support.
Notice that, when Ricard “enlists the support of cognitive science,” Ricard is using evidence from cognitive science “in making his argument.”

Thus, “illustrate a point about the kinds of evidence that Ricard uses in making his argument” means essentially what we said in our Question Analysis, “provide an example of the “cognitive science” that Ricard ‘enlists’ for support.”
So, this choice matches the specific purpose that we identified.
(D) support Ricard’s assertion that friends and family are often recipients of instinctive kindness from others
Trap: Nearby
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S10 mentions “virtually instinctive kindness typically offered to friends and family.”

However, we must focus on answering the question about the specific purpose of mentioning Daniel Batson’s hypothesis.It’s important to notice that S11 states a new point that is separate from what’s said in S10.So, the purpose of mentioning Daniel Batson’s hypothesis is not to support the point in S10 about friends and family.
(E) suggest that Ricard is correct that anonymous altruism can be cultivated through meditation
Trap: Nearby

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S10 says that “Ricard demonstrates using examples that it (anonymous altruism) can be substantially cultivated through specific practices, such as meditating.”

However, we must focus on answering the question about the specific purpose of mentioning Daniel Batson’s hypothesis.It’s important to notice that S11 states a new point that is separate from what’s said in S10.So, the purpose of mentioning Daniel Batson’s hypothesis is not to support Ricard’s point in S10 that anonymous altruism can be cultivated through meditation.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting some information about “people’s benevolence”; S1 says that people’s benevolence both is directed toward their kin and extends to those outside their families.

This information presented by S1 turns out to be background information; the first paragraph makes its point in S2 and S3.

S2 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals both that it’s likely that an important point is coming and that a contrast is being made. What are the two things that are contrasted? S1 has introduced one type of generous behavior, which is often reciprocated, and now, S2 introduces a different type of generous behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is a “perplexing phenomenon to scientists.” Anonymous altruistic behavior turns out to be the main topic of the passage.

Having brought up “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2, the first paragraph sets the stage for the rest of the passage with S3. S3 has a rhetorical question marker(question mark). Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question begins with “What would motivate individuals to …,” which means, essentially, “Why?” When the author asks “Why?” we have a causation marker, indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then discussed, in the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. We must pay close attention whenever explanations are discussed, because explanations tend to be important parts of passages.

S3 also defines “anonymous altruistic behavior, saying that it is “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” In defining “anonymous altruistic behavior,” S3 gives the reader a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the generous behavior described in S1, which is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

1st paragraph summary: Both scientists and other scholars are interested in explaining anonymous altruistic behavior. Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible answer the rhetorical question in S3. The possible answer, or explanation, is that “there are neural bases for such acts.” The look above marker in S4, “SUCH acts,” refers to the “anonymous deeds” in S3. So, this marker helps us understand the relationship between S4 and S3.

Then, in S5, S6, and S7, the 2nd paragraph provides some details of how “researchers” investigated this possible explanation and what they found. These details are rather involved. So, our goal in reading these sentences is not necessarily to totally understand or seek to memorize every one of these details but rather to understand in general how the study described worked.

In S8, we see the opinion marker, “contended.” Here, the main conclusion of the researchers is presented: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author’s tone in the 2nd paragraph is descriptive, and the author does not provide her own opinion.

2nd paragraph summary: The author presents one possible explanation for anonymous altruistic behavior: having studied brain activity, researchers concluded that hormones that cause positive sensations, i.e., biological factors, play an important role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

The 3rd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

In this case, by using “while X, Y” in S9, the author connects the 2nd paragraph to the 3rd paragraph; in the “while X” part of S9, the author concedes that the argument presented in the 2nd paragraph “may … lead to some useful insights,” and then, in the Y part of S9, the author presents the topic of the 3rd paragraph, the “more compelling perspective” of Matthieu Ricard. (Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.)

S10 and S11 provide details about Ricard’s perspective and the types of evidence he uses to support his points.

Finally, in S12, the author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.” Notice that, in judging Ricard’s conclusion “persuasive,” the author is once again stating an opinion of her own.

Also, we can see that there is clear contrast because Ricard’s conclusion and that of the researchers discussed in the second paragraph. S12 says that Ricard’s “persuasive case” is that serving others can be “genuinely selfless,” whereas S8 says that the researchers discussed in the second paragraph contend that such behavior can be “seemingly selfless.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author presents a second explanation that she believes is more compelling than the “biological argument” in the 2nd paragraph: Ricard’s perspective is that the motivation for serving others can be genuinely selfless and that this behavior can be cultivated through specific practices.

Passage summary: The author poses a question about why people engage in anonymous altruistic behavior, presents a biological explanation based on a brain imaging study, and then presents a second explanation that the author prefers.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.
