Note: To answer the questions correctly, we don’t need to know the technical vocabulary: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), mesolimbic pathway, neurotransmitter, dopamine, hypothalamus, oxytocin, hormones, empathy-altruism hypothesis. We can understand the meaning of each sentence from the surrounding language and the definitions that are provided in the passage. (For example, the passage defines the mesolimbic pathway: “the mesolimbic pathway, the reward center of the brain that is responsible for releasing the neurotransmitter associated with pleasure.”)
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice causation marker “because.”

Simplification: People are often kind to not only relatives but also others, because this kindness is often returned.

S2: Notice the contrast marker “however.” This sentence introduces a different type of kind behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is “perplexing to scientists,” meaning that it is harder to explain.

Note: we may not be sure what the meaning of “anonymous altruistic behavior” is at first, but we are basically given a definition in the next sentence. In general, we can look to the surrounding context if we don’t understand something.

Simplification: A different type of kind behavior, anonymous altruistic behavior, has been puzzling for scientists to explain and an important topic for other scholars.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

The question posed, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …?” is asking about the reason/cause/explanation for these anonymous deeds. So, we can expect the rest of the passage to discuss possible explanations.

This sentence also helps the reader to understand the full meaning of S2. It defines what is meant by the “anonymous altruistic behavior” introduced in S2: “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” It also gives us a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the kind behavior described in S1 that is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

Simplification: Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

S4: Notice the look above marker “SUCH acts.” We must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to. Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” As we noted in our analysis of S3, these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible explanation to answer the rhetorical question in S3.

Note: if we don’t know what “neural” means, we can see from S5 that it’s related to brain activity: “researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brain.”

Simplification: A certain study sought to find an explanation, related to brain activity, for anonymous altruistic behavior.

S5: Provides details about the design of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: Using fMRI, the researchers analyzed the subjects’ brain activity while the subjects decided whether to keep money or make a donation.

S6: Provides details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: The reward center of the brain that releases dopamine, which is associated with pleasure, became active when a donation was made.

S7: Adds more details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” S7 is very similar to S6, but discusses a different brain region and hormone.

Simplification: The region of the brain that produces oxytocin, which is associated with cooperation and trust, also became active when a donation was made.

S8: Notice the opinion marker “contended.” Also, notice that the word “so” connects a premise and a conclusion, in the structure “Premise, so Conclusion”

This sentence presents what the researchers have concluded from the findings of their study presented in the previous sentences.

Simplification: Since dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations, the scientists argue that biological factors play a role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

S9: Introduces a “compelling,” i.e., persuasive, alternative to the “biological argument” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Notice that the wording “THIS biological argument” is a look above marker and refers to the biological argument directly above in S8: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument.

Simplification: There is another perspective, that of Matthieu Ricard, which is more compelling than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

S10: Provides details about Ricard’s perspective.

Notice that “but” is a contrast marker in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard’s perspective is that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally, but it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.

S11: Discusses a type of evidence that Ricard uses, cognitive science research.

Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.

S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.”

Notice that the author repeats her positive judgment of Ricard’s perspective: S9 says that it’s “compelling,” and S12 says that it’s “persuasive.”

Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be genuinely selfless.” It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.

question.text: The author of the passage mentions name recognition (see highlighted text) most probably in order to

answers.text: explain why lack of media coverage causes candidates to struggle to win elections
suggest a way in which reporters could improve their coverage of candidates in elections
demonstrate that certain candidates would have had a positive impact on the lives of the electorate
compare the importance of different drivers of election outcomes
provide evidence in support of the assertion that lack of media coverage leaves candidates almost invisible to voters

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

Since the purpose of something mentioned by an author in a Specific Purpose question is often to support an earlier part of the same sentence, our first move in answering this question is to read the entire sentence in which “name recognition” appears.

We see that the sentence in which “name recognition” appears, S5, uses the causation marker “because” to present a cause-and-effect relationship: “such candidates,” meaning the candidates mentioned in S4, who are “left almost invisible … because of lack of media coverage,” “struggle to win electionsbecauseone of the biggest drivers of election outcomes is name recognition.”
Thus, we can see that the author mentions “name recognition” to explain why candidates who are not covered by the media struggle to win elections.
YOUR CHOICE(A) explain why lack of media coverage causes candidates to struggle to win elections
As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the author mentions “name recognition” to explain why candidates who are not covered by the media struggle to win elections.
So, this choice perfectly answers the question.
(B) suggest a way in which reporters could improve their coverage of candidates in elections
The author does not suggest any ways in which “reporters could improve their coverage of candidates in elections.”
So, this choice describes something the author could have done but does not do in this passage.
(C) demonstrate that certain candidates would have had a positive impact on the lives of the electorate
We have to be careful not to choose a choice like this one just because it matches wording that appears before the thing mentioned by the author that the question is about.

The fact that certain candidates’ “being elected would have had a positive impact” is additional information mentioned in the middle of the author’s explanation of why “such candidates struggle to win elections.”

As we discussed in our Question Analysis, “because one of the biggest drivers of election outcomes is name recognition” explains why these candidates struggle to win elections, not why they would have had a positive impact.
(D) compare the importance of different drivers of election outcomes

The passage does say that “name recognition” is “one of the biggest drivers of election outcomes,” and thus the passage does in a way compare “name recognition” with other drivers of election outcomes.
However, the correct answer to a Specific Purpose question must describe the purpose the thing mentioned by the authorserves in the passage.

So, notice that, in the passage, the author does not mention “name recognition” for the purpose of comparing “name recognition” with other drivers of election outcomes.Rather, as we discussed in our Question Analysis, the author mentions “name recognition” to explain why candidates who are not covered by the media struggle to win elections.
(E) provide evidence in support of the assertion that lack of media coverage leaves candidates almost invisible to voters

It’s true that the purpose of a thing mentioned by the author is often to support an assertion stated by the preceding sentence.However, to correctly answer a Specific Purpose question, we have to read for understanding rather than just go with what is often the case.

In this particular case, as we discussed in our Question Analysis, the purpose of mentioning “name recognition” is not to support an assertion stated by the preceding sentence.It’s to explain something in the same sentence, which is why “such candidates struggle to win elections.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In the 1st paragraph, the author introduces two sides of a situation. The two sides of the situation are that the freedom of the press is a “cornerstone,” meaning “important feature,” of American democracy and that the freedom of the press allows the media to influence outcomes of elections.

Notice that, while the author does not directly state an opinion in this paragraph, the tone clues “cornerstone” and “provide voters with information they need” indicate that the author believes that the freedom of the press is important and beneficial.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st.paragraph introduces the topic that the passage goes on to discuss, which is the two sides of the freedom of the press.

The 2nd paragraph begins with the look above marker “SUCH influence,” and the 2nd paragraph presents an example of how the media influences elections, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph. This way is that the media chooses what it conveys, i.e., covers.

2nd paragraph summary: The 2nd paragraph presents an example of how the media influences elections, as mentioned in the 1st paragraph.

The 3rd paragraph further discusses the issue of the media influencing elections by bringing up another aspect of this influence, which is that both social media platforms and major media outlets present news in a biased way. The social media platforms present news in a biased way to attract users, and the major media outlets present news in a biased way to attract the interest of as large an audience as possible.

This paragraph also shows a negative result of the biased way in which major media outlets present news: voters get funneled “to either the far right or the far left of the political spectrum.”

Notice that, so far, the author has not presented an opinion about all this media influence. He’s simply describing it, though clearly presenting a somewhat negative picture.

3rd paragraph summary: The 3rd paragraph discusses the fact that, to achieve goals, social media platforms and major media outlets present news in a biased way.

So far, the author has described the situation involving the media influencing the outcomes of elections and the electorate without presenting any opinions about the situation, but now in the 4th paragraph, the author presents some opinions

The first opinion is that of “some people,” who have “questioned whether the virtually complete freedom of the press enjoyed by the American media makes sense.” Notice that this opinion is not the author’s opinion, though the author does follow up this opinion by expressing an opinion of his own, which is that “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.”

Then, the author wraps up the passage with two important points.

The first important point is signaled by the contrast marker “however,” since contrasts are generally connected to important points, and this point is that “the free press is integral to the functioning of a democratic society.” We can see that with this sentence, the author is once again bringing up the other side of the situation that was brought up in the 1st paragraph, which is that freedom of the press is important.

Then the most important point of the passage is introduced by the conclusion marker “therefore.” Why is this last sentence the most important point? Because, in this sentence, the author finally states a strong opinion about the situation involving the freedom of the press by using the strong language “a better question to ask” and then saying that what that better question is, which is “what can be done within the context of freedom of the press to reduce the distortion of electoral results by the media.”

We can see that the author believes that the freedom of the press is important and has therefore concluded that the thing to do is to figure out what to do about media influence while leaving the freedom of the press in place.

4th paragraph summary: The 4th paragraph discusses some considerations related to addressing the issue of the distorted influence of the media on electoral outcomes and then presents a final conclusion of the author’s about what how this issue should be addressed.

Passage summary: The passage introduces a situation that has two sides, the importance of the freedom of the press and the influence that the media has on the outcomes of elections, illustrates the issue of the influence of the media on the outcomes of election with examples, and wraps up by discussing how the issue might best be addressed.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Introduces the first side of the situation.

The first side of the situation is that freedom of the press is a “cornerstone,” meaning “important feature,” of American democracy.

Simplification: The freedom of the press is important for American democracy.

S2: Introduces the second side of the situation. 

The second side of the situation is that the media exploits freedom of the press to influence election outcomes, not always in a good way. 

By presenting these two sides of a situation, this sentence foreshadows that the passage will discuss these two sides of this situation.

Simplification: The media uses freedom of the press to influence the outcomes of elections.

S3: Uses the look above marker “SUCH influence” to introduce one example of how the media influences elections as introduced in S2, by choosing what to convey.

Simplification: One way in which the media influences elections is by choosing what to talk about.

S4: Provides detail on the fact introduced by S3 that the media can choose what to convey.

Simplification: Reporters can choose what to cover, so sometimes candidates don’t get coverage and are “left almost invisible to voters.”

S5: Uses the look above marker “SUCH candidates” to describe what happens when the candidates mentioned in the previous sentence are “left almost invisible to voters.”

Simplification: The candidates “left almost invisible to voters” struggle to win elections even when they would have done good things, because voters don’t recognize the candidates’ names.

S6: Uses the additional point marker “in addition” to introduce another example of how the media influences elections, the use of “sophisticated algorithms” by social media platforms.

Simplification: Many people access news by using social media platforms, which use algorithms to decide what news to show users.

S7: Expands on the discussion of social media “algorithms” begun by S6 by presenting the results of the use of algorithms by social media.

Notice the strong language, “it would be hard to argue ….” This strong language is a clue indicating that the author is stating an important point in this sentence.

Also, notice that “it would be hard to argue that that user is getting unbiased political news,” is sort of a double negative that means, essentially, “it can be argued that that user is getting biased political news.”

Simplification: Social media algorithms show users stories meant to capture their attention, and as a result, users get biased political news.

S8: Introduces another reason why media influence elections: major media outlets have the “goal” of attracting the interest of as large an audience as possible.

S9: Uses the look above marker “THIS aim” to continue to discuss the “goal” mentioned in S8.

Notice that this sentence uses the causation marker “since” to show a cause and effect. The cause is “voters have the tendency to seek out news that does not challenge what they believe is true,” and the effect is that major media outlets can achieve their “aim” or “goal” by “reflecting biases.” Sentences that mention causes and effects are usually closely connected to the primary purpose of the passage, and we can see that this sentence is mentioning “biases” as S7 did. So, bias in media appears to be a key theme of this passage.

Simplification: Since people seek out news they agree with, major media outlets can achieve their goal of attracting a large audience by being biased.

S10: Uses the look above marker “THIS ‘selective exposure’” to continue the discussion of major media outlets presenting biased news to attract a large audience.

“Funnels many voters to either the far right or the far left” means, basically, “directs many voters to either the far right or the far left.” So, this sentence is showing how media outlets affect elections by pushing voters to the right or left.

Also, notice that “not-so-objective” is another way of saying “biased.” So, this sentence is once again discussing media bias.

Simplification: There are many biased news outlets that push voters to the far right or far left.

S11: Uses the causation marker “consequently” to present a result of what the passage has said in the preceding paragraphs: “Consequently, some people have questioned whether … freedom of the press … makes sense.”

Notice that this sentence presents an opinion of the author’s: “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.” Directly stated opinions of the author are good clues about the primary purpose of the passage.

Simplification: Because of the effects of media’s biased coverage, some people question whether freedom of the press makes sense, and this questioning is reasonable since “the fact that the free press has a distorted influence on electoral results is a significant issue.”

S12: Uses the additional point marker “furthermore” to present more support for the author’s opinion “this questioning is not completely unwarranted.”

Simplification: Another reason why “this questioning is not completely unwarranted” is that it would be hard to do something about the not so good influence of the media on elections.

S13: Uses the contrast marker “however” to present information that contrasts with what has just been said.

“Integral” means “essential” or “necessary.” So, since something “essential” is important, this sentence is a strong statement about freedom of the press.

Simplification: A fact that contrasts with the facts that support the questioning of the freedom of the press is the fact that freedom of the press is necessary.

S14: Uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to state a final opinion of the author’s.

When the last sentence of the passage states an opinion of the author’s, that opinion is usually either the main point of the passage or very closely connected to the main point and primary purpose of the passage. So, this sentence will be a useful clue when it comes time to answer a primary purpose question about this passage.

Simplification: Therefore, rather than question the freedom of the press, people should focus on reducing media “distortion” or “bias” without changing the freedom of the press itself.

question.text: The author’s attitude toward freedom of the press can best be described as

answers.text: strongly condemning
completely neutral
somewhat cautious
overwhelmingly enthusiastic
sharply critical

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
To determine the author’s attitude toward freedom of the press, we can consider tone clues and other clues in the passage.

The first tone clues we see are the words “guaranteed” and “cornerstone” in the 1stparagraph.Something that’s “guaranteed” by the government would likely be a positive thing, and likewise, a “cornerstone of democracy” would be a positive thing, because democracy is generally considered positive.

Also, the 1stparagraph describes the freedom of the press as allowing the media “to openly provide voters with the information theyneed.” Providing people with what they “need” seems positive as well.However, S2 indicates that the author is not completely positive towards freedom of the press: the author is concerned with how "the media has also exploited it to influence the outcomes of elections."

The 2ndand 3rdparagraphs have an overall negative tone, but notice that those paragraphs are not really about “freedom of the press.” They are about the “influence” of the media.So, the negative tone of those paragraphs doesn’t really indicate the author’s attitude toward freedom of the press itself.

In the 4thparagraph, the author says that “some people have questioned whether … freedom of the press … makes sense,” but that question is not the author’s own question, and the author goes on to say that freedom of the press is “integral,” meaning “essential.”
So, it appears that, overall, the author has a somewhat positive attitude toward the freedom of the press, with some reservations about how it is used.
(A) strongly condemning
In the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs, the author takes a somewhat negative tone that could be considered “condemning.”
However, those paragraphs are not about the freedom of the press itself, but rather about ways in which the media uses it to influence elections.
So, the author’s attitude toward freedom of the press is less negative than “condemning.”
(B) completely neutral
As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the author’s attitude toward freedom of the press is somewhat positive rather than “neutral.”
YOUR CHOICE(C) somewhat cautious

As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the author’s attitude toward the freedom of the press as a concept is positive overall.At the same time, the author does discuss issues related to how the freedom of the press is used by the media and states the opinion that questioning the freedom of the press is “not completely unwarranted.”
So, the author’s attitude toward freedom of the press could be described as “somewhat cautious.”
(D) overwhelmingly enthusiastic

While the author is positive towards the freedom of the press, most of the passage is spent discussing negative aspects of how the media uses that freedom.We can see that the author takes issue with how the freedom of the press is applied.
So, the author’s attitude toward freedom of the press is less positive than “overwhelmingly enthusastic.”
(E) sharply critical

In the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs, the author takes a somewhat negative tone that could be considered “critical,” though “sharply critical” is a strong term that goes beyond the generally descriptive nature of those paragraphs.In addition, those paragraphs are about how the freedom of the press is used, not freedom of the press itself.
So, the author’s attitude toward freedom of the press is less negative than “sharply critical.”

question.text: Which of the following most accurately describes the function of the highlighted sentence?

answers.text: It casts doubt on an assertion made in the next sentence.
It presents a theory to explain a difference discussed in the passage.
It supports an assertion made in the previous sentence.
It states a claim that is supported by the sentences that follow.
It presents examples to illustrate the effects of a phenomenon mentioned earlier in the passage.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To get a sense of the function of the highlighted sentence, we can first read the sentence itself.We see that the highlighted sentence presents a comparison between the situation “in 1859” and the situation “now.” In other words, the highlighted sentence simply presents some facts.
So, what is the purpose of presenting those facts? To find out, we can read the surrounding sentences for context, beginning with the sentence directly preceding the highlighted sentence.

The sentence directly preceding the highlighted sentence, S3, states an assertion of the author’s, which is basically that a fact about what occurred in 1859 does not mean that “today’s communication systems are safe from the effects of CMEs.” Reading S3 and the highlighted sentence together, we can see that the comparison expressed by the highlighted sentence supports the assertion made in S3 by showing how communications systems of 1859 and communications systems of today differ.

Notice also that the sentence that follows the highlighted sentence, S5, begins with the conclusion marker “therefore.” Reading the highlighted sentence along with S5, we see that the highlighted sentence also supports S5.After all, the fact that people live in a “globally wired society” explains why “a Carrington Event-level storm today could cause a devastating “internet apocalypse.”
So, the function of the highlighted sentence is to support the sentence before it and support the sentence after it.
(A) It casts doubt on an assertion made in the next sentence.
As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the highlighted sentencesupportsthe next sentence.
So, this choice describes the opposite of the function of the highlighted sentence.
(B) It presents a theory to explain a difference discussed in the passage.
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the highlighted sentence mentions a “difference” between “electrical infrastructure” of the past and today’s “globally wired society.”
However, the highlighted sentence does not present a “theory.” It presents facts only.

Notice that, to avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful to determine exactly what the highlighted sentence does rather than go with a vague impression of what it does, because it would be easy to get the impression that the highlighted sentence presents a theory about why today’s communication systems are not safe from CMEs even though, in reality, it presents only facts.
YOUR CHOICE(C) It supports an assertion made in the previous sentence.
As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the facts presented by the highlighted sentence support the author’s assertion stated by the previous sentence, S3.
So, this choice accurately describes the function of the highlighted sentence.
(D) It states a claim that is supported by the sentences that follow.
We can evaluate this choice by simply scanning the sentences that follow the highlighted sentence to see whether they support the highlighted sentence as this choice says they do.
S5 does not support the highlighted sentence.It uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to present a conclusion.
Also, none of the other sentences that follow the highlighted sentence support what the highlighted sentence says about past and present infrastructure.
So, we can see that the highlighted sentence does not state “a claim that is supported by the sentences that follow.”
(E) It presents examples to illustrate the effects of a phenomenon mentioned earlier in the passage.
The facts that “electrical infrastructure was primitive” and that “people now live in a globally wired society” are not examples ofeffectsof CMEs.
So, we can eliminate this choice.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage begins by introducing the concept of a coronal mass ejection (CME), and first thing that jumps out at us as we’re reading is that S1 says that “in most cases” a CME “never reaches Earth BUT.” Contrasts words such as “but” normally introduce important points. So, the important point to notice here is “but, when a CME does reach Earth, it induces a flurry of strong magnetic and electrical activity.”

Then, the passage presents an example of a CME, the CME that caused “the largest geomagnetic storm ever recorded” during the Carrington Event in 1859.

So far, the passage has been descriptive, simply presenting information about CMEs and the example of the Carrington Event.

Then, in S3, the contrast word “however” and the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating …” indicate that S3 is stating an important point. In fact, with S3, the author begins to present his main point.

So, what is the point the author is making?

We can see that, in saying, “the fact … CANNOT be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems ARE safe from the effects of CMEs,” the author is implying that today’s communication systems are NOT safe from CMEs.

Then, S4 begins to explain why today’s communication systems are not safe from CMEs – the systems are not “primitive”; rather society is now “highly dependent upon electrical signals” – and S5 uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to present a conclusion about the current situation, “a Carrington Event-level storm today could cause a devastating ‘internet apocalypse.’”

Then the rest of the passage simply presents another example to support the point that today’s communications systems are not safe from CMEs, the example of the “Miyake event” of 775. The implications of this part of the passage are that storms even bigger than the Carrington Event are possible and could cause “almost … complete destruction of communication infrastructure” and “near total loss of the date stored on computer hard drives.”

Passage summary: The passage introduces the concept of a CME and then proceeds to make the argument that today’s communication infrastructure is not safe from CMEs by saying that today’s communication infrastructure is not primitive like past electrical infrastructure and by describing what would occur if storms like ones that occurred in the past were to occur today.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

We don’t need to understand the technical words “magnetosphere” and “geomagnetic.” We can understand the passage and correctly answer every question without knowing exactly what these words mean.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: Introduces the concept of a coronal mass ejection (CME) and the effect a CME can have on Earth.

Simplification: A coronal mass ejection (CME) that reaches Earth causes strong magnetic and electrical activity

S2: Introduces an example of storm caused by a CME, the Carrington Event in 1859.

Notice that this sentence says that the geomagnetic storm “induced,” meaning “caused,” by the Carrington Event was “the largest geomagnetic storm ever recorded.” An extreme fact such as this one will likely play an important role in the passage. After all, it’s almost certain that the author has mentioned such an extreme fact for the purpose of making a point.

S3: There are two signs that this sentence states an important point.

The first is the contrast marker “however,” since contrasts are often connected to important points of a passage.

The second is the strong language “the fact … cannot be taken as indicating ….”

We can see that, in this sentence, the author is making a strong assertion that will likely be supported and discussed further as the passage progresses.

Simplification: The fact that the 1859 CME didn’t cause much trouble doesn’t mean that present communications systems are safe from CMEs.

S4: Uses the comparison marker “whereas” to express a comparison.

Comparisons are usually important parts of passages, and this comparison is indeed important, because the fact that, unlike in 1859, people now live in a “globally wired society” both explains why “today’s communications systems” are not safe from the effects of CMEs, as implied by S3, and supports S5, as we will see.

S5: Uses the conclusion marker “therefore” to present the consequence of what S4 says.

Simplification: Because, as S4 says, people live in a “globally wired society,” if an event like the Carrington Event were to occur today, it would cause huge communication problems.

S6: Uses the additional point marker “furthermore” to provide addition support for the author’s strong assertion in S3 that the fact that the 1859 CME didn’t cause much trouble doesn’t mean that present communications systems are safe from a large CME.

The implication of S6 is that, since such a large storm occurred in 775, a similar one could occur today.

Simplification: The Carrington event isn’t even the biggest “geomagnetic storm” ever. A much bigger one occurred in 775.

SB: Continues to support the strong assertion in S3 by saying that a storm like the one that occurred in 775 would cause “incalculably great” damage.

S9: Continues to support the strong assertion in S3 by providing more detail on the damage that would be caused by a storm like that one that occurred in 775.

question.text: The author of the passage mentions magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong as those produced in the Carrington Event most probably in order to

answers.text: explain why the Carrington Event caused only minor disruptions to communications systems
support the assertion that more should be done to protect communication infrastructure from magnetic and electrical fields
explain why the Carrington event is not classified as a “Miyake event”
support the claim that the fact that the 1859 CME caused only minor disruptions cannot be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems are safe from CMEs
show that the CME of 775 was the largest CME ever to reach Earth

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To determine the purpose of mentioning “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong as those produced in the Carrington Event,” we first find “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong as those produced in the Carrington Event” in the passage and read the entire sentence in which that phrase is located.

We see that that sentence, S6, begins with the additional point marker “furthermore,” which indicates that the purpose of S6 is to provide support for a statement made by a sentence more than one sentence above.
So, what does S6 provide support for?
Scanning the sentences above S6, we see that S6 does not support the conclusion stated by S5 or the facts stated by S4.

However, S6 does support the author’s assertion stated by S3.After all, the fact that a “megastorm” much bigger than the Carrington event can occur supports the assertion stated by S3, which is basically that what occurred in 1859 does not indicate that “today’s communication systems are safe from the effects of CMEs.” In other words, the fact that a “megastorm” could occur indicates that, as S3 implies, today’s communications systems arenotsafe from CMEs.
So, the purpose of mentioning “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong as those produced in the Carrington Event” is basically to support the assertion stated by S3.
(A) explain why the Carrington Event caused only minor disruptions to communications systems

The fact that the megastorm of 775 involved “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong as those produced in the Carrington Event” does not “explain why the Carrington Event caused only minor disruptions.”
The passage implies that what explains why the Carrington event caused only minor disruptions it that communications systems were “primitive” in 1859.
(B) support the assertion that more should be done to protect communication infrastructure from magnetic and electrical fields

Notice that the passage never makes the assertion that “more should be done.” So, the purpose of mentioning “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong …” is not to support that assertion.
(C) explain why the Carrington event is not classified as a “Miyake event”

The fact that the megastorm of 775 involved “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong as those produced in the Carrington Event” could help to explain why that megastorm is classified as a “Miyake event” and the Carrington Event is not so classified.

However, to correctly answer this Specific Purpose question, we need to find a choice that describeswhy the author mentions“magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong …,” and in our question analysis, we saw that the author mentions that information for the purpose of supporting the assertion stated by S3, not to “explain why the Carrington event is not classified as a ‘Miyake event.’”
YOUR CHOICE(D) support the claim that the fact that the 1859 CME caused only minor disruptions cannot be taken as indicating that today’s communication systems are safe from CMEs

As we discussed in our Question Analysis, by considering the additional point marker “furthermore” along with what S6 and S3 say, we can see that the purpose of mentioning “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong …” is to support the author’s assertion stated by S3.
So, this choice accurately describes why the author mentions “magnetic and electrical fields up to 100 times as strong ….”
(E) show that the CME of 775 was the largest CME ever to reach Earth
While it’s possible that the CME of 775 was “the largest CME ever to reach Earth,” the passage does not say that it was.
So, in saying, “the largest,” this choice goes beyond what the passage says.

question.text: Which of the following most accurately describes the function of the highlighted sentence?

answers.text: To demonstrate that the rate of chronic disease varied throughout the twentieth century
To provide details that serve to counter a possible reason for an increase in retirement rates
To cite data that gives credence to an assertion made by historians other than Costa
To provide evidence to refute an assertion about reduced demand for older workers
To make note of the most important factor in explaining the decrease in the LFPRE

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

In a Specific Purpose question (stem says “in order to” or “function of”), the question asks about a specific part of the passage.The purpose of that specific part of the passage is most often to support a point madeabovethat part of the passage.However, sometimes, the purpose could involve somethingbelow.

The question asks about the function of S6, so a good place to start re-reading is one sentence before the highlight, S5.S5 says that Costa debunks alternative explanations for the decrease in LFPRE, and provides two examples, “health issues” and “reduced demand.” There is a reasonable chance that the next sentence, S6, will expand on this topic of “debunking.”

Indeed, the phrase “perhaps most notably” at the beginning of S6 indicates that S6 presents anotable exampleof what was discussed in S5, Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decline in the LFPRE.

Specifically, in S6, “Costa’s observation that, among older workers, the rate of chronic disease was much higher during the first half of the twentieth century than since that period implies that the LFPRE decreased despite improving health,” means that Costa debunked the “health issues” explanation for “the decrease in the LFPRE.”
To see why, we can review our simplification of S6 from our Sentence Analysis.

Simplification of S6: One notable example of Costa’s debunking is her observation that, in older people, “the rate of chronic disease was higher” when the LFPRE was higher “during the first half of the twentieth century.” In other words, she observed that, when older people had more “health issues,” more older people worked.Thus, she debunked the explanation offered by the “other historians” in S5 that “health issues” caused the LFPRE to decrease from a higher level “during the first half of the twentieth century” to a lower level “since that period.”
(A) To demonstrate that the rate of chronic disease varied throughout the twentieth century
Trap: Matches but Incorrect
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S6 does mention that “the rate of chronic disease was much higher during the first half of the twentieth century than since that period.”

However, demonstrating this fact about chronic disease is not thepurposeof S6.In other words, the author did not include S6 in the passage to make a point about rates of chronic disease.We can see that the purpose of S6 is to discuss “Costa’s observation” and that this fact about chronic disease is just a part of “Costa’s observation.”

We must keep in mind that the correct answer to a specific purpose question will show the connection between the referenced text and other language nearby in the passage, as opposed to just describing content of the referenced text.
YOUR CHOICE(B) To provide details that serve to counter a possible reason for an increase in retirement rates
In our Question Analysis, we saw that the highlighted sentence, S6, provides details that support the “debunking” of the “health issues” explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE.
Notice that “a possible reason” in this choice matches the “health issues” explanation.After all, a “reason” for the decrease in the LFPRE is an explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE.
So, “to provide details that serve tocountera possible reason” in this choice matches with “provides details that support the “debunking” of the “health issues” explanation.
Finally, is “reason foran increase in retirement rates” correct?

The wording used in the passage, in S2, S3, and S6, is “decrease in the LFPRE.” So, we must realize that the wording in this choice, “increase in retirement rates,” isreversedbut has the same meaning.To understand why, note that LFPRE is defined in S2 as “the percentage of the elderly still working.” Also note that, mathematically, the percentage still working + the percentage that are retired must add to 100 percent.So, if the percentage still working, which is the LFPRE, decreases, the percentage retired must increase.Note that “rate” has the same meaning as “percentage.”

So, “To provide details that serve to counter a possible reason for an increase in retirement rates” in this choice matches what the highlighted sentence, S6, does, which is provide details that support the “debunking” of the “health issues” explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE.
(C) To cite data that gives credence to an assertion made by historians other than Costa
Trap: Opposite

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S6 does indeed “cite data” and is related to “an assertion made by historians other than Costa.” That assertion is the “health issues” explanation introduced in S5.
However, note that “gives credence to” means “supports.” We must make sure to notice the direction of what the choice says and of what the passage says.
In our Question Analysis, we saw that S6 serves todebunkthe health issues explanation of the other historians, notsupporttheir assertion.
(D) To provide evidence to refute an assertion about reduced demand for older workers
Trap: Nearby / Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S5 does indeed discuss debunking the explanation of “a reduced demand for older workers.”

However, S6 does not continue this discussion of “reduced demand for older workers.” Rather, S6 expands on Costa’s debunking the “health issues” explanation mentioned in S5, as we can tell by noticing that S6 mentions “chronic disease” and “improving health.”
So, this choice is a half-right trap: “provide evidence to refute an assertion” is correct, but “about a reduced demand for older workers” does not match S6.
(E) To make note of the most important factor in explaining the decrease in the LFPRE
Trap: Opposite

In S5, the author says that “Costacredibly debunksseveral alternative explanations, including health issues.” We can see from this wording, “credibly debunks,” that the author agrees with Costa that “health issues” do NOT explain the decrease in the LFPRE.
So, the function of S6 is to support Costa’s point that “health issues” are NOT an important factor, whereas this choice says “the most important factor.”

Also, it’s helpful to be aware of the extreme marker, “MOST important factor,” which is a yellow flag.Note that S3 and S4 do make note of the two most important factors in explaining the decrease in the LFPRE, for two different time periods, but S6 does not.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage; the main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author implies that that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Having discussed in S1 that retirement topics have received attention among economists, in S2, the author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa. Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

In S2, also notice the causation marker, “causes of X,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. The main point of S2 is that Costa’s book explains the causes of the decline of the LFPRE.

LFPRE, the labor force participation rate of the elderly, will continue to appear throughout the passage, so we must keep track of what it means. It is helpful to connect this term to the topic of retirement; a decline in the LFPRE would mean that the retirement rate is increasing. To understand why, note that LFPRE is defined in S2 as “the percentage of the elderly still working.” Mathematically, the percentage still working + the percentage that are retired must add to 100 percent. So, if the percentage of the elderly still working decreases, the percentage retired must increase.

Then, S3 through S6 go into the specifics of Costa’s analysis. S3 and S4 contrast the first and second halves of the twentieth century. S5 and S6 discuss Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decrease in the LFPRE.

It’s helpful to notice that causation/explanation is a repeated theme throughout the passage. A useful clue is that there are many causation markers: causes (S2), attributed to (S3), cause (S4), as a result of (S4), explanations (S5).

In S5, notice another tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Finally, S7 sums up the author’s review of Costa’s work. In saying that “Costa’s work exemplifies a much-needed resurgence of research on retirement,” the author reiterates the point in S1 that this topic of retirement is important, as well as indicates once again that he has a positive opinion of Costa’s work. After all, something that “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” would presumably be something positive.

Notice that, overall, the style of the passage could be described as a “book review.” The author is clearly focused on the work of Costa, and a clue is that the keyword “Costa” appears in five of the seven sentences of the passage. The author presents some key points of Costa’s work, and as we’ve seen in our analysis of the tone clues, the author expresses a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Passage Summary: With a positive tone, the author discusses a scholarly work about retirement, specifically focusing on the causes of the decrease in the LFPRE during the twentieth century.

Word in Passage

Similar Words, and Definitions

demographic transformations

changes in the characteristics of the population (demographics include age, gender, occupation, etc.)

far-reaching

significant

implications

impacts

contemporary

recent

trends

developments, changes (a “trend” means “a general direction in which something is developing or changing”)

consideration

attention

econometric

the application of statistical methods to economic data (note: this technical word is not important to the understanding the main point of this sentence, but we might guess that “econometric” is a combination of “economic” and “metrics,” and “metrics” are another word for “statistics.”)

illuminating

helpful, enlightening, informative (note: this is an important word because it signals that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis)

twentieth century

the years 1901 through 2000 (note: be sure not to forget that the 20th century started in the 1900’s, not the 2000’s)

Also, note that when the passage says “first half of the twentieth century,” that means the years 1901 through 1950.

latter half

second half

innovations

credibly

debunks

note: if we break the word into 2 pieces, the prefix “de” means “remove” and “bunk” means “nonsense”

health issues

health problems (note: in the context of health, “issues” generally means “problems,” and we also get more context for this with the word “disease” in the next sentence)

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: This sentence introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage.

The main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author also implies that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Simplification: Because demographic changes, such as an increase in the percentage of the population that is elderly, have a significant economic impact, topics about retirement have recently received greater attention from economists.

S2: The author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa.

Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Notice the causation marker “causes of,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. Costa is explaining the decline of the LFPRE.

Simplification: Costa provides a helpful explanation of the causes of the decline in LFPRE during the twentieth century.

S3: Provides details about Costa’s primary explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE for the first half of the twentieth century.

Notice the causation marker, “X can be attributed to Y.” In this structure, X is the result and Y is the cause. So, Costa is saying that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE is caused by “rising incomes.” In other words, Costa says that “rising incomes” are the primary cause for the decrease, but other factors account for 40 percent of the decrease.

Notice that the time frame is limited to the first half of the twentieth century, 1901 through 1950.

S4: Contrast with S3 — the primary cause of the decrease is different during a later timeframe, the second half of the twentieth century.

Notice the contrast marker, “In contrast.” As always, we must pay close attention when we notice a contrast marker — what are the two things that are in contrast?

S3: During the first half of the century, the primary cause was rising incomes.

S4: During the second half of the century, the primary cause was increased affordability of leisure activities resulting from technological improvements

Note that wording such as “increased affordability” could easily be reversed to something like “decreased cost” in an answer choice and retain the same meaning.

S5: Introduces Costa’s work in debunking alternative explanations.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” The author is providing another example of how Costa provides an “illuminating analysis of the causes of the decline … in the LFPRE.”

Note that “debunks” means “expose the falseness of a myth.” So, we can see that Costa disagrees with some alternative explanations, “including health issues and a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers.”

Notice the tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue indicating that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

S6: Expands on S5.

The phrase “perhaps most notably” indicates that this sentence presents a notable example of what was discussed in the previous sentence, Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decline in the LFPRE.

The example presented by this sentence is Costa’s “observation.”

Simplification: One notable example of Costa’s debunking is her observation that, in older people, “the rate of chronic disease was higher” when the LFPRE was higher “during the first half of the twentieth century.” In other words, she observed that, when older people had more “health issues,” more older people worked. Thus, she debunked the explanation offered by the “other historians” in S5 that “health issues” caused the LFPRE to decrease from a higher level “during the first half of the twentieth century” to a lower level “since that period.”

S7: Concludes the passage similarly to how the passage began.

The passage began by implying that the topic of retirement is important, and this sentence conveys essentially the same thing by indicating that “research on retirement” is “much needed.”

Also, the tone clue “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” indicates once again that the author has a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Simplification: While Costa’s work does not provide all the analysis needed for prediction of retirement trends, Costa’s work is an example the type of research needed on this important topic.

question.text: The author mentions Neanderthals’ routine use of fire most probably in order to

answers.text: show that many archeologists’ conclusion that use of fire by Neanderthals was unusual is inaccurate
identify a step involved in Neanderthals’ development of a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch
provide an example of a discovery indicating that Neanderthals were as cognitively advanced as ancestors of modern humans who inhabited Eurasia with Neanderthals
show that the material most commonly used in fires by Neanderthals was birch bark
suggest that claiming that development of a method of pitch production by Neanderthals was other than unintentional is unjustified

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To answer this Specific Purpose question about something mentioned by the author, we first find in the passage the specified thing the author mentioned.So, we find “Neanderthal’s routine use of fire” in S11.

Rereading all of S11, we see that S11 uses the support marker “since” and the conclusion marker “it is clear that” to present a premise, “Since Neanderthals’ routine use of fire likely commonly involved burning of birch bark near a vertical stone surface,” and a conclusion, “it is clear that development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally.”

So, basically, the author mentions “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” to support the conclusion that “development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally.”
(A) show that many archeologists’ conclusion that use of fire by Neanderthals was unusual is inaccurate
This choice is partially correct, because the author does mention “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” to show that the conclusion of the archeologists’ mentioned in the 1stparagraph is inaccurate.
However, this choice is also partially incorrect, because the archeologists’ conclusion is not that “use of fire by Neanderthals was unusual.”
(B) identify a step involved in Neanderthals’ development of a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch
Reading the sentence above “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire,” which is S10, we see that S10 calls the approach of producing pitch by “burning bark near a stone surface” a “simple approach.”

Notice that S11 connects “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire to this “simple approach” by saying that “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire likely commonly involved burning of birch bark near a vertical stone surface.”

So, unlike what this choice says, the author mentions “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” as a step involved in development of a “simpleapproach” to producing pitch, not “a step involved in … development of asophisticatedmethod.”
(C) provide an example of a discovery indicating that Neanderthals were as cognitively advanced as ancestors of modern humans who inhabited Eurasia with Neanderthals

While it may be that “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” is an example of a discovery indicating that “Neanderthals were as cognitively advanced as ancestors of modern humans who inhabited Eurasia with Neanderthals,” the passage does not indicate that “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” is such an example.
So, the purpose of mentioning “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” is not to provide such an example.
(D) show that the material most commonly used in fires by Neanderthals was birch bark

What S11 says, “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire likely commonly involved burning of birch bark,” does not “show that the materialmostcommonly used in fires by Neanderthals was birch bark.” After all, even if Neanderthals commonly burned birch bark, the materialmostcommonly used in fires by Neanderthals could have been something else.

Also, as we discussed in our question analysis, the purpose of mentioning “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” is to support the conclusion that “development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally,” not to simply show what material Neanderthals used in fires.
YOUR CHOICE(E) suggest that claiming that development of a method of pitch production by Neanderthals was other than unintentional is unjustified

As we discussed in our question analysis, the purpose of mentioning “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” is to support the conclusion that “development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally.”
Notice that “claiming” in this choice means the same thing as “concluding,” and that “unintentional” in this choice means basically the same thing as “accidental.”
Thus, this choice means “suggest thatconcludingthat development of a method of pitch production by Neanderthals was other thanaccidentalis unjustified.”
So, here’s the key thing to see about this choice.

If as the passage says, “development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally,” then it follows that “concluding that development of a method of pitch production by Neanderthals was other than accidental is unjustified.”

After all, if Neanderthals may have developed a way to produce pitch “accidentally,” then concluding that their development of a method of pitch production was “other than accidental,” meaning “not accidental,” is not justified.

Thus, in mentioning “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire” to support the conclusion that “development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally,” the author is also suggesting that “claiming that development of a method of pitch production by Neanderthals was other than unintentional is unjustified.”
So, this choice accurately describes the purpose of mentioning “Neanderthals’ routine use of fire.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting a common belief, the belief that Neanderthals were “less cognitively advanced than the other ancestors of modern humans who inhabited Eurasia along with the Neanderthals.”

Having presented that common belief, S1 continues by presenting a development related to that common belief: “recent archeological work has yielded multiple discoveries indicating that this perception of Neanderthals is inaccurate.” Since “THIS perception” is a look above marker, we can see that “this perception of Neanderthals” refers to the common belief mentioned earlier in the sentence. So, the passage is saying that recent discoveries indicate that the belief that Neanderthals were “less cognitively advanced” is inaccurate.

Then S2 presents an example of one of the recent “discoveries” mentioned in S1. That example is “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch for the purpose of making tools.”

S3 goes on to say why “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch for the purpose of making tools” is “particularly interesting.”

Then S4 pulls together everything else said by the 1st paragraph to say that, because of the evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch, many archeologists have concluded that Neanderthals were “sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch.”

So, it appears that the purpose of the 1st paragraph is to present the situation involving beliefs and evidence related to Neanderthals to lead up to the 1st paragraph’s most important point, which is the point about what the archeologists have concluded.

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents the common belief that Neanderthals were “less cognitively advanced” than other ancestors of modern humans, introduces “discoveries” that indicate that that common belief is inaccurate, presents an example of one those discoveries, which is “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch,” and presents many archeologists’ conclusion based on that evidence, “Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch.”

Continuing to the 2nd paragraph, the contrast word “however” at the beginning of S5 indicates that now the passage is presenting information that contrasts with what the 1st paragraph says. “THIS conclusion is a look above marker. So, we can see that S5 is saying that “recent experiments” have served to call into question the archeologists’ conclusion discussed above.

S6 and S7 begin to describe the experiments mentioned in S5, and then, at the beginning of S8, we come to another “however,” which signals that S8 likely expresses an important point. In fact, S8 does express an important point, the point that, by simply burning birch bark “near a vertical stone surface,” the researchers were able to produce “a significant quantity of pitch.”

We now begin to see the point of these experiments discussed in the 2nd paragraph. Whereas the archeologists mentioned in the 1st paragraph concluded that Neanderthals “must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch,” the researchers produced pitch using a method that wasn’t sophisticated at all, simply burning bark near a stone.

Then, S9 and S10 describe how the researchers confirmed that the pitch produced via the method of burning bark near a stone is “suitable for the creation of tools.”

Finally, S11 uses the support marker “since” and the conclusion marker “it is clear that” to support and state a conclusion of the author’s. We can see that, in S11, the author is concluding that, given the results of the experiments and the fact that Neanderthals likely “commonly” burned birch bark near a vertical stone surface, Neanderthals may have figured out how to make pitch accidentally not, as the “many archeologists” concluded, by using “advanced cognition” to “develop a sophisticated method.”

2nd paragraph summary: In contrast to the conclusion of “many archeologists” mentioned in the 1st paragraph, which is that “evidence that Neanderthals produced pitch” indicates that “Neanderthals must have been … cognitively advanced,” experiments show that Neanderthals could have developed a method of pitch production accidentally.

Passage summary: The passage introduces the fact that recently discovered evidence indicates that Neanderthals used pitch for making tools, presents many archeologists’ conclusion based on that fact, and then presents experimental results that challenge the archeologists’ conclusion.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.
