analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Opens the passage by using the contrast marker “while” to present a contrast between what has been “commonly believed” about Neanderthals and what recent discoveries indicate.

Simplification: People have commonly believed that Neanderthals were not as cognitively advanced (intelligent) as other human ancestors, but recent discoveries indicate that this common belief is not correct.

S2: Uses the look above marker “these discoveries” to introduce an example of one of the discoveries mentioned in S1, the discovery of “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch for the purpose of making tools.”

Simplification: One of the discoveries that indicate that the common belief mentioned in S1 is not correct is the discovery of evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch.

S3: Uses the look above marker “this discovery” to expand on S2 by saying why the discovery mentioned above in S2 is “particularly interesting.”

Notice that this sentence uses the strong language “particularly interesting” and the causation marker “because.” Strong language and causation markers generally appear in sentences that state important points. So, what this sentence says is likely important in the scheme of the passage.

Simplification: The evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch to make tools is particularly interesting because making pitch requires a high temperature, but pitch burns if exposed to fire.

S4: Uses the opinion marker “concluded” to present the conclusion that “many archeologists” have arrived at because of the “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch.”

Notice that, in presenting the conclusion of “many archeologists,” this sentence pulls together much of what the 1st paragraph says about Neanderthals and the recent discoveries. In other words, the 1st paragraph states some facts, and now, this sentence uses “therefore” to present a result of those facts, the result that “many archeologists have concluded that, in order to produce pitch, Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method.”

Simplification: Because of the evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch to make tools, archeologists have concluded that Neanderthals must have been smart enough to develop a sophisticated method of making pitch.

S5: Uses the contrast marker “however” to present a fact that contrasts with what the 1st paragraph says.

Notice that the look above marker “this” in “this conclusion” indicates that the conclusion mentioned in this sentence is the conclusion of the archeologists presented above in the 1st paragraph.

Simplification: In contrast with what the 1st paragraph says, experiments have generated evidence indicating that the conclusion of the archeologists may not be correct.

S6: Begins to describe the experiments mentioned in S5.

S7: Continues to describe the experiments mentioned in S5.

S8: Notice the contrast marker “however” at the beginning of this sentence. It signals that this sentence likely expresses an important point.

In fact, S8 does express an important point, the point that the researchers discovered a simple way to produce pitch, which was “birch bark was burned near a vertical stone surface.”

Simplification: The researchers discovered that, by burning birch bark near a stone, they produced pitch.

S9: Continues to describe the experiments by saying that the researchers used the pitch to make tools.

S10: Notice that the topic of this sentence, “a result indicating,” is likely important in the passage. After all, the results of an experiment discussed in the passage are likely closely connected to the main point and primary purpose of the passage.

Simplification: The experiments showed that pitch produced via the method of burning bark near a stone is good for making tools.

S11: Notice that this sentence uses the support marker “since” and the conclusion marker “it is clear that” to presents a premise, “Since Neanderthals’ routine use of fire likely commonly involved burning of birch bark near a vertical stone surface,” and a conclusion, “it is clear that development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally.”

In fact, in this sentence, the author states a conclusion that is based on almost everything said above in the 2nd paragraph.

Simplification: Since it’s likely that Neanderthals commonly burned birch bark near stones in their normal use of fire, they may have discovered the method of producing pitch by burning birch bark by near a stone by accident, rather than come up with a sophisticated method of producing pitch by using advanced thinking.

question.text: The highlighted sentence serves primarily to

answers.text: introduce an assertion that is supported by the sentences that follow
cast doubt on the assertion stated by the previous sentence
provide support for an assertion stated later in the same paragraph
present a contrast between two examples mentioned in the preceding sentence
provide an example to support a conclusion presented in the first paragraph

explanation_parts.text: To answer this Specific Purpose question about the function of a sentence, we first reread the highlighted sentence, S8.We see that S8 states the fact that “when birch bark was burned near a vertical stone surface … the process resulted in the production of a significant quantity of pitch.”
Our next step is to reread the sentences around S8 for context, starting with the sentence just above S8.
In this case, the sentence directly above S8, S7, isn’t very important.All S7 says is that burning birch bark usually does not produce usable pitch.

In fact, in scanning the sentences above S8, we don’t get to an important point until the first sentence of the 2ndparagraph, S5.S5 says, “recent experiments have generated evidence that serves to call this conclusion into question.”
Thus, since S8 describes an experiment involving burning bark, it’s possible that the correct answer will be that the S8 presents an example of one of the experiments mentioned in S5.

However, the truth is that the entire 2ndparagraph is about the experiments mentioned in S5.Thus, S8 is closely related to much of the 2ndparagraph, not just to S5.So, in this case, we’re probably not going to know what the correct answer is until we see it, because S8 is related to the rest of the paragraph in multiple ways.
So, our move is to carefully consider each choice, eliminate the ones that clearly don’t describe the purpose of S8, and find the one choice that does accurately describe the purpose of S8.
(A) introduce an assertion that is supported by the sentences that follow
Scanning the sentences below S8, we see that none of them supports the assertion introduced by S8.Rather, they describe other steps in the same experiment performed by the researchers.
So, this choice is clearly incorrect.
(B) cast doubt on the assertion stated by the previous sentence

Seeing the contrast word “however” at the beginning of the highlighted sentence, we might be tempted to go with the idea that the highlighted sentence “casts doubt on the assertion stated by the previous sentence,” S7.

However, rereading S7 and S8 together, we see that they simply describe different types of situations: S7 describes situations in which no usable pitch was produced, and in contrast, S8 describes a situation in which “a significant quantity of pitch” was produced.
So, S8 does not “cast doubt on” S7.It simply discusses something that contrasts with what S7 discusses.
YOUR CHOICE(C) provide support for an assertion stated later in the same paragraph

Scanning the 2ndparagraph for an “assertion stated later” that the highlighted sentence could “provide support for,” we find the conclusion stated by the last sentence, S11, “it is clear that development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally.”

Notice that the highlighted sentence, “when birch bark was burned near a vertical stone surface … the process resulted in the production of a significant quantity of pitch,” describes one of the series of experiments that support the conclusion stated by S11.
So, this choice correctly describes the purpose of the highlighted sentence.
(D) present a contrast between two examples mentioned in the preceding sentence
This choice could be tempting because the highlighted sentence, S8, begins with the contrast word “however.”
However, rereading the highlighted sentence and the preceding sentence, S7, we see that S7 does not mention two examples, and that S8 does not present a contrast between any examples mentioned in S7.
So, this choice is clearly incorrect.
(E) provide an example to support a conclusion presented in the first paragraph

Scanning the 1stparagraph for a conclusion that it presents, we see that it presents only one conclusion, the conclusion of the archeologists, which is “in order to produce pitch, Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method of heating bark to produce pitch without burning the pitch.”
Does the highlighted sentence provide an example to support that conclusion?
Notice that the highlighted sentence provides an example of asimpleway to produce pitch, burning bark near a stone surface, not an example of a “sophisticatedmethod of heating bark to produce pitch.”
So, the highlighted sentence actually presents areason todoubt, rather than “an example to support,” the archeologists’ conclusion.

After all, if it’s possible to produce pitch by simply burning bark near a stone, then it’s not the case, that “in order to produce pitch, Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method.”
So, this choice describes the opposite of the purpose of the highlighted sentence.

2nd paragraph summary: In contrast to the conclusion of “many archeologists” mentioned in the 1st paragraph, which is that “evidence that Neanderthals produced pitch” indicates that “Neanderthals must have been … cognitively advanced,” experiments show that Neanderthals could have developed a method of pitch production accidentally.

Passage summary: The passage introduces the fact that recently discovered evidence indicates that Neanderthals used pitch for making tools, presents many archeologists’ conclusion based on that fact, and then presents experimental results that challenge the archeologists’ conclusion.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Opens the passage by using the contrast marker “while” to present a contrast between what has been “commonly believed” about Neanderthals and what recent discoveries indicate.

Simplification: People have commonly believed that Neanderthals were not as cognitively advanced (intelligent) as other human ancestors, but recent discoveries indicate that this common belief is not correct.

S2: Uses the look above marker “these discoveries” to introduce an example of one of the discoveries mentioned in S1, the discovery of “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch for the purpose of making tools.”

Simplification: One of the discoveries that indicate that the common belief mentioned in S1 is not correct is the discovery of evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch.

S3: Uses the look above marker “this discovery” to expand on S2 by saying why the discovery mentioned above in S2 is “particularly interesting.”

Notice that this sentence uses the strong language “particularly interesting” and the causation marker “because.” Strong language and causation markers generally appear in sentences that state important points. So, what this sentence says is likely important in the scheme of the passage.

Simplification: The evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch to make tools is particularly interesting because making pitch requires a high temperature, but pitch burns if exposed to fire.

S4: Uses the opinion marker “concluded” to present the conclusion that “many archeologists” have arrived at because of the “evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch.”

Notice that, in presenting the conclusion of “many archeologists,” this sentence pulls together much of what the 1st paragraph says about Neanderthals and the recent discoveries. In other words, the 1st paragraph states some facts, and now, this sentence uses “therefore” to present a result of those facts, the result that “many archeologists have concluded that, in order to produce pitch, Neanderthals must have been sufficiently cognitively advanced to develop a sophisticated method.”

Simplification: Because of the evidence indicating that Neanderthals produced pitch to make tools, archeologists have concluded that Neanderthals must have been smart enough to develop a sophisticated method of making pitch.

S5: Uses the contrast marker “however” to present a fact that contrasts with what the 1st paragraph says.

Notice that the look above marker “this” in “this conclusion” indicates that the conclusion mentioned in this sentence is the conclusion of the archeologists presented above in the 1st paragraph.

Simplification: In contrast with what the 1st paragraph says, experiments have generated evidence indicating that the conclusion of the archeologists may not be correct.

S6: Begins to describe the experiments mentioned in S5.

S7: Continues to describe the experiments mentioned in S5.

S8: Notice the contrast marker “however” at the beginning of this sentence. It signals that this sentence likely expresses an important point.

In fact, S8 does express an important point, the point that the researchers discovered a simple way to produce pitch, which was “birch bark was burned near a vertical stone surface.”

Simplification: The researchers discovered that, by burning birch bark near a stone, they produced pitch.

S9: Continues to describe the experiments by saying that the researchers used the pitch to make tools.

S10: Notice that the topic of this sentence, “a result indicating,” is likely important in the passage. After all, the results of an experiment discussed in the passage are likely closely connected to the main point and primary purpose of the passage.

Simplification: The experiments showed that pitch produced via the method of burning bark near a stone is good for making tools.

S11: Notice that this sentence uses the support marker “since” and the conclusion marker “it is clear that” to presents a premise, “Since Neanderthals’ routine use of fire likely commonly involved burning of birch bark near a vertical stone surface,” and a conclusion, “it is clear that development by Neanderthals of an effective method of pitch production may have occurred accidentally.”

In fact, in this sentence, the author states a conclusion that is based on almost everything said above in the 2nd paragraph.

Simplification: Since it’s likely that Neanderthals commonly burned birch bark near stones in their normal use of fire, they may have discovered the method of producing pitch by burning birch bark by near a stone by accident, rather than come up with a sophisticated method of producing pitch by using advanced thinking.

question.text: The highlighted sentence serves primarily to

answers.text: support the assertion that Carlyle’s work holds a certain attraction because he captures something essential
demonstrate that many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.
present evidence that undermines the claims of the critics of Carlyle’s book mentioned in the first paragraph.
call into question the assertion that many whose worldviews resemble that of Carlyle find comfort in reading his book
support the assertion made in the first paragraph that Carlyle’s book is widely read and discussed

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

To answer this Specific Purpose question, we first reread the highlighted sentence.In rereading the highlighted sentence, S7, we see that it says that neither of “these factors” can fully explain why so many people read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.

“THESE factors” in S7 is a look above marker.Looking above, we see that “these factors” are the two factors presented in S5 and S6: Carlyle’s “accounts … are engaging” and “many … find comfort in reading his work.” So, notice that, by saying, “neither can fully explain,” S7 isruling outthe possibility that the two explanations presented in S5 and S6 fully explain why Carlyle’s book is so popular.

Also, we see that, S8, the sentence below the highlighted sentence, begins with the conclusion marker “thus” and that S8 is a conclusion supported by S7.How is S8 supported by S7? S7 supports S8 by ruling out the possibility that two explanations in S5 and S6 fully explain the popularity of Carlyle’s book and thus providing reason to believe that the explanation presented by S8 does explain why so many people read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.
So, the purpose of the highlighted sentence, S7, is to support the explanation presented by S8.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) support the assertion that Carlyle’s work holds a certain attraction because he captures something essential

As we saw in our Question Analysis, the highlighted sentence rules out the possibility that two explanations presented by S5 and S6 fully explain the popularity of Carlyle’s book and thus provides support for the explanation presented by S8.

Since the explanation presented by S8 is that “Carlyle’s work holds a certain attraction because he captures something essential,” this choice accurately describes the specific purpose of the highlighted sentence.
(B) demonstrate that many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.
As we know, the correct answer to a Specific Purpose question about a highlighted sentence must describe the purpose of the sentence in the passage, not just the sentence itself.

So, notice that, while it is true that the highlighted sentence does say that “many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book,” saying that is not thepurposeof the sentence in the passage.

As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the purpose of the highlighted sentence is to rule out the possibility that two explanations presented by S5 and S6 fully explain the popularity of Carlyle’s book and thus support the explanation presented by S8.
(C) present evidence that undermines the claims of the critics of Carlyle’s book mentioned in the first paragraph.

Notice that the highlighted sentence says only that the two explanations presented in S5 and S6 do not fully explain why many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.This information does not “undermine the claims of the critics of Carlyle’s book.” After all, the fact that the explanations do not fully explain why many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book does not conflict with the critics’ claims.

It could seem that the fact mentioned in S7 that many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book undermines the critics’ claims about the book.However, notice that the critics’ claims could be completely valid even if many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.In other words, that fact that many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book does not mean that he didn’t overemphasize the role of the individual and think carelessly.
YOUR CHOICE(D) call into question the assertion that many whose worldviews resemble that of Carlyle find comfort in reading his book

This choice could be tempting, because we could see the contrast marker “however” at the beginning of the highlighted sentence and get the impression that the highlighted sentence calls into question what directly precedes it, which is the idea that “many whose worldviews resemble that of Carlyle find comfort in reading his book.”

However, to get RC questions correct, we have to go beyond simply noticing keywords such as “however” and read for understanding, and by reading the highlighted sentence, we can see that it does not “call into question the assertion that many whose worldviews resemble that of Carlyle find comfort in reading his book.” Rather it says that that assertiondoes not fully explainwhy many types of individuals read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.
(E) support the assertion made in the first paragraph that Carlyle’s book is widely read and discussed

This choice could be tempting, because what the highlighted sentence mentions, that “individuals from a wide spectrum of cultures, lifestyles, occupations, and schools of thought read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book,” could seem to provide support for the assertion that “Carlyle’s book is widely read and discussed.”

However, as we know, the correct answer to a Specific Purpose question about a highlighted sentence must describe the purpose of the sentence in the passage, and the purpose of the highlighted sentence is not to support that assertion.

As we discussed in our Question Analysis, the purpose of the highlighted sentence is to rule out the possibility that two explanations presented by S5 and S6 fully explain the popularity of Carlyle’s book and thus support the explanation presented by S8.

analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author introduces Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History, along with the fact that the book “has many critics.”

Also, in S1, we see the opinion marker “who contend.” “Who” refers to the “many critics” of Thomas Carlyle’s book, and S1 goes on to present two of these critics’ opinions: “that Carlyle overemphasizes the role of the individual in shaping historical events and that his doing so results from his carelessness of thought and willingness to arrive at conclusions based on scant evidence.”

Since opinions are usually closely connected to important points and the primary purpose of a passage, we should note these two opinions and the role they play in the passage.

Then S2 and S3 expand on what S1 says by discussing two ways in which some critics of Carlyle have contrasted Carlyle with his contemporary Henry Thomas Buckle.

It’s important to notice that, in contrasting Carlyle and Buckle, the critics are implying that Carlyle is not as good as Buckle, both in terms of Carlyle’s and Buckle’s views and in terms of how Carlyle and Buckle arrive at their views.

It’s also important to notice that, in S1, S2, and S3, the author is descriptive; the author simply presents facts. In other words, the criticism of Carlyle’s book and the comparisons presented are not the author’s own. Rather, they are opinions and comparisons made by the critics of Carlyle’s book.

The last sentence of the 1st paragraph, S4, begins with the contrast marker “yet,” which signals that the passage is likely expressing an important point with S4. We see that S4 points out an interesting contrast: whereas the first three sentences say that critics have negative opinions of Carlyle and his work and believe that he and his work compare unfavorably with Buckle and his work, S4 says that Buckle is “largely forgotten,” while Carlyle’s book, On Heroes, is “still widely read and discussed.”

1st paragraph summary: The 1st paragraph presents a somewhat paradoxical situation: Carlyle’s book On Heroes has many critics who have negative opinions of Carlyle’s book and believe that he and his book compare unfavorably with Buckle and his work, yet Buckle is “largely forgotten,” whereas Carlyle’s book is “still widely read and discussed.”

The 2nd paragraph begins, in S5, by stating something that “it would be reasonable to believe,” which is “that Carlyle’s work has endured for so long simply because his accounts of the lives and accomplishments of ‘great’ individuals are engaging.” Notice that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long” in S5 must be referring to “nearly two centuries after the publication of On Heroes, the book is still widely read nearly and discussed” in S4. So, S5 is presenting a possible explanation for the fact that “nearly two centuries after the publication of On Heroes, the book is still widely read and discussed.”

Also, it’s important to notice that, by saying, “It would be reasonable to believe,” the author has shifted from simply presenting facts in the 1st paragraph to now presenting her own opinion about what would be “reasonable to believe.”

S6 presents another opinion of the author’s, which is that an “alternative possibility,” in other words, another possible explanation for the fact that On Heroes “is still widely read and discussed,” is that “many whose worldviews resemble that of Carlyle find comfort in reading his work.”

Then, S7 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals that S7 likely states an important point of the passage. Indeed, S7 does present an important point, which is that neither of the explanations presented by S5 and S6 can fully explain “why individuals from a wide spectrum of cultures, lifestyles, occupations, and schools of thought read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.” We can see that S7 is setting up the passage to say what the author believes can fully explain why Carlyle’s book is so widely read, cited, and debated.

Indeed, S8 begins with the conclusion marker “thus” and presents the author’s final conclusion, which is that the reason why Carlyle’s book is still “widely read and discussed,” is “mostly likely” that Carlyle’s book “holds a certain attraction because … Carlyle captures something essential.”

2nd paragraph summary: The 2nd paragraph presents two possible explanations for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed nearly two centuries after it was published, explains why those two explanations cannot fully explain why Carlyle’s book is still widely read, cited, and debated about, and closes with the author’s final conclusion regarding what explains why Carlyle’s book is still widely read, cited, and debated about.

Passage summary: The passage begins by presenting a somewhat paradoxical situation in which Carlyle’s book “has many critics” yet is “still widely read and discussed,” presents two possible explanations for that situation, and concludes by stating the author’s own conclusion about what explains that situation, which is that “what seems most likely is that” Carlyle’s book “holds a certain attraction because … Carlyle captures something essential.”

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: This sentence introduces Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History, along with the fact that the book “has many critics.”

It also expands on the fact that the book has many critics by presenting what these critics “contend” about the book.

The opinion marker “contend” signals that this sentence is closely connected to an important point of the passage, since opinions that appear in passages are generally connected to important points.

Simplification: Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes has many critics, and the opinion of these critics is that Carlyle overemphasizes the role of the individual in historical events because his work is careless.

S2: This sentence expands on what S1 says about the opinion of critics of Carlyle by describing a contrast between Carlyle and Henry Thomas Buckle that some of the critics of Carlyle’s book have pointed out.

Simplification: Some critics of Carlyle have pointed out a contrast between how Carlyle virtually worships certain “heroic” individuals and the fact that Buckle wrote, “individuals count for nothing.”

S3: This sentence also expands on what S1 says about the opinion of critics of Carlyle’s book by presenting a contrast that the critics “have noted,” which is that Carlyle “relies on strings of barely substantiated hypotheses or even hunches,” whereas Buckle “based his ideas on careful scientific analyses.”

Notice that S3 is basically expanding on what S1 says about Carlyle’s “carelessness of thought and willingness to arrive at conclusions based on scant evidence.”

Simplification: Carlyle relies on guesses, whereas Buckle based his ideas on scientific analyses.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “Yet.” This sentence presents an idea that contrasts with everything that has been said so far by the passage. Whereas the first three sentences are about criticism of Carlyle’s book and how some critics contrast Carlyle in a negative way with Buckle, this sentence presents an outcome that is a bit surprising given what has been said so far: Buckle is now “largely forgotten,” while Carlyle’s book is still “widely read and discussed.

Simplification: Despite everything critics say about Carlyle and Buckle, Buckle is forgotten, while Carlyle’s book is still widely read.

S5: This sentence uses the causation marker “because” to present a possible explanation for the fact that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long.”

Notice that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long,” in S5 refers to “nearly two centuries after the publication of On Heroes, the book is still widely read and discussed,” in S4.

Simplification: One reasonable explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed is that it is engaging to both serious and casual students of history.

S6: By beginning with “an alternative possibility,” this sentence signals that, having presented in S5 one reasonable explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed, the passage is now presenting an alternative explanation.

Simplification: An alternative possible explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed is that some people find it engaging.

S7: The contrast marker “however” signals that this sentence presents a contrast and that it probably expresses an important point.

Then S7 uses a “while X, Y,” structure. In a “while X, Y,” structure, the “while X part makes a concession, and the Y part states the main point of the “while X, Y” structure. In this case, the main point stated by the Y part of the “while X, Y,” structure is that “neither” of the explanations presented in S5 and S6 can fully explain why “individuals from a wide spectrum of cultures, lifestyles, occupations, and schools of thought read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.”

Simplification: The explanations presented in S5 and S6 cannot fully explain why Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed.

S8: Notice the conclusion marker, “thus.” This sentence states the author’s final conclusion about the seemingly paradoxical situation presented by the 1st paragraph.

In addition to the fact that conclusions are important points of a passage, another clue indicating that this sentence states an important point is the strong language “what seem most likely is that.” We can see that the author considers this explanation the best one.

Simplification: The most likely explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book “is still widely read and discussed” is that “Carlyle captures something essential.”

question.text: The author’s attitude toward Carlyle’s work is best characterized as which of the following?

answers.text: ambivalent
deeply skeptical
moderately critical
whole-heartedly enthusiastic
mildly positive

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
To determine the author’s attitude toward Carlyle’s work, we can consider tone clues and other clues in the passage.

Most of the 1stparagraph is about opinions of critics, and the only thing the author says in the 1stparagraph about Carlyle’s work itself is “the book is still widely read and discussed.” That statement is completely neutral.
Scanning the 2ndparagraph for tone clues, we find two.

In S6, we see that the author terms Carlyle’s work “an energetic affirmation.” While that wording is used in the context of describing how other people find Carlyle’s work, the author still used it.So, in determining the author’s attitude toward Carlyle’s work, we can consider that wording, which seems to indicate that the author has a positive attitude toward Carlyle’s work, since “energetic” is generally considered a positive characteristic.
Then, in S8, the author acknowledges that Carlyle’s work may have some “shortcomings” but terms Carlyle’s work a “spirited discussion,” which is a positive way of describing Carlyle’s work.

Also, in S8, the author says that “what seems most likely is that” Carlyle’s work “holds a certain attraction because … Carlyle captures something essential.” In saying that, the author indicates that she believes that Carlyle’s work “most likely” has some positive qualities.After all, capturing something essential is a positive quality.
These clues indicate that the author’s attitude toward Carlyle’s work is at least somewhat positive.
(A) ambivalent

“Ambivalent” means “uncertain” or “having both positive and negative opinions about something.” However, the author does not indicate that she has any negative opinions about Carlyle’s work.The only negative opinions about Carlyle’s work presented by the passage are those of critics of Carlyle’s work.

In fact, as we saw in our question analysis, all the clues about the author’s attitude toward Carlyle’s work indicate that the author’s opinion of Carlyle’s work is at least somewhat positive, not “ambivalent.”
(B) deeply skeptical
Notice that, while the passage describes critics of Carlyle’s work as having negative or “skeptical” opinions of it, the author does not express any skepticism of her own regarding Carlyle’s work.
(C) moderately critical
Notice that all the criticism of Carlyle’s work mentioned in the passage is that of critics of Carlyle’s work.The author herself does not criticize Carlyle’s work.
YOUR CHOICE(D) whole-heartedly enthusiastic

In our question analysis, we saw that all the clues about the author’s attitude toward Carlyle’s work indicate that the author’s opinion of Carlyle’s work is at least somewhat positive.However, those tone clues do not indicate that the author is “whole-heartedly enthusiastic” about Carlyle’s work.
In fact, in S8, the author implies that Carlyle’s work may have “some shortcomings.”
“Whole-heartedly enthusiastic” is much more positive than the author’s attitude indicated by the clues in the passage.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) mildly positive
In our question analysis, we saw that all the clues about the author’s attitude toward Carlyle’s work indicate that the author’s opinion of Carlyle’s work is at least somewhat positive.
Since “mildly positive” is basically the same as “at least somewhat positive,” this choice is the correct answer.

Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: This sentence introduces Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes, Hero-Worship, and the Heroic in History, along with the fact that the book “has many critics.”

It also expands on the fact that the book has many critics by presenting what these critics “contend” about the book.

The opinion marker “contend” signals that this sentence is closely connected to an important point of the passage, since opinions that appear in passages are generally connected to important points.

Simplification: Thomas Carlyle’s book On Heroes has many critics, and the opinion of these critics is that Carlyle overemphasizes the role of the individual in historical events because his work is careless.

S2: This sentence expands on what S1 says about the opinion of critics of Carlyle by describing a contrast between Carlyle and Henry Thomas Buckle that some of the critics of Carlyle’s book have pointed out.

Simplification: Some critics of Carlyle have pointed out a contrast between how Carlyle virtually worships certain “heroic” individuals and the fact that Buckle wrote, “individuals count for nothing.”

S3: This sentence also expands on what S1 says about the opinion of critics of Carlyle’s book by presenting a contrast that the critics “have noted,” which is that Carlyle “relies on strings of barely substantiated hypotheses or even hunches,” whereas Buckle “based his ideas on careful scientific analyses.”

Notice that S3 is basically expanding on what S1 says about Carlyle’s “carelessness of thought and willingness to arrive at conclusions based on scant evidence.”

Simplification: Carlyle relies on guesses, whereas Buckle based his ideas on scientific analyses.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “Yet.” This sentence presents an idea that contrasts with everything that has been said so far by the passage. Whereas the first three sentences are about criticism of Carlyle’s book and how some critics contrast Carlyle in a negative way with Buckle, this sentence presents an outcome that is a bit surprising given what has been said so far: Buckle is now “largely forgotten,” while Carlyle’s book is still “widely read and discussed.

Simplification: Despite everything critics say about Carlyle and Buckle, Buckle is forgotten, while Carlyle’s book is still widely read.

S5: This sentence uses the causation marker “because” to present a possible explanation for the fact that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long.”

Notice that “Carlyle’s work has endured for so long,” in S5 refers to “nearly two centuries after the publication of On Heroes, the book is still widely read and discussed,” in S4.

Simplification: One reasonable explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed is that it is engaging to both serious and casual students of history.

S6: By beginning with “an alternative possibility,” this sentence signals that, having presented in S5 one reasonable explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed, the passage is now presenting an alternative explanation.

Simplification: An alternative possible explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed is that some people find it engaging.

S7: The contrast marker “however” signals that this sentence presents a contrast and that it probably expresses an important point.

Then S7 uses a “while X, Y,” structure. In a “while X, Y,” structure, the “while X part makes a concession, and the Y part states the main point of the “while X, Y” structure. In this case, the main point stated by the Y part of the “while X, Y,” structure is that “neither” of the explanations presented in S5 and S6 can fully explain why “individuals from a wide spectrum of cultures, lifestyles, occupations, and schools of thought read, cite, and debate about Carlyle’s book.”

Simplification: The explanations presented in S5 and S6 cannot fully explain why Carlyle’s book is still widely read and discussed.

S8: Notice the conclusion marker, “thus.” This sentence states the author’s final conclusion about the seemingly paradoxical situation presented by the 1st paragraph.

In addition to the fact that conclusions are important points of a passage, another clue indicating that this sentence states an important point is the strong language “what seem most likely is that.” We can see that the author considers this explanation the best one.

Simplification: The most likely explanation for the fact that Carlyle’s book “is still widely read and discussed” is that “Carlyle captures something essential.”

question.text: The passage suggests that TAs differ from teachers in which of the following ways?

answers.text: They spend less time with the unruliest students than teachers spend.
The most useful task that they perform is to supervise difficult students.
They tend to have more experience than teachers have.
The number of them in Britain increased while Tony Blair was in power.
They tend to have less educational training than teachers have.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, make sure we understand exactly what the stem is asking for.We can see from “TAs differ from teachers” that this question involves a comparison.So, we must find specific language in the passage about a difference between BOTH the “TAs” and “teachers.”

Whenever we notice a comparison, we must be aware that it can be easy to mix things up if we don’t pay close attention.Therefore, this stem is a signal that we must be proactive and mindful to watch out for opposite traps!
Notice the pronoun “they” in the answer choices; “they” refers to “TAs” (since “TAs” is the subject of the verb “differ”).
Where in the passage are TAs and teachers directly compared? Our accuracy and speed will be maximized if we narrow our focus and carefully review the section where they are directly compared.

S5 and S6 say, “teachers may have often used their TAs as convenient supervisors for the unruliest students, rather than attend to these students themselves.Before the change in education policy, these difficult students had beenBETTER served by experienced teachers who had received the proper training.”
Notice the comparison markerin S6, “better.” This sentence indicates that teachers have more experience and training than TAs.
It’s optional to find additional proof, but we have more support for this fact about TAs, in S1, where TAs were first mentioned: “usually haveminimal educationalqualifications.”
(A) They spend less time with the unruliest students than teachers spend.
Trap: Opposite

S5 suggests that the TAs spend MORE, rather than less, time with the unruliest students than teachers spend: “teachers may have often used their TAs as convenient supervisors for the unruliest students, rather than attend to these students themselves.”
(B) The most useful task that they perform is to supervise difficult students.
Trap: Distortion
Notice the extreme marker, “the most,” which is a yellow flag, and signifies acomparisonto other tasks that TAs perform— “themostuseful task” would mean that allothertasks are less useful.
In the passage, is any comparison made to other tasks that TAs perform? No, so we can eliminate B.
(C) They tend to have more experience than teachers have.
Trap: Opposite
In our question analysis, we saw that S5 and S6 say that TAs have LESS, rather than more, experience and training than teachers have:
In addition, S1 says that TAs “usually haveminimaleducational qualifications.”
(D) The number of them in Britain increased while Tony Blair was in power.
Trap: Matches but Incorrect

An essential GMAT habit is to be extra careful to answer the exact question asked.Here, since the stem asks how “TAs differ from teachers,” we must find specific language in the passage about a difference between BOTH the “TAs” and “teachers.”

If you chose this answer choice, you may have noticed that this statement about TAs is indeed supported by the passage, because S1 says, “during Tony Blair’s stewardship… the number of TAs… surged from 60,600 to 176,000.”
However, the passage does not say anything about the number of teachers.So, the passage does not indicate that, in having increased in number, TAsdifferfrom teachers.
YOUR CHOICE(E) They tend to have less educational training than teachers have.
Where in the passage are TAs and teachers directly compared?
S6 says, “Before the change in education policy, these difficult students had beenBETTER served by experienced teachers who had received the proper training.”
Notice the comparison markerin S6, “better.” S6 indicates that teachers haveMOREexperience and training than TAs.
Does this comparison in the passage match the meaning of this answer choice?
Yes, this choice says that TAs “have LESS educational training than teachers have,” and this indeed has the same meaning as “teachers have MORE experience than TAs have.”

It is helpful to notice this common pattern in RC, in which the language in the answer choice is reversed from what the passage says, but the meaning matches.(Another way to think about reversed language is through a math example: if the passage were to say, “X is greater than Y,” the correct answer could say, “Y is less than X”)

It’s optional to find additional evidence in the passage, but there is additional support for this choice’s statement about the educational training of TAs, in S1, where TAs were first mentioned: “usually haveminimaleducationalqualifications.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author begins by describing a change in a government policy and public education spending: “Britain had a reputation for spending frugally on public education, lagging behind most other developed countries, BUT the country’s policy was transformed under Labour government leadership.” Notice the contrast marker “but.” “But” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, the fact that “the country’s policy was transformed” is an important point.

How was the country’s policy transformed? Notice that, later in S1, details about the change are specified: “the number of teaching assistants (TAs) … surged from 60,600 to 176,000.”

The first paragraph continues by discussing the underwhelming, i.e., disappointing, results of the policy change: S2 says, “Initially optimistic about the effects of this change, proud government ministers boasted about the increase, BUT despite the substantial investment involved, the results were underwhelming.” Here again, we must pay close attention to what follows the contrast marker, “but.” The key point to see is that the results of the policy change were underwhelming.

Notice that, so far, the author is completely descriptive and is presenting historical facts; he or she does make any arguments of his or her own. In other words, he or she has not yet taken a strong position.

1st paragraph summary: The author discusses a change in Britain’s public education policy that involved an increase in the number of teaching assistants in Britain and discusses the disappointing results of the change.

Notice that the 2nd paragraph opens with a rhetorical question, S3, “What is the most plausible reason for these results?” Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question uses the causation marker “reason,” indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then mentioned. We must pay close attention whenever an author presents an explanation for a phenomenon, because explanations tend to be important parts of the passages.

In S4, the author notes a flaw in the explanation of the “defenders,” and then, in S5, the author proceeds with his or her own explanation, “a more convincing explanation is that ….” We want to pay close attention whenever an author uses strong language, and “a more convincing explanation is that” certainly qualifies as strong language. “More” is a comparison marker, and “Convincing” means “believable,” so when the author says “a more convincing” explanation, the author is making the strong statement that he or she believes that this second explanation is better than the defenders’ explanation.

2nd paragraph summary: The author discusses two possible explanations for the results described in the first paragraph, and the author favors one of these explanations over the other.

For understanding the structure of the entire passage, it’s helpful to ask ourselves, “What is the relationship between the paragraphs?” Given that, in the first paragraph, the author has presented results that were disappointing and unexpected, it makes sense that the author would be interested in explaining these results, and explaining “these results” is exactly what the author does in the second paragraph.

In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: This sentence begins by providing some background information: For many years, Britain had a reputation for spending frugally on public education, lagging behind most other developed countries.”

Then, the contrast marker “but” signals a main point: “BUT the country’s policy was transformed.” As always, when we see a contrast marker, we must pay extra close attention and notice what two things are in contrast.

Simplification: Britain was frugal in public education spending but increased spending and the number of TAs significantly between 1997 and 2009.

S2: Contrast between expectations and results:

“Initially optimistic … ministers boasted” … “BUT…the results were underwhelming.” (Note: if we didn’t know the word “underwhelming,” we could guess that it means “disappointing” because of the context and contrast.)

Also, since “this X” is a look above marker, when we see “this change,” we must understand what is above that “this change” refers to. We see that “this change” refers to “the number of teaching assistants (TAs) … surged from 60,600 to 176,000.”

After the “:,” details are provided about how the results were underwhelming: “a consistent negative relationship between the amount of support a student received from TAs and the progress the student made.”

Simplification: Government ministers thought more TAs would help, but the results were disappointing.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Also, S3 includes the causation marker “reason.” This marker signals that possible explanations will follow.

Finally, notice the look above marker “these results.” “These results” refers to the “underwhelming results” described in the first paragraph.

Simplification: What is the most likely cause of the disappointing results described above?

S4: The contrast marker “while” in “While some defenders … argue” signals that the author is noting a flaw in the argument of the “defenders.”

“Successfully accounted for this variable” means “successfully adjusted so that the results were not biased.”

“Argue that” is an opinion marker, so we must make sure not to mix up different opinions.

Simplification: Policy defenders argue that the results were biased, but the defenders are wrong because they failed to take something into account.

S5: As we might expect, the author follows up by presenting a “more convincing explanation,” after noting the flaw of the defenders’ argument in S4.

Simplification: A better explanation is how the TAs were used.

S6: The author elaborates on the explanation in S5.

Simplification: Before more TAs were hired, the most difficult students had been better served by experienced teachers.

question.text: The author would most likely agree with which of the following statements about the defenders referred to in the highlighted text?

answers.text: They are wrong to defend the government policy because doing so will lead to additional government spending programs that are ineffective.
They do not account for the fact that the authors of the DCSF study accounted for pupil characteristics that could have biased the results.
They believe that TAs are just as skilled in their interactions with students as are teachers.
They assert that the government policy succeeded in achieving many of its goals.
Their argument is motivated primarily by a desire for increased spending in public education.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
Since the stem says, “the author would most likely agree … about thedefenders,” we want to focus on the author’s tone and opinion about the “defenders.”

In “While somedefendersof the government policy argue that the less skilled pupils were assigned more help, the DCSF researchers successfully controlled for pupil skill,” the contrast marker “while” signals that the author is noting a flaw in the argument of the “defenders.” When we have the structure “While X, Y,” the X and Y elements are in contrast.In this sentence, we have a structure that simplifies to: “While the defenders argue X, (the reality is that) they are wrong because Y.”
Specifically, why does the author believe that the defenders are wrong?

In the context of research, the phrase “controlled for” means “removed bias.” When the defenders “argue that the less skilled pupils were assigned more help,” they are implying that the study results were biased.In contrast, when the author says that “the DCSF researchers successfully controlled for pupil skill,” this statement basically means “the researchers correctly adjusted/accounted for the concern/argument of the defenders (that the results were biased because less skilled pupils were assigned more help).”
(A) They are wrong to defend the government policy because doing so will lead to additional government spending programs that are ineffective.
Trap: Speculation
Trap: Half-Right
As discussed above, the author does appear to disagree with the defenders, but the author doesn’t specify anything about “lead to additional government spending programs that are ineffective.”
If you picked this choice, you may have made a speculation about the author’s opinion that goes beyond what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(B) They do not account for the fact that the authors of the DCSF study accounted for pupil characteristics that could have biased the results.

We see that the “defenders” are mentioned only in S4, the sentence in which “defenders” is highlighted: “While somedefendersof the government policy argue that the less skilled pupils were assigned more help, the DCSF researchers successfully controlled for pupil skill.”

The contrast marker “while” at the beginning of S4 signals that the author is noting a flaw in the argument of the “defenders.” In other words, the “While X, Y” structure implies, “While some defenders argue X, the reality is that they’re wrong because Y.”

Thus, the author is saying that the defenders are wrong since, even though “researchers successfully controlled forpupil skill,” which is the variable mentioned by the defenders, the defenders suggest that the results were biased because “the less skilled pupils were assigned more help.”

So, we can see that the author implies exactly what this choice says, that the defenders do not account for the fact that the authors of the study accounted for variables, such as pupil characteristics, that could bias the results.
We can confirm that each part of the answer choice matches what the passage says, as shown below.

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagedo not account for the fact thatWhile X, Yauthors of the DCSF studyresearchersaccounted forcontrolled forpupil characteristics that could bias the resultsthe less skilled pupils were assigned more help
(C) They believe that TAs are just as skilled in their interactions with students as are teachers.
Trap: Distortion
We must be careful to notice what the passage says and not go beyond it.

The passage does not specify that the defenders have any opinion about the skills of teachers and TAs.Rather, the passage mentions only the defenders’ general argument about thereasonfor the disappointing results described in paragraph one: “defenders of the government policy argue that the less skilled pupils were assigned more help.”

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the “defenders of the government policy” disagree with the author’s explanation (in S5 and S6) of the cause of the disappointing results, and that the author does indeed discuss a difference in thetraining and experiencelevels of teachers and TAs.

However, the passage doesn’t say that the defenders believe the direct opposite of everything the author believes, such as what the author believes about the skills of TAs.So, this choice does not match what’s said about the defenders.
(D) They assert that the government policy succeeded in achieving many of its goals.
Trap: Distortion
We must always be careful to notice what the passage says and not go beyond it.

“Succeeded inachievingmany of its goals” is a distortion.The defenders are described only as giving a reason why perhaps the results weren’t as bad as they seemed, not as asserting anything about “achieving many goals.”

A defense/explanation of the disappointing results is different from “achieving many of its goals.” “Achieving many of its goals” means that there were positive results.An explanation of the disappointing results is different from saying that the policy had many positive results.
(E) Their argument is motivated primarily by a desire for increased spending in public education.
Trap: Speculation
Notice the wording here, “Their argument ismotivatedprimarily by ….”
Does the passage say anything about the defenders’motivations?
No.This statement aboutmotivationscould be true in real life, but as always, we must find proof in the passage before choosing an answer choice.
In the passage, the discussion of the defenders is limited to just one sentence, S4.The only thing we know about the defenders is that they are giving a reason for the disappointing results.
