S3: The author provides an additional example of planned obsolescence.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” S2 said that there are “certain cases” that are hard to justify, and provided one example. We can see that S3 provides a second example of planned obsolescence that is “hard to justify”: “designers explicitly discuss how to design products that will wear out as rapidly as possible while leaving customers with sufficient faith in a product to replace it immediately.”

Simplification: Annie Leonard cites evidence of the planned obsolescence strategy being discussed in industrial-design journals.

S4: The author introduces a different possible reason for frequent product replacement.

Notice the contrast marker, “at the same time,” which has a similar meaning to “meanwhile,” and can be thought of as a softer version of “nevertheless.” This marker is used here to transition to discussing a different possible reason for frequent product replacement; this reason is “a demand-side phenomenon.”

At this point, it may not be totally clear what is meant by “demand-side phenomenon,” but we can expect to gain more context as we read further. Indeed, the next sentence starts with “For example.”

Particularly once we’ve read further, we can see that “demand-side phenomenon” means “related to consumer preferences.”

Also notice that “demand-side” is in contrast to the word “supplying” in S1: “‘planned obsolescence’ — the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Notice that the author’s tone is descriptive, and the author doesn’t express a strong opinion of his own; the wording used is “an interesting argument has been made that.” The word “interesting” is quite neutral; it does not express strong support of or opposition to this argument.

S5: Provides an example to support the point made in S4.

Notice the support marker “for example.”

Notice the contrast marker “although.” This example is being cited as a “demand-side phenomenon” unlike the “planned obsolescence” described in the 1st paragraph. Although the phones are replaced very frequently, the reason for it is NOT that the phones have worn out; S5 says that “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.” The author is mentioning this example because it is different from the reason for frequent replacement discussed in the 1st paragraph — products that are intentionally designed to wear out quickly.

S6: Expands on the example in S5, by adding details about why people replace their smartphones so frequently.

Notice the contrast marker “while X, Y.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Here, the Y part says that Walker “has a strong desire to stay current with the latest impressive technological innovations.” This is an example of the “demand-side phenomenon” introduced in S4.

S7: Expands on the point in S6 about Walker’s motivation

Notice the conclusion marker “therefore,” which connects Walker’s “strong desire to stay current,” in S6, to the point in S7.

Notice the contrast marker “instead of X, Y”: “instead of resenting the cost of replacement, he looks forward to upgrading his device regularly.” This positive attitude is different from the critical attitude of the “consumer protection advocates” in S1.

S8: Quotes Walker’s general conclusion.

The quote of Walker’s conclusion summarizes the key point in S4 that “frequent product replacement has sometimes become a demand-side phenomenon.” Note that “exactly what we want” connects closely with the meaning of “demand-side.”

question.text: Which of the following most clearly exemplifies a product designed in accordance with the strategy discussed in the journals referred to in the highlighted text?

answers.text: A washing machine that breaks down often but can be repaired by the owner with expensive parts sold by the manufacturer.
A running shoe that reliably delivers a high level of safety and performance with a sole engineered to lose its springiness after a few races.
A laptop computer that uses a battery that commonly stops working properly and is particularly difficult for the customer to replace.
A smartphone that has exciting new features that are quickly surpassed by those of newer models.
A luxury car from the mid-twentieth century that looked good in the showroom but deteriorated unusually quickly as a result of poor manufacturing standards.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

The wording of the stem, “which of the following most clearly exemplifies” signals that this is an Application question.Application questions ask us to apply information from the passage to a context that is outside of the passage.If we were unsure that this was an Application question at first, a good clue would be that its answer choices mention things not discussed in the passage.

Looking at the full stem, notice that the correct answer must “exemplify,” or be an example of, “a product designed in accordance with the strategy discussed in the journals referred to in the highlighted text.”
So, our next step is to determine what exactly this “strategy discussed in the journals” involves.
S3 says there are two parts to the strategy:
(1) “design products that will wear out as rapidly as possible”
(2) “while leaving customers with sufficient faith in a product to replace it immediately”

The correct answer should cover both of these parts.Note that “wear out” means that a product loses its ability to function, and “sufficient faith” implies that the product has some positive qualities.
(A) A washing machine that breaks down often but can be repaired by the owner with expensive parts sold by the manufacturer.

This choice does represent a way for the manufacturer to make a profit by selling “expensive parts,” but it doesn’t match the strategy discussed in the journals: motivating a customer to “replaceit immediately.” Notice that “repaired” in this choice is different from “replace.”
Furthermore, we are looking for a strategy in which the product is intentionally designed to “wear out as rapidly as possible,” but “break down often” is not the same as “wear out.”
Finally, a product that “breaks down often” is not a product that leaves customers with “faith” in that product.
YOUR CHOICE(B) A running shoe that reliably delivers a high level of safety and performance with a sole engineered to lose its springiness after a few races.
First, notice that “a sole engineered to lose its springiness after a few races” matches with part 1 of the strategy we are looking for, “design products that will wear out as rapidly as possible.”

Second, notice that the positive quality of “reliably delivers ahigh level of safety and performance” matches with part 2 of the strategy we are looking for, “leaving customers withsufficient faithin a product to replace it immediately.”
So, the running shoe described by this choice exemplifies a product designed in accordance with the strategy discussed in the journals.
(C) A laptop computer that uses a battery that commonly stops working properly and is particularly difficult for the customer to replace.
This choice doesn’t match either part of the strategy.

First, we are looking for a product intentionally designed to “wear out as rapidly as possible,” but this choice doesn’t say that the computer is intentionally designed to “use a battery that commonly stops working.” The passage doesn’t say why the battery stops working; perhaps there is an unintentional flaw in the manufacturing.
Notice also that this choice does not say that the computer itself wears out.
Meanwhile, there is no positive quality mentioned in this choice that would “leave customers withsufficient faithin a product to replace it immediately.”
(D) A smartphone that has exciting new features that are quickly surpassed by those of newer models.

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that this example appears in the passage, in S5 and S6.However, we have to be careful not to be tempted into choosing this choice just because it matches what’s discussed in the passage.
The correct answer to this Application question must be an example that matches the strategy discussed in the journals.
So, we are looking for a product designed to “wear out as rapidly as possible,” but this choice doesn’t say that the smartphone wears out quickly.
(E) A luxury car from the mid-twentieth century that looked good in the showroom but deteriorated unusually quickly as a result of poor manufacturing standards.

This choice is tempting because it mentions a product from “the mid-twentieth century,” and the journals mentioned in the highlighted text are “from the 1950s,” which are years in the middle of the twentieth century.

However, we are looking for a product designed to “wear out as rapidly as possible,” and this choice does NOT say that the car isdesignedto deteriorate unusually quickly.Rather, the car “deteriorated unusually quickly” because of “poor manufacturing standards,” which are different from how the car is “designed.”

Furthermore, “looked good in the showroombut deteriorated unusually quickly” is not a good match with the second part of the strategy discussed in the journals, “leaving customers withsufficient faithin a product to replace it immediately.” These characteristics of the car are unlikely to inspire faith.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, notice the opinion marker, “conviction that X.” “Conviction” means “a strong belief.” Whenever we notice an opinion, we must make sure we understand both the “who” and the “what” — whose opinion is being presented and what that opinion involves.

Here, consumer protection advocates have the negative belief that “planned obsolescence” is “exploitative of consumers,” meaning that it unfairly takes advantage of consumers.

Notice that a key term, “planned obsolescence,” is specifically defined in between the two dashes in S1: planned obsolescence is the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.

In S2, notice the opinion marker, “indeed.” “Indeed” signals that the author supports the consumer protection advocates’ opinion above that planned obsolescence is exploitative.

Then, the author presents two examples to support this opinion: the “Phoebus cartel” and the citation made by Annie Leonard.

1st paragraph summary: The author introduces the concept of “planned obsolescence,” the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases, and provides two examples that support consumer protection advocates’ belief that planned obsolescence unfairly takes advantage of consumers.

In S4, notice the contrast marker “at the same time,” which has a similar meaning to “meanwhile,” and can be thought of as a softer version of “nevertheless.” This marker is being used here to transition to discussing a different possible reason for frequent product replacement; this reason is “a demand-side phenomenon.” Notice that “demand-side” is in contrast to the word “supplying” in S1: “‘planned obsolescence’ — the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

At this point, it may not be totally clear what is meant by “demand-side phenomenon,” but we can expect to gain more context as we read further. Indeed, the next sentence starts with the support marker “For example.”

In S5, the example of frequent smartphone replacement is cited as a “demand-side phenomenon” rather than the “planned obsolescence” described in the 1st paragraph. Although the phones are replaced very frequently, the reason for it is NOT that the phones have worn out; S5 says, “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.” The author is mentioning this example because it is different from the reason for frequent replacement discussed in the 1st paragraph — products that are intentionally designed to wear out quickly.

The rest of the paragraph expands on this example, and in S8, the quote of Walker’s conclusion summarizes the key point in S4 that “frequent product replacement has sometimes become a demand-side phenomenon.” Note that “the market gives us exactly what we want” connects closely with the meaning of “demand-side.”

It’s important to notice that this paragraph does NOT directly dispute the consumer protection advocates’ negative opinion about “planned obsolescence” in the 1st paragraph. Rather, the second paragraph introduces a different reason for frequent product replacement, consumer demand/preferences. This “demand-side phenomenon” is NOT cited as an example of “planned obsolescence,” because the 2nd paragraph doesn’t say that the products are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases. Rather, the second paragraph focuses on the desires of consumers.

2nd paragraph summary: The author introduces a different possible reason for frequent product replacement, consumer demand/preferences, and supports this point with the example of frequent smartphone replacement; unlike the products described in the 1st paragraph that are designed to wear out quickly, these discarded smartphones are “still in good working order.”

Passage summary: The author discusses two different reasons for frequent product replacement: the business strategy of intentionally designing products to wear out quickly and, contrastingly, consumer demand for frequent upgrades.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

(Note: It’s not required to know this word because “planned obsolescence” is defined in the passage, with a very specific contextual definition, in between the two dashes in S1. Then, examples are given in S2 and S3.)

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

Simplification: Consumer protection advocates believe that “planned obsolescence” unfairly takes advantage of consumers. Planned obsolescence is the business strategy of designing products in a way that requires more frequent replacement.

Notice the opinion marker “indeed.” “Indeed” signals that the author supports the consumer protection advocates’ opinion above that planned obsolescence is exploitative.

Simplification: Annie Leonard cites evidence of the planned obsolescence strategy being discussed in industrial-design journals.

At this point, it may not be totally clear what is meant by “demand-side phenomenon,” but we can expect to gain more context as we read further. Indeed, the next sentence starts with “For example.”

Particularly once we’ve read further, we can see that “demand-side phenomenon” means “related to consumer preferences.”

Also notice that “demand-side” is in contrast to the word “supplying” in S1: “‘planned obsolescence’ — the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Notice that the author’s tone is descriptive, and the author doesn’t express a strong opinion of his own; the wording used is “an interesting argument has been made that.” The word “interesting” is quite neutral; it does not express strong support of or opposition to this argument.

S5: Provides an example to support the point made in S4.

Notice the support marker “for example.”

Notice the contrast marker “although.” This example is being cited as a “demand-side phenomenon” unlike the “planned obsolescence” described in the 1st paragraph. Although the phones are replaced very frequently, the reason for it is NOT that the phones have worn out; S5 says that “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.” The author is mentioning this example because it is different from the reason for frequent replacement discussed in the 1st paragraph — products that are intentionally designed to wear out quickly.

S6: Expands on the example in S5, by adding details about why people replace their smartphones so frequently.

Notice the contrast marker “while X, Y.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Here, the Y part says that Walker “has a strong desire to stay current with the latest impressive technological innovations.” This is an example of the “demand-side phenomenon” introduced in S4.

S7: Expands on the point in S6 about Walker’s motivation

Notice the conclusion marker “therefore,” which connects Walker’s “strong desire to stay current,” in S6, to the point in S7.

Notice the contrast marker “instead of X, Y”: “instead of resenting the cost of replacement, he looks forward to upgrading his device regularly.” This positive attitude is different from the critical attitude of the “consumer protection advocates” in S1.

S8: Quotes Walker’s general conclusion.

The quote of Walker’s conclusion summarizes the key point in S4 that “frequent product replacement has sometimes become a demand-side phenomenon.” Note that “exactly what we want” connects closely with the meaning of “demand-side.”

question.text: The passage suggests that each of the following is true about smartphones EXCEPT:

answers.text: Many consumers do not want to miss out on improvements to the speed of smartphones.
They are currently replaced by Americans at an average rate of under two years.
Most of the smartphones that are discarded do not function properly.
The disposal of them can create electronic waste.
They belong to a product category in which there are rapid technological improvements.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

This is an Inference EXCEPT question.So, to arrive at the correct answer, we will seek to eliminate four facts about smartphones that the passage indicates are true.The correct answer will be the one choice that the passage does not indicate is true.

As always, we must be aware of choices that distort information presented in the passage.In most questions, such choices are incorrect.However, in EXCEPT questions, choices that distort information presented in the passage are often correct answers.
In considering each choice, we can search the passage for references to “smartphones” and for keywords in the choice.

In answering an EXCEPT question, we’re often able to eliminate multiple choices by using information from one area of the passage.In this case, notice that smartphones are first mentioned in S5, and are discussed in the sentences that follow.So, we would probably benefit from re-reading the 2ndparagraph before addressing the answer choices.
(A) Many consumers do not want to miss out on improvements to the speed of smartphones.
Notice that S5 says, “he, along withmany other consumers, has a strong desire tostay currentwith the latest impressive technological innovations, includingquicker processors.”
Does each part of this choice match this quote from the passage?

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagemany consumersmany other consumersdo not want to miss out onhas a strongdesire tostay currentimprovements to thespeedof smartphoneslatest impressive technological innovations, includingquicker processors
Since everything in this choice matches the passage, we can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.
(B) They are currently replaced by Americans at an average rate of under two years.

Scanning the 2ndparagraph, we see that the keywords “replaced,” “Americans,” and “rate” in this choice appear in S5: “Americanconsumers are currentlyreplacingtheir smartphones at the remarkablerateof every 18 months.”
Notice that “replacing … every 18 months” matches the meaning of “replaced … at an average rate of under two years.”
Since everything in this choice matches the passage, we can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Most of the smartphones that are discarded do not function properly.
In this choice, notice the keyword “discarded,” which appears in S5: “the vast majority of the phones theydiscardare still in good working order.”
Also, “most” in this choice has the same meaning as “the majority” in S5.

Thus, this choice says the opposite of what the passage says, since “Most of the smartphones that are discarded do not function properly,” is the opposite of “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.”
Since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage, this choice is the correct answer.
Supported by the passage? No.
(D) The disposal of them can create electronic waste.
In this choice, notice the keyword phrase “electronic waste,” which appears in S6: “While he expresses concern about the resulting problem of electronic waste.”
Note that “theresultingproblem” is a look above marker; the word “resulting” implies that the “problem” is the result of something that was just discussed above.So, what was discussed directly above?
S5 says, “although American consumers are currently replacing theirsmartphonesat the remarkable rate of every 18 months, the vast majority of thephones they discardare still in good working order.”

Thus, S6 is saying that that the problem of “electronic waste” is the result of “the phones they discard”; note that “discard” has a similar meaning to “disposal” in this choice.Therefore, this choice is supported by the passage.
So, we can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.
(E) They belong to a product category in which there are rapid technological improvements.
In this choice, notice the keyword phrase “rapid technological improvements.” This theme appears in 2ndparagraph in three places:
S4: “current era of fast-paced technological developments”
S6: “stay current with the latest impressive technological innovations”
S7: “upgrading his device regularly”

Of these three quotes, the most direct evidence to support this choice is in S4.Smartphones are cited in S5 as an example within the “current era of fast-paced technological developments.” Note that “rapid” matches with “fast-paced,” and “improvements” matches with “developments.”
Since everything in this choice matches the passage, we can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Defines a key term/theme, the strategy of “planned obsolescence,” and presents a negative opinion about it.

Notice the opinion marker, “conviction that X.” “Conviction” means “a strong belief.”

Notice that a key term, “planned obsolescence,” is specifically defined in between the two dashes.

Simplification: Consumer protection advocates believe that “planned obsolescence” unfairly takes advantage of consumers. Planned obsolescence is the business strategy of designing products in a way that requires more frequent replacement.

S2: The author expresses support for the negative opinion presented in S1, by citing an example.

Notice the opinion marker “indeed.” “Indeed” signals that the author supports the consumer protection advocates’ opinion above that planned obsolescence is exploitative.

Note that “would be hard-pressed to justify” means “would have trouble justifying.” Basically, when the author says, “there are certain cases that even the most pro-business advocates would be hard-pressed to justify,” he is saying that there are some examples that are almost indisputably exploitative. Notice the strong language, “even the most.”

Notice the support marker “such as,” which introduces the example of the “infamous Phoebus cartel.” We can clearly see that this case of deliberately reducing the lifespan of bulbs to 1,000 hours is an example of “planned obsolescence” as defined in S1 — “supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for the reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Simplification: Indeed, some examples of planned obsolescence are almost indisputably exploitative, such as the Phoebus cartel’s deliberate reduction of the lifespan of light bulbs.

S3: The author provides an additional example of planned obsolescence.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” S2 said that there are “certain cases” that are hard to justify, and provided one example. We can see that S3 provides a second example of planned obsolescence that is “hard to justify”: “designers explicitly discuss how to design products that will wear out as rapidly as possible while leaving customers with sufficient faith in a product to replace it immediately.”

Simplification: Annie Leonard cites evidence of the planned obsolescence strategy being discussed in industrial-design journals.

S4: The author introduces a different possible reason for frequent product replacement.

Notice the contrast marker, “at the same time,” which has a similar meaning to “meanwhile,” and can be thought of as a softer version of “nevertheless.” This marker is used here to transition to discussing a different possible reason for frequent product replacement; this reason is “a demand-side phenomenon.”

At this point, it may not be totally clear what is meant by “demand-side phenomenon,” but we can expect to gain more context as we read further. Indeed, the next sentence starts with “For example.”

Particularly once we’ve read further, we can see that “demand-side phenomenon” means “related to consumer preferences.”

Also notice that “demand-side” is in contrast to the word “supplying” in S1: “‘planned obsolescence’ — the business strategy that involves supplying the market with products that are intentionally designed for reduction of the time between repeat purchases.”

Notice that the author’s tone is descriptive, and the author doesn’t express a strong opinion of his own; the wording used is “an interesting argument has been made that.” The word “interesting” is quite neutral; it does not express strong support of or opposition to this argument.

S5: Provides an example to support the point made in S4.

Notice the support marker “for example.”

Notice the contrast marker “although.” This example is being cited as a “demand-side phenomenon” unlike the “planned obsolescence” described in the 1st paragraph. Although the phones are replaced very frequently, the reason for it is NOT that the phones have worn out; S5 says that “the vast majority of the phones they discard are still in good working order.” The author is mentioning this example because it is different from the reason for frequent replacement discussed in the 1st paragraph — products that are intentionally designed to wear out quickly.

S6: Expands on the example in S5, by adding details about why people replace their smartphones so frequently.

Notice the contrast marker “while X, Y.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Here, the Y part says that Walker “has a strong desire to stay current with the latest impressive technological innovations.” This is an example of the “demand-side phenomenon” introduced in S4.

S7: Expands on the point in S6 about Walker’s motivation

Notice the conclusion marker “therefore,” which connects Walker’s “strong desire to stay current,” in S6, to the point in S7.

Notice the contrast marker “instead of X, Y”: “instead of resenting the cost of replacement, he looks forward to upgrading his device regularly.” This positive attitude is different from the critical attitude of the “consumer protection advocates” in S1.

S8: Quotes Walker’s general conclusion.

The quote of Walker’s conclusion summarizes the key point in S4 that “frequent product replacement has sometimes become a demand-side phenomenon.” Note that “exactly what we want” connects closely with the meaning of “demand-side.”

question.text: According to the passage, which of the following is true of the “acts” referred to in the highlighted text?

answers.text: They are motivated by the anticipation of material rewards.
Scientists have found a neural explanation for them that is beyond dispute.
They are intended to promote social acceptance and friendship.
People other than the person performing the acts are generally not aware of who performed these acts.
The primary motivation for these acts is to produce the hormones dopamine and oxytocin.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.
We can see from “According to the passage” that this is a Detail question.
The question asks about the “acts” in the highlighted text.So, we should see whether there are any clues indicating where we are likely to find important details about these acts.

A very useful clue here is the look above marker, “SUCH acts,” which tells us that we must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to.Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” Note that these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.In S2, the contrast marker “However” is a clue that these deeds are different from the acts of kindness discussed in S1 (kindness to family and kindness that is reciprocated).
We can see that S3 provides three details about the “acts”:
- They are “anonymous,” which means “of unknown origin, unnamed, unidentified.”
- They are “in the service of others.” Other similar wording in the passage includes “kindness, benevolence.”
- These others “have no ability to return the favor.” In other words, unlike the other type of acts of kindness described in S1, these acts are not reciprocated.
The correct answer will likely connect to some aspect of these three details.
(A) They are motivated by the anticipation of material rewards.
Trap: Mix-up

If you picked this choice, you may have been looking at S1, which says “it is well recognized that this kindness will often bereciprocated, whetherfinancially, socially, or otherwise.” “Reciprocated … financially” matches with “anticipation of material rewards.”
However, is “this kindness” in S1 referring to the same “acts” that this question is asking about?
No.As we saw in our Question Analysis, unlike the other type of acts of kindness described in S1, the “acts” in the highlighted text are anonymous and are not reciprocated.
(B) Scientists have found a neural explanation for them that is beyond dispute.
Trap: Half-Right / Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “Scientists have found a neural explanation for them” matches the passage.In S4, “neural bases” matches with “neural explanation,” and the rest the of the 2ndparagraph provides the details of the scientists’ findings and explanations.
However, does the passage support the end of this choice, “that is beyond dispute”?
Note that “beyond dispute” is a very strong statement that means “proven, unquestionable.”
In S8, the passage uses the wording “scientistscontendedthat X,” which suggests that the scientists’ explanation is an opinion and is not necessarily proven.

Furthermore, additional evidence that goes against this choice comes in S9, in which the author says that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the scientists’ explanation.So, the passage is not making the strong statement that the scientists’ explanation is “beyond dispute.”
(C) They are intended to promote social acceptance and friendship.
Trap: Mix-Up

If you picked this choice, you may have been looking at S1, which says “it is well recognized that this kindness will often bereciprocated, whether financially,socially, or otherwise.” “Reciprocated … socially” potentially matches with “intended to promote social acceptance and friendship.”
However, is “this kindness” in S1 referring to the same “acts” that this question is asking about?
No, as we saw in our Question Analysis, unlike the type of kindness described in S1, the “acts” in the highlighted text are anonymous and are not reciprocated.
YOUR CHOICE(D) People other than the person performing the acts are generally not aware of who performed these acts.
We saw in our Question Analysis that the acts in the highlighted text are “anonymous,” which means “of unknown origin, unnamed, unidentified.”
This choice uses different wording, but does the meaning match?
Yes, if acts are “anonymous,” it means that other people are not aware of who performed the acts.
So, this choice matches what the passage says about the acts referred to in the highlighted text.
(E) The primary motivation for these acts is to produce the hormones dopamine and oxytocin.
Trap: Stretch / Mix-Up

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S8 says that “Both hormones,dopamine and oxytocin, cause an individual to experience immediate positive sensations, sothese scientists contended that biological factorsplay an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”
So, the scientists do indeed contend that dopamine and oxytocin are involved in the motivation for these acts.
However, there are two problems with this choice:

First, notice theextreme marker, “PRIMARY motivation,” which means “the most important motivation,” in comparison with all other possible motivations.However, S8 does not say that these hormones are themostimportant motivation.It uses the less extreme wording “play an important role.”

Second, the question asks what is true “according to the passage,” but it is the scientists, not the passage or the author, who are of the opinion that “factors” involving dopamine and oxytocin “play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.” Notice that S8 includes theopinion marker“thesescientists contendedthat X.” So, this choice is aMix-Uptrap, since the question asks what the passage or author says, while this choice resembles what the scientists, not the passage, contended.It’s important to remember to distinguish the author’s view from the opinions of people mentioned in the passage.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting some information about “people’s benevolence”; S1 says that people’s benevolence both is directed toward their kin and extends to those outside their families.

This information presented by S1 turns out to be background information; the first paragraph makes its point in S2 and S3.

S2 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals both that it’s likely that an important point is coming and that a contrast is being made. What are the two things that are contrasted? S1 has introduced one type of generous behavior, which is often reciprocated, and now, S2 introduces a different type of generous behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is a “perplexing phenomenon to scientists.” Anonymous altruistic behavior turns out to be the main topic of the passage.

Having brought up “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2, the first paragraph sets the stage for the rest of the passage with S3. S3 has a rhetorical question marker(question mark). Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question begins with “What would motivate individuals to …,” which means, essentially, “Why?” When the author asks “Why?” we have a causation marker, indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then discussed, in the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. We must pay close attention whenever explanations are discussed, because explanations tend to be important parts of passages.

S3 also defines “anonymous altruistic behavior, saying that it is “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” In defining “anonymous altruistic behavior,” S3 gives the reader a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the generous behavior described in S1, which is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

1st paragraph summary: Both scientists and other scholars are interested in explaining anonymous altruistic behavior. Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible answer the rhetorical question in S3. The possible answer, or explanation, is that “there are neural bases for such acts.” The look above marker in S4, “SUCH acts,” refers to the “anonymous deeds” in S3. So, this marker helps us understand the relationship between S4 and S3.

Then, in S5, S6, and S7, the 2nd paragraph provides some details of how “researchers” investigated this possible explanation and what they found. These details are rather involved. So, our goal in reading these sentences is not necessarily to totally understand or seek to memorize every one of these details but rather to understand in general how the study described worked.

In S8, we see the opinion marker, “contended.” Here, the main conclusion of the researchers is presented: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author’s tone in the 2nd paragraph is descriptive, and the author does not provide her own opinion.

2nd paragraph summary: The author presents one possible explanation for anonymous altruistic behavior: having studied brain activity, researchers concluded that hormones that cause positive sensations, i.e., biological factors, play an important role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

The 3rd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

In this case, by using “while X, Y” in S9, the author connects the 2nd paragraph to the 3rd paragraph; in the “while X” part of S9, the author concedes that the argument presented in the 2nd paragraph “may … lead to some useful insights,” and then, in the Y part of S9, the author presents the topic of the 3rd paragraph, the “more compelling perspective” of Matthieu Ricard. (Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.)

S10 and S11 provide details about Ricard’s perspective and the types of evidence he uses to support his points.

Finally, in S12, the author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.” Notice that, in judging Ricard’s conclusion “persuasive,” the author is once again stating an opinion of her own.

Also, we can see that there is clear contrast because Ricard’s conclusion and that of the researchers discussed in the second paragraph. S12 says that Ricard’s “persuasive case” is that serving others can be “genuinely selfless,” whereas S8 says that the researchers discussed in the second paragraph contend that such behavior can be “seemingly selfless.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author presents a second explanation that she believes is more compelling than the “biological argument” in the 2nd paragraph: Ricard’s perspective is that the motivation for serving others can be genuinely selfless and that this behavior can be cultivated through specific practices.

Passage summary: The author poses a question about why people engage in anonymous altruistic behavior, presents a biological explanation based on a brain imaging study, and then presents a second explanation that the author prefers.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: To answer the questions correctly, we don’t need to know the technical vocabulary: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), mesolimbic pathway, neurotransmitter, dopamine, hypothalamus, oxytocin, hormones, empathy-altruism hypothesis. We can understand the meaning of each sentence from the surrounding language and the definitions that are provided in the passage. (For example, the passage defines the mesolimbic pathway: “the mesolimbic pathway, the reward center of the brain that is responsible for releasing the neurotransmitter associated with pleasure.”)
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice causation marker “because.”

Simplification: People are often kind to not only relatives but also others, because this kindness is often returned.

S2: Notice the contrast marker “however.” This sentence introduces a different type of kind behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is “perplexing to scientists,” meaning that it is harder to explain.

Note: we may not be sure what the meaning of “anonymous altruistic behavior” is at first, but we are basically given a definition in the next sentence. In general, we can look to the surrounding context if we don’t understand something.

Simplification: A different type of kind behavior, anonymous altruistic behavior, has been puzzling for scientists to explain and an important topic for other scholars.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

The question posed, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …?” is asking about the reason/cause/explanation for these anonymous deeds. So, we can expect the rest of the passage to discuss possible explanations.

This sentence also helps the reader to understand the full meaning of S2. It defines what is meant by the “anonymous altruistic behavior” introduced in S2: “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” It also gives us a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the kind behavior described in S1 that is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

Simplification: Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

S4: Notice the look above marker “SUCH acts.” We must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to. Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” As we noted in our analysis of S3, these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible explanation to answer the rhetorical question in S3.

Note: if we don’t know what “neural” means, we can see from S5 that it’s related to brain activity: “researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brain.”

Simplification: A certain study sought to find an explanation, related to brain activity, for anonymous altruistic behavior.

S5: Provides details about the design of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: Using fMRI, the researchers analyzed the subjects’ brain activity while the subjects decided whether to keep money or make a donation.

S6: Provides details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: The reward center of the brain that releases dopamine, which is associated with pleasure, became active when a donation was made.

S7: Adds more details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” S7 is very similar to S6, but discusses a different brain region and hormone.

Simplification: The region of the brain that produces oxytocin, which is associated with cooperation and trust, also became active when a donation was made.

S8: Notice the opinion marker “contended.” Also, notice that the word “so” connects a premise and a conclusion, in the structure “Premise, so Conclusion”

This sentence presents what the researchers have concluded from the findings of their study presented in the previous sentences.

Simplification: Since dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations, the scientists argue that biological factors play a role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

S9: Introduces a “compelling,” i.e., persuasive, alternative to the “biological argument” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Notice that the wording “THIS biological argument” is a look above marker and refers to the biological argument directly above in S8: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument.

Simplification: There is another perspective, that of Matthieu Ricard, which is more compelling than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

S10: Provides details about Ricard’s perspective.

Notice that “but” is a contrast marker in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard’s perspective is that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally, but it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.

S11: Discusses a type of evidence that Ricard uses, cognitive science research.

Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.

S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.”

Notice that the author repeats her positive judgment of Ricard’s perspective: S9 says that it’s “compelling,” and S12 says that it’s “persuasive.”

Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be genuinely selfless.” It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.

question.text: The passage suggests that Ricard would most likely agree with which of the following statements about generous behavior towards others?

answers.text: It can seem more natural for people to direct generosity towards people whom they know than towards people unfamiliar to them.
Generous behavior is not affected by changes in the levels of dopamine and oxytocin in people’s brains.
It is more virtuous to practice anonymous altruism than to be generous towards friends and family.
Anonymous altruism will be engaged in by anyone who has meditated on love and compassion.
In most cases, generous behavior is genuinely selfless.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
Notice that the stem says, “the passagesuggests.” So, this is an Inference question.

More specifically, the wording “Ricard would most likely agree withwhich of the following statements about generous behavior towards others” means that we must find Ricard’s opinion about “generous behavior towards others.”
Since “Ricard” is capitalized, it is relatively easy to notice that “Ricard” appears in S10, S11, and S12.

Ideally, by using other keywords in the stem, we could narrow the area of the passage in which to look for information.However, in this case, the phrase “generous behavior towards others” has a more general meaning that is discussed throughout the 3rdparagraph.So, we will need to read all three sentences carefully.
Let’s review some of our Sentence Analysis:

S10: Notice that “but” is a contrast marker, in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard is saying that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally but that it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.
S11: Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.
S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion that “the motivation for serving others can be genuinely selfless.”
Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CANbe genuinely selfless” in S12.It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.
YOUR CHOICE(A) It can seem more natural for people to direct generosity towards people whom they know than towards people unfamiliar to them.
Since the word “natural” is similar to the words “instinctive” and “innate” in S10, we must examine S10 closely to see whether the wording of this choice matches Ricard’s opinion in the passage.

In our Question Analysis, we noted a contrast mentioned by S10.S10 communicates that Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.
Notice that “generosity” is this choice has a similar meaning to “kindness” in S10.
Also, “people whom they know” in this choice matches “friends and family” in S10.
Finally, “people unfamiliar to them” in this choice matches with “anonymous altruism” in S10.After all, “anonymous altruism” means “kindness from an unknown person.”

So, this choice uses different wording to say in a new way what S10 says, which is that Ricard believes that “in contrast to the virtually instinctive kindness typically offered to friends and family, anonymous altruism does not always appear to be an innate behavior.”
(B) Generous behavior is not affected by changes in the levels of dopamine and oxytocin in people’s brains.
Trap: Speculation / Mix-Up
If you picked this choice, you may have speculated that Ricard disagrees with the conclusion of the scientists of the study, in S8.

However, the passage does not say that Ricard has any specific opinion about the scientists’ conclusion regarding dopamine and oxytocin.The passage indicates only that Ricard has a completely separate perspective.
It’s important to notice that in S9, where it says “a more compelling perspective … can be found in … Ricard,” this comparison is theauthor’sopinion, not Ricard’s opinion.
(C) It is more virtuous to practice anonymous altruism than to be generous towards friends and family.
Trap: Speculation

If you picked this choice, you may have made a speculation about Ricard’s opinion that isn’t supported by the passage.Note that “virtuous” means “showing high moral standards.” Sure, in real life, many people might believe that it is “more virtuous” to be generous towards people that you don’t know, in an “anonymous” manner.However, what does the passage actually say about Ricard’s opinion?

Notice that the two things compared in this choice, “anonymous altruism” and generosity “towards friends and family,” are indeed compared in the passage in S10 (with thecontrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y”).
However, whatmeasureis being used to compare these two things?
In the passage, the comparison is about how natural (instinctive, innate) the generosity is.In contrast, this choice uses the measure “morevirtuous.”
We can’t equate how natural something is and how “virtuous” it is, so we must eliminate this trap choice.
(D) Anonymous altruism will be engaged in by anyone who has meditated on love and compassion.
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S10 says that Ricard demonstrates that anonymous altruism “can be substantially cultivated through specific practices, such as meditating on love and compassion.”

However, notice that the passage uses the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be … cultivated.” This wording allows for possible exceptions and means that it’s possible that not every person who has meditated on love and compassion will engage in anonymous altruism.
On the other hand, this choice uses the extreme marker “anyone.” In this context, “anyone” conveys that every person who has meditated on love and compassion will engage in anonymous altruism.
So, what this choice says is more extreme than what the passage says.
As always, we must read carefully, notice markers, and not go beyond what the passage says.
(E) In most cases, generous behavior is genuinely selfless.
Trap: Stretch
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S12 says: “Ricard formulates a persuasive case that the motivation for serving otherscan be genuinely selfless.”

However, notice that the passage uses the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “can.” Much like the word “some,” the word “can” has a vague meaning and doesn’t specify how common this selfless behavior is.
On the other hand, this choice uses “MOST cases,” which has the specific definition of “more than half of the cases.”
So, we can see that the exact wording of this choice goes beyond what the passage says.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting some information about “people’s benevolence”; S1 says that people’s benevolence both is directed toward their kin and extends to those outside their families.

This information presented by S1 turns out to be background information; the first paragraph makes its point in S2 and S3.

S2 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals both that it’s likely that an important point is coming and that a contrast is being made. What are the two things that are contrasted? S1 has introduced one type of generous behavior, which is often reciprocated, and now, S2 introduces a different type of generous behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is a “perplexing phenomenon to scientists.” Anonymous altruistic behavior turns out to be the main topic of the passage.

Having brought up “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2, the first paragraph sets the stage for the rest of the passage with S3. S3 has a rhetorical question marker(question mark). Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question begins with “What would motivate individuals to …,” which means, essentially, “Why?” When the author asks “Why?” we have a causation marker, indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then discussed, in the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. We must pay close attention whenever explanations are discussed, because explanations tend to be important parts of passages.

S3 also defines “anonymous altruistic behavior, saying that it is “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” In defining “anonymous altruistic behavior,” S3 gives the reader a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the generous behavior described in S1, which is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

1st paragraph summary: Both scientists and other scholars are interested in explaining anonymous altruistic behavior. Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible answer the rhetorical question in S3. The possible answer, or explanation, is that “there are neural bases for such acts.” The look above marker in S4, “SUCH acts,” refers to the “anonymous deeds” in S3. So, this marker helps us understand the relationship between S4 and S3.

Then, in S5, S6, and S7, the 2nd paragraph provides some details of how “researchers” investigated this possible explanation and what they found. These details are rather involved. So, our goal in reading these sentences is not necessarily to totally understand or seek to memorize every one of these details but rather to understand in general how the study described worked.

In S8, we see the opinion marker, “contended.” Here, the main conclusion of the researchers is presented: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author’s tone in the 2nd paragraph is descriptive, and the author does not provide her own opinion.

2nd paragraph summary: The author presents one possible explanation for anonymous altruistic behavior: having studied brain activity, researchers concluded that hormones that cause positive sensations, i.e., biological factors, play an important role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

The 3rd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

In this case, by using “while X, Y” in S9, the author connects the 2nd paragraph to the 3rd paragraph; in the “while X” part of S9, the author concedes that the argument presented in the 2nd paragraph “may … lead to some useful insights,” and then, in the Y part of S9, the author presents the topic of the 3rd paragraph, the “more compelling perspective” of Matthieu Ricard. (Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.)

S10 and S11 provide details about Ricard’s perspective and the types of evidence he uses to support his points.

Finally, in S12, the author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.” Notice that, in judging Ricard’s conclusion “persuasive,” the author is once again stating an opinion of her own.

Also, we can see that there is clear contrast because Ricard’s conclusion and that of the researchers discussed in the second paragraph. S12 says that Ricard’s “persuasive case” is that serving others can be “genuinely selfless,” whereas S8 says that the researchers discussed in the second paragraph contend that such behavior can be “seemingly selfless.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author presents a second explanation that she believes is more compelling than the “biological argument” in the 2nd paragraph: Ricard’s perspective is that the motivation for serving others can be genuinely selfless and that this behavior can be cultivated through specific practices.

Passage summary: The author poses a question about why people engage in anonymous altruistic behavior, presents a biological explanation based on a brain imaging study, and then presents a second explanation that the author prefers.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.
