Note: To answer the questions correctly, we don’t need to know the technical vocabulary: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), mesolimbic pathway, neurotransmitter, dopamine, hypothalamus, oxytocin, hormones, empathy-altruism hypothesis. We can understand the meaning of each sentence from the surrounding language and the definitions that are provided in the passage. (For example, the passage defines the mesolimbic pathway: “the mesolimbic pathway, the reward center of the brain that is responsible for releasing the neurotransmitter associated with pleasure.”)
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice causation marker “because.”

Simplification: People are often kind to not only relatives but also others, because this kindness is often returned.

S2: Notice the contrast marker “however.” This sentence introduces a different type of kind behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is “perplexing to scientists,” meaning that it is harder to explain.

Note: we may not be sure what the meaning of “anonymous altruistic behavior” is at first, but we are basically given a definition in the next sentence. In general, we can look to the surrounding context if we don’t understand something.

Simplification: A different type of kind behavior, anonymous altruistic behavior, has been puzzling for scientists to explain and an important topic for other scholars.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

The question posed, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …?” is asking about the reason/cause/explanation for these anonymous deeds. So, we can expect the rest of the passage to discuss possible explanations.

This sentence also helps the reader to understand the full meaning of S2. It defines what is meant by the “anonymous altruistic behavior” introduced in S2: “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” It also gives us a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the kind behavior described in S1 that is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

Simplification: Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

S4: Notice the look above marker “SUCH acts.” We must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to. Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” As we noted in our analysis of S3, these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible explanation to answer the rhetorical question in S3.

Note: if we don’t know what “neural” means, we can see from S5 that it’s related to brain activity: “researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brain.”

Simplification: A certain study sought to find an explanation, related to brain activity, for anonymous altruistic behavior.

S5: Provides details about the design of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: Using fMRI, the researchers analyzed the subjects’ brain activity while the subjects decided whether to keep money or make a donation.

S6: Provides details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: The reward center of the brain that releases dopamine, which is associated with pleasure, became active when a donation was made.

S7: Adds more details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” S7 is very similar to S6, but discusses a different brain region and hormone.

Simplification: The region of the brain that produces oxytocin, which is associated with cooperation and trust, also became active when a donation was made.

S8: Notice the opinion marker “contended.” Also, notice that the word “so” connects a premise and a conclusion, in the structure “Premise, so Conclusion”

This sentence presents what the researchers have concluded from the findings of their study presented in the previous sentences.

Simplification: Since dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations, the scientists argue that biological factors play a role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

S9: Introduces a “compelling,” i.e., persuasive, alternative to the “biological argument” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Notice that the wording “THIS biological argument” is a look above marker and refers to the biological argument directly above in S8: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument.

Simplification: There is another perspective, that of Matthieu Ricard, which is more compelling than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

S10: Provides details about Ricard’s perspective.

Notice that “but” is a contrast marker in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard’s perspective is that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally, but it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.

S11: Discusses a type of evidence that Ricard uses, cognitive science research.

Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.

S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.”

Notice that the author repeats her positive judgment of Ricard’s perspective: S9 says that it’s “compelling,” and S12 says that it’s “persuasive.”

Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be genuinely selfless.” It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.

question.text: According to the passage, which of the following statements about the hypothalamus region of the brain is true?

answers.text: It is never inactive when a person makes a donation to a societal cause.
The role it plays in anonymous altruistic behavior is as important as that played by the mesolimbic pathway.
The more cooperative a person is, the more oxytocin it produces.
If it is engaged, then a person has made a donation.
The activity of it can be examined through the use of fMRI.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
We can see from “According to the passage” that this is a Detail question.

The question asks about the “hypothalamus region of the brain.” Since “hypothalamus” is a technical word, we know that we will be able to find this exact keyword in the passage.We find “hypothalamus” in S7.
S7 says two things about the hypothalamus region:
- It “produces oxytocin.”
- It “usually also became engaged when a donation was made.”

Also, notice the additional point marker at the start of S7, “furthermore.” This marker tells us that this sentence about the hypothalamus is probably connected to another point located more than one sentence above.So, we can check to see whether there are any related details, more than one sentence above, that connect.

Looking above S7, we see that S5 provides an additional related detail, “The researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brains.” So, S5 indicates that the information in S7 was found by scientists using fMRI to analyze the activity of “the hypothalamus region of the brain.”
(A) It is never inactive when a person makes a donation to a societal cause.
Trap: Stretch
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S7 says that the hypothalamus region “usually also became engaged when a donation was made.” (Note that “engaged” is the opposite of “inactive.”)

However, notice the extreme marker in this choice, “NEVER inactive,” whereas the passage includes the qualifier marker “USUALLY engaged.” The word “usually” allows for some potential exceptions, in which the hypothalamus region is inactive despite a donation being made.So, this choice goes beyond what the passage says.
(B) The role it plays in anonymous altruistic behavior is as important as that played by the mesolimbic pathway.
Trap: Wrong Comparison
Notice the comparison marker in this choice, “AS important AS.” What two things are compared?

This choice compares the importance of the hypothalamus and the mesolimbic pathway, but the passage does not make this comparison between them.The passage only suggests that they are both important, in S8.
(C) The more cooperative a person is, the more oxytocin it produces.
Trap: Nearby / Wrong Comparison

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S7 says, “the hypothalamus region of the brain, which produces oxytocin, a neurotransmitter that increases cooperation and trust.” Indeed, this sentence does suggest that there is a connection between oxytocin and cooperation.
However, this choice does not match what the passage says.
First, the passage says that production of oxytocin increases cooperation, but this choice says the reverse, that cooperation increases oxytocin.

Second, this choice implies that there is a consistent correlation between the two things: the more one increases, the more the second increases.However, such a correlation is not supported by the passage.The passage says that the two things are connected, but not necessarily in such a consistent manner.
(D) If it is engaged, then a person has made a donation.
Trap: Distortion / Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S7 says that there is a connection between making a donation and the engagement of the hypothalamus: it “usually also became engaged when a donation was made.”
However, this choice says the reverse of what the passage says.
Basically, the passage says, “If a donation is made, then usually the hypothalamus is engaged.”
On the other hand, this choice means, “If the hypothalamus is engaged, then a donation has been made.”

Notice that what this choice says doesn’t necessarily follow from what the passage says.After all, given what the passage says, it could be that the hypothalamus becomes engaged not only when a donation is made but also when other things occur.In that case, if the hypothalamus is engaged, then it’s possible that a donation has been made, but it’s also possible that something else has occurred to cause the hypothalamus to become engaged and that a donation has not been made.
Thus, what the passage says does not mean that, if the hypothalamus is engaged, “then a person has made a donation.”
YOUR CHOICE(E) The activity of it can be examined through the use of fMRI.

In our Question Analysis, we noted that S5 and S7 indicate that information about the hypothalamus was found by scientists using fMRI to analyze the activity of “the hypothalamus region of the brain.”
Thus, according to the passage, the activity of the hypothalamus can be analyzed, or examined through the use of fMRI.
So, we can see that this choice is supported by the passage and answers the question.

Note: we may not be sure what the meaning of “anonymous altruistic behavior” is at first, but we are basically given a definition in the next sentence. In general, we can look to the surrounding context if we don’t understand something.

Simplification: A different type of kind behavior, anonymous altruistic behavior, has been puzzling for scientists to explain and an important topic for other scholars.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

The question posed, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …?” is asking about the reason/cause/explanation for these anonymous deeds. So, we can expect the rest of the passage to discuss possible explanations.

This sentence also helps the reader to understand the full meaning of S2. It defines what is meant by the “anonymous altruistic behavior” introduced in S2: “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” It also gives us a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the kind behavior described in S1 that is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

Simplification: Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

S4: Notice the look above marker “SUCH acts.” We must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to. Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” As we noted in our analysis of S3, these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible explanation to answer the rhetorical question in S3.

Note: if we don’t know what “neural” means, we can see from S5 that it’s related to brain activity: “researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brain.”

Simplification: A certain study sought to find an explanation, related to brain activity, for anonymous altruistic behavior.

S5: Provides details about the design of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: Using fMRI, the researchers analyzed the subjects’ brain activity while the subjects decided whether to keep money or make a donation.

S6: Provides details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: The reward center of the brain that releases dopamine, which is associated with pleasure, became active when a donation was made.

S7: Adds more details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” S7 is very similar to S6, but discusses a different brain region and hormone.

Simplification: The region of the brain that produces oxytocin, which is associated with cooperation and trust, also became active when a donation was made.

S8: Notice the opinion marker “contended.” Also, notice that the word “so” connects a premise and a conclusion, in the structure “Premise, so Conclusion”

This sentence presents what the researchers have concluded from the findings of their study presented in the previous sentences.

Simplification: Since dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations, the scientists argue that biological factors play a role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

S9: Introduces a “compelling,” i.e., persuasive, alternative to the “biological argument” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Notice that the wording “THIS biological argument” is a look above marker and refers to the biological argument directly above in S8: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument.

Simplification: There is another perspective, that of Matthieu Ricard, which is more compelling than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

S10: Provides details about Ricard’s perspective.

Notice that “but” is a contrast marker in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard’s perspective is that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally, but it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.

S11: Discusses a type of evidence that Ricard uses, cognitive science research.

Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.

S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.”

Notice that the author repeats her positive judgment of Ricard’s perspective: S9 says that it’s “compelling,” and S12 says that it’s “persuasive.”

Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be genuinely selfless.” It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.

question.text: It can be inferred that the author of the passage would most likely agree with which of the following statements about the safety of nuclear power generation?

answers.text: Concerns about the potential for the use of nuclear power to result in radiation exposure are baseless.
Nuclear power generation is safer than the vast majority of other methods of energy production.
Nuclear power generation is less safe than at least some methods of generating electricity.
US residents’ understanding of the threats posed by nuclear power generation has room for improvement.
The fact that nuclear waste can be safely stored in dry casks shows that any safety concerns about nuclear power are unwarranted.

explanation_parts.text: The correct answer to this question will state something with which the author would agree about “safety” of “nuclear power generation.” Nuclear power generation is discussed throughout the passage, but “safety” is more specific.So, we must ask ourselves: where is the author’s opinion aboutsafetydiscussed?
The author discusses “risk” and “peril” in S4, and his main point is that “theactualperil involved in nuclear power generation ishighly overstated.”
Simply put, the author argues thatnuclear power is much safer than people think.

“Safety” is also specifically discussed in S5 and S6, so it is a good idea to make sure we clearly understand these sentences as well.From our Sentence Analysis, S5 says that statistics show that nuclear power generation is safer than other common methods of energy production, particularly use of coal power.S6 says that nuclear waste isn’t a significant problem, because it takes up a very small volume and can be safely stored.
(A) Concerns about the potential for the use of nuclear power to result in radiation exposure are baseless.
Trap: Stretch
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the author does challenge the safety concerns of the “populace” discussed in the 1stparagraph.
However, note that “baseless” a strong judgment word that means “wrong, unsupported, without foundation in fact.”

Therefore, this choice has the extreme meaning that there are no legitimate reasons for any concerns about radiation exposure, whereas the author’s opinion in the passage is less extreme.The author is saying that nuclear power is muchsaferthan people think.This is a comparison statement, not an absolute statement.

It is also helpful to notice the “although X, Y” structure at the start of S4.In the “X” part, the author acknowledges that there is “at least some risk to every method of energy production.” So, the author is taking a balanced view of the situation, and this choice is more extreme than what the passage says.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Nuclear power generation is safer than the vast majority of other methods of energy production.
Trap: Stretch

This choice is a tempting trap.If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that S4 says that “nuclear power generation is statistically the safest among the eight most common methods of energy production.”
If we carefully compare this quote from the passage to this choice, can we notice a difference?

Yes, we can.“The eight most common” is different from “the vast majority of other methods.” After all, it could be that, aside from the eight most common, there are many other methods that are safer than nuclear power generation.We must be careful not to go beyond what the passage says.
(C) Nuclear power generation is less safe than at least some methods of generating electricity.
Trap: Opposite
Trap: Speculation

Notice that S4 says that “nuclear power generation is statistically the safest among the eight most common methods of energy production.” So, looking at just these eight most common methods, the passage says the opposite of this choice.

What about methods aside from these eight most common? It’s easy to speculate that nuclear power generation is less safe than some of these other methods, but the passage doesn’t say anything about other methods.So, we have no reason to believe that the author would agree with this choice.
We must stick to what the passage says and watch out for Speculation traps.
CORRECT ANSWER(D) US residents’ understanding of the threats posed by nuclear power generation has room for improvement.

The author discusses “risk” and “peril” in S4, and his main point is that “theactualperil involved in nuclear power generation ishighly overstated.” Notice that the author is challenging the belief of the “populace of the United States” mentioned in S1.“Populace of the United States” matches with “US residents” in this choice.
Simply put, the author argues thatnuclear power is much safer than many US residents think.
If we compare this choice with the passage, we can see that “understanding of the threats posed … has room for improvement” has similar meaning to “the actual peril … is highly overstated.”
So, it makes sense that the author would agree with what this choice says.
(E) The fact that nuclear waste can be safely stored in dry casks shows that any safety concerns about nuclear power are unwarranted.
Trap: Stretch
Notice the extreme marker “any,” which is a yellow flag.In this context, “any” means “all.”
Also note that “unwarranted” is a strong opinion word that means “unjustified.”
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that, in S6, the author suggests that the concerns about nuclear waste are overstated, because this waste can be safely stored in dry casks.

However, this choice goes too far in saying that ANY safety concerns about nuclear power are unwarranted.In citing this fact about dry casks in S6, the author is suggesting that thisone specific concern about nuclear wasteis undue

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It’s helpful to notice that the passage begins by presenting a common belief: S1 says, “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.” When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion, with the two contrasting views forming the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented by S1 involves two negative ideas about nuclear power plants: that they are “particularly hazardous” and that they are “uniquely expensive.”

S2 and S3 then add supporting details that explain why people have fears of nuclear power. S2 provides the example of nuclear disaster in Chernobyl, and S3 mentions fear of radiation exposure and the health problems that can result from it.

Notice that, in the 1st paragraph, the author presents a common opinion but doesn’t present an opinion of his own.

1st paragraph summary: Many people in the US believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive and associate nuclear power plants with scary images of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster and with radiation exposure.

In S4, notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part of S4:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s “trepidation and disgust” related to nuclear power.

In the X part of S4, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that there is “some risk” involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.” Indeed, details about both safety and environmental benefits follow.

As we might expect, the rest of the 2nd paragraph provides details to support the author’s main point in S4. S5 and S6 address safety concerns, with specific statistics about the safety benefits of nuclear power. S7 introduces environmental benefits. S8 cites an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

2nd paragraph summary: The author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that there are both safety-related and environmental benefits to overcoming negative perceptions of nuclear power. The author supports his point with statistics, facts, and examples.

At this point, we may have a sense that the primary purpose of this passage is to challenge many people’s concerns that nuclear power plants are “particularly hazardous” and “uniquely expensive.”

We have seen that, in the 2nd paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, in the 3rd paragraph, the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” So, in the 3rd paragraph, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

In S9, notice the contrast marker “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part. Here, the author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10 supports the point stated by S9, saying that recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

In S11, the additional point marker “moreover” is a useful clue indicating that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above — in this case, the point above in S9. The author concludes the passage by stating a point similar to S9’s point, “overcoming these hurdles rapidly is … feasible,” saying “the future of nuclear power is promising.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly. The author supports his point that a rapid shift to nuclear power is possible, by citing two specific developments: new economical reactors and political support.

In reviewing the passage as a whole, it is important to note that the author’s overall tone is argumentative, not just descriptive. Throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the author presents a strong opinion in support of nuclear power and provides evidence of multiple types of benefits of nuclear power: safety, environmental, and economic.

Passage summary: The author presents and then challenges the impression widely held in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and uniquely expensive.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: We don’t need to understand the technical word “terawatt-hour” in S5 to answer the questions correctly. However, from the context, we can infer that it is a unit of energy. If you are curious, “tera” is a unit prefix in the metric system that means multiplication by one trillion, or 1012. Another common use of “tera” is in “terabytes” (TB), in the context of memory capacity.

We also don’t need to understand the technical phrase “closed fuel cycle,” in S10.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice that a common belief is presented: “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.”

When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion. So, it is helpful to be aware of the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented in this sentence includes two negative ideas about nuclear power plants:

- “particularly hazardous”

- “uniquely expensive”

Simplification: Many people in the U.S. believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.

S2: Supports S1, with an example about why people believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous.

Simplification: These plants bring to mind scary images of the nuclear disaster in Chernobyl.

S3: Supports S1, with a detail about the fear of “radiation exposure.”

Simplification: Thinking about the dangers of radiation exposure provokes a fearful mindset.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s excessive fear of nuclear power.

In the X part, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that some risk is involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.”

Simplification: the author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that it is important to overcome this fear.

S5: Supports S4, with a statistic about the safety of nuclear power, as compared with the safety of other methods of energy production.

Simplification: Statistics show that nuclear power generation is safer than other common methods, particularly coal power.

S6: Supports S4, by addressing a concern about nuclear waste

Notice that “in reality” is an opinion/contrast marker. The author is saying that “concerns about nuclear waste” are not really a big deal, once you take into account certain facts.

Simplification: Nuclear waste isn’t a significant problem, because it can be safely stored and takes up a very small volume of space.

S7: Supports the point stated by S4 that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above.

S5 and S6 provided details about the safety benefits of nuclear power. Now, S7 introduces the environmental benefits.

Simplification: Also, nuclear power could have great environmental benefits, because it is the best way to prevent further global warming.

S8: Supports S4, with an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

Simplification: With a supportive political and regulatory situation, there can be a rapid shift to nuclear power, as we can see from the examples of France and Sweden.

S9: Here the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” In the second paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

Notice the contrast marker, “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Simplification: The author acknowledges that there are indeed economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US, but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10: Specifies an economic obstacle/problem and a potential solution:

Problem: “lack of standardization and repetition”

Solution: “Generation IV reactors that can be mass-produced”

Also, notice the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.” The final part of the sentence, “consequently, they will likely generate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation,” is a positive prediction about the future low relative cost of nuclear power.

Simplification: Recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

S11: Supports the main point in S9 that overcoming the economic obstacles rapidly is possible.

Notice the additional point marker “moreover,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above. We also see the phrase “additional economic benefits.” So, this sentence is expanding on the economic benefits discussed in S10 and playing a role similar to that of S10 in supporting S9.

Simplification: Since production of these new reactors may have many additional economic benefits, it has inspired significant political support, which is an encouraging sign for the future of nuclear power.

question.text: The passage suggests which of the following about nuclear power generation in France and Sweden?

answers.text: The cost of nuclear power generated electricity in these countries is equal to or lower than the prior cost of their fossil fuel generated electricity.
After these countries increased their nuclear power generation significantly, they no longer produced any electricity with fossil fuels.
The regulatory frameworks in these countries are more manageable than that in the United States.
The leaders of these countries believed that nuclear power generation was the best way to prevent further global warming.
Had these countries’ politicians been less favorable toward the use of nuclear power, their adoption of nuclear power may have proceeded more slowly.

explanation_parts.text: First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking.
Notice that the stem says “the passagesuggests,” so this is an Inference question.

More specifically, notice that the question asks about “France and Sweden.” These keywords are very helpful, because capitalized words are relatively easy to find in the passage.Notice that France and Sweden are discussed only in S8.So, we must review S8 carefully.
Simply put, S8 says that, with a supportive political and regulatory situation, there can be a rapid shift to nuclear power, as we can see from the examples of France and Sweden.
(A) The cost of nuclear power generated electricity in these countries is equal to or lower than the prior cost of their fossil fuel generated electricity.
Trap: Speculation
Trap: Nearby
It’s important to notice that S8 is the only sentence that discusses France and Sweden, so we must be careful to not make speculations based on other information in the passage.
Notice that S8 does not specify anything about the cost of nuclear power generation in these countries.

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the costs of nuclear power and fossil fuel generation are compared at the end of S10.However, S10 is about Generation IV reactors in the US, not nuclear power in France and Sweden.
So, this choice is not supported by the passage.
(B) After these countries increased their nuclear power generation significantly, they no longer produced any electricity with fossil fuels.
Trap: Stretch
This is a tempting trap, because it is quite close to what the passage says, but we must compare the passage and choice very carefully.
Notice the extreme marker “any,” which is a yellow flag.
Notice that S8 says, “which were able to replace VIRTUALLY ALL of their fossil fuel generated electricity.”
Since “virtually” means “almost,” the passage is saying that there were some exceptions.So, this choice is more extreme than what the passage says.
(C) The regulatory frameworks in these countries are more manageable than that in the United States.
Trap: Speculation
Trap: Wrong Comparison
If you picked this choice, you may have speculated that this choice is true, as it may seem logical in real life.
However, we must stick with what the passage actually says.
S10 does imply that the regulatory frameworks in France and Sweden are manageable.

Notice, however, that the passage does not say anything about the regulatory framework in the United States or make a comparison between the regulatory frameworks in France and Sweden and that in the United States.So, the passage does not support the statement that the regulatory frameworks in France and Sweden are “more” manageable than that in the United States.
(D) The leaders of these countries believed that nuclear power generation was the best way to prevent further global warming.
Trap: Speculation
If you picked this choice, you may have speculated that this choice is true, as it may seem logical in real life.

However, the passage doesn’t specify what the leadersbelieved.We must be careful not to spin a story about what’s going through people’s heads or what their motivations are.There are multiple scenarios that are possible that could run counter to this choice.For example, what if the leaders thought nuclear power generation was the 2ndbest way to prevent global warming but that it was a better political decision than the 1stbest option? What if the leaders didn’t care about global warming but feared that other power sources weren’t reliable?
YOUR CHOICE(E) Had these countries’ politicians been less favorable toward the use of nuclear power, their adoption of nuclear power may have proceeded more slowly.

Notice that the beginning of S8 uses the conditional “If X, (then) Y” structure: “if there is sufficient political support as well as a manageable regulatory framework in place, there can be rapid transformation.”

Then, S8 continues by using the examples of the success stories of France and Sweden as evidence that “if there is sufficient political support … there can be rapid transformation.” So, we can safely infer that there was “sufficient political support” for “rapid transformation” in France and Sweden.

Now, we can see that, in S8, “rapid transformation” refers to “replace virtually all of their fossil fuel generated electricity with nuclear power generated electricity within 20 years.” So, “transformation” matches the wording in this choice, “adoption of nuclear power.”
Thus, S8 implies that“sufficient political support”led torapid adoptionof nuclear power in France and Sweden.
This answer choice uses wording different from that of S8, but does it have the same meaning?
Yes, this choice says thatless political supportmay have led toslower adoptionof nuclear power in France and Sweden.

It is helpful to notice this common pattern in RC, in which the language in the answer choice is reversed from what the passage says, but the meaning matches.(Another way to think about reversed language is through a math example: if the passage were to say that “X is greater than Y,” an answer choice that says “Y is less than X” would be correct.)

In this case, the wording of this choice, “Had these countries’ politicians been less favorable … adoption of nuclear power may have proceeded more slowly,”is in a way the reverse of the wording in the passage, “if there is sufficient political support …, there can be rapid transformation,” while meaning essentially the same thing.

Notice that, in the 1st paragraph, the author presents a common opinion but doesn’t present an opinion of his own.

1st paragraph summary: Many people in the US believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive and associate nuclear power plants with scary images of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster and with radiation exposure.

In S4, notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part of S4:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s “trepidation and disgust” related to nuclear power.

In the X part of S4, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that there is “some risk” involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.” Indeed, details about both safety and environmental benefits follow.

As we might expect, the rest of the 2nd paragraph provides details to support the author’s main point in S4. S5 and S6 address safety concerns, with specific statistics about the safety benefits of nuclear power. S7 introduces environmental benefits. S8 cites an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

2nd paragraph summary: The author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that there are both safety-related and environmental benefits to overcoming negative perceptions of nuclear power. The author supports his point with statistics, facts, and examples.

At this point, we may have a sense that the primary purpose of this passage is to challenge many people’s concerns that nuclear power plants are “particularly hazardous” and “uniquely expensive.”

We have seen that, in the 2nd paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, in the 3rd paragraph, the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” So, in the 3rd paragraph, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

In S9, notice the contrast marker “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part. Here, the author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10 supports the point stated by S9, saying that recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

In S11, the additional point marker “moreover” is a useful clue indicating that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above — in this case, the point above in S9. The author concludes the passage by stating a point similar to S9’s point, “overcoming these hurdles rapidly is … feasible,” saying “the future of nuclear power is promising.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly. The author supports his point that a rapid shift to nuclear power is possible, by citing two specific developments: new economical reactors and political support.

In reviewing the passage as a whole, it is important to note that the author’s overall tone is argumentative, not just descriptive. Throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the author presents a strong opinion in support of nuclear power and provides evidence of multiple types of benefits of nuclear power: safety, environmental, and economic.

Passage summary: The author presents and then challenges the impression widely held in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and uniquely expensive.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: We don’t need to understand the technical word “terawatt-hour” in S5 to answer the questions correctly. However, from the context, we can infer that it is a unit of energy. If you are curious, “tera” is a unit prefix in the metric system that means multiplication by one trillion, or 1012. Another common use of “tera” is in “terabytes” (TB), in the context of memory capacity.

We also don’t need to understand the technical phrase “closed fuel cycle,” in S10.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice that a common belief is presented: “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.”

When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion. So, it is helpful to be aware of the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented in this sentence includes two negative ideas about nuclear power plants:

- “particularly hazardous”

- “uniquely expensive”

Simplification: Many people in the U.S. believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.

S2: Supports S1, with an example about why people believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous.

Simplification: These plants bring to mind scary images of the nuclear disaster in Chernobyl.

S3: Supports S1, with a detail about the fear of “radiation exposure.”

Simplification: Thinking about the dangers of radiation exposure provokes a fearful mindset.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s excessive fear of nuclear power.

In the X part, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that some risk is involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.”

Simplification: the author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that it is important to overcome this fear.

S5: Supports S4, with a statistic about the safety of nuclear power, as compared with the safety of other methods of energy production.

Simplification: Statistics show that nuclear power generation is safer than other common methods, particularly coal power.

S6: Supports S4, by addressing a concern about nuclear waste

Notice that “in reality” is an opinion/contrast marker. The author is saying that “concerns about nuclear waste” are not really a big deal, once you take into account certain facts.

Simplification: Nuclear waste isn’t a significant problem, because it can be safely stored and takes up a very small volume of space.

S7: Supports the point stated by S4 that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above.

S5 and S6 provided details about the safety benefits of nuclear power. Now, S7 introduces the environmental benefits.

Simplification: Also, nuclear power could have great environmental benefits, because it is the best way to prevent further global warming.

S8: Supports S4, with an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

Simplification: With a supportive political and regulatory situation, there can be a rapid shift to nuclear power, as we can see from the examples of France and Sweden.

S9: Here the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” In the second paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

Notice the contrast marker, “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Simplification: The author acknowledges that there are indeed economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US, but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10: Specifies an economic obstacle/problem and a potential solution:

Problem: “lack of standardization and repetition”

Solution: “Generation IV reactors that can be mass-produced”

Also, notice the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.” The final part of the sentence, “consequently, they will likely generate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation,” is a positive prediction about the future low relative cost of nuclear power.

Simplification: Recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

S11: Supports the main point in S9 that overcoming the economic obstacles rapidly is possible.

Notice the additional point marker “moreover,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above. We also see the phrase “additional economic benefits.” So, this sentence is expanding on the economic benefits discussed in S10 and playing a role similar to that of S10 in supporting S9.

Simplification: Since production of these new reactors may have many additional economic benefits, it has inspired significant political support, which is an encouraging sign for the future of nuclear power.

question.text: The passage suggests that which of the following statements about Generation IV reactors is true?

answers.text: The political attitude toward their creation is more positive than that toward some other matters.
They differ from most other nuclear reactors in that they use a closed fuel cycle.
The cost of fossil fuel generated electricity cannot be made lower than the expected cost of electricity produced by Generation IV reactors.
If they become mass-produced, there will no longer be economic obstacles to broad utilization of nuclear power in the United States.
For the United States to quickly make progress in nuclear power generation, it is essential that they are developed.

explanation_parts.text: First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.
We can see from “The passage suggests” that this is an Inference question.

Notice that the question asks about “Generation IV reactors.” These keywords are very helpful, because capitalized words are relatively easy to find in the passage.We find these keywords in S10.So, we know to focus in this area.

There is a good chance that the correct answer could come from S10, but we should also check whether the Generation IV reactors are discussed elsewhere.In this case, notice that the look above marker, “these reactors,” appears in S11, and is referring to the Generation IV reactors introduced in S10.
So, let’s review our Sentence Analysis and simplifications for both S10 and S11:
S10: Recent development of new (Generation IV) reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.
S11: Since production of these new reactors may have many additional economic benefits, it has inspired significant political support, which is an encouraging sign for the future of nuclear power.
YOUR CHOICE(A) The political attitude toward their creation is more positive than that toward some other matters.
This choice mentions “political attitude” toward the creation of Generation IV reactors.Where is this topic discussed in S10 or S11?

Notice that S11 says that “the production of these reactors has recently receivedrare bipartisan political support.” As discussed in our Question Analysis, “these reactors” is referring to the Generation IV reactors that this question asks about and that are first introduced above in S10.Thus, since “creation” and “production” are synonyms, “their creation” in this choice matches “production of these reactors” in the passage.

Next, we must consider whether “received rare bipartisan political support” matches the meaning of the comparison made in this choice, “political attitude … is more positive than that toward some other matters.”

Does the phrase “rare … political support” suggest that the political attitude toward the creation of these reactors “is more positive than that toward some other matters”? Yes.After all, if the support that production of Generation IV reactors has gotten is “rare,” then other matters must not get as much support.So, the statement “the production of these reactors has … received rare … political support” also implies that that the political attitude toward the creation of these reactors “is more positive than that toward some other matters.”
So, this is a tricky choice, because the wording is different from the passage, but we must notice that the meaning matches.
(B) They differ from most other nuclear reactors in that they use a closed fuel cycle.
Trap: Speculation
Trap: Wrong Comparison
If you picked this choice, you may have made a speculation that goes beyond what the passage says.
When we notice a comparison marker in an answer choice, such as “differ from” in this choice, we must be particularly careful to confirm that the comparison is fully supported by the passage.

In S10, the passage doesn’t specify whether a “closed fuel cycle” is a difference between the Generation IV reactors and most other reactors.The passage just says that the new reactors “use a highly sustainable and economical closed fuel cycle.”

So, while the passage does imply that the new reactors have some advantages over older ones, it’s possible that these advantages do not include using a “closed fuel cycle.” After all, other reactors may also use a “closed fuel cycle” even if they are not as sustainable and economical as Generation IV reactors.
(C) The cost of fossil fuel generated electricity cannot be made lower than the expected cost of electricity produced by Generation IV reactors.
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S10 says that “they (Generation IV reactors)will likelygenerate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation.” Note that the word “likely” is a qualifier marker (non-extreme); it allows for the possibility that this result will not end up happening.
On the other hand, the wording in this choice, “cannot be made lower,” has the extreme meaning of “it isimpossibleto make the cost of fossil fuel generated electricity lower.”
When we compare the meanings of these two statements, we can see that this choice goes beyond what the passage says.
(D) If they become mass-produced, there will no longer be economic obstacles to broad utilization of nuclear power in the United States.
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the Generation IV reactors are cited in S10 as an example of “promising recent developments” to support the point in S9 that overcoming economic obstacles is possible.

However, this choice goes too far.Notice that “there will no longer be economic obstacles” is an extreme statement that means thatthere will not be any economic obstacles at all.In contrast, S10 mentions Generation IV reactors only as asingle exampleof “promising recent developments” that may help to solve “one problem in the US,” the “lack of standardization and repetition.”

So, we can see that the passage does not suggest that, if Generation IV reactors “become mass-produced” there will no longer be any “economic obstacles to broad utilization of nuclear power in the United States.”
(E) For the United States to quickly make progress in nuclear power generation, it is essential that they are developed.
Trap: Mix-Up
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the word “essential” appears in both this choice and S9.
To avoid a trap, we must check whether the meanings match.
S9 says that “overcoming these hurdles is … essential.” Notice that “these hurdles” is referring to the “economic obstacles” mentioned earlier in S9.
So, what S9 says about “essential” does not match this choice, because S9 is about “overcoming hurdles” being “essential,” whereas this choice is about “Generation IV reactors” being “essential.”
Trap: Stretch
So, what does the passage say about Generation IV reactors?

In S10, the passage cites Generation IV reactors as an example of “promising recent developments” and says that “the production of these reactors has recently received rare bipartisan political support.” So, the author has a positive view of these reactors.

However, does the author believe that these reactors are “essential” for the United States “to quickly make progress”? Note that the wording “it is essential that” is a very strong statement that means “it is required that.”
It’s possible that the author holds this strong belief, but the passage does not say anything that strong about Generation IV reactors.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
