question.text: It can be inferred from the passage that the populace of the United States widely believes which of the following?

answers.text: Nuclear power generation is currently risky but could be made safer in the future.
In the United States, there is not sufficient political support for rapid adoption of nuclear power to occur.
There exist methods of power generation that are less costly than nuclear power generation.
The potential for radiation exposure is the most dangerous aspect of nuclear power plants.
The negative environmental impact of a nuclear accident lasts for many years.

explanation_parts.text: First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.
Notice that the stem says “it can beinferred.” So, this is an Inference question.

More specifically, the question asks about the “populace of the United States.” These keywords are very helpful, because capitalized words are relatively easy to find in the passage.We find an exact match for “populace of the United States” in S1, so we must review S1 carefully.
Also notice that the question asks for what the populace “believes.” So, we are looking for their opinion.In S1, the word “impression” has the meaning of a “belief.”
We can see that the belief presented in S1 includes two negative ideas about nuclear power plants:
- “particularly hazardous”
- “uniquely expensive”
Simply put, the populace of the United States believes that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.
Note that we can gain a solid understanding of this point from just S1, but we can also see that the theme of “dangerous,” i.e., “hazardous,” continues to be discussed in S2 through S6.
(A) Nuclear power generation is currently risky but could be made safer in the future.
Trap: Half-Right
Trap: Speculation

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “currently risky” in this choice matches with “particularly hazardous” in S1, as well as with the discussion of the populace’s belief in S2 through S4.
However, does the rest of this choice match the passage? Does the passage also suggest that the populace believes that nuclear power generation could be made safer in the future?

No.The author has a strong positive opinion about the safety of nuclear power, but if we stick to what the passage says, we notice that the populace’s opinion about the safety of nuclear power is only negative.
YOUR CHOICE(B) In the United States, there is not sufficient political support for rapid adoption of nuclear power to occur.
Trap: Mix-Up
Notice that the passage doesn’t say anything about the populace’s belief about “political support.”
The populace’s beliefs are discussed only in S1 through S4.
“Political support” is discussed only in S8 and S11, in whichthe author, notthe populace,suggests that political support is an important factor for the rapid adoption of nuclear power.
We must not spin any stories about the populace’s belief that go beyond what is said in the passage.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) There exist methods of power generation that are less costly than nuclear power generation.

S1 says that the populace of the United States believe that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.” “Uniquely expensive” implies that nuclear power is more costly than some other methods of power generation.
Therefore, we can infer that they believe that “there exist other methods … that are less costly.”

It is helpful to notice this common pattern in RC, in which the language in the answer choice is reversed from what the passage says, but the meaning matches.(Another way to think about reversed language is through a math example: if the passage were to say that “X is greater than Y,” an answer choice that says “Y is less than X” would be correct.)

In this choice, “There exist methods … that are less costly than nuclear power” is language that is essentially the reverse of what the passage says, “nuclear power plants are … uniquely expensive,” while expressing essentially the same meaning that the language in the passage expresses.
(D) The potential for radiation exposure is the most dangerous aspect of nuclear power plants.
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S3 indicates that the populace is concerned about radiation exposure: “thoughts of radiation exposure, which can result in both acute and chronic health issues, engender a mindset of trepidation and disgust.”

However, notice in this choice the extreme marker “the MOST,” which is a yellow flag and signifies acomparisonto other aspects of nuclear power plants: “themostdangerous aspect” means thatall otheraspects are less dangerous.
In the passage’s description of the populace’s belief in the 1stparagraph, is any comparison made between the relative dangerousness of the various aspects of nuclear power?
No, no such comparison is made.So, we can eliminate this choice.
(E) The negative environmental impact of a nuclear accident lasts for many years.
Trap: Speculation
If you picked this choice, you may have speculated that the populace holds this belief, because what this choice says may seem logical and true in real life.
Meanwhile, from S2, we can see that the populace is concerned about nuclear accidents.

However, the passage does not provide many details about this concern; it provides only the example of Chernobyl.The passage doesn’t indicate that the populace has any specific beliefs about how long the impact of a nuclear accident lasts.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It’s helpful to notice that the passage begins by presenting a common belief: S1 says, “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.” When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion, with the two contrasting views forming the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented by S1 involves two negative ideas about nuclear power plants: that they are “particularly hazardous” and that they are “uniquely expensive.”

S2 and S3 then add supporting details that explain why people have fears of nuclear power. S2 provides the example of nuclear disaster in Chernobyl, and S3 mentions fear of radiation exposure and the health problems that can result from it.

Notice that, in the 1st paragraph, the author presents a common opinion but doesn’t present an opinion of his own.

1st paragraph summary: Many people in the US believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive and associate nuclear power plants with scary images of the Chernobyl nuclear disaster and with radiation exposure.

In S4, notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part of S4:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s “trepidation and disgust” related to nuclear power.

In the X part of S4, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that there is “some risk” involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.” Indeed, details about both safety and environmental benefits follow.

As we might expect, the rest of the 2nd paragraph provides details to support the author’s main point in S4. S5 and S6 address safety concerns, with specific statistics about the safety benefits of nuclear power. S7 introduces environmental benefits. S8 cites an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

2nd paragraph summary: The author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that there are both safety-related and environmental benefits to overcoming negative perceptions of nuclear power. The author supports his point with statistics, facts, and examples.

At this point, we may have a sense that the primary purpose of this passage is to challenge many people’s concerns that nuclear power plants are “particularly hazardous” and “uniquely expensive.”

We have seen that, in the 2nd paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, in the 3rd paragraph, the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” So, in the 3rd paragraph, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

In S9, notice the contrast marker “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part. Here, the author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10 supports the point stated by S9, saying that recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

In S11, the additional point marker “moreover” is a useful clue indicating that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above — in this case, the point above in S9. The author concludes the passage by stating a point similar to S9’s point, “overcoming these hurdles rapidly is … feasible,” saying “the future of nuclear power is promising.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author acknowledges some economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly. The author supports his point that a rapid shift to nuclear power is possible, by citing two specific developments: new economical reactors and political support.

In reviewing the passage as a whole, it is important to note that the author’s overall tone is argumentative, not just descriptive. Throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs, the author presents a strong opinion in support of nuclear power and provides evidence of multiple types of benefits of nuclear power: safety, environmental, and economic.

Passage summary: The author presents and then challenges the impression widely held in the US that nuclear power is both dangerous and uniquely expensive.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: We don’t need to understand the technical word “terawatt-hour” in S5 to answer the questions correctly. However, from the context, we can infer that it is a unit of energy. If you are curious, “tera” is a unit prefix in the metric system that means multiplication by one trillion, or 1012. Another common use of “tera” is in “terabytes” (TB), in the context of memory capacity.

We also don’t need to understand the technical phrase “closed fuel cycle,” in S10.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice that a common belief is presented: “the impression that X (belief) has been widely held.”

When a passage starts with wording similar to “Many people believe X,” this structure is often followed by a contrasting opinion. So, it is helpful to be aware of the common myth structure, summarized as “Many people believe X. However, Y.”

Specifically, the belief presented in this sentence includes two negative ideas about nuclear power plants:

- “particularly hazardous”

- “uniquely expensive”

Simplification: Many people in the U.S. believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous and expensive.

S2: Supports S1, with an example about why people believe that nuclear power plants are dangerous.

Simplification: These plants bring to mind scary images of the nuclear disaster in Chernobyl.

S3: Supports S1, with a detail about the fear of “radiation exposure.”

Simplification: Thinking about the dangers of radiation exposure provokes a fearful mindset.

S4: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point. The X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

There are two aspects to the author’s opinion in the Y part:

- the actual peril … is overstated

- the US would benefit from overcoming this unjustified perception

When the author says, “the actual peril … is overstated,” the author is arguing that the people’s fears that were detailed in the first paragraph are exaggerated. So, “this unjustified perception” refers to people’s excessive fear of nuclear power.

In the X part, the author makes his main point (about safety) less absolute, by acknowledging that some risk is involved.

Note that “the US would benefit in many respects” foreshadows that the rest of the paragraph will provide details about these “many respects.”

Simplification: the author argues that nuclear power is much safer than people think and that it is important to overcome this fear.

S5: Supports S4, with a statistic about the safety of nuclear power, as compared with the safety of other methods of energy production.

Simplification: Statistics show that nuclear power generation is safer than other common methods, particularly coal power.

S6: Supports S4, by addressing a concern about nuclear waste

Notice that “in reality” is an opinion/contrast marker. The author is saying that “concerns about nuclear waste” are not really a big deal, once you take into account certain facts.

Simplification: Nuclear waste isn’t a significant problem, because it can be safely stored and takes up a very small volume of space.

S7: Supports the point stated by S4 that “the US would benefit in many respects from overcoming this unjustified perception.”

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above.

S5 and S6 provided details about the safety benefits of nuclear power. Now, S7 introduces the environmental benefits.

Simplification: Also, nuclear power could have great environmental benefits, because it is the best way to prevent further global warming.

S8: Supports S4, with an example of the rapid success stories of France and Sweden.

Simplification: With a supportive political and regulatory situation, there can be a rapid shift to nuclear power, as we can see from the examples of France and Sweden.

S9: Here the specific topic shifts to overcoming “economic obstacles.” In the second paragraph, the author challenged the concern of safety. Now, the author is challenging the second concern mentioned in S1, that nuclear power is “uniquely expensive.”

Notice the contrast marker, “while it is true that.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Simplification: The author acknowledges that there are indeed economic obstacles to widespread use of nuclear power in the US, but argues that these obstacles can and should be overcome quickly.

S10: Specifies an economic obstacle/problem and a potential solution:

Problem: “lack of standardization and repetition”

Solution: “Generation IV reactors that can be mass-produced”

Also, notice the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.” The final part of the sentence, “consequently, they will likely generate electricity at a cost lower than that of fossil fuel generation,” is a positive prediction about the future low relative cost of nuclear power.

Simplification: Recent development of new reactors that can be mass-produced will likely result in cheap electricity.

S11: Supports the main point in S9 that overcoming the economic obstacles rapidly is possible.

Notice the additional point marker “moreover,” which tells us that this sentence supports a point more than one sentence above. We also see the phrase “additional economic benefits.” So, this sentence is expanding on the economic benefits discussed in S10 and playing a role similar to that of S10 in supporting S9.

Simplification: Since production of these new reactors may have many additional economic benefits, it has inspired significant political support, which is an encouraging sign for the future of nuclear power.

question.text: The passage suggests that Teixeira and Huber would most likely agree with which of the following statements about the “certain genetic mutations” in the highlighted text?

answers.text: Their existence serves to challenge the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk.
A species that has them will never go extinct.
They do not generally improve a species’ ability to survive.
They do not result in genetic diversity of species.
They can reduce the risk of a species’ becoming extinct.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.We can see from “the passage suggests” that this is an Inference question.The wording “Teixeira and Huber would most likely agree” means that we must find their opinion about the “certain genetic mutations.”
Let’s review S2 and S3 to make sure we understand Teixeira and Huber’s opinion.

S2 indicates that Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk”;in other words, they disagree with the assumption that, when genetic diversity is high, extinction risk is low.
Then, S3 uses the contrast marker “while” and follows the structure “while X, Y.” Such a structure often presents an exception in the X part, and then states a point in the Y part.

Here, the X part, “While they acknowledge that, insomecases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” presents anexceptionto Teixeira and Huber’s overall view.In other words, this part of S3 means that Teixeira and Huber “acknowledge” that, insomecases, when genetic diversity is high, extinction risk is low.

Then, the Y part, “they maintain that themajorityof genetic diversity is ‘neutral’ …,” goes back to their overall view that, inmostcases, it does not make sense to assume that genetic diversity affects extinction risk.
(Note: The pronoun “they” or “their” or “them” appears in all of the answer choices.Since the question asks about “certain genetic mutations,” these pronouns refer to “certain genetic mutations.”)
(A) Their existence serves to challenge the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk.
Trap: Nearby

If you picked this choice, you may have thought that “certain genetic mutationscan allow species to thrive” supports Teixeira and Huber’s challenge in S2: “challenges the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”

However, as we discussed in our Question Analysis, “certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” is an exception that Teixeira and Huber “acknowledge” and is in contrast to their overall view.
In other words, the fact that “certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” does not support Teixeira and Huber’s “challenge.” Rather, it is in contrast to their challenge.
So, this choice says essentially the opposite of what Teixeira and Huber believe.

To avoid choosing a choice like this one, we have to be careful not to choose an incorrect choice just because it’s based on wording in the passage located near to highlighted text that the question is asking about.
(B) A species that has them will never go extinct.
Trap: Stretch
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the passage says that Teixeira and Huber “acknowledge that … certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive.”
However, notice the extreme marker “never,” which is a yellow flag.
Does “can allow a species to thrive” have the same meaning as “will never go extinct”?

No, “will never go extinct” is a very extreme statement about eternity.After all, a species could, for example, be thriving because of “certain genetic mutations,” but then an asteroid impact or other natural disaster could suddenly eliminate the species.
YOUR CHOICE(C) They do not generally improve a species’ ability to survive.
Trap: Opposite
Trap: Nearby

If you picked this choice, you may have focused on the end of S3, ‘the majority of genetic diversity is “neutral,” in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce,’ and you may have missed that the question asks about the “certaingenetic mutations,” not “themajorityof genetic diversity.”

To eliminate this choice, we have to see that the beginning of S3 says, “they (Teixeira and Huber) acknowledge that, in some cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive.” In other words, the passage says that Teixeira and Huber believe, essentially, that the “certaingenetic mutations” in the highlighted text DO improve a species’ ability to survive.
So, this choice says the opposite of what the passage says.
(D) They do not result in genetic diversity of species.
Trap: Nearby
Trap: Mix-up
This choice mixes up two things that Teixeira and Huber believe: “certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” and “the majority of genetic diversity is ‘neutral.’”
The passage never says that Teixeira and Huber believe that the “certain genetic mutations” mentioned in the highlighted text “do not result in genetic diversity of species.”
To get RC questions correct, we have to be careful not to fall for traps that mix up ideas mentioned in the passage.
CORRECT ANSWER(E) They can reduce the risk of a species’ becoming extinct.

In our Question Analysis, we saw that S2 says that Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”
Then, S3 says that Teixeira and Huber acknowledge an exception to their challenge in S2.They acknowledge that “certain genetic mutations can allow a species tothrive.”

From the context, we can see that “thrive” means “reduce the risk of a species’ becoming extinct.” So, in acknowledging that “certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive,” Teixeira and Huber are agreeing that certain genetic mutations “can reduce the risk of a species’ becoming extinct.”
So, this choice is supported by the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage has only one paragraph. Therefore, we don’t have the structural clues that come from the transitions from one paragraph to another. So, when there is only one paragraph, it is particularly helpful to notice contrast markers and opinion markers, which give us clues to the structure and important points.

As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast or opinions in the first two sentences. In this passage, the most helpful clues to the structure are markers in S2: the contrast marker, “although,” and the opinion marker, “challenges.” These markers signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.” in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship is a relation such that, when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

It’s important to notice that the tone of these sentences is descriptive: the author is not making an argument of his own. Rather, the author is describing two contrasting views: that of the scientists in S1, and that of Teixeira and Huber in S2.

So, from these first two sentences, we already have a good sense of the basic structure of the passage, and it makes sense that the sentences that follow will expand upon Teixeira and Huber’s view presented in S2.

Indeed, notice the many additional opinion markers in the final three sentences: “they acknowledge that,” “maintain that,” “contend that,” “should,” “argue that,” and “must.”

Let’s summarize these additional points of Teixeira and Huber:

S3: although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival.

S4: to prevent species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

Passage summary: In the context of preventing species’ extinction, Teixeira and Huber disagree with many scientists’ focus on genetic diversity and contend that it makes more sense to focus on habitat destruction and to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Simpler Similar Words

extinction

disappearance, dying out

unprecedented

unmatched, unequaled, never experienced before

reputable

respected, highly regarded

bases (plural of “basis”)

support, foundation, justification

assessments

evaluation, estimation

genetic mutations

genetic changes or variations (can result in increased genetic diversity)

thrive

grow, prosper, expand, flourish

contend

argue, believe, assert

vast

large, immense

widespread ecological disruption

significant environmental damage

mitigation

lessening, alleviation (reducing the severity or painfulness of something)

measures

actions, steps
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: By describing what some scientists have been doing because they are “Concerned by the fact that the rate of species extinction … is at a level unprecedented,” this sentence implies that these scientists are of the view that “measuring the genetic diversity of species” makes sense or is important in the context of species extinction.

Simplification: Because scientists are concerned about the high rate of species extinction, they have been using measures of genetic diversity to assess extinction risk.

S2: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point.

Also notice the opinion marker, “challenges the assumption that.”

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, the presence of “this type of analysis” signals that we must look above to understand what “this type of analysis” refers to. We see that “this type of analysis” refers to the scientists’ work of “measuring” and “assessments” in S1.

These markers, particularly the word “challenges,” signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists mentioned above in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk”; in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.

S3: Notice two more opinion markers, “they acknowledge that” and “they maintain that.” This sentence follows up on S2 with more details and reasoning behind Teixeira and Huber’s disagreement with the scientists in S1.

Also, notice the contrast marker, “while,” which follows the structure “while X, Y.” This structure often concedes exceptions in the X part, and then makes the main point in the Y part.

Here, the X part, “they acknowledge that, in SOME cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” represents an exception to Teixeira and Huber’s main point in the Y part, “they maintain that the MAJORITY of genetic diversity is ‘neutral,’ in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival. (Note that this sentence supports the main point of S2: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.)

S4: Notice two more opinion markers, “contend that” and “should.”

Also, notice the contrast marker “instead of.” What are the two things that are in contrast?

Genetic diversity and habitat destruction – “instead of genetic diversity, the primary concern … should be the issue of habitat destruction.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, in preventing species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: This sentence starts with the support marker “Citing the fact that X,” which is logically followed by the opinion marker “they argue that Y.” Often, both support and opinion are presented in a sentence, with a structure similar to “Since X (support), Y (opinion).”

There is another opinion marker, “must,” at the end of the sentence: “mitigation measures must be taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.” This wording signifies a proposal for what should be done about the problem. In this case, the problem cited is habitat loss and ecological disruption.

Simplification: Since large areas of wildlife habitat have been destroyed, Teixeira and Huber argue that humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

question.text: It can be inferred from the passage that, often, the levels of genetic diversity of species are measured in an attempt to

answers.text: find ways to reduce the rate of species extinction on Earth
gain insight into the likelihood of the disappearance of species
acquire evidence to contradict the findings of Teixeira and Huber
become a reputable scientist in the area of conservation genetics
show that relatively high genetic diversity of a species is a reliable indicator of relatively low extinction risk

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, we must make sure we understand exactly what the stem is asking and determine where in the passage to find the information we need for answering the question.
We see that the focus of the question is on what people are attempting when “levels of genetic diversity” are “measured.”

The keyword “measured” in the stem matches “measuring” in S1, which says, “scientists … have been measuring the genetic diversity of species and using the results as bases for assessments of species extinction risk.”
So, S1 indicates that, when “levels of genetic diversity” are “measured,” people are attempting to assess “species extinction risk.”
We can expect the correct answer to match the meaning of this part of S1.
(A) find ways to reduce the rate of species extinction on Earth
Trap: Speculation
This choice is tempting because, when we read S1, it may appear that this choice describes why “levels of genetic diversity are measured.”
However, in answering an Inference question, we must be careful to not spin our own stories.We must stick to what the passage says.
From our Question Analysis, we see that the passage says that scientists measure genetic diversity to assess species extinction risk.
Does that match with “find ways to reduce the rate of species extinction” in this choice?

Not really.“Find ways to reduce the rate” means “find ways to solve a problem,” whereas “assessment” means “evaluation.” In other words, “assessment” is determining how big the problem is, rather than solving the problem.
So, while could be true that the scientists want to “find ways to reduce the rate of species extinction on Earth,” the passage does not support this choice.
YOUR CHOICE(B) gain insight into the likelihood of the disappearance of species
From our Question Analysis, we can expect the correct answer to match with “assess species extinction risk.”
Let’s determine whether this choice matches the passage:
Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagegain insight intoassess (S1)likelihoodof the disappearance of speciesspecies extinctionrisk
Yes, this choice matches the passage and answers the question.
(C) acquire evidence to contradict the findings of Teixeira and Huber
Trap: Distortion

The passage doesn’t say that the goal of measuring genetic diversity is to contradict Teixeira and Huber.All that the passage says is that Teixeira and Huber challenge and disagree with the scientists.

Furthermore, from what we know from the timeline in the passage, with the present perfect verb “have been measuring,” it seems that these measurements were an ongoing process BEFORE Teixeira and Huber’s “recent study.” Therefore, the motivation of the scientists couldn’t have been to “contradict the findings of Teixeira and Huber,” because these findings happened later.
(D) become a reputable scientist in the area of conservation genetics
Trap:Nearby
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed similar wording in S1: “reputable scientists in the field of conservation genetics.”
However, S1 mentions “reputable scientists” only to identify who is doing the measuring.
The passage does not say that scientists measure genetic diversity to become reputable.
(E) show that relatively high genetic diversity of a species is a reliable indicator of relatively low extinction risk
Trap:Nearby

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed similar wording in S2: “a recent study… challenges the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”
However, the passage doesn’t say that the goal of the measuring is to “show” that “high genetic diversity of a species is a reliable indicator.”

Notice that the passage refers to the idea that “high genetic diversity of a species is a reliable indicator” as an “assumption.” In other words, “high genetic diversity of a species is a reliable indicator” is not something the scientists attempt to “show” by “measuring.” It’s something they assume.
We have to be careful not to choose a choice simply because it resembles wording in the passage located near the keywords from the stem.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage has only one paragraph. Therefore, we don’t have the structural clues that come from the transitions from one paragraph to another. So, when there is only one paragraph, it is particularly helpful to notice contrast markers and opinion markers, which give us clues to the structure and important points.

As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast or opinions in the first two sentences. In this passage, the most helpful clues to the structure are markers in S2: the contrast marker, “although,” and the opinion marker, “challenges.” These markers signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.” in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship is a relation such that, when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

It’s important to notice that the tone of these sentences is descriptive: the author is not making an argument of his own. Rather, the author is describing two contrasting views: that of the scientists in S1, and that of Teixeira and Huber in S2.

So, from these first two sentences, we already have a good sense of the basic structure of the passage, and it makes sense that the sentences that follow will expand upon Teixeira and Huber’s view presented in S2.

Indeed, notice the many additional opinion markers in the final three sentences: “they acknowledge that,” “maintain that,” “contend that,” “should,” “argue that,” and “must.”

Let’s summarize these additional points of Teixeira and Huber:

S3: although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival.

S4: to prevent species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

Passage summary: In the context of preventing species’ extinction, Teixeira and Huber disagree with many scientists’ focus on genetic diversity and contend that it makes more sense to focus on habitat destruction and to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: By describing what some scientists have been doing because they are “Concerned by the fact that the rate of species extinction … is at a level unprecedented,” this sentence implies that these scientists are of the view that “measuring the genetic diversity of species” makes sense or is important in the context of species extinction.

Simplification: Because scientists are concerned about the high rate of species extinction, they have been using measures of genetic diversity to assess extinction risk.

S2: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point.

Also notice the opinion marker, “challenges the assumption that.”

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, the presence of “this type of analysis” signals that we must look above to understand what “this type of analysis” refers to. We see that “this type of analysis” refers to the scientists’ work of “measuring” and “assessments” in S1.

These markers, particularly the word “challenges,” signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists mentioned above in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk”; in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.

S3: Notice two more opinion markers, “they acknowledge that” and “they maintain that.” This sentence follows up on S2 with more details and reasoning behind Teixeira and Huber’s disagreement with the scientists in S1.

Also, notice the contrast marker, “while,” which follows the structure “while X, Y.” This structure often concedes exceptions in the X part, and then makes the main point in the Y part.

Here, the X part, “they acknowledge that, in SOME cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” represents an exception to Teixeira and Huber’s main point in the Y part, “they maintain that the MAJORITY of genetic diversity is ‘neutral,’ in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival. (Note that this sentence supports the main point of S2: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.)

S4: Notice two more opinion markers, “contend that” and “should.”

Also, notice the contrast marker “instead of.” What are the two things that are in contrast?

Genetic diversity and habitat destruction – “instead of genetic diversity, the primary concern … should be the issue of habitat destruction.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, in preventing species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: This sentence starts with the support marker “Citing the fact that X,” which is logically followed by the opinion marker “they argue that Y.” Often, both support and opinion are presented in a sentence, with a structure similar to “Since X (support), Y (opinion).”

There is another opinion marker, “must,” at the end of the sentence: “mitigation measures must be taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.” This wording signifies a proposal for what should be done about the problem. In this case, the problem cited is habitat loss and ecological disruption.

Simplification: Since large areas of wildlife habitat have been destroyed, Teixeira and Huber argue that humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

question.text: The passage suggests that Teixeira and Huber would be most likely to disagree with many conservation geneticists over which of the following?

answers.text: Whether there are benefits to measuring the levels of genetic diversity of species
Whether there is consistently a negative correlation between extinction risk and genetic diversity of a species
Whether habitat destruction is a significant contributing factor in the context of species extinction
Whether genetic mutations are ever a factor in the ability of a species to survive
Whether the pace of species extinction is faster than it has been in the past

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, make sure we understand exactly what the stem is asking for.We can see that this question is asking about a disagreement: “Teixeira and Huber would be most likely todisagreewith many conservation geneticists over ….”
To determine what the passage says about how they disagree, we must find information about BOTH Teixeira’s opinion and the conservation geneticists’ opinion.

In S2, notice the contrast marker “although,” and the opinion marker “challenges.” These markers signal a main point: Teixeira and Huberdisagreewith the conservation geneticists in S1.What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”In other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative correlation between genetic diversity and extinction risk.A negative correlation means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.
(A) Whether there are benefits to measuring the levels of genetic diversity of species
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that Teixeira and Huber put less importance on measuring genetic diversity than the conservation geneticists put.Indeed, S4 says, “Teixeira and Huber contend that, instead of genetic diversity, the primary concern … should be the issue of habitat destruction.”

However, the wording of this choice, “whether there are benefits,” has the extreme meaning of “whether there are ANY benefits to measuring genetic diversity.” In other words, for this choice to be correct, the passage would have to say that Teixeira and Huber believe that there are no benefits at all to measuring the levels of genetic diversity of species.
However, Teixeira and Huber are not described as believing that measuring levels of genetic diversity has no benefits at all.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether there is consistently a negative correlation between extinction risk and genetic diversity of a species

From our Question Analysis, we see that Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”In other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative correlation between genetic diversity and extinction risk.A negative correlation means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.
So, this choice matches the disagreement that is asked about in the stem and discussed in the passage.
(C) Whether habitat destruction is a significant contributing factor in the context of species extinction

It’s important to remember that, to determine how Teixeira and Huber disagree with the conservation geneticists, we must find information about BOTH Teixeira and Huber’s opinion and the conservation geneticists’ opinion.

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that Teixeira and Huber do believe that “habitat destruction is a significant contributing factor in the context of species extinction.” Indeed, S4 says, “Teixeira and Huber contend that … the primary concern in the effort to prevent the disappearance of species should be the issue of habitat destruction.”
However, the passage does not say that theconservation geneticistshave an opinion abouthabitat destruction,so we can eliminate this choice.
(D) Whether genetic mutations are ever a factor in the ability of a species to survive
Trap: Stretch

To determine whether Teixeira and Huber disagree with the conservation geneticists regarding whether genetic mutations are ever a factor in the ability of a species to survive, we have to find information about BOTH Teixeira and Huber’s opinion and the conservation geneticists’ opinion.

Since S1 says that the conservation geneticists “have been measuring the genetic diversity of species and using the results as bases for assessments of species extinction risk” we can reasonably presume that the opinion of the conservation geneticists is that genetic mutations are a factor in the ability of a species to survive.
Now, what about Teixeira and Huber’s opinion?
Notice in this choice the extreme marker “ever,” which is a yellow flag.

We can find “genetic mutations” discussed in S3, which says, “they (Teixeira and Huber) acknowledge that, in SOME cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive.” Of course, if genetic mutations “can allow a species to thrive,” they are “a factor in the ability of a species to survive.”
So, we can see that this choice is a Stretch.

While the passage suggests that Teixeira and Huber disagree with other scientists about theimportanceof genetic diversity, they don’t disagree about whether genetic mutations areevera factor in the ability of a species to survives.All agree that genetic mutations can be such a factor.
(E) Whether the pace of species extinction is faster than it has been in the past
First, where in the passage is “the pace of species extinction” discussed?

“Pace” has the same meaning as “rate,” and S1 says: “Concerned by the fact that the rate of species extinction on Earth is at a level unprecedented in modern history, many reputable scientists in the field of conservation genetics ….”

Note that “unprecedented” means “unmatched, unequaled, never experienced before.” So, from S1, we know that the conservation geneticists are aware that the “the pace of species extinction is faster than it has been in the past.”
However, does the passage say that Teixeira and Huber disagree with this fact about the pace of species extinction?
No, Teixeira and Huber do not debate this fact, so we can eliminate this choice.

question.text: According to the passage, which of the following statements about habitat destruction is true?

answers.text: Some scientists argue that humans are not responsible for significant habitat destruction.
If efforts are made to conserve and restore natural ecosystems, habitat destruction will no longer be a problem.
According to prevailing scholarly opinion, habitat destruction is the greatest threat to the survival of many species.
Significant portions of wildlife habitat have been destroyed during the 21st century.
Human activity is the primary cause of habitat destruction.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.
We can see from “According to the passage” that this is a Detail question.

Notice that the question asks about “habitat destruction.” We first find “habitat destruction” mentioned in S4, and then find specific details in S5: “vast stretches of wildlifehabitat, amounting to about eight times the area of the United Kingdom, were lost between 2000 and 2020.”
(A) Some scientists argue that humans are not responsible for significant habitat destruction.
Trap: Speculation
Trap: Opposite

If you picked this choice, you may have speculated that this statement about “some scientists” is true.It may be true in real life, since “some scientists” just means “more than one scientist,” and there are many people in the world with various opinions.
However, we must not think this way, about what might be true in real life.Rather, we must always make sure to stick to what the passage says.
The passage says that Teixeira and Huber “argue that human activity is responsible for widespread ecological disruption.” So, Teixeira and Huber argue the opposite of what this choice says.
Meanwhile, passage does not specify any other opinions about whether humans are responsible for habitat destruction.So, this choice is not supported by the passage.
(B) If efforts are made to conserve and restore natural ecosystems, habitat destruction will no longer be a problem.
Trap: Stretch
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that S5 says that Teixeira and Huber argue that “mitigation measures must be taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.”

However, this choice goes too far, with the strong statement that “habitat destruction will no longer be a problem.” Note that the word “mitigation” in the passage means “REDUCING the severity or painfulness of something.” We must notice that “reducing” is a less strong statement than “no longer be a problem.”
(C) According to prevailing scholarly opinion, habitat destruction is the greatest threat to the survival of many species.
Trap: Half-Right
Trap: Distortion

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that S4 says: “Teixeira and Huber contend that, instead of genetic diversity, theprimary concernin the effort to prevent thedisappearance of speciesshould be the issue ofhabitat destruction.” Notice that “primary concern” matches with “greatest threat.”

However, we must always make sure to read the entire choice.Notice the start of this choice, “According toprevailing scholarly opinion.” The passage presents only Teixeira and Huber’s opinion, not “prevailing scholarly opinion.” So, this choice is only half right and, thus, completely wrong.
YOUR CHOICE(D) Significant portions of wildlife habitat have been destroyed during the 21st century.

In our Question Analysis, we found specific details about habitat destruction in S5: “vast stretches of wildlifehabitat, amounting to about eight times the area of the United Kingdom, were lost between 2000 and 2020.”
Let’s determine whether this choice matches the quote above from the passage:
Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagevast stretches of wildlife habitatsignificant portions of wildlife habitatdestroyedlostduring the 21st centurybetween 2000 and 2020
Yes, this choice matches the passage and answers the question.
(E) Human activity is the primary cause of habitat destruction.
Trap: Stretch
First, notice the extreme marker “primary,” which is a yellow flag.Note that “primary cause” has the meaning of “greatest cause.” Does the passage support this extreme comparison statement?

Note that the word “primary” does appear in S4, but the context is different.S4 says that the “primary concern in the effort to prevent the disappearance of species should be the issue of habitat destruction.” In other words, S4 says thathabitat destructionis the primary concern, but this choice says thathuman activityis the primary cause.So, S4 does not connect to this choice, because S4 discusses a different topic.

“Human activity” appears in S5 and is mentioned as a cause of habitat destruction, but it is not compared with other possible causes or called “the primary cause.” So, the wording in this choice, “primary cause,” goes beyond what the passage says.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
This passage has only one paragraph. Therefore, we don’t have the structural clues that come from the transitions from one paragraph to another. So, when there is only one paragraph, it is particularly helpful to notice contrast markers and opinion markers, which give us clues to the structure and important points.

As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast or opinions in the first two sentences. In this passage, the most helpful clues to the structure are markers in S2: the contrast marker, “although,” and the opinion marker, “challenges.” These markers signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.” in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship is a relation such that, when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

It’s important to notice that the tone of these sentences is descriptive: the author is not making an argument of his own. Rather, the author is describing two contrasting views: that of the scientists in S1, and that of Teixeira and Huber in S2.

So, from these first two sentences, we already have a good sense of the basic structure of the passage, and it makes sense that the sentences that follow will expand upon Teixeira and Huber’s view presented in S2.

Indeed, notice the many additional opinion markers in the final three sentences: “they acknowledge that,” “maintain that,” “contend that,” “should,” “argue that,” and “must.”

Let’s summarize these additional points of Teixeira and Huber:

S3: although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival.

S4: to prevent species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

Passage summary: In the context of preventing species’ extinction, Teixeira and Huber disagree with many scientists’ focus on genetic diversity and contend that it makes more sense to focus on habitat destruction and to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: By describing what some scientists have been doing because they are “Concerned by the fact that the rate of species extinction … is at a level unprecedented,” this sentence implies that these scientists are of the view that “measuring the genetic diversity of species” makes sense or is important in the context of species extinction.

Simplification: Because scientists are concerned about the high rate of species extinction, they have been using measures of genetic diversity to assess extinction risk.

S2: Notice the contrast marker, “although.” The structure “although X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point.

Also notice the opinion marker, “challenges the assumption that.”

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, the presence of “this type of analysis” signals that we must look above to understand what “this type of analysis” refers to. We see that “this type of analysis” refers to the scientists’ work of “measuring” and “assessments” in S1.

These markers, particularly the word “challenges,” signal that Teixeira and Huber disagree with the scientists mentioned above in S1. What exactly do they disagree about?

Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk”; in other words, they disagree with the assumption that there is a negative relationship between genetic diversity and extinction risk. A negative relationship means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down.

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.

S3: Notice two more opinion markers, “they acknowledge that” and “they maintain that.” This sentence follows up on S2 with more details and reasoning behind Teixeira and Huber’s disagreement with the scientists in S1.

Also, notice the contrast marker, “while,” which follows the structure “while X, Y.” This structure often concedes exceptions in the X part, and then makes the main point in the Y part.

Here, the X part, “they acknowledge that, in SOME cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive” represents an exception to Teixeira and Huber’s main point in the Y part, “they maintain that the MAJORITY of genetic diversity is ‘neutral,’ in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, although there can be some exceptions, most genetic diversity is neutral and doesn’t affect survival. (Note that this sentence supports the main point of S2: Teixeira and Huber disagree that a high degree of genetic diversity is associated with a low extinction risk.)

S4: Notice two more opinion markers, “contend that” and “should.”

Also, notice the contrast marker “instead of.” What are the two things that are in contrast?

Genetic diversity and habitat destruction – “instead of genetic diversity, the primary concern … should be the issue of habitat destruction.”

Simplification: Teixeira and Huber argue that, in preventing species’ extinction, the issue of habit destruction should be the priority, instead of genetic diversity.

S5: This sentence starts with the support marker “Citing the fact that X,” which is logically followed by the opinion marker “they argue that Y.” Often, both support and opinion are presented in a sentence, with a structure similar to “Since X (support), Y (opinion).”

There is another opinion marker, “must,” at the end of the sentence: “mitigation measures must be taken immediately to conserve and restore natural ecosystems.” This wording signifies a proposal for what should be done about the problem. In this case, the problem cited is habitat loss and ecological disruption.

Simplification: Since large areas of wildlife habitat have been destroyed, Teixeira and Huber argue that humans are responsible for significant environmental damage and that immediate actions must be taken to reduce this damage.

question.text: The passage suggests that, in the context of prevention of species extinction, Teixeira and Huber would agree with which of the following statements regarding genetic diversity?

answers.text: It is less important than at least one other factor that affects species extinction.
It is the only issue that many conservation geneticists consider.
It does not have an impact on the ability of a species to procreate or survive.
In some situations, it is the most significant determinant of a species’ likelihood of avoiding extinction.
Generally, the greater it is within a species, the lower is that species’ risk of extinction.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, make sure we understand exactly what the stem is asking for.There are three main keyword phrases in the stem: “Teixeira and Huber would agree,” “genetic diversity,” and “prevention of species extinction.”
So, we need to find Teixeira and Huber’s opinion about the connection between “genetic diversity” and “prevention of species extinction.”
Where exactly in the passage are all three of these discussed?

Although “Teixeira and Huber” and “genetic diversity” are discussed throughout the passage, “PREVENTION of species extinction” is specifically mentioned only in S4: “Teixeiraand Huber contend that, instead ofgenetic diversity, the primary concern in the effort toPREVENT the disappearance of speciesshould be the issue of habitat destruction.” Notice that the word “disappearance” is different from “extinction,” but has the same meaning.
Let’s simplify Teixeira’s opinion in S4: to prevent species’ extinction, the issue ofhabit destructionshould be the priority, instead ofgenetic diversity.
(Note: The pronoun “it” appears in all of the answer choices.Since the question asks about “statements regardinggenetic diversity,” “it” refers to “genetic diversity.”)
YOUR CHOICE(A) It is less important than at least one other factor that affects species extinction.
S4 says that Teixeira and Huber contend that “instead of genetic diversity, theprimary concernin the effort to prevent the disappearance of speciesshould bethe issue ofhabitat destruction.”
Does that opinion match this choice?

Yes, if Teixeira and Huber believe that “habitat destruction” should be the“primary concern,”we can infer that they believe that “habitat destruction” is more important than other concerns, such as “genetic diversity.” Of course, in that case, Teixeira and Huber believe “genetic diversity” is “less important than at least one other factor,” habitat destruction.
Therefore, this choice is supported by the passage and answers the question.
(B) It is the only issue that many conservation geneticists consider.
Trap: Stretch
Notice the extreme marker “only,” which is a yellow flag.

From our Question Analysis, we can see that Teixeira and Huber do believe that the conservation geneticists do put too much emphasis on genetic diversity: “instead of genetic diversity, the primary concern in the effort to prevent the disappearance of species should be the issue of habitat destruction.”

However, “theonlyissue” would mean that conservation geneticists do not consider other issues at all.The passage does not say that Teixeira and Huber believe that conservation geneticists consider no other issues.
(C) It does not have an impact on the ability of a species to procreate or survive.
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S3 says that Teixeira and Huber “maintain that the majority of genetic diversity is ‘neutral,’ in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce.”
However, notice that “does not have an impact” in this choice has the extreme meaning “does not have any impact at all.” Is Teixeira and Huber’s opinion really this strong?

In the quote above, we see that the exact wording is “the MAJORITY of genetic diversity is neutral.” Note that “majority” does not mean “all.” It means “most.” So, Teixeira and Huber’s opinion is thatmostgenetic diversity has no impact on the ability of a species to survive and procreate, butsomegenetic diversity does have such an impact.
So, we can see that, in saying, “does not have an impact,” this choice goes too far.
(D) In some situations, it is the most significant determinant of a species’ likelihood of avoiding extinction.
Trap: Stretch
Notice the extreme marker “the most,” which is a yellow flag.

For “the MOST significant determinant” to be correct, the passage would have to indicate that Teixeira and Huber believe that “genetic diversity” is more significant than any other determinant of a species’ likelihood of avoiding extinction.

S3 says that Teixeira and Huber “acknowledge that, in some cases, certain genetic mutations can allow a species to thrive.” That quote is somewhat similar to this choice, but it does not indicate that genetic diversity is “the most significant determinant.”
(E) Generally, the greater it is within a species, the lower is that species’ risk of extinction.
Trap: Opposite

In S2, notice the opinion marker, “challengestheassumptionthat.” “Challenges” means “disagrees with.” So, Teixeira and Huber disagree with “the assumption that a high degree of genetic diversity within a species is a reliable indicator of a low extinction risk.”

Also, S3 says that Teixeira and Huber “maintain that the majority of genetic diversity is “neutral,” in that it neither reduces nor improves a species’ ability to survive or reproduce,” meaning that, in most cases, genetic diversity does not affect species’ risk of extinction.
So, this choice is basically the opposite of what Teixeira and Huber’s would agree with.
