question.text: According to the passage, the opinion of the athletes in the highlighted text differs from that of Kurz in that the athletes believe that

answers.text: some alternative stretching techniques can be more effective than Static Stretching
Static Stretching involves maintaining your body in a fixed position
it is better to perform Static Stretching before a workout than to do it at a different time
PNF and Dynamic Stretching are not useful stretching approaches
the purpose of stretching is to increase range of motion and performance.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, we must always make sure we understand exactly what the stem is asking for.We can see from “According to the passage, the opinion of theathletes… differs from that ofKurz” that this is a Detail question about a difference between opinions.So, we must find proof in the passage about BOTH the opinion of “athletes” and the opinion of “Kurz.”

Also, we must notice the wording, “in that the ATHLETES believe that” — the answer must be what the ATHLETES believe, not Kurz.Whenever we notice a comparison, we must be aware that it can be easy to mix things up if we don’t pay close attention.Therefore, this stem is a signal that we must be proactive and mindful to watch out for Opposite traps!
So, we must identify the highlighted athletes’ opinion.S1 describes the athletes’ opinion: “the ideal stretching protocol … involves Static Stretching prior to vigorous physical activity.”

Next, we need to find the capitalized keyword “Kurz” in the passage.We find Kurz in S2, which says that he “calls into question multiple aspects of this method.” As we might expect, the next sentence, S3, specifies these “multiple aspects.” In S3, it’s helpful to notice that the “not only X but also Y'' structure signals that there aretwomain points of disagreement:
(1) Static Stretching before a workout is inferior to doing it after a workout.
(2) There are superior alternative approaches, such as PNF and DS.
So, either one of these two points of disagreement could be involved in the correct answer.
(A) some alternative stretching techniques can be more effective than Static Stretching
Trap: Opposite
Notice the wording in the stem, “in that the ATHLETES believe that” — the answer must be what the ATHLETES believe, not Kurz.
In our Question Analysis, we saw that this choice is the opinion of Kurz in S2 & S3, not the “athletes” in S1.

According to S1, the athletes believe that Static Stretching is “the ideal stretching protocol,” in other words, that Static Stretching is better than other stretching techniques, basically the opposite of what this choice says.
(B) Static Stretching involves maintaining your body in a fixed position
Trap: Matches but Incorrect
If you picked this answer, you may have correctly noticed that it is implied that the athletes in S1 believe this definition of what Static Stretching involves.

However, the passage says nothing about Kurz’s opinion regarding what Static Stretchinginvolves.In fact, while we can’t make any assumptions, it’s likely that Kurz agrees with this definition of Static Stretching.
So, this choice matches something that the passage says but does not describe how the athletes’ opinion differs from that of Kurz.
CORRECT ANSWER(C) it is better to perform Static Stretching before a workout than to do it at a different time
If we read carefully, we can notice the following difference between the athlete’s opinion and Kurz’s opinion as described in the passage:
Regarding the sequence of stretching and physical activity,
S1 says that the athletes’ opinion is that “ideal stretching protocol … involves … Static Stretching PRIOR TO vigorous physical activity.”
S3 says that Kurz’s opinion is that “this sequence of activities produces results inferior to those produced by performance of Static stretching AFTER physical exertion.”

So, we can see that the athletes’ opinion and that of Kurz differ as described by this choice.While Kurz’s opinion is that Static Stretching after physical exertion” is better, the athletes’ opinion is that it is better to perform Static Stretching before a workout than to do it at a different time (after physical exertion).
We must confirm that this entire choice matches the passage:

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passagebetterto perform Static Stretchingidealprotocol (S1)beforea workoutprior to vigorousphysical activity (S1)at a different timeafterphysical exertion (S3)
(D) PNF and Dynamic Stretching are not useful stretching approaches
Trap: Stretch
Notice that “not useful” is strong opinion language.

The passage says nothing specific about what the “athletes” think about PNF and Dynamic Stretching.If you picked this choice, you may have thought that “ideal protocol … involves Static Stretching” means that the athletes areagainstother methods.

However, “ideal” means “best.” So, S1 expresses only acomparisonof Static Stretching with other approaches, and doesn’t specify any additional details about the athletes’ opinion.“Not best” would be correct, but “not useful” is a stretch.After all, the idea that one method is “ideal” does not mean that other methods are “not useful.”
YOUR CHOICE(E) the purpose of stretching is to increase range of motion and performance.
Trap: Half-Right / Matches but Incorrect
It’s important to remember that we must find proof in the passage of opposite opinions for BOTH the “athletes” and “Kurz.”

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the athletes mentioned in S1 believe what this choice says.Indeed, S1 says, “the ideal stretching protocolto increase performance and range of motion.”

However, the passage says nothing about Kurz’s opinion regarding the purpose of stretching.In fact, although we can’t make any assumptions, given what the passage says, it’s likely that Kurzagreeswith this choice as well.
So, this choice matches something that the passage says but does not describe how the athletes’ opinion differs from that of Kurz.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It is helpful to notice that the first two sentences of the 1st paragraph follow the common myth structure “Many people believe X. HOWEVER, Y.” The wording in S1, “prevalent perception,” has the same meaning as “many people believe.” Then, S2 begins with the contrast marker, “however,” which signals that an idea in contrast with the “prevalent perception” is being presented, the opinion of “leading researchers and … coaches.”

“HOWEVER” is often followed by an important point, and in this case, the two statements that follow “however,” which are “leading researchers and strength and conditioning coaches … call into question multiple aspects of this method …” and “These experts make the case …,” present important points.

Now, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity,” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers” and “coaches,” in S2. What exactly do they disagree about?

In S1, “amateur athletes” believe that the “ideal stretching protocol … involves Static Stretching … prior to vigorous activity.” S2 then says, “HOWEVER, researchers … call into question multiple aspects of this method.”

It’s important to notice that the researchers call into question the stretching method, not the author of the passage. The author is describing the view of the experts, and doesn’t convey a strong opinion of her own. In S3, the author simply says, “These experts make the case.”

Then, after S2 and S3 describe the experts’ “case” about “superior alternative stretching approaches,” S4 and S5 provide details related to the “case” by describing two “superior alternative stretching approaches.”

1st paragraph summary: The author describes a common perception of a stretching method, presents a contrasting opinion held by experts, and then provides some details related to the experts’ opinion.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S6, the author acknowledges that the experts mentioned in the 1st paragraph make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S6 foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow. Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, considerations.” Three caveats are then specified:

- “it is most effective to perform most PNF stretches, aside from spine and neck stretches, only twice per week”

- “athletes frequently counteract their progress by contracting the targeted muscle with excessive force”

- “an aggressive approach can jeopardize the safety of a stretch if the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly

2nd paragraph summary: The author says that there is a body of research that supports the view of the experts and then warns that there are certain things to be aware of regarding the PNF approach described above and presents three of those things.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear throughout the passage?

The theme of the experts’ “case” or “view” appears repeatedly in the passage.

While the first sentence is about “a prevalent perception,” most of the 1st paragraph is about the experts’ case and details related to that case.

Then, the 2nd paragraph presents some caveats. At the same time, notice that the caveats are presented in the context of the statement “there is a convincing body of research to substantiate the view of these experts.” So, even the 2nd paragraph is, in a way, further discussion of the “view” of the experts.

Passage summary: The passage begins by mentioning “a prevalent perception” and then continues by presenting a contrasting “case” or “view” and discussing some details and caveats related to that contrasting “case” or “view.”
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Simpler Similar Words

prevalent

common

perception

opinion

ideal

best

protocol

process, method, procedure

call into question

disagree with

physical exertion

physical activity, exercise

dynamic

moving (this is explained in S5)

mimic

imitate, are similar to

substantiate

support

caveats

warnings, conditions, considerations

aside from

except for

moreover

furthermore, in addition, also (Note: This is a helpful additional point marker)

counteract

impede, neutralize

likewise

similarly, also (Note: This is a helpful additional point marker.)

excessive

too great

jeopardize

threaten, expose to risk

We don’t need to understand the technical vocabulary: isometric, inverse myotatic reflex, Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation. We can correctly answer every question without knowing what any of these things are.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: First opinion presented: amateur athletes’ opinion.

Let’s translate this sentence’s wording into simpler similar words:

Prevalent perception = common opinion

Ideal stretching protocol = best stretching method

Prior to vigorous activity = before activity

Simplification: Amateur athletes commonly believe that the best stretching method for achieve three goals (performance, range of motion, preventing injury) is Static Stretching before activity.

S2: Contrast with S1: Experts disagree.

Notice the contrast marker“however” and the opinion marker “call into question.” “However” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, it signals that a contrasting opinion is being presented.

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers and coaches,” in S2.

Simplification: Experts disagree with multiple aspects of the amateur athletes’ stretching method described above.

S3: The author outlines the experts’ disagreement.

The look above marker “this sequence of activities” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity”

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure that’s common in SC. This structure signifies that there are 2 reasons why the experts disagree.

Simplification: The experts make a strong case that:

1 - Static Stretching before a workout is inferior to doing it after a workout

2 - There are superior alternative approaches, such as PNF and DS. (Notice that this statement implies that the Static Stretching approach is inferior.)

S4: Details the PNF approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Naturally, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of PNF and why PNF is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “calming the muscle to prevent injury and allowing the athlete to stretch further”

S5: Details the DS approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Just like S4, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of DS and why DS is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “reducing the likelihood that the athlete will suffer an injury”

S6: The author acknowledges that the experts above make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

This sentence foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow.

Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, stipulations, considerations.”

There is also a tricky but important nuance to notice here: the contrast marker “although” at the start of the sentence can be used for different kinds and degrees of contrast. Here, it is not being used to signal direct opposites; rather, the sentence is acting as more of a “qualifier” (making a statement less absolute).

Example of opposite: Although the experts like this method, it is terrible.

Example of qualifier: Although there is evidence to support this method, it’s important to take certain precautions.

S7: Details about the first caveat

Notice that “while” is a contrast marker. So, we must check: what are the two things that are in contrast? The frequency of stretching is being contrasted: “every day” vs. “twice per week.”

Simplification: Many athletes do the same stretches every day, BUT Laughlin believes that it can be more effective to do most of them only twice per week.

Note: this simplification captures the main idea of this sentence, but we may need to analyze it in more detail for a specific question.

For example, notice that the language “most” and “aside from spine and neck stretches” means that there are exceptions; the passage does not say “all.”

S8: Details about the second caveat

Notice that “moreover” is an additional point marker. This marker tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion, more than one sentence above. Here, that conclusion is in S6: “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S9: Details about the third caveat

Notice that “likewise” (which means “similarly” or “also”) is an additional point marker that tells us that this sentence will state a point similar to the one stated by the previous sentence.

This sentence presents an additional point in support of the conclusion in S6.

question.text: The passage suggests that Laughlin would agree with which of the following points regarding the PNF method?

answers.text: Athletes should always perform PNF stretches only twice per week.
As long as PNF stretches are performed sufficiently slowly, they are no less safe than other types of stretches.
The PNF approach is more effective than some other stretching approaches.
Regarding spine and neck stretches, is most effective to do them every day of the week.
For best results, athletes must refrain from seeking to put the most energy possible into every aspect of the procedure.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, by noticing the keywords in the stem, we can search for “Laughlin.” Capitalized words tend to be easier to find in the passage.We find “Laughlin” in S7.

Next, notice that the stem also says, “regarding the PNF method.” Often, we would need to narrow our focus, taking into consideration these additional stem keywords, but in this case, the entire 2ndparagraph discusses the PNF method.
Notice that the pronouns “he” and “his” in S8 and S9 refer back to “Laughlin” in S7.
So, correctly answering this question is going to take understanding the details of S7, S8, and S9, which discuss what Kit Laughlin has “found,” “observed,” and “experienced.”
(A) Athletes should always perform PNF stretches only twice per week.
Trap: Stretch
Notice the extreme marker “always,” which is a yellow flag.Before picking this choice, we would need proof in the passage to support “always.”

In “Kit Laughlin has found that it is most effective to perform most PNF stretches, aside from spine and neck stretches, only twice per week,” notice the language “most” and “aside from spine and neck stretches.” This language indicates that there are exceptions.In other words, the passage does not say thatallPNP stretches “should always” be performed only twice per week.
(B) As long as PNF stretches are performed sufficiently slowly, they are no less safe than other types of stretches.
Trap: Wrong Comparison
This choice is tempting, because much of it matches well with S8: “jeopardize the safety of the stretch if the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly.”
However, we must notice the comparison marker “no less safe” in this choice.We can see that this choice compares the safety of PNF stretches with that of other stretches.
In the passage, no comparison is made of the safety of types of stretches.
(C) The PNF approach is more effective than some other stretching approaches.

As always, we must be careful to notice singular and plural nouns.In this case, the plural “some other stretching approaches” does not match the passage.After all, in the passage the effectiveness of the PNF approach is mentioned in comparison with the effectiveness of onlyoneother stretching approach, Static Stretching, not with the effectiveness of multiple “stretching approaches.”

Furthermore, the passage does not make clear that Kit Laughlin is one of the “researchers and strength and conditioning coaches” mentioned in S2.Thus, it is not even clear that Kit Laughlin is among those who are of the opinion that the PNF approach is more effective than Static Stretching.
(D) Regarding spine and neck stretches, is most effective to do them every day of the week.
Trap: Stretch
Notice the extreme marker, “every,” which is a yellow flag.

Before picking this choice, we would need proof in the passage to support “every.” The passage does indeed say that “spine and neck stretches” are an exception to the guideline of “only twice per week.” But does that mean that “it is most effective to do them EVERY day”? No.
YOUR CHOICE(E) For best results, athletes must refrain from seeking to put the most energy possible into every aspect of the procedure.
Notice that this choice is implied by what S8 says.
S8 says the following about PNF: “athletes frequently counteract their progress by contracting the targeted muscle with excessive force — only 20 to 30 percent of their maximum effort is necessary.”
We can see that that statement implies that “For best results, athletes must refrain from seeking to put the most energy possible into every aspect of the procedure.”

In other words, according the passage, Kit Laughlin has observed that by “contracting … with excessive force” athletes “counteract” their results.So, it can be inferred that Kit Laughlin would recommend that, “for best results,” athletes “refrain from seeking to put the most energy possible into” contraction, which is an “aspect of the procedure.”

question.text: According to the passage, which of the following is true of the inverse myotatic reflex?

answers.text: It creates an isometric force in opposition to resistance.
The stretched muscle contracts once the inverse myotatic reflex is activated.
It helps an athlete to warm up before an activity.
Without it, the PNF stretching approach would be useless.
The limit to which an athlete can stretch changes when the inverse myotatic reflex is induced.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, by noticing the keywords in the stem of this Detail question, we can search for “inverse myotatic reflex.” Note that this is a very specific and technical phrase, so we can expect these exact keywords to appear in the passage.We find these keywords in S4.

In S4, notice the present participial phrase (a type of modifier that we learned about in SC): “calming the muscle to prevent injury and allowing the athlete to stretch further than was previously possible.” This structure is often used to present effects of a cause.

So, “the inverse myotatic reflex is triggered, calming the muscle to prevent injury and allowing the athlete to stretch further,” means that the cause of the effects is “the inverse myotatic reflex is triggered.”
Two effects of that cause are mentioned in the present participial phrase:
- calming the muscle to prevent injury
- allowing the athlete to stretch further than was previously possible
There is a good chance that one of these effects is mentioned in the correct answer.

Also, notice the transition word “consequently,” which means “as a result.” It is also possible that the correct answer could connect to the first half of S4, which suggests that “the inverse myotatic reflex” is a result of muscle contraction and isometric force: “the athlete contracts the muscle being stretched and applies an isometric force against resistance from a partner or immovable object; consequently, the inverse myotatic reflex is triggered.”
(A) It creates an isometric force in opposition to resistance.
Trap: Opposite
In this choice, notice that the word “creates” means “causes.”
However, in our Question Analysis, we saw that the “reflex” is aresultof an isometric force, not acause.Our biggest clue in S4 is the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.”
(B) The stretched muscle contracts once the inverse myotatic reflex is activated.
Trap: Opposite
In this choice, notice that the word “once” implies that the reflex is thecauseof the stretched muscle contracting.
However, in our Question Analysis, we saw that the “reflex” is aresultof the muscle contraction, not acause.Our biggest clue in S4 is the transition word, “consequently,” which means “as a result.”
(C) It helps an athlete to warm up before an activity.
Trap: Nearby
This choice mentions information from the wrong sentence, S5, which mentions “a warm-up” in association with a completely different stretching approach, DS.
The passage mentions no connection between warming up and “the inverse myotatic reflex.”
(D) Without it, the PNF stretching approach would be useless.
Trap:Stretch
Notice that “useless” is a strong negative opinion marker.Does the passage support this strong opinion?

The passage says that the “reflex” does cause some positive effects (prevent injury, stretch further).However, we don’t know whether there areotherpositive effects from the PNF approach that don’t relate to the reflex.So, the passage does not suggest that the PNP approach would be completely useless without the inverse myotatic reflex.
YOUR CHOICE(E) The limit to which an athlete can stretch changes when the inverse myotatic reflex is induced.

In our Question Analysis, we saw that the passage indicates that “the inverse myotatic reflex is triggered” is the cause of two effects and that one of those effects is “allowing the athlete to stretch further than was previously possible.”
To see that this choice is correct, we have to notice that, if the athlete can “stretch further than was previously possible,” then “the limit to which an athlete can stretch” has changed.
So, we can see that the passage suggests exactly what this choice says, that, when the inverse myotatic reflex is “triggered” or “induced,” the limit to which an athlete can stretch changes.
As always, we must confirm that the wording in this choice matches the passage:

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It is helpful to notice that the first two sentences of the 1st paragraph follow the common myth structure “Many people believe X. HOWEVER, Y.” The wording in S1, “prevalent perception,” has the same meaning as “many people believe.” Then, S2 begins with the contrast marker, “however,” which signals that an idea in contrast with the “prevalent perception” is being presented, the opinion of “leading researchers and … coaches.”

“HOWEVER” is often followed by an important point, and in this case, the two statements that follow “however,” which are “leading researchers and strength and conditioning coaches … call into question multiple aspects of this method …” and “These experts make the case …,” present important points.

Now, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity,” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers” and “coaches,” in S2. What exactly do they disagree about?

In S1, “amateur athletes” believe that the “ideal stretching protocol … involves Static Stretching … prior to vigorous activity.” S2 then says, “HOWEVER, researchers … call into question multiple aspects of this method.”

It’s important to notice that the researchers call into question the stretching method, not the author of the passage. The author is describing the view of the experts, and doesn’t convey a strong opinion of her own. In S3, the author simply says, “These experts make the case.”

Then, after S2 and S3 describe the experts’ “case” about “superior alternative stretching approaches,” S4 and S5 provide details related to the “case” by describing two “superior alternative stretching approaches.”

1st paragraph summary: The author describes a common perception of a stretching method, presents a contrasting opinion held by experts, and then provides some details related to the experts’ opinion.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S6, the author acknowledges that the experts mentioned in the 1st paragraph make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S6 foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow. Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, considerations.” Three caveats are then specified:

- “it is most effective to perform most PNF stretches, aside from spine and neck stretches, only twice per week”

- “athletes frequently counteract their progress by contracting the targeted muscle with excessive force”

- “an aggressive approach can jeopardize the safety of a stretch if the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly

2nd paragraph summary: The author says that there is a body of research that supports the view of the experts and then warns that there are certain things to be aware of regarding the PNF approach described above and presents three of those things.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear throughout the passage?

The theme of the experts’ “case” or “view” appears repeatedly in the passage.

While the first sentence is about “a prevalent perception,” most of the 1st paragraph is about the experts’ case and details related to that case.

Then, the 2nd paragraph presents some caveats. At the same time, notice that the caveats are presented in the context of the statement “there is a convincing body of research to substantiate the view of these experts.” So, even the 2nd paragraph is, in a way, further discussion of the “view” of the experts.

Passage summary: The passage begins by mentioning “a prevalent perception” and then continues by presenting a contrasting “case” or “view” and discussing some details and caveats related to that contrasting “case” or “view.”
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: First opinion presented: amateur athletes’ opinion.

Let’s translate this sentence’s wording into simpler similar words:

Prevalent perception = common opinion

Ideal stretching protocol = best stretching method

Prior to vigorous activity = before activity

Simplification: Amateur athletes commonly believe that the best stretching method for achieve three goals (performance, range of motion, preventing injury) is Static Stretching before activity.

S2: Contrast with S1: Experts disagree.

Notice the contrast marker“however” and the opinion marker “call into question.” “However” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, it signals that a contrasting opinion is being presented.

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers and coaches,” in S2.

Simplification: Experts disagree with multiple aspects of the amateur athletes’ stretching method described above.

S3: The author outlines the experts’ disagreement.

The look above marker “this sequence of activities” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity”

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure that’s common in SC. This structure signifies that there are 2 reasons why the experts disagree.

Simplification: The experts make a strong case that:

1 - Static Stretching before a workout is inferior to doing it after a workout

2 - There are superior alternative approaches, such as PNF and DS. (Notice that this statement implies that the Static Stretching approach is inferior.)

S4: Details the PNF approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Naturally, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of PNF and why PNF is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “calming the muscle to prevent injury and allowing the athlete to stretch further”

S5: Details the DS approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Just like S4, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of DS and why DS is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “reducing the likelihood that the athlete will suffer an injury”

S6: The author acknowledges that the experts above make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

This sentence foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow.

Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, stipulations, considerations.”

There is also a tricky but important nuance to notice here: the contrast marker “although” at the start of the sentence can be used for different kinds and degrees of contrast. Here, it is not being used to signal direct opposites; rather, the sentence is acting as more of a “qualifier” (making a statement less absolute).

Example of opposite: Although the experts like this method, it is terrible.

Example of qualifier: Although there is evidence to support this method, it’s important to take certain precautions.

S7: Details about the first caveat

Notice that “while” is a contrast marker. So, we must check: what are the two things that are in contrast? The frequency of stretching is being contrasted: “every day” vs. “twice per week.”

Simplification: Many athletes do the same stretches every day, BUT Laughlin believes that it can be more effective to do most of them only twice per week.

Note: this simplification captures the main idea of this sentence, but we may need to analyze it in more detail for a specific question.

For example, notice that the language “most” and “aside from spine and neck stretches” means that there are exceptions; the passage does not say “all.”

S8: Details about the second caveat

Notice that “moreover” is an additional point marker. This marker tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion, more than one sentence above. Here, that conclusion is in S6: “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S9: Details about the third caveat

Notice that “likewise” (which means “similarly” or “also”) is an additional point marker that tells us that this sentence will state a point similar to the one stated by the previous sentence.

This sentence presents an additional point in support of the conclusion in S6.

question.text: Which of the following conclusions about Dynamic Stretching is best supported by the passage?

answers.text: If it is performed, the athlete will gain the ability to stretch further.
Some experts argue that it produces results superior to those produced by the PNF approach.
It does not provide value to an athlete when it is performed after a workout or competition.
If it is not performed, an athlete may have a higher probability of sustaining damage to his or her body.
It is more effective than Static Stretching only when performed as a warm-up.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, by noticing the keywords in the stem, we can search for “Dynamic Stretching.” Capitalized words tend to be easier to find in the passage.We find “Dynamic Stretching” in S3, where it is mentioned as an example of a “superior alternative stretching approach” and is abbreviated as “DS.” Within S3, notice that no further details are given about DS.
Next, where are there more details about “DS”? S5 starts with “In the DS approach,” so we should focus on S5.
S5 includes 3 details about “DS”:
- often completed as a warm-up in advance of a workout or competition
- functional movements that mimic the activity or sport (e.g., lunges or trunk rotations) are performed
- these movements reduce the likelihood that the athlete will suffer an injury
Therefore, we can expect the correct answer to involve one of these details.

There’s one more thing to be aware of when we go to the answer choices.Notice that all of the answer choices use the pronoun “it”.Since the stem asks about Dynamic Stretching, this pronoun “it” refers to Dynamic Stretching.
(A) If it is performed, the athlete will gain the ability to stretch further.
Trap: Nearby
We must always make sure we are focused on the right piece of the passage to answer the specific question in the stem.

In this choice, the wording “gain the ability to stretch further” matches S4, which is talking about a different stretching approach, PNF.As discussed in our Question Analysis, we must focus on the DS approach in S5.
(B) Some experts argue that it produces results superior to those produced by the PNF approach.
Trap: Wrong Comparison
Notice the comparison marker, “superior results.” However, the DS and PNF approaches are not compared in the passage.
Rather, in S3, both DS and PNF are cited as “superior alternative stretching approaches” in comparison with the “Static Stretching” method that the “amateur athletes” prefer.
(C) It does not provide value to an athlete when it is performed after a workout or competition.
Trap: Speculation
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that the time frame of performing DS is mentioned in S5: “often completed as a warm-upin advance of a workout.”

However, notice that “does not provide value” is a strong opinion, and there is no proof in the passage for this strong opinion.The passage doesn’t say anything specific about “aftera workout.” We do know that it is “often completed as a warm-upin advanceof a workout,” but we can’t make any speculations about “aftera workout.”

It is helpful to notice that the word “often” in S5 does not mean “always”, and implies that there could be exceptions to the time frame “in advance”.In other words, it is possible that sometimes the DS approach is performed after a workout and does provide value.
YOUR CHOICE(D) If it is not performed, an athlete may have a higher probability of sustaining damage to his or her body.
Notice that this choice mentions “sustaining damage to his or her body”.Does this quote match with one of the three details about DS that we found in our Question Analysis?

Yes, the passage says that “these movements reduce the likelihood that the athlete will suffer an injury.” “Injury” is a different way of saying “sustaining damage to his or her body.” So, the passage says, essentially, “doing DS lowers the risk of damage.”

Next, we must carefully determine whether the rest of this choice matches the passage.Notice the comparison marker, “higherprobability … of damage”, and the conditional statement, “if it is NOT performed.”
So, to simplify, this choice says, “not doing DS increases the risk of damage.”
When we compare these two simplified statements, do the meanings match?
Passage: doing DS lowers the risk of damage.
This choice: not doing DS increases the risk of damage.
Yes, the wording is different, but the meanings are identical.

It is helpful to notice this common pattern in RC, in which the language in the answer choice is reversed from what the passage says, but the meaning matches.(Another way to think about reversed language is through a math example: if the passage were to say that “X is greater than Y,” we could correctly conclude in the answer choice that “Y is less than X”)
(E) It is more effective than Static Stretching only when performed as a warm-up.
Trap: Half-Right
Notice the extreme marker “only,” which is a yellow flag.
What does the passage actually say?
S3 says, essentially, that Dynamic stretching is more effective than Static stretching: “there are superior alternative approaches, such as … Dynamic Stretching.”
Then, S5 says, “The DS approach, which is often completed as a warm-up.”
Notice, however, that the passage does not combine these two ideas.In other words, the passage never says that DS is more effectiveonly whencompleted as a warm-up.
So, this choice does not match the passage.

question.text: It can be inferred that the author of the passage would be most likely to make which of the following recommendations to an athlete considering the PNF method?

answers.text: Focus his or her energy on consciously activating the inverse myotatic reflex.
Use an alternative to the PNF approach in order to stretch with low risk of injury.
Avoid putting too much power into the contraction of the specified muscle.
Be careful not to move excessively slowly into the stretch.
Perform a similar routine for stretching each day.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, we must always make sure that we understand exactly what the stem is asking for.

From the keywords in the stem, we know that we must focus on a “recommendation” about “PNF” from “the author.” The word “recommendation” signals that we must find the opinion of the author.Where is the author expressing an opinion about the PNF method?

From our Passage Analysis, we see that the author’s opinion regarding the PNF method is at the start of the second paragraph: in S6, the author says, “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”
This sentence foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow.Indeed, three caveats are specified, in S7, S8, and S9.

Thus, we can infer that the author’s opinion is that the caveats are important.In other words, even though the caveats are expressed as things that “Kit Laughlin has found,” they are also recommendations of the author.
So, correctly answering this question requires keeping those caveats straight in our minds and finding a choice that matches one or more of them.
(A) Focus his or her energy on consciously activating the inverse myotatic reflex.
Trap: Nearby / Distortion

If picked this choice, you may have noticed that S4 discusses the PNF approach and that “activating the inverse myotatic reflex” is mentioned in S4: “consequently, the inverse myotatic reflex is triggered.”

Notice, however, that S4 doesn’t represent arecommendation or opinionof the author; rather, it’s just a neutral description of how the PNF approach works.We must always remember to focus on the portion of the passage that connects to the specific question asked in the stem.

Furthermore, there is nothing in the passage about “focushis or her energy onconsciouslyactivating” the reflex.Since passage uses the causation marker, “consequently,” it seems more likely that the reflex is just a natural consequence of other conscious efforts: “the athlete contracts the muscle being stretched and applies an isometric force …;consequently, the inverse myotatic reflex is triggered.”
(B) Use an alternative to the PNF approach in order to stretch with low risk of injury.
Trap: Half-Right / Wrong Comparison

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “risk of injury” is related to “jeopardize the safety of the stretch” in S9.However, we must always make sure that the entire answer choice matches the passage and answers the question in the stem.

Notice that the wording in this choice, “use an alternative…approach,” implies a comparison between the risk associated with the PNP approach and that associated with alternative stretching approaches.

However, we can see that the author doesn’tcomparePNF with alternative approaches.Rather, S9 discusses improper use of the PNF approach: “can jeopardize the safety of a stretch if the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly into the fully stretched position.” In fact, for all we know from what the passage says, this statement about jeopardizing the safety of a stretch applies to alternative stretching approaches as well.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Avoid putting too much power into the contraction of the specified muscle.
Does this choice match one of the three “caveats” that we mentioned in our Question Analysis?
S8 says, “athletes frequentlycounteract their progressby contracting the targeted muscle withexcessive force— only 20 to 30 percent of their maximum effort is necessary.”
As we often see in correct answers, the wording in this choice is a bit different from the passage, but the meaning matches.
Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in Passageavoidcounteract their progresstoo much powerexcessive forcecontraction of the specified musclecontracting the targeted muscle
So, we can infer that this choice expresses a recommendation that the author would make.
(D) Be careful not to move excessively slowly into the stretch.
Trap: Opposite
Notice the word “not” in this choice.We must be careful that we understand the direction, to avoid falling into an Opposite trap.

Let’s simplify this choice: “be careful not to move excessively slowly” means “don’t move too slowly.” In other words, this choice says that “moving too slowly” is a PROBLEM: basically, “slow is BAD.”

However, S9 says that moving slowly is the SOLUTION: “an aggressive approach can jeopardize the safety of a stretchif the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly into the fully stretched position.” In other words, the passage says that slow is GOOD.
So, we must be careful not to mix up the direction and fall into an Opposite trap.
(E) Perform a similar routine for stretching each day.
Trap: Opposite

Notice that S7 uses the contrast marker “while”: “While many athletes are accustomed to using the same stretching routineevery day of the week, longtime PNF instructor Kit Laughlin has found thatit is most effectiveto perform most PNF stretches, aside from spine and neck stretches,only twice per week.”

To simplify, S7 is saying that performing most PNF stretches “only twice per week” ismore “effective”than “the same stretching routine every day.” Therefore, the author would NOT recommend what this choice says: “a similar routine for stretching each day.”
So, this choice says essentially the opposite of what the passage says.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
It is helpful to notice that the first two sentences of the 1st paragraph follow the common myth structure “Many people believe X. HOWEVER, Y.” The wording in S1, “prevalent perception,” has the same meaning as “many people believe.” Then, S2 begins with the contrast marker, “however,” which signals that an idea in contrast with the “prevalent perception” is being presented, the opinion of “leading researchers and … coaches.”

“HOWEVER” is often followed by an important point, and in this case, the two statements that follow “however,” which are “leading researchers and strength and conditioning coaches … call into question multiple aspects of this method …” and “These experts make the case …,” present important points.

Now, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity,” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers” and “coaches,” in S2. What exactly do they disagree about?

In S1, “amateur athletes” believe that the “ideal stretching protocol … involves Static Stretching … prior to vigorous activity.” S2 then says, “HOWEVER, researchers … call into question multiple aspects of this method.”

It’s important to notice that the researchers call into question the stretching method, not the author of the passage. The author is describing the view of the experts, and doesn’t convey a strong opinion of her own. In S3, the author simply says, “These experts make the case.”

Then, after S2 and S3 describe the experts’ “case” about “superior alternative stretching approaches,” S4 and S5 provide details related to the “case” by describing two “superior alternative stretching approaches.”

1st paragraph summary: The author describes a common perception of a stretching method, presents a contrasting opinion held by experts, and then provides some details related to the experts’ opinion.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S6, the author acknowledges that the experts mentioned in the 1st paragraph make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S6 foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow. Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, considerations.” Three caveats are then specified:

- “it is most effective to perform most PNF stretches, aside from spine and neck stretches, only twice per week”

- “athletes frequently counteract their progress by contracting the targeted muscle with excessive force”

- “an aggressive approach can jeopardize the safety of a stretch if the athlete does not move sufficiently slowly

2nd paragraph summary: The author says that there is a body of research that supports the view of the experts and then warns that there are certain things to be aware of regarding the PNF approach described above and presents three of those things.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear throughout the passage?

The theme of the experts’ “case” or “view” appears repeatedly in the passage.

While the first sentence is about “a prevalent perception,” most of the 1st paragraph is about the experts’ case and details related to that case.

Then, the 2nd paragraph presents some caveats. At the same time, notice that the caveats are presented in the context of the statement “there is a convincing body of research to substantiate the view of these experts.” So, even the 2nd paragraph is, in a way, further discussion of the “view” of the experts.

Passage summary: The passage begins by mentioning “a prevalent perception” and then continues by presenting a contrasting “case” or “view” and discussing some details and caveats related to that contrasting “case” or “view.”
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

We don’t need to understand the technical vocabulary: isometric, inverse myotatic reflex, Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation. We can correctly answer every question without knowing what any of these things are.
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: First opinion presented: amateur athletes’ opinion.

Let’s translate this sentence’s wording into simpler similar words:

Prevalent perception = common opinion

Ideal stretching protocol = best stretching method

Prior to vigorous activity = before activity

Simplification: Amateur athletes commonly believe that the best stretching method for achieve three goals (performance, range of motion, preventing injury) is Static Stretching before activity.

S2: Contrast with S1: Experts disagree.

Notice the contrast marker“however” and the opinion marker “call into question.” “However” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, it signals that a contrasting opinion is being presented.

Also, since “THIS X” is a look above marker, when we see “this method,” we must understand what is above that “this method” refers to. We see that “this method” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity” in S1.

So, we can see that there is a disagreement between the “amateur athletes,” in S1, and the “researchers and coaches,” in S2.

Simplification: Experts disagree with multiple aspects of the amateur athletes’ stretching method described above.

S3: The author outlines the experts’ disagreement.

The look above marker “this sequence of activities” refers to “Static Stretching … prior to vigorous physical activity”

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure that’s common in SC. This structure signifies that there are 2 reasons why the experts disagree.

Simplification: The experts make a strong case that:

1 - Static Stretching before a workout is inferior to doing it after a workout

2 - There are superior alternative approaches, such as PNF and DS. (Notice that this statement implies that the Static Stretching approach is inferior.)

S4: Details the PNF approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Naturally, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of PNF and why PNF is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “calming the muscle to prevent injury and allowing the athlete to stretch further”

S5: Details the DS approach. (Note that these details are a lower priority, so it’s generally best to review these details more carefully later if there’s a question about it.)

Just like S4, this sentence supports S3, with info on the benefits of DS and why DS is an example of a “superior alternative approach”: “reducing the likelihood that the athlete will suffer an injury”

S6: The author acknowledges that the experts above make a strong case, but also warns that “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

This sentence foreshadows that details about “some potential caveats” will follow.

Note that “caveats” means “warnings, conditions, stipulations, considerations.”

There is also a tricky but important nuance to notice here: the contrast marker “although” at the start of the sentence can be used for different kinds and degrees of contrast. Here, it is not being used to signal direct opposites; rather, the sentence is acting as more of a “qualifier” (making a statement less absolute).

Example of opposite: Although the experts like this method, it is terrible.

Example of qualifier: Although there is evidence to support this method, it’s important to take certain precautions.

S7: Details about the first caveat

Notice that “while” is a contrast marker. So, we must check: what are the two things that are in contrast? The frequency of stretching is being contrasted: “every day” vs. “twice per week.”

Simplification: Many athletes do the same stretches every day, BUT Laughlin believes that it can be more effective to do most of them only twice per week.

Note: this simplification captures the main idea of this sentence, but we may need to analyze it in more detail for a specific question.

For example, notice that the language “most” and “aside from spine and neck stretches” means that there are exceptions; the passage does not say “all.”

S8: Details about the second caveat

Notice that “moreover” is an additional point marker. This marker tells us that the purpose of this sentence is to support a conclusion, more than one sentence above. Here, that conclusion is in S6: “it is necessary to discuss some caveats associated with the PNF method.”

S9: Details about the third caveat

Notice that “likewise” (which means “similarly” or “also”) is an additional point marker that tells us that this sentence will state a point similar to the one stated by the previous sentence.

This sentence presents an additional point in support of the conclusion in S6.

question.text: The passage implies which of the following about physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime?

answers.text: Such traits are less useful than physical traits passed from parents to offspring by means of information encoded in DNA.
Such traits were not observed by biologists before the late 19th century.
Lamarck hypothesized that organisms developed such traits through modification of gene expression.
The development of such traits may or may not represent evolution.
Such traits never persist for more than a few generations.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.
We can see from “the passageimplies” that this is an Inference question.
The stem asks about “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime.” Since this topic is discussed throughout the passage, it is harder than usual to know where to focus.

However, when the topic in the stem is particularly broad, the correct answer will often connect to important points in the passage.In our Passage and Sentence Analyses, we noted that the author offers conclusions of her own at the end of the 2ndand 3rdparagraphs.So, these are key points to keep in mind:

S9: “So, this type of passing of traits from generation to generation does not result in evolution.” (The author concludes that the discovery introduced in S5 does not support Lamarck’s theory of evolution)

S12: "Thus, the changes in traits resulting from these organisms’ changes to their genes truly constitute evolution.” (The author concludes that the discovery discussed in S10 and S11 does represent evolution.)
Also, as we evaluate each answer choice, we can use keywords from the choice to find information related to the choice in the passage.
(Note: the term “SUCH traits” in each of the answer choices in this question refers to the phrase in the stem “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime.”)
(A) Such traits are less useful than physical traits passed from parents to offspring by means of information encoded in DNA.
Trap: Wrong Comparison
When we notice a comparison marker in an answer choice, such as “less useful than” in this choice, we must be particularly careful to confirm that the comparison is fully supported by the passage.
Is any comparison about theusefulnessof traits made in the passage?
No, so we can safely eliminate this choice.
(B) Such traits were not observed by biologists before the late 19th century.
Trap: Mix-Up
Scanning the passage to see what it says about “the late 19th century,” we see that S2 says, “with the discovery of genetics in the late 19th and early 20th centuries.”

So, the passage indicates that genetics were discovered during the late 19th century, but does it say anything about “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime” and “the late 19th century”?

With a quick scan of the rest of the first paragraph, we can see that the passage mentions “physical traits” but never sayswhenbiologists observed “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime.”
So, this choice is not supported by the passage.
(C) Lamarck hypothesized that organisms developed such traits through modification of gene expression.
Trap: Speculation / Distortion
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that the 2ndparagraph discusses whether the discovery of modification of gene expression supports Lamarck’s theory.

However, the passage does not indicate that “Lamarck hypothesized” about modification of gene expression.Gene expression modification is presented in the passage as discovered “more recently” (S5), whereas Lamarck’s theory was proposed “in 1809” (S1).
YOUR CHOICE(D) The development of such traits may or may not represent evolution.
To find the information we need for determining whether this choice is correct, we can consider where the passage discusses “may or may not represent evolution.”
In our question analysis, we saw that the author states the following two conclusions at the end of the 2ndparagraph and at the end of the 3rdparagraph:
S9: “So, this type of passing of traits from generation to generation does not result in evolution.”
S12: "Thus, the changes in traits resulting from these organisms’ changes to their genes truly constitute evolution.”

We can see that S9 says that passing of “traits” does NOT represent evolution and that S12 says that changes in “traits” DO represent evolution.So, these two sentences seem to match what this choice says, that development of traits “mayormay notrepresent evolution.”

However, to ensure that this choice is the correct answer, we have to confirm that the “traits” mentioned in S9 and S12 are the traits this question is about, “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime.”

Checking the 2ndparagraph, we see that S6 mentions “that an organism can modify its gene expression todevelop particular physical traits during its lifetime.” So, the “traits” discussed in the 2ndparagraph are “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime.”

Thus, S9 conveys basically that development of “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime” does NOT represent evolution, which matches the “may not represent evolution” part of this choice.

Checking the 3rdparagraph, we see that S11 mentions “these changes made by these organismsduring their lifetimes.” Then, looking above to see what “these changes” are, we see “able tochange … their physical traits” in S10.So, the “changes in traits” discussed in the 3rdparagraph are changes in physical traits “during their lifetimes.”
Thus, S12 says basically that development of “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime” DOES represent evolution, which matches the “may … represent evolution” part of this choice.
So, S9 and S12 say basically what this choice says, and therefore this choice is supported by the passage.
(E) Such traits never persist for more than a few generations.
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S8 mentions that, in the case of gene expression modifications, “traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring foronly a few generations.”
However, notice in this choice the extreme marker “never,” which is a yellow flag.

The fact that the 2ndparagraph mentions one situation in which “physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime” persist “for only a few generations” doesn’t mean that “such traitsneverpersist for more than a few generations.” After all, there could be other situations in which such traits do persist for more than a few generations.

Furthermore, the 3rdparagraph presents a situation in which traits that organisms develop “during their lifetimes” are “passed on from generation to generationindefinitely,” in other words, for many generations.This information in the third paragraph directly contradicts this choice.
So, this choice goes beyond what the passage says.

When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:
