The passage provides context and details about what this means so we don’t have to know on our own. We must not make the assumption that this term means that the genes themselves are being changed (modification); rather, “gene expression” has a different meaning from just “genes.” We can see this most clearly in the passage with the comparison made in S11: “UNLIKE gene expression modifications, these changes made by these organisms during their lifetimes involve changes to the information encoded in genetic material and are passed from generation to generation indefinitely.”
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. For best results, as you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice the opinion marker, “Lamarck proposed that ….” This sentence introduces Lamarck’s theory of evolution, which is an opinion on how evolution occurs.

Simplification: Lamarck’s theory is that evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.

S2: The contrast marker “however” signals that this sentence presents an important point, which is “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted.”

Note that the tone clue “seemed,” signals some uncertainty about whether Lamarck’s theory had actually been refuted and foreshadows that there may be further debate or insights on this topic.

Simplification: The discovery of genetics seemed to disprove Lamarck’s theory.

S3: Follows up the important point in S2 by presenting reasons why “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted.”

Notice the opinion marker “biologists decided.” So, this sentence presents biologists’ opinions.

The biologists had two opinions:

“all physical traits are passed from parents to offspring by means of information encoded in genetic material”

“such information is unaffected by an organism’s development of traits during its lifetime”

Notice the extreme marker “all” in “ALL physical traits are passed from parents to offspring” by means of genetic information that doesn’t change during an organism’s lifetime. In other words, the meaning is that NO physical traits are passed from parents to offspring in other ways, such as the way that Lamarck theorizes: “organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.”

Simplification: Biologists arrived at two conclusions about the passing of traits from parents to offspring that conflicted with Lamarck’s theory.

S4: Notice the premise and conclusion marker “SINCE premise, conclusion.”

We can see that premise part of S4 directly counters Lamarck’s theory:

S1: “Lamarck proposed that evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring”

S4: “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring”

Also notice that the conclusion part of S4 has a meaning similar to the point of S2:

S2: “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted

S4: “they had made it appear that evolution as Lamarck had described it is impossible”

Simplification: Since biologists believed that traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could NOT be passed on to its offspring, it seemed that Lamarck’s theory of evolution is impossible.

S5: Introduces a recent discovery, “modification of gene expression,” that counters the biologists’ premise in S4.

Notice the contrast marker “However.”

It’s also helpful to notice the look above marker, “SUCH a mechanism”; we must understand what is above that “such a mechanism” refers to. We see that S4 says, “Since … biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.”

So, the contrast presented by S5 is between the view of “biologists” in the past and a recent discovery about the existence of this “mechanism.” The biologists’ view (in the timeframe mentioned in S2, the “late 19th and early 20th centuries”) was that this mechanism doesn’t exist.

(Note: The technical term “modification of gene expression” is introduced here. This term and “gene expression modifications” appear throughout the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. The passage provides context and details about what this means so we don’t have to know on our own.)

S6: Provides details about the discovery introduced in S5

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure. This structure (“found not only that X but also that Y”) signifies that there are two findings:

“an organism can modify its gene expression to develop particular physical traits during its lifetime”

“gene expression modifications made during an organism’s lifetime and the traits associated with those modifications CAN be passed on from the organism to its offspring.

What’s the key difference between this discovery and the biologists’ belief in S4? Whereas S4 says, “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring,” this discovery is that new traits developed during an organism’s lifetime CAN be passed on to offspring. We know from the 1st paragraph that passing on traits is an important element of “evolution.”

S7: The author poses a rhetorical question about the implications of the discovery presented in S5 and S6.

Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Simplification: The author is asking whether the discovery presented in S5 and S6 at least partly counters the biologists’ refutation of Lamarck’s evolutionary theory by proving Lamarck’s theory at least somewhat valid.

S8: Answers the rhetorical question in S7, with the tone of “not really.”

Notice the contrast structure, “X, BUT Y.” This structure puts emphasis on the Y part. In this case, we can see the contrast between “some people contend that it has” and “most evolutionary biologists point out that ….”

In the Y part, notice the qualifier marker “only a few.” The biologists point out a key fact about the discovery that “the traits associated with those modifications can be passed on.” That fact is that the “traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for ONLY a few generations and eventually disappear.”

Simplification: Some people argue that this discovery supports Lamarck’s theory, but most evolutionary biologists argue that Lamarck’s theory is still not valid, because the traits are passed on for only a few generations and eventually disappear.

S9: The author answers her rhetorical question, S7, by concluding that the discovery introduced in S5 does not support Lamarck’s theory of evolution.

Notice the conclusion marker, “So,” which is similar to “therefore.”

Notice also that S9 is a direct conclusion of the author’s, not the opinion of another person or group.

Notice the look above marker, “THIS type of passing of traits”; we must understand what is above that “this type of passing of traits” refers to. We see that S8 says, “such gene expression modifications and their associated traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for only a few generations and eventually disappear.”

Simplification: The “type of passing of traits” discussed in the 2nd paragraph is not evolution.

S10: Introduces another discovery, which supports Lamarck’s theory.

Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.”

Notice the contrast marker “nevertheless,” which conveys, “while the statements above are true, the following is also true.”

The contrast is that, whereas S8 and S9 go against Lamarck’s theory, S10 supports Lamarck’s theory: “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge.”

Simplification: Nevertheless, one discovery does support Lamarck’s theory: single-celled organisms that can change their genes and traits during their lifetimes.

S11: Describes a difference between these genetic changes and the “gene expression modifications” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker structure, “Unlike X, Y.” S11 is saying that “these changes made by these organisms” are “unlike” the gene expression modifications discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Simplification: Unlike the changes described in the 2nd paragraph, the changes made by these organisms during their lifetimes are passed from generation to generation indefinitely (for an unlimited period of time).

S12: The author concludes that the discovery discussed in S10 and S11 does represent evolution.

Notice the conclusion marker “Thus,” which is similar to “therefore.”

Notice also that S12 is a direct conclusion of the author, not the opinion of another group.

Simplification: The changes in traits made by these organisms represent evolution.

question.text: Which of the following can be inferred about the biologists mentioned in the first paragraph?

answers.text: They were not aware that a modification of gene expression and its associated traits could be passed on from an organism to its offspring.
If they had not discovered genetics, they would have continued to accept Lamarck’s theory as correct.
The results of their research served to confirm the validity of the evolutionary theory proposed by Charles Darwin.
Their research did not include any study of single-celled organisms.
They failed to understand that an organism can develop traits during its lifetime even if the information encoded in its genetic material remains unchanged.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.
We can see from “can be inferred” that this is an Inference question.
The stem asks about “the biologists mentioned in the first paragraph,” so it’s a good idea to review the biologists’ views in S3 and S4.

Also, since the first sentence of the second paragraph, S5, presents information that contrasts with the views of “the biologists mentioned in the first paragraph,” it could help to review S5 as well.
CORRECT ANSWER(A) They were not aware that a modification of gene expression and its associated traits could be passed on from an organism to its offspring.
Notice that this choice mentions “modification of gene expression,” which is introduced in S5.S5 also includes the contrast marker “however.” So, what are the two things that are in contrast?
S4: “biologists had essentiallyruled outthe existence of a mechanism via whichtraits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.”
S5: “more recently, such a mechanism has been discovered in the form of modification of gene expression.”
Note that “more recently, … discovered” in S5 suggests that these biologists were “not aware” earlier of this phenomenon involving “modification of gene expression.”

Additionally, the wording in S4, “ruled out,” matches with “not aware.” After all, if the biologists “ruled out” the existence of “a mechanism via which traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring,” and then later, such a mechanism was “discovered in the form of modification of gene expression,” then it follows that the biologists were “not aware” that a modification of gene expression and its associated traits could be passed on from an organism to its offspring.
So, we can see that this choice is supported by the passage.
(B) If they had not discovered genetics, they would have continued to accept Lamarck’s theory as correct.
Trap: Distortion / Speculation

A useful clue that this choice may involve an unsupported speculation is the grammatical structure for a hypothetical situation: “IfX, theywouldhave Y.” There isn’t support in the passage for this hypothetical statement.Given what the passage says, it’s possible that the biologists would have rejected Lamarck’s theory for another reason if they had not discovered genetics.

Additionally, the specific wording here, “continued to acceptLamarck’s theory as correct,” implies that before the discovery of genetics, the biologists did accept Lamarck’s theory as correct.However, the passage does not indicate that the biologists ever accepted Lamarck’s theory as correct.
YOUR CHOICE(C) The results of their research served to confirm the validity of the evolutionary theory proposed by Charles Darwin.
Trap: Stretch
The passage says that their research seemed to “refute” Lamarck’s theory.Does that also mean that their research “confirmed the validity” of Darwin’s theory?

No.Perhaps the biologists found both theories to be incorrect.Perhaps the biologists were undecided.From what the passage says, we know very little about Darwin’s theory; we know only that it was proposed a half decade after Lamarck’s theory.
(D) Their research did not include any study of single-celled organisms.
Trap: Stretch / Speculation
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S10 mentions a discovery involving single-celled organisms.

However, the exact wording in S10 is “discovered to be able to change their genes.” So, the discovery is about a particular phenomenon involving single-celled organisms.Thus, S10 does not mean that biologists did not study single-celled organisms earlier.
Also, notice the extreme marker in this choice, “any,” which is a yellow flag.The passage doesn’t say that the biologists’ research did not includeanystudy of single-celled organisms.
(E) They failed to understand that an organism can develop traits during its lifetime even if the information encoded in its genetic material remains unchanged.
Trap: Distortion
Notice that S4 says, “biologists had essentiallyruled outthe existence of a mechanism via whichtraits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.”

So, the biologists ruled out thepassingonof the traits, not the development of the traits during an organisms’ lifetime.The contrast in the 2ndparagraph goes on to explain a recent discovery, gene expression modifications, in which traits developed during an organisms’ lifetime ARE passed on to offspring.So, this discovery aboutpassing ontraits is what the biologists “failed to understand,” not “that an organism candeveloptraits during its lifetime even if the information encoded in its genetic material remains unchanged.”

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
As we start reading a passage, we will often notice contrast, opinions, and tone clues in the first two sentences.

In this passage, there is an opinion marker in S1, “Lamarck proposed that ….” S1 sets the stage for the rest of the passage by presenting the theory of evolution that Lamarck proposed, which is “evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.”

Then, in S2, the contrast marker “however” signals an important point, which is “it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted.”

Note that the tone clue, “seemed” signals some uncertainty about whether Lamarck’s theory had actually been refuted and foreshadows that there may be further debate or insights on this topic.

So, from these first two sentences, we already get the sense that the main topic of the passage is likely to be discussion of the validity of Lamarck’s evolutionary theory.

Then, S3 and S4 go on to explain why it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been completely refuted, the main reason being “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.” In other words, S3 and S4 communicate that what “biologists decided” indicated that evolution could not work in accordance with Lamarck’s theory.

Notice that, so far, the author’s tone is descriptive; she presents Lamarck’s theory and the biologists’ reasoning against it, but she does not make an argument of her own. In other words, she has not yet expressed her own opinion.

1st paragraph summary: Lamarck proposed a theory of evolution, “evolution occurs as organisms develop new physical traits during their lifetimes and then pass those traits on to their offspring.” Then, with the discovery of genetics, it seemed that Lamarck’s theory had been refuted.

Next, we see that the 2nd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals that what the 2nd paragraph says is in contrast with what’s said at the end of the first paragraph.

In the first sentence of the 2nd paragraph, S5, we also see the look above marker “SUCH a mechanism.” What “such a mechanism” refers to is the mechanism mentioned earlier, in S4, “a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring.”

What’s said in the 2nd paragraph contrasts with what’s said in the 1st paragraph in the following way. Whereas S4 says, “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring,” now S5 says, “However, more recently, such a mechanism has been discovered.” In other words, the biologists’ belief has been shown to be incorrect.

Then, S6 provides details about this recent discovery. This discovery is that new traits developed during an organism’s lifetime CAN be passed on to offspring. We know from the 1st paragraph that passing on traits is an important element of “evolution.”

Next, in S7, we want to put extra emphasis on the rhetorical question “Has Lamarck’s evolutionary theory been proven at least somewhat valid?” Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Indeed, S8 and S9 answer this question, with basically a “No” answer. Notice that S8 provides the reasoning of “most evolutionary biologists,” and then S9 goes on to provide a direct conclusion of the author, not the opinion of another group.

2nd paragraph summary: A recent discovery involving gene expression modifications shows that traits acquired during an organisms’ lifetime can be passed on to the organism’s offspring, but only for a few generations. So, this phenomenon isn’t evolution and does not support Lamarck’s theory.

Like the 2nd paragraph, the 3rd paragraph begins with a contrast marker, “nevertheless.” So, what is the contrast?

Whereas S8 and S9 go against Lamarck’s theory, S10 supports Lamarck’s theory: “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge, in the world of a certain few single-celled organisms that have been discovered to be able to change their genes ….” Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.” So, S10 means essentially “There is one situation in which Lamarck’s theory does apply, the world of certain single-celled organism that have been discovered to be able to change their genes.”

As we might expect, S11 then provides additional details to explain why this discovery supports Lamarck’s theory. Notice the contrast marker structure, “Unlike X, Y.” S11 is saying that “these changes made by these organisms” are “unlike” the gene expression modifications discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

What is the key difference between “gene expression modifications” and “these changes”?

Whereas S8 said that “gene expression modifications and their associated traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for only a few generations and eventually disappear,” S11 says that “these changes” made by the single-celled organisms are “passed from generation to generation indefinitely,” which means “for an unlimited period of time.” Simply put, the difference is about traits passed on temporarily versus traits passed on indefinitely.

In S12, the author’s final conclusion is signaled by the conclusion marker “thus.”

We can see that this conclusion by the author contrasts with the author’s conclusion in S9. Whereas in S9, the “gene expression modification” type of passing of traits does NOT result in evolution, the “changes to genes” type of passing of traits in S12 DOES result in evolution.

3rd paragraph summary: However, there is one discovery that DOES support Lamarck’s evolutionary theory: single-celled organisms that change their genes and traits during their lifetimes and pass on those traits indefinitely.

If we look at the passage as a whole, do any consistent themes appear?

Throughout the passage, there is a lot of contrast, and the author goes back and forth on the question of whether Lamarck’s evolutionary theory is valid.

Passage summary: The author considers the validity of Lamarck’s theory of evolution, in light of biologists’ understanding in the past and two more recent discoveries.

S6: Provides details about the discovery introduced in S5

Notice the “not only X, but also Y” structure. This structure (“found not only that X but also that Y”) signifies that there are two findings:

“an organism can modify its gene expression to develop particular physical traits during its lifetime”

“gene expression modifications made during an organism’s lifetime and the traits associated with those modifications CAN be passed on from the organism to its offspring.

What’s the key difference between this discovery and the biologists’ belief in S4? Whereas S4 says, “biologists had essentially ruled out the existence of a mechanism via which physical traits developed during an organism’s lifetime could be passed on to its offspring,” this discovery is that new traits developed during an organism’s lifetime CAN be passed on to offspring. We know from the 1st paragraph that passing on traits is an important element of “evolution.”

S7: The author poses a rhetorical question about the implications of the discovery presented in S5 and S6.

Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Simplification: The author is asking whether the discovery presented in S5 and S6 at least partly counters the biologists’ refutation of Lamarck’s evolutionary theory by proving Lamarck’s theory at least somewhat valid.

S8: Answers the rhetorical question in S7, with the tone of “not really.”

Notice the contrast structure, “X, BUT Y.” This structure puts emphasis on the Y part. In this case, we can see the contrast between “some people contend that it has” and “most evolutionary biologists point out that ….”

In the Y part, notice the qualifier marker “only a few.” The biologists point out a key fact about the discovery that “the traits associated with those modifications can be passed on.” That fact is that the “traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for ONLY a few generations and eventually disappear.”

Simplification: Some people argue that this discovery supports Lamarck’s theory, but most evolutionary biologists argue that Lamarck’s theory is still not valid, because the traits are passed on for only a few generations and eventually disappear.

S9: The author answers her rhetorical question, S7, by concluding that the discovery introduced in S5 does not support Lamarck’s theory of evolution.

Notice the conclusion marker, “So,” which is similar to “therefore.”

Notice also that S9 is a direct conclusion of the author’s, not the opinion of another person or group.

Notice the look above marker, “THIS type of passing of traits”; we must understand what is above that “this type of passing of traits” refers to. We see that S8 says, “such gene expression modifications and their associated traits are typically passed on from organisms to their offspring for only a few generations and eventually disappear.”

Simplification: The “type of passing of traits” discussed in the 2nd paragraph is not evolution.

S10: Introduces another discovery, which supports Lamarck’s theory.

Note that “sole refuge” means “one and only shelter/safe place.”

Notice the contrast marker “nevertheless,” which conveys, “while the statements above are true, the following is also true.”

The contrast is that, whereas S8 and S9 go against Lamarck’s theory, S10 supports Lamarck’s theory: “Lamarck’s theory has found a sole refuge.”

Simplification: Nevertheless, one discovery does support Lamarck’s theory: single-celled organisms that can change their genes and traits during their lifetimes.

S11: Describes a difference between these genetic changes and the “gene expression modifications” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker structure, “Unlike X, Y.” S11 is saying that “these changes made by these organisms” are “unlike” the gene expression modifications discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Simplification: Unlike the changes described in the 2nd paragraph, the changes made by these organisms during their lifetimes are passed from generation to generation indefinitely (for an unlimited period of time).

S12: The author concludes that the discovery discussed in S10 and S11 does represent evolution.

Notice the conclusion marker “Thus,” which is similar to “therefore.”

Notice also that S12 is a direct conclusion of the author, not the opinion of another group.

Simplification: The changes in traits made by these organisms represent evolution.

question.text: The passage suggests that Costa and the other historians in the highlighted text would most likely disagree over which of the following?

answers.text: Whether rising incomes played a role in the decrease in the LFPRE
Whether health problems were an important reason for the increase in the retirement rate
Whether the cost of leisure activities is a consideration in the retirement decisions of the elderly
Whether the rate of chronic disease was lower during the twentieth century than during earlier periods
Whether the demand for younger workers increased

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

First, make sure we understand exactly what the stem is asking for.We can see from “the passage suggests” and “disagree” that this is an Inference question involving a disagreement.So, we must find specific language in the passage about a disagreement between “Costa” and “the historians in the highlighted text.”

Notice that, in order to determine what the passage says about how they disagree, we have to find information about BOTH Costa’s opinion and the opinion of the other historians in the highlighted text.

Since S5 includes the highlighted text, that’s a good place to begin re-reading.Note that “debunks” means “expose the falseness of a myth,” so we can see that Costa disagrees with some alternative explanations offered by other historians for the decrease in LFPRE.Notice that S5 mentions two specific examples of these alternative explanations “offered by other historians”:
“health issues”
“a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers”
So, there is a good chance that the correct answer will connect to one of these two examples.

It’s important to note that the passage doesn’t say whether the “other historians” directly dispute the explanations that Costa offers in S3 and S4.So, the content of S3 and S4 has the potential to appear in trap answer choices.
(A) Whether rising incomes played a role in the decrease in the LFPRE
Trap: Speculation / Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S3 says that “Costa calculated that 60 percent ofthe decrease in the LFPREthat occurred during the first half of the twentieth centurycan be attributed to rising incomes.”

However, we can’t assume that the other historians have the complete opposite opinion from Costa’s.The passage doesn’t say that the other historians directly dispute that “rising incomes played a role.” In other words, this choice would be correct only if the passage gave evidence that the other historians believed that rising incomes did not play ANY role at all.This is an extreme statement that goes beyond what the passage says.For example, the other historians could believe that “health issues” played a role in the decrease, but that rising incomes also played a role.
YOUR CHOICE(B) Whether health problems were an important reason for the increase in the retirement rate
In our Question Analysis, we noted that S5 says that Costa “debunks,” and therefore disagrees with, two specific alternative explanations offered by other historians:
“health issues”
“a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers”
“Health problems” in this choice matches with “health issues” in S5.So, we can see that S5 indicates that Costa disagrees with the “other historians” about “health problems.”
Let’s confirm whether the rest of this choice matches the passage and answers the question.
This choice says that Costa and the “other historians” disagree regarding “whether health problems were an importantreasonfor the increase in the retirement rate.”
S5 indicates that Costa and the “other historians” disagree regarding whether “health issues” are anexplanationfor the decrease in the LFPRE.
Notice that “reason for” in this choice and “explanations” in S5 are the basically the same.
Thus, so far, the choice matches the passage.
Finally, what about “the increase in the retirement rate”?

The wording used in the passage, in S2, S3, and S6, is “decrease in the LFPRE.” So, we must realize that the wording in this choice, “increase in retirement rate,” is reversed from what the passage says but has the same meaning.

To understand why, note that LFPRE is defined in S2 as “the percentage of the elderly still working.” Also note that, mathematically, the percentage still working + the percentage that are retired must add to 100 percent.So, if the percentage still working decreases, the percentage that are retired must increase.Note that “rate” has the same meaning as “percentage.”

So, what this choice says, that Costa and the “other historians” disagree regarding “whether health problems were an important reason for the increase in the retirement rate,” means what the passage says, that Costa and the “other historians” disagree regarding whether “health issues” are an explanation for “the decrease in the LFPRE.”
(C) Whether the cost of leisure activities is a consideration in the retirement decisions of the elderly
Trap: Speculation
Since this question asks about a disagreement, we have to find information about BOTH Costa’s opinion and the opinion of the historians in the highlighted text.

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S4 says that, in the context of Costa’s calculations about the cause of the decrease in the LFPRE, “the primary cause was increased affordability of leisure activities.” So, this choice does connect to Costa’s opinion.

However, we can’t assume that the other historians have the complete opposite opinion from Costa’s.The passage doesn’t say that the other historians directly dispute that “the cost of leisure activities is a consideration.” In other words, this choice would be correct only if the passage gave evidence that the other historians believed that the cost of leisure activities is NOT a consideration at all.This is an extreme statement that goes beyond what the passage says.For example, the other historians could believe that health issues are the primary consideration in the retirement decisions of the elderly, but that the cost of leisure activities is also a consideration.
(D) Whether the rate of chronic disease was lower during the twentieth century than during earlier periods
Trap: Distortion / Wrong Comparison

Notice the comparison marker in this choice, “lowerduring Xthanduring Y.” Whenever we notice a comparison, we must be extra careful to check whether it matches the passage and answers the question.Notice that this choice is comparing the rate of chronic disease during two different time frames, “the twentieth century” and “earlier periods.” We can see that a comparison is made about “the rate chronic disease” in S6, so let’s review S6 carefully.

Notice that S6 compares different time frames different from those compared by this choice.S6 compares “the first half of the twentieth century” to “since that period.” In other words, the comparison in S6 is onlywithinandafterthe twentieth century, whereas this choice makes a comparison involving a time framebeforethe twentieth century.

Furthermore, the passage doesn’t say whether the other historians directly dispute Costa’s observation about the data concerning the rate of chronic disease.It’s possible that the other historians were completely ignorant of this data but wouldn’t necessarily argue the opposite; perhaps their alternative explanation about “health issues” was based on speculation rather than data.
(E) Whether the demand for younger workers increased
Trap: Distortion
In our Question Analysis, we noted that S5 says that Costa “debunks,” and therefore disagrees with, two specific alternative explanations offered by other historians:
“health issues”
“a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers”
If you picked this choice, you may have thought that “demand for younger workers increased” matches with the second example above, “a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers.”
However, notice that this choice is talking about an increase in just the demand for younger workers, whereas the passage talks about a decrease in relative demand for older workers.

We can’t assume that a decrease in relative demand for older workers involves an increase in demand for younger workers.After all, there could be reduced demand for older workers without an increase in demand for younger workers.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage; the main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author implies that that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Having discussed in S1 that retirement topics have received attention among economists, in S2, the author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa. Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

In S2, also notice the causation marker, “causes of X,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. The main point of S2 is that Costa’s book explains the causes of the decline of the LFPRE.

LFPRE, the labor force participation rate of the elderly, will continue to appear throughout the passage, so we must keep track of what it means. It is helpful to connect this term to the topic of retirement; a decline in the LFPRE would mean that the retirement rate is increasing. To understand why, note that LFPRE is defined in S2 as “the percentage of the elderly still working.” Mathematically, the percentage still working + the percentage that are retired must add to 100 percent. So, if the percentage of the elderly still working decreases, the percentage retired must increase.

Then, S3 through S6 go into the specifics of Costa’s analysis. S3 and S4 contrast the first and second halves of the twentieth century. S5 and S6 discuss Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decrease in the LFPRE.

It’s helpful to notice that causation/explanation is a repeated theme throughout the passage. A useful clue is that there are many causation markers: causes (S2), attributed to (S3), cause (S4), as a result of (S4), explanations (S5).

In S5, notice another tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Finally, S7 sums up the author’s review of Costa’s work. In saying that “Costa’s work exemplifies a much-needed resurgence of research on retirement,” the author reiterates the point in S1 that this topic of retirement is important, as well as indicates once again that he has a positive opinion of Costa’s work. After all, something that “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” would presumably be something positive.

Notice that, overall, the style of the passage could be described as a “book review.” The author is clearly focused on the work of Costa, and a clue is that the keyword “Costa” appears in five of the seven sentences of the passage. The author presents some key points of Costa’s work, and as we’ve seen in our analysis of the tone clues, the author expresses a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Passage Summary: With a positive tone, the author discusses a scholarly work about retirement, specifically focusing on the causes of the decrease in the LFPRE during the twentieth century.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Word in Passage

Also, note that when the passage says “first half of the twentieth century,” that means the years 1901 through 1950.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: This sentence introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage.

The main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author also implies that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Simplification: Because demographic changes, such as an increase in the percentage of the population that is elderly, have a significant economic impact, topics about retirement have recently received greater attention from economists.

S2: The author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa.

Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Notice the causation marker “causes of,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. Costa is explaining the decline of the LFPRE.

Simplification: Costa provides a helpful explanation of the causes of the decline in LFPRE during the twentieth century.

S3: Provides details about Costa’s primary explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE for the first half of the twentieth century.

Notice the causation marker, “X can be attributed to Y.” In this structure, X is the result and Y is the cause. So, Costa is saying that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE is caused by “rising incomes.” In other words, Costa says that “rising incomes” are the primary cause for the decrease, but other factors account for 40 percent of the decrease.

Notice that the time frame is limited to the first half of the twentieth century, 1901 through 1950.

S4: Contrast with S3 — the primary cause of the decrease is different during a later timeframe, the second half of the twentieth century.

Notice the contrast marker, “In contrast.” As always, we must pay close attention when we notice a contrast marker — what are the two things that are in contrast?

S3: During the first half of the century, the primary cause was rising incomes.

S4: During the second half of the century, the primary cause was increased affordability of leisure activities resulting from technological improvements

Note that wording such as “increased affordability” could easily be reversed to something like “decreased cost” in an answer choice and retain the same meaning.

S5: Introduces Costa’s work in debunking alternative explanations.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” The author is providing another example of how Costa provides an “illuminating analysis of the causes of the decline … in the LFPRE.”

Note that “debunks” means “expose the falseness of a myth.” So, we can see that Costa disagrees with some alternative explanations, “including health issues and a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers.”

Notice the tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue indicating that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

S6: Expands on S5.

The phrase “perhaps most notably” indicates that this sentence presents a notable example of what was discussed in the previous sentence, Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decline in the LFPRE.

The example presented by this sentence is Costa’s “observation.”

Simplification: One notable example of Costa’s debunking is her observation that, in older people, “the rate of chronic disease was higher” when the LFPRE was higher “during the first half of the twentieth century.” In other words, she observed that, when older people had more “health issues,” more older people worked. Thus, she debunked the explanation offered by the “other historians” in S5 that “health issues” caused the LFPRE to decrease from a higher level “during the first half of the twentieth century” to a lower level “since that period.”

S7: Concludes the passage similarly to how the passage began.

The passage began by implying that the topic of retirement is important, and this sentence conveys essentially the same thing by indicating that “research on retirement” is “much needed.”

Also, the tone clue “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” indicates once again that the author has a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Simplification: While Costa’s work does not provide all the analysis needed for prediction of retirement trends, Costa’s work is an example the type of research needed on this important topic.

question.text: With which of the following characterizations of Costa’s contribution to the understanding of retirement trends would the author of the passage be most likely to agree?

answers.text: Costa has refrained from making any predictions about future trends in retirement.
Costa has shown that most of the decrease in the LFPRE since 1950 is due to rising incomes.
Costa has clarified the relationship between an element of people’s personal financial resources and their retirement decisions.
Costa’s research has not helped economists make decisions about financial projections.
Costa’s explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE involved a greater number of calculations than other historians’ explanations involved.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

The question asks about “Costa’s contribution to the understanding of retirement trends.” This topic is discussed throughout the passage, so it is difficult to narrow down which part of the passage we must focus on.We will need to re-read carefully as we analyze each answer choice to see whether it’s supported by the passage.

It’s helpful to make sure we understand any important points of the author’s.The author first discusses Costa’s contribution in S2, saying that Costa’s book “provides an illuminating analysis of thecausesof the consistent decline during the twentieth century in the labor force participation rate of the elderly (LFPRE).”

Also, causation/explanation is a repeated theme throughout the passage, and therefore has a high chance of appearing in the answer choices, either as a trap or as a correct answer.Notice that there are many causation markers: causes (S2), attributed to (S3), cause (S4), as a result of (S4), explanations (S5).
(A) Costa has refrained from making any predictions about future trends in retirement.
Trap: Speculation
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S7 says, “While further analysis is needed for accurate prediction of long-term retirement trends ….”
So, the author is saying thatfurtheranalysis is needed foraccurateprediction, but notice that this choice says that Costa has not made “ANY predictions about future trends in retirement.”
The passage does not support this extreme statement.Given what the passage says, it’s possible that that Costa has madesomepredictions about future trends in retirement.
(B) Costa has shown that most of the decrease in the LFPRE since 1950 is due to rising incomes.
Trap: Distortion

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S3 says, “Costa calculated that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE that occurred during the first half of the twentieth century can be attributed to rising incomes.” Indeed, when we compare this choice to the passage, we see that “most” matches with “60 percent,” and “due to” matches with “attributed to.”
However, do the timeframes match?

This choice says, “since 1950” which means “from 1950 to the present,” but the timeframe in S3 is “the first halfof the twentieth century,” which means “from 1901 to 1950.” So, we can see that this choice is a distortion.
YOUR CHOICE(C) Costa has clarified the relationship between an element of people’s personal financial resources and their retirement decisions.

Scanning the passage for “personal financial resources,” we see that S3 says, “Costa calculated that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE that occurred during the first half of the twentieth century can be attributed to risingincomes.”

Notice that “incomes” in S3 means essentially the same thing as “an element of people’s personal financial resources” in this choice.After all, people’s “incomes” are “an element of people’s personal financial resources.”

Meanwhile, “can be attributed to” means “was caused by.” So, in calculating that “60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE … can be attributed to rising incomes,” Costa has “clarified” a cause-and-effect relationship between “rising incomes” and “the decrease in the LFPRE.”

Finally, as we noted in our Passage Analysis, “the decrease in the LFPRE” means that there was an increase in the retirement rate and therefore a change in “retirement decisions.” So, in defining a cause-and-effect relationship between “rising incomes” and “the decrease in the LFPRE,” Costa has “clarified” a relationship between “rising incomes” and “retirement decisions.”

Thus, “Costa calculated that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE … can be attributed to rising incomes,” in the passage means, “Costahas clarified the relationship between an element of people’s personal financial resources and their retirement decisions.”
To review:

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in PassageCosta has clarifiedDora Costa’s book, The Evolution of Retirement, provides anilluminating analysis of the causes(S2)Costa calculated that (S3)an element of people’s personal financial resourcesrising incomes (S3)retirement decisionsthe decrease in the LFPRE (S3)(LFPRE), defined as the percentage of the elderly still working.(S2)
We can see that this choice matches the passage and is the correct answer.
(D) Costa’s research has not helped economists make decisions about financial projections.
Trap: Distortion

The only piece of the passage that may appear to be related to this choice is S7, in which the author says, “While further analysis is needed for accurate prediction of long-term retirement trends ….” However, S7 does not say that Costa’s research has “not helped.” Rather, the author is just making the qualifierstatement that additional analysis is needed.

Furthermore, notice that the passage doesn’t discuss “economists” making “decisions about financial projections”; “accurate prediction of long-term retirement trends” does not have the same meaning as “decisions about financial projections,” and the rest of the passage is about Costa’s explanations of the decrease in LFPRE.In other words, the passage doesn’t say whether Costa’s research has or has not helped “economists make decisions about financial projections.”
(E) Costa’s explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE involved a greater number of calculations than other historians’ explanations involved.
Trap: Wrong Comparison

Notice the comparison marker in this choice, “X … greater ...than Y.” Does the passage say that Costa’s explanation involved a “greaternumber of calculations” than other historians’ explanations involved?

No.The passage does mention Costa’s calculations in S2 and S3, but it doesn’t specify thenumberof them, and it does not provide information on the number of calculations made by other historians.So, the passage does not support the comparison expressed by this choice.

LFPRE, the labor force participation rate of the elderly, will continue to appear throughout the passage, so we must keep track of what it means. It is helpful to connect this term to the topic of retirement; a decline in the LFPRE would mean that the retirement rate is increasing. To understand why, note that LFPRE is defined in S2 as “the percentage of the elderly still working.” Mathematically, the percentage still working + the percentage that are retired must add to 100 percent. So, if the percentage of the elderly still working decreases, the percentage retired must increase.

Then, S3 through S6 go into the specifics of Costa’s analysis. S3 and S4 contrast the first and second halves of the twentieth century. S5 and S6 discuss Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decrease in the LFPRE.

It’s helpful to notice that causation/explanation is a repeated theme throughout the passage. A useful clue is that there are many causation markers: causes (S2), attributed to (S3), cause (S4), as a result of (S4), explanations (S5).

In S5, notice another tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Finally, S7 sums up the author’s review of Costa’s work. In saying that “Costa’s work exemplifies a much-needed resurgence of research on retirement,” the author reiterates the point in S1 that this topic of retirement is important, as well as indicates once again that he has a positive opinion of Costa’s work. After all, something that “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” would presumably be something positive.

Notice that, overall, the style of the passage could be described as a “book review.” The author is clearly focused on the work of Costa, and a clue is that the keyword “Costa” appears in five of the seven sentences of the passage. The author presents some key points of Costa’s work, and as we’ve seen in our analysis of the tone clues, the author expresses a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Passage Summary: With a positive tone, the author discusses a scholarly work about retirement, specifically focusing on the causes of the decrease in the LFPRE during the twentieth century.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: This sentence introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage.

The main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author also implies that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Simplification: Because demographic changes, such as an increase in the percentage of the population that is elderly, have a significant economic impact, topics about retirement have recently received greater attention from economists.

S2: The author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa.

Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Notice the causation marker “causes of,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. Costa is explaining the decline of the LFPRE.

Simplification: Costa provides a helpful explanation of the causes of the decline in LFPRE during the twentieth century.

S3: Provides details about Costa’s primary explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE for the first half of the twentieth century.

Notice the causation marker, “X can be attributed to Y.” In this structure, X is the result and Y is the cause. So, Costa is saying that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE is caused by “rising incomes.” In other words, Costa says that “rising incomes” are the primary cause for the decrease, but other factors account for 40 percent of the decrease.

Notice that the time frame is limited to the first half of the twentieth century, 1901 through 1950.

S4: Contrast with S3 — the primary cause of the decrease is different during a later timeframe, the second half of the twentieth century.

Notice the contrast marker, “In contrast.” As always, we must pay close attention when we notice a contrast marker — what are the two things that are in contrast?

S3: During the first half of the century, the primary cause was rising incomes.

S4: During the second half of the century, the primary cause was increased affordability of leisure activities resulting from technological improvements

Note that wording such as “increased affordability” could easily be reversed to something like “decreased cost” in an answer choice and retain the same meaning.

S5: Introduces Costa’s work in debunking alternative explanations.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” The author is providing another example of how Costa provides an “illuminating analysis of the causes of the decline … in the LFPRE.”

Note that “debunks” means “expose the falseness of a myth.” So, we can see that Costa disagrees with some alternative explanations, “including health issues and a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers.”

Notice the tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue indicating that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

S6: Expands on S5.

The phrase “perhaps most notably” indicates that this sentence presents a notable example of what was discussed in the previous sentence, Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decline in the LFPRE.

The example presented by this sentence is Costa’s “observation.”

Simplification: One notable example of Costa’s debunking is her observation that, in older people, “the rate of chronic disease was higher” when the LFPRE was higher “during the first half of the twentieth century.” In other words, she observed that, when older people had more “health issues,” more older people worked. Thus, she debunked the explanation offered by the “other historians” in S5 that “health issues” caused the LFPRE to decrease from a higher level “during the first half of the twentieth century” to a lower level “since that period.”

S7: Concludes the passage similarly to how the passage began.

The passage began by implying that the topic of retirement is important, and this sentence conveys essentially the same thing by indicating that “research on retirement” is “much needed.”

Also, the tone clue “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” indicates once again that the author has a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Simplification: While Costa’s work does not provide all the analysis needed for prediction of retirement trends, Costa’s work is an example the type of research needed on this important topic.

question.text: The information in the passage suggests that Costa believes that, prior to 1950, which of the following was true about the decline of the LFPRE?

answers.text: The majority of this decline can be attributed to causes other than rising incomes.
Technological innovations did not have an impact on this decline.
Health issues played a significant role in this decline.
The primary cause of this decline was a factor other than affordability of leisure activities.
The rate of this decline was rapid.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:
First, we must understand exactly what the stem is asking for.
We can see from “the passage suggests” that this is an Inference question.

Notice that the question asks about a certain time frame, “prior to 1950.” It is often relatively easy to find numbers in the passage; they stand out even more than capitalized words.However, in this passage, year numbers do not appear, so we must find other wording that matches the meaning.Notice that S3 mentions the “first half of the twentieth century” and S4 mentions the “latter half of the century.” Since the “first half of the twentieth century” matches the timeframe of “prior to 1950”, our correct answer could match the content of S3 or be a reversed version of the content of S4.
Notice that S6 also mentions the “first half of the twentieth century.”
Which of these two areas of the passage must we focus on to answer this question?

The other keywords in the stem give us a clue.The stem says, “Costa believes … about thedecline in the LFPRE.” S6 is comparing “the rate ofchronic disease” in the different timeframes, whereas S3 and S4 more directly discuss two different primary causes ofthe decline in the LFPREduring these two timeframes.So, the correct answer will likely come from S3 or S4.
So, let’s re-read S3 and S4 carefully.In S4, notice the contrast marker, “In contrast.” What are the two things that are in contrast?

S3 simplification: During thefirst halfof the twentieth century, 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE was caused byrising incomes.In other words, the primary cause of the decrease in the LFPRE was rising incomes.
S4 simplification: In contrast, during thesecond halfof the twentieth century, the primary cause wasincreased affordability of leisure activitiesresulting from technological improvements.
(A) The majority of this decline can be attributed to causes other than rising incomes.
Trap: Opposite
Notice that this choice says, “other thanrising incomes.”
This choice would be correct if the stem asked about the time frame AFTER 1950, rather than PRIOR to 1950.

S3 says that 60 percent, i.e., “the majority,” of the decline during the first half of the twentieth century CAN be attributed to rising incomes.So, the wording “causesother thanrising incomes” in this choice is incorrect.
(B) Technological innovations did not have an impact on this decline.
Trap: Stretch

If you picked this choice, you may have noticed the contrast between S3 and S4.Indeed, the passage suggests that “technological innovations” were not theprimarycause of the decline prior to 1950, because S3 says that “60 percent of the decrease” during the first half of the twentieth century “can be attributed to rising incomes.”
However, the wording of this choice, “did not have an impact,” has the extreme meaning that technological innovations didn’t have any impact AT ALL.

This extreme meaning is not supported by the passage.After all, we know only that 60 percent of the decrease can be attributed to rising incomes.So, it’s possible that some of the other 40 percent can be attributed to “technological innovations.”
(C) Health issues played a significant role in this decline.
Trap: Opposite

Notice that S5 says that Costa “crediblydebunksseveral alternative explanations, including health issues.” The word “debunks” tells us that Costa believes the opposite of this choice, that health issues did NOT play a significant role.
YOUR CHOICE(D) The primary cause of this decline was a factor other than affordability of leisure activities.
Let’s review the contrast expressed by S3 and S4:

S3 simplification: During thefirst halfof the twentieth century, 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE was caused byrising incomes.In other words, the primary cause of the decrease in the LFPRE was rising incomes.
S4 simplification: In contrast, during thesecond halfof the twentieth century, the primary cause wasincreased affordability of leisure activitiesresulting from technological improvements.
Notice the wording “other than” in this choice.This choice uses “other than” to convey that “affordability of leisure activities” was NOT the primary cause of the decrease in the LFPRE prior to 1950.

Now, notice that S3 says that, during the first half of the twentieth century, or “prior to 1950,” “rising incomes” was the primary cause of the decrease in the LFPRE.Since “rising incomes” is “a factor other than affordability of leisure activities,” S3 says essentially what this choice says.
So, this choice is supported by the passage.
(E) The rate of this decline was rapid.
Trap: Distortion
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that S2 mentions “the consistent decline during the twentieth century.”
However, “consistent decline” does not mean that the decline was “rapid.” So, S2 does not support this choice.
In other words, this choice is a distortion of what the passage says.
Trap: Mix-Up

The word “rapid” does appear in S1.However, what S1 describes as “rapid” is the “increase in the proportion of the population living well past the age of 80,” not “the rate of this decline” in the LFPRE.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.

Finally, S7 sums up the author’s review of Costa’s work. question.text: Which of the following developments would be most consistent with Costa’s explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE after 1950, as described in the passage?

answers.text: Developments in electronics led to substantial reductions in prices of entertainment devices.
Improved technology resulted in an increase in the quality of electronic games.
Deregulation of airlines caused the cost of air travel to decrease.
Economic growth led to a significant increase in the quantity of golf courses.
Technological improvements in health care resulted in a rapid decrease in the frequency of health problems.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

The wording of the stem, “Which of the following developments WOULD be most consistent …” signals that this is an Application question.Application questions ask us to apply information from the passage to a context that is outside of the passage.If we were unsure that this was an Application question at first, a good clue would be to notice that many of the nouns in the answer choices (entertainment devices, electronic games, deregulation of airlines, golf courses) do not appear at all in the passage.
Looking at the full stem, we can see that the correct answer must be “consistent with Costa’s explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE after 1950.”
So, the next step is to ask what exactly is Costa’s explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE after 1950.

Since timeframe is “after 1950,” we must focus on S4, since S4 says “during the latter half of the century, the primary cause was ….” The “latter half of the century” is the second half of the century, in other words, the half of the century “after 1950.”
Also, we know from the context of S2 and S3 that, when S4 says, “the primary cause was,” S4 is presenting “the primary cause” of “the decrease of the LFPRE.”

So, we can see that the correct answer must match with the cause presented in S4, which is “increased affordability of leisure activities, as a result of technological innovations.” The correct answer should match ALL THREE parts of this cause:
“increased affordability”
“of leisure activities”
“as a result of technological innovations”
YOUR CHOICE(A) Developments in electronics led to substantial reductions in prices of entertainment devices.
In our Question Analysis, we noted that the correct answer should be an example that is consistent with “increased affordability of leisure activities, as a result of technological innovations.”

Wording in Answer ChoiceSimilar Wording in PassageDevelopments in electronicstechnological innovationsled toas a result of (note: “X led to Y” has a similar meaning to “Y, as a result of X”)substantial reductions in pricesincreased affordabilityof entertainment devicesleisure activities
We can see that this choice matches the passage and answers the question.
(B) Improved technology resulted in an increase in the quality of electronic games.
Trap: Distortion / Half-Right

If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “improved technology resulted in” matches with “as a result of technological innovations.” Additionally, “electronic games” matches with “leisure activities.”
However, does this choice capture what we are looking for, regarding “increased affordability”?
No.This choice mentions “an increase in thequality,” not increased affordability.
(C) Deregulation of airlines caused the cost of air travel to decrease.
Trap: Distortion / Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have correctly noticed that “the cost of air travel to decrease” matches with “increased affordability of leisure activities.”
However, does “deregulation of airlines” also match what we are looking for?
No.The passage says “as a result oftechnological innovations.” “Deregulation of airlines” is not an example of a “technological innovation.” So, we can eliminate this choice.
(D) Economic growth led to a significant increase in the quantity of golf courses.
Trap: Distortion / Half-Right
If you picked this choice, you may have noticed that “golf courses” is related to “leisure activities.”
However, “economic growth” doesn’t match with “technological innovations.”

Furthermore, an increase in thequantityof golf courses doesn’t match with “increased affordability.” (If you are thinking that increased supply/quantity of golf courses would drive down prices, that outcome is certainly possible, but it is not certain.After all, other factors, such as increased demand, could also be affecting prices.)
(E) Technological improvements in health care resulted in a rapid decrease in the frequency of health problems.
Trap: Distortion

If you picked this choice, you may have thought that this choice is supported by S6 because the stem asks about “after 1950” and the timeframe mentioned in S6 is since “the first half of the twentieth century.” However, what S6 says does not fit what this question asks.
Notice that the stem asks what would be “consistent with Costa’s explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE.”

Now, notice that S6 says that Costa’s “observation … implies that the LFPRE decreaseddespite improving health.” We can see that S6 doesn’t mean that Costa believes “improving health” is anexplanationfor “the decrease in the LFPRE.” Rather, S6 means that Costa believes that “the decrease in the LFPRE” occurredeven thoughthere was “improving health.”

Thus, since “improving health” in the passage means essentially the same thing as “a rapid decrease in the frequency of health problems” in this choice, we can see that Costa does not consider “a rapid decrease in the frequency of health problems” an explanation for “the decrease in the LFPRE.”
So, “technological improvements … resulted in a rapid decrease in the frequency of health problems” is not “consistent with Costa’s explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE.”
