If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage; the main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author implies that that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Having discussed in S1 that retirement topics have received attention among economists, in S2, the author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa. Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

In S2, also notice the causation marker, “causes of X,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. The main point of S2 is that Costa’s book explains the causes of the decline of the LFPRE.

LFPRE, the labor force participation rate of the elderly, will continue to appear throughout the passage, so we must keep track of what it means. It is helpful to connect this term to the topic of retirement; a decline in the LFPRE would mean that the retirement rate is increasing. To understand why, note that LFPRE is defined in S2 as “the percentage of the elderly still working.” Mathematically, the percentage still working + the percentage that are retired must add to 100 percent. So, if the percentage of the elderly still working decreases, the percentage retired must increase.

Then, S3 through S6 go into the specifics of Costa’s analysis. S3 and S4 contrast the first and second halves of the twentieth century. S5 and S6 discuss Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decrease in the LFPRE.

It’s helpful to notice that causation/explanation is a repeated theme throughout the passage. A useful clue is that there are many causation markers: causes (S2), attributed to (S3), cause (S4), as a result of (S4), explanations (S5).

In S5, notice another tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Finally, S7 sums up the author’s review of Costa’s work. In saying that “Costa’s work exemplifies a much-needed resurgence of research on retirement,” the author reiterates the point in S1 that this topic of retirement is important, as well as indicates once again that he has a positive opinion of Costa’s work. After all, something that “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” would presumably be something positive.

Notice that, overall, the style of the passage could be described as a “book review.” The author is clearly focused on the work of Costa, and a clue is that the keyword “Costa” appears in five of the seven sentences of the passage. The author presents some key points of Costa’s work, and as we’ve seen in our analysis of the tone clues, the author expresses a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Passage Summary: With a positive tone, the author discusses a scholarly work about retirement, specifically focusing on the causes of the decrease in the LFPRE during the twentieth century.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
S1: This sentence introduces the broader context of the rest of the passage.

The main point of S1 is that topics about retirement have recently received greater attention among economists. The author also implies that these topics are important by conveying that they have “received greater consideration” because of “far-reaching economic implications.”

Simplification: Because demographic changes, such as an increase in the percentage of the population that is elderly, have a significant economic impact, topics about retirement have recently received greater attention from economists.

S2: The author introduces the work of one specific economist, Dora Costa.

Notice the tone clue, “illuminating,” modifying the word “analysis.” Since “illuminating” means “helpful, enlightening,” we can see that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

Notice the causation marker “causes of,” because causes/explanations tend to be important parts of passages. Costa is explaining the decline of the LFPRE.

Simplification: Costa provides a helpful explanation of the causes of the decline in LFPRE during the twentieth century.

S3: Provides details about Costa’s primary explanation for the decrease in the LFPRE for the first half of the twentieth century.

Notice the causation marker, “X can be attributed to Y.” In this structure, X is the result and Y is the cause. So, Costa is saying that 60 percent of the decrease in the LFPRE is caused by “rising incomes.” In other words, Costa says that “rising incomes” are the primary cause for the decrease, but other factors account for 40 percent of the decrease.

Notice that the time frame is limited to the first half of the twentieth century, 1901 through 1950.

S4: Contrast with S3 — the primary cause of the decrease is different during a later timeframe, the second half of the twentieth century.

Notice the contrast marker, “In contrast.” As always, we must pay close attention when we notice a contrast marker — what are the two things that are in contrast?

S3: During the first half of the century, the primary cause was rising incomes.

S4: During the second half of the century, the primary cause was increased affordability of leisure activities resulting from technological improvements

Note that wording such as “increased affordability” could easily be reversed to something like “decreased cost” in an answer choice and retain the same meaning.

S5: Introduces Costa’s work in debunking alternative explanations.

Notice the additional point marker “Additionally.” The author is providing another example of how Costa provides an “illuminating analysis of the causes of the decline … in the LFPRE.”

Note that “debunks” means “expose the falseness of a myth.” So, we can see that Costa disagrees with some alternative explanations, “including health issues and a reduced demand for older workers relative to younger workers.”

Notice the tone clue, “credibly,” modifying the verb “debunks.” Since “credibly” means “believably, convincingly,” “credibly” is another clue indicating that the author has a positive opinion of Costa’s analysis.

S6: Expands on S5.

The phrase “perhaps most notably” indicates that this sentence presents a notable example of what was discussed in the previous sentence, Costa’s debunking of alternative explanations for the decline in the LFPRE.

The example presented by this sentence is Costa’s “observation.”

Simplification: One notable example of Costa’s debunking is her observation that, in older people, “the rate of chronic disease was higher” when the LFPRE was higher “during the first half of the twentieth century.” In other words, she observed that, when older people had more “health issues,” more older people worked. Thus, she debunked the explanation offered by the “other historians” in S5 that “health issues” caused the LFPRE to decrease from a higher level “during the first half of the twentieth century” to a lower level “since that period.”

S7: Concludes the passage similarly to how the passage began.

The passage began by implying that the topic of retirement is important, and this sentence conveys essentially the same thing by indicating that “research on retirement” is “much needed.”

Also, the tone clue “exemplifies a much-needed resurgence” indicates once again that the author has a positive opinion about Costa’s work.

Simplification: While Costa’s work does not provide all the analysis needed for prediction of retirement trends, Costa’s work is an example the type of research needed on this important topic.

question.text: The passage suggests that each of the following is true about the labor force participation rate of the elderly EXCEPT

answers.text: It was higher at the start of the twentieth century than in 1951.
The understanding of its decline has been enhanced through the use of calculations.
It dropped throughout the twentieth century.
From 1901 through 1950, less than half of its decrease was due to factors other than income increases.
Most experts are mistaken about the causes of its decline.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

This is an EXCEPT question.So, to arrive at the correct answer, we will seek to eliminate four statements about “the labor force participation rate of the elderly” (LFPRE) that the passage supports.The correct answer will be the one choice that the passage does not indicate is true.

As always, we must be aware of choices that distort information presented in the passage.In most questions, such choices are incorrect.However, in EXCEPT questions, choices that distort information presented in the passage are often correct answers.
In considering each choice, we can search the passage for references to the “labor force participation rate of the elderly” or the acronym “LFPRE” and for keywords in the choice.

In answering an EXCEPT question, we’re often able to eliminate multiple choices by using information from one area of the passage.However, in this case, the topic of the LFPRE is discussed throughout the passage.Therefore, we must carefully assess each choice individually to determine whether it is supported by the passage.
(A) It was higher at the start of the twentieth century than in 1951.
S2 mentions “theconsistent decline during the twentieth centuryin the labor force participation rate of the elderly.”

If the LFPRE had a “consistent decline” during the twentieth century, we can indeed conclude that the LFPRE was higher at the start of the twentieth century than in 1951.After all, something that declined consistently was higher earlier in time and lower later in time.
We can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.
(B) The understanding of its decline has been enhanced through the use of calculations.
This choice mentions “calculations.” Where do “calculations” appear in the passage?

S2 says, “With an emphasis on sophisticated econometriccalculations, Dora Costa’s book, The Evolution of Retirement, provides anilluminating analysis of the causes of the consistent declineduring the twentieth century in the labor force participation rate of the elderly (LFPRE).” Note that since “illuminating” (S2) means “helpful, enlightening, informative,” this word matches well with “the understanding has been enhanced.”

Additionally, S3 elaborates on the calculations: “Costacalculatedthat 60 percent of the decrease … can beattributed torising incomes.” Note that “X can be attributed to Y” is a causation marker and means that “X can be explained by Y.An explanation matches well with “the understanding has been enhanced.”
Since everything in this choice matches the passage, we can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.
(C) It dropped throughout the twentieth century.
S2 mentions “theconsistent decline during the twentieth centuryin the labor force participation rate of the elderly.”
If the LFPRE had a “consistent declineduring the twentieth century,” we can conclude that the LFPRE “dropped throughoutthe twentieth century.”

So, we can see that this choice is supported by the passage.We can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.
(D) From 1901 through 1950, less than half of its decrease was due to factors other than income increases.

First, let’s find where to focus in the passage.Notice that the timeframe in this choice, “1901 through 1950,” matches with “the first half of the twentieth century,” in S3.Also, thecausation marker, “due to,” matches with “attributed to,” in S3.

Does the meaning of this choice match with S3? S3 says that 60% of the decrease can be attributed to rising incomes.Since the percentages attributed to all the causes combined must add up to 100%, we can infer that 40% of the decrease can indeed be attributed to “factorsother thanincome increases.” Since 40% matches with “less than half,” this choice is supported by the passage.
We can eliminate this choice since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage.
Supported by the passage? Yes.
YOUR CHOICE(E) Most experts are mistaken about the causes of its decline.

If you thought that this choice was supported by the passage, you may have noticed that S5 does indeed discuss the idea of “experts” being “mistaken about the causes of its decline.” S5 says, “Costa crediblydebunks several alternative explanations… that have often been offered byother historians.
However, notice that the phrase “MOST experts” in this choice means “more than 50 percent of experts.” Can we make this numerical inference from what the passage says?
No, the passage does not specify anything about the percentage of experts (other historians) who are mistaken.
Thus, since our job in answering this EXCEPT question is to find the one choice that is not supported by the passage, this choice is the correct answer.
Supported by the passage? No.

question.text: Which of the following, if true, would most seriously weaken the support for the conclusion stated by the highlighted text?

answers.text: Some TAs were as experienced and well trained as the teachers.
The students who did not receive any support from the TAs made the fastest academic progress.
If not effectively supervised, the unruliest students in a class can make teaching extremely difficult.
In designing the study, the DCSF researchers did not account for student motivation.
The teachers used their TAs to supervise the unruliest students because they preferred to avoid the stress involved.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

Weaken the Argument questions that appear in RC are similar to those we have already encountered in CR.Therefore, all of the concepts, strategies, and skills that we mastered for Weaken the Argument CR questions will again be valuable tools for us in answering RC questions.

Notice that the second paragraph opens with a rhetorical question, S3, “What is the most plausible reason for these results?” The question uses the causation marker “reason,” indicating that possible explanations will follow.Indeed, in the highlighted text, there is another causation marker, “explanation.” These markers signal that the author is making a cause-and-effect argument: the results described in the first paragraph are the effects, and the author is seeking to explain the cause of these effects.

Also, notice the comparison marker “more convincing explanation” — the author is comparing his or her own explanation with the defenders’ explanation in the previous sentence.By using the comparison phrase “more convincing,” the author conveys to us that she believes that her explanation is better than the defenders’ explanation.
So, our job in answering this question is to weaken the author’s case for believing that her explanation is the correct one.
When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.

Thus, in the case of this question, a choice that indicates that there could be an alternative explanation for the “underwhelming results” will weaken the support for the author’s conclusion.After all, such a choice will cast doubt on the author’s conclusion by showing that the author’s explanation may not be the correct explanation.
(A) Some TAs were as experienced and well trained as the teachers.
In S6, the author adds detail to his or her explanation, saying that “difficult students had been better served by experienced teachers who had received the proper training.”
So, does this choice weaken the author’s argument?

“Some” is extremely vague, and just means “more than one.” So, all this choice indicates is that there were “some” exceptions to what S6 implies, which is that, in general, the teachers were more experienced and better trained than TAs.In other words, even given what this choice says, that general fact about the training and experience of teachers and TAs remains true.
Thus, this choice doesn’t truly conflict with the author’s point about the TAs in S6.
So, this choice does not affect the support for the author’s explanation.
(B) The students who did not receive any support from the TAs made the fastest academic progress.

This answer is basically a reversed description of the result at the end of paragraph 1: “there was a consistent negative relationship between the amount of support a student received from TAs and the progress the student made.” (Note that a negative relationship means that when one variable goes up, the other variable goes down; another term for this is “inverse relationship” or “inverse correlation.”)

In other words, the passage says that more support from TAs correlated with slower progress, and this choice says that less support from TAs correlated with faster progress.These two statements are reversed but have the same meaning.
So, this choice says the same thing as the passage says about theresultitselfand, therefore, doesn’t weaken the support for the author’sexplanationfor the result.
(C) If not effectively supervised, the unruliest students in a class can make teaching extremely difficult.
This choice is tempting because wording it uses, “supervised” and “unruliest students,” matches wording from the passage.

However, this choice is incorrect because it weakens the support for a conclusion different from the author’s conclusion.Rather than weaken the support for the author’s explanation for the negative relationship between TAs support and student progress, this choice casts doubt on a different conclusion, such as the conclusion that teachers’ having TAs supervise the unruliest students was unjustified.After all, if what this choice says is true, then there may be some justification for having TAs supervise the unruliest students.

Since the statement “if not effectively supervised, the unruliest students in a class can make teaching extremely difficult” is, if anything, in line with the idea that teachers used their TAs as supervisors for the unruliest students, this choice does not cast doubt on the author’s conclusion.

(D) Correct.When the defenders mentioned in S4 “argue that the less skilled pupils were assigned more help,” they are implying that the study results were biased.In other words, the defenders are suggesting that, even though it may haveseemedthat having TAs support students had a negative effect on student progress, whatwas really going onwas that the students supported by TAs were less skilled than other students and made less progress for that reason.

Then, when the author says, “the DCSF researchers successfully accounted for pupil skill,” this statement basically means “the researchers ruled out the explanation proposed by the defenders, that less skilled pupils were assigned more help.”

OK, so, the researchers successfully accounted for pupil skill, but what if there were another variable that the researchers didn’t account for? What if, as this choice says, “the DCSF researchers did not account for student motivation”?

In that case, we would have a possible alternative explanation for the difference between the progress of the students supported by TAs and that of students not supported by TAs.After all, if the researchers didn’t account for motivation, then differences in motivation, rather than anything teachers or TAs did, could have caused the differences in progress.

So, by presenting a possible alternative cause for the lack of progress of the students supervised by TAs, this choice casts doubt on the author’s conclusion that the reason for the lack of progress was that teachers had TAs supervise the unruliest students.
(E) The teachers used their TAs to supervise the unruliest students because they preferred to avoid the stress involved.
This answer choice does not have the effect we need.

In considering this answer choice, it is helpful to notice the phrase “becausethey preferred.” This answer is discussing themotivesof the teachers, but their motives do not have any direct connection to the author’s explanation, which says that “these difficult students had been better served by experienced teachers who had received the proper training.”

Another way to think about this answer is to ask yourself: “would it make any difference whether the teachers’ motive was to “avoid stress” or whether they had some other motive, such as “manage their time effectively”? No.
In summary, this answer mentions a detail that would be consistent with the author’s explanation.

analysis_tabs.strategy-review:
When we first read the passage, our goal is to understand the author’s main point(s), the purpose of the passage, the organization of the passage, and the author’s overall feelings, sentiments, and tone.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

Also, we can ask these key questions:

What is the main point of this passage?

Why did the author take the time to write this passage?

Is the author in agreement with someone or something? In disagreement? Is the author happy about something? Bothered? Indifferent? Is the author simply presenting information?

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
In S1, the author begins by describing a change in a government policy and public education spending: “Britain had a reputation for spending frugally on public education, lagging behind most other developed countries, BUT the country’s policy was transformed under Labour government leadership.” Notice the contrast marker “but.” “But” often signals that an important point is coming, and in this case, the fact that “the country’s policy was transformed” is an important point.

How was the country’s policy transformed? Notice that, later in S1, details about the change are specified: “the number of teaching assistants (TAs) … surged from 60,600 to 176,000.”

The first paragraph continues by discussing the underwhelming, i.e., disappointing, results of the policy change: S2 says, “Initially optimistic about the effects of this change, proud government ministers boasted about the increase, BUT despite the substantial investment involved, the results were underwhelming.” Here again, we must pay close attention to what follows the contrast marker, “but.” The key point to see is that the results of the policy change were underwhelming.

Notice that, so far, the author is completely descriptive and is presenting historical facts; he or she does make any arguments of his or her own. In other words, he or she has not yet taken a strong position.

1st paragraph summary: The author discusses a change in Britain’s public education policy that involved an increase in the number of teaching assistants in Britain and discusses the disappointing results of the change.

Notice that the 2nd paragraph opens with a rhetorical question, S3, “What is the most plausible reason for these results?” Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question uses the causation marker “reason,” indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then mentioned. We must pay close attention whenever an author presents an explanation for a phenomenon, because explanations tend to be important parts of the passages.

In S4, the author notes a flaw in the explanation of the “defenders,” and then, in S5, the author proceeds with his or her own explanation, “a more convincing explanation is that ….” We want to pay close attention whenever an author uses strong language, and “a more convincing explanation is that” certainly qualifies as strong language. “More” is a comparison marker, and “Convincing” means “believable,” so when the author says “a more convincing” explanation, the author is making the strong statement that he or she believes that this second explanation is better than the defenders’ explanation.

2nd paragraph summary: The author discusses two possible explanations for the results described in the first paragraph, and the author favors one of these explanations over the other.

For understanding the structure of the entire passage, it’s helpful to ask ourselves, “What is the relationship between the paragraphs?” Given that, in the first paragraph, the author has presented results that were disappointing and unexpected, it makes sense that the author would be interested in explaining these results, and explaining “these results” is exactly what the author does in the second paragraph.
analysis_tabs.vocabulary-clarification:
In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Simpler Similar Words

stewardship

leadership

analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: This sentence begins by providing some background information: For many years, Britain had a reputation for spending frugally on public education, lagging behind most other developed countries.”

Then, the contrast marker “but” signals a main point: “BUT the country’s policy was transformed.” As always, when we see a contrast marker, we must pay extra close attention and notice what two things are in contrast.

Simplification: Britain was frugal in public education spending but increased spending and the number of TAs significantly between 1997 and 2009.

S2: Contrast between expectations and results:

“Initially optimistic … ministers boasted” … “BUT…the results were underwhelming.” (Note: if we didn’t know the word “underwhelming,” we could guess that it means “disappointing” because of the context and contrast.)

Also, since “this X” is a look above marker, when we see “this change,” we must understand what is above that “this change” refers to. We see that “this change” refers to “the number of teaching assistants (TAs) … surged from 60,600 to 176,000.”

After the “:,” details are provided about how the results were underwhelming: “a consistent negative relationship between the amount of support a student received from TAs and the progress the student made.”

Simplification: Government ministers thought more TAs would help, but the results were disappointing.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

Also, S3 includes the causation marker “reason.” This marker signals that possible explanations will follow.

Finally, notice the look above marker “these results.” “These results” refers to the “underwhelming results” described in the first paragraph.

Simplification: What is the most likely cause of the disappointing results described above?

S4: The contrast marker “while” in “While some defenders … argue” signals that the author is noting a flaw in the argument of the “defenders.”

“Successfully accounted for this variable” means “successfully adjusted so that the results were not biased.”

“Argue that” is an opinion marker, so we must make sure not to mix up different opinions.

Simplification: Policy defenders argue that the results were biased, but the defenders are wrong because they failed to take something into account.

S5: As we might expect, the author follows up by presenting a “more convincing explanation,” after noting the flaw of the defenders’ argument in S4.

Simplification: A better explanation is how the TAs were used.

S6: The author elaborates on the explanation in S5.

Simplification: Before more TAs were hired, the most difficult students had been better served by experienced teachers.

question.text: Which of the following, if found to be true, would provide the most additional support for the conclusion mentioned in the last sentence of the second paragraph?

answers.text: Dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations by interacting with the pleasure and reward centers of the brain.
The majority of the individuals who participated in the study described in the passage chose to use the $128 to make anonymous donations to one or more societal causes.
The hypothalamus region of the brain performs many functions in addition to the production of oxytocin.
People whose mesolimbic pathways or hypothalamus regions are impaired are less likely than the average person to engage in anonymous altruistic behavior.
The levels of dopamine and oxytocin in people’s brains increase when people spend money on themselves.

explanation_parts.text: Question Analysis:

Our first move in answering this question is to notice that the stem asks, “Which of the following, if found to be true,wouldprovide the most additional supportfor the conclusion …?” So, this question is a Strengthen the Argument question.

To correctly answer this Strengthen question, we must understand exactly what conclusion is mentioned in the last sentence of the 2ndparagraph, S8.By scanning S8, we see that the conclusion mentioned is that of the scientists, “biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the conclusion “biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior,” is essentially a cause-and-effect claim that biological factorscauseseemingly selfless behavior.So, to strengthen this argument, we must find an additional reason to believe that biological factors cause seemingly selfless behavior.

Our next move is to see whether the passage provides any support for the conclusion.In this case, there is support for the conclusion, and we can summarize the argument in the 2ndparagraph in the following way:
Premise: When a person makes a charitable contribution, i.e., engages in seemingly selfless behavior, the person’s mesolimbic pathway and hypothalamus regions become active.
Premise: The mesolimbic pathway releases dopamine, and the hypothalamus region produces oxytocin.
Premise: Dopamine and oxytocin cause “positive sensations” (which, presumably, people want to experience).
Conclusion: Biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.(In other words, people made the donations to experience “positive sensations” caused by the hormones.)
Having identified the conclusion and the support for the conclusion, we now look for a choice that provides additional support.
(A) Dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations by interacting with the pleasure and reward centers of the brain.
This choice could be tempting because it could seem to confirm a key part of the support for the conclusion, the fact that dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations.

Notice, however, that we don’t need such confirmation, because the passage states as fact that “both hormones, dopamine and oxytocin, cause an individual to experience immediate positive sensations,” and as we know from our study of CR, a stated fact cannot be made any stronger.
Furthermore, if we really consider what the effect of this choice is, we see that what this choice does isexplain howdopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations.
So, since explaining a stated fact used to support the conclusion does not make the argument any stronger, this choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion mentioned in S8.
(B) The majority of the individuals who participated in the study described in the passage chose to use the $128 to make anonymous donations to one or more societal causes.

This choice has no effect on the support for the conclusion.After all, the fact that the individuals who used the $128 to donate to societal causes donated to the same societal causes does not indicate that biological factors caused them to donate.Any of a number of factors could have caused them to donate to the same societal causes.
(C) The hypothalamus region of the brain performs many functions in addition to the production of oxytocin.
If anything, this choice weakens, rather than strengthens, the support for the scientists’ conclusion.Let’s review the part of the passage where the hypothalamus is mentioned to see why.

The hypothalamus region is mentioned in S7: “Furthermore, the hypothalamus region of the brain, which produces oxytocin, a neurotransmitter that increases cooperation and trust, usually also became engaged when a donation was made.”

Notice that, while S7 says that the hypothalamus “produces oxytocin,” the passage does not say that the scientists observed the hypothalamus producing oxytocin.Rather, what the passage says is that the hypothalamus “usually also became engaged when a donation was made.”

Now what this choice says, “the hypothalamus region of the brain performs many functions,” indicates that the fact that the hypothalamus became engaged does not mean that the hypothalamus was producing oxytocin.After all, if the hypothalamus performs many functions, then it could have been performing any of those functions when it “became engaged.”

So, what this choice indicates it that, when people made donations, the hypothalamus may not have been producing oxytocin, and that information casts doubt on the conclusion that people are motivated by the effects of oxytocin to engage in seemingly selfless behavior.After all, the less sure we are that the hypothalamus was producing oxytocin when participants made donations, the less sure we are that production of oxytocin plays an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.
YOUR CHOICE(D) People whose mesolimbic pathways or hypothalamus regions are impaired are less likely than the average person to engage in anonymous altruistic behavior.
Let’s review the line of reasoning laid out in the 2ndparagraph:
Premise: When a person makes a charitable contribution, i.e., engages in seemingly selfless behavior, the person’s mesolimbic pathway and hypothalamus regions become active.
Premise: The mesolimbic pathway releases dopamine, and the hypothalamus region produces oxytocin.
Premise: Dopamine and oxytocin cause “positive sensations” (which, presumably, people want to experience).
Conclusion: Biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.(In other words, people made the donations to experience “positive sensations” caused by the hormones.)

Now, as we learned in our discussion of Critical Reasoning, one way to strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect claim is to show that, when the presumed cause is not present, the effect is not present either.
So, notice what this choice does.

If what this choice says were found to be true, then it would be the case that, when a person’s mesolimbic pathway or hypothalamus does not function properly, in other words, when one of the “biological factors” is not present, then the person is less likely than the average person to engage in anonymous altruistic behavior.In other words, this choice indicates that, when the cause is not present, the effect is less likely to be present.

So, by showing that, when the presumed cause, “biological factors,” is not present, the effect, “altruistic behavior,” is less likely to be present, this choice tends to confirm that “biological factors” cause “altruistic behavior.
So, this choice provides additional support for the scientists’ conclusion that “biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”
(E) The levels of dopamine and oxytocin in people’s brains increase when people spend money on themselves.
If anything, this choice weakens, rather than strengthens, the support for the scientists’ conclusion.

After all, if what this choice says is true, then regardless of whether people engage in anonymous altruistic behavior or spend money on themselves, they will experience the positive sensations caused by dopamine and oxytocin.In that case, the fact that people experience those positive sensations when they engage in anonymous altruistic behavior does not explain why they engage in anonymous altruistic behavior rather than spend money on themselves.After all, they could experience those sensations either way.

As we read the passage, we want to distinguish whether the author’s tone is descriptive (for example, “Mary has proposed that X”), or argumentative (for example, “People should X”).

If the author is making an argument, how strong is his or her conviction in that position? Is the author approaching an argument from an objective point of view?

Is the author questioning a finding or belief? Comparing findings or beliefs?

The main idea of a passage is often captured in the leading sentences of the paragraphs. In addition, contrast markers and opinion markers often signal important points. Finally, when an author argues in favor of a specific point, he or she often captures his or her main idea in the final sentence of the passage.

Because most passages will have some kind of contrast between different ideas or opinions, pay close attention to contrast markers, such as “however,” “but, and “although.” These contrast markers often signal important points that we should pay extra attention to.

Also, in reading a passage, we must be careful to keep different opinions, themes, and topics straight. It is easy to mix up things that are discussed or contrasted in a passage. We must also must be careful not to mix up the author’s opinion and any other opinions discussed in the passage.

Finally, don’t try to memorize the supporting details provided. Think “comprehension, not memorization.” For the majority of questions that ask about something specific, we will need to go back to specific parts of the passage anyway, with sharp focus on exactly what the question is asking and on the keywords in the stem and answer choices. We want to be able to prove that our answer is correct to someone else by citing specific language in the passage. Our accuracy will decrease if we rely on our memory or personal interpretation.
analysis_tabs.passage-analysis:
The passage begins by presenting some information about “people’s benevolence”; S1 says that people’s benevolence both is directed toward their kin and extends to those outside their families.

This information presented by S1 turns out to be background information; the first paragraph makes its point in S2 and S3.

S2 begins with the contrast marker “however,” which signals both that it’s likely that an important point is coming and that a contrast is being made. What are the two things that are contrasted? S1 has introduced one type of generous behavior, which is often reciprocated, and now, S2 introduces a different type of generous behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is a “perplexing phenomenon to scientists.” Anonymous altruistic behavior turns out to be the main topic of the passage.

Having brought up “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2, the first paragraph sets the stage for the rest of the passage with S3. S3 has a rhetorical question marker(question mark). Generally, when an author asks a rhetorical question, that question is connected strongly to the primary purpose of the passage. In this case, the question begins with “What would motivate individuals to …,” which means, essentially, “Why?” When the author asks “Why?” we have a causation marker, indicating that possible explanations will follow. Indeed, two possible explanations are then discussed, in the 2nd and 3rd paragraphs. We must pay close attention whenever explanations are discussed, because explanations tend to be important parts of passages.

S3 also defines “anonymous altruistic behavior, saying that it is “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” In defining “anonymous altruistic behavior,” S3 gives the reader a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the generous behavior described in S1, which is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

1st paragraph summary: Both scientists and other scholars are interested in explaining anonymous altruistic behavior. Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible answer the rhetorical question in S3. The possible answer, or explanation, is that “there are neural bases for such acts.” The look above marker in S4, “SUCH acts,” refers to the “anonymous deeds” in S3. So, this marker helps us understand the relationship between S4 and S3.

Then, in S5, S6, and S7, the 2nd paragraph provides some details of how “researchers” investigated this possible explanation and what they found. These details are rather involved. So, our goal in reading these sentences is not necessarily to totally understand or seek to memorize every one of these details but rather to understand in general how the study described worked.

In S8, we see the opinion marker, “contended.” Here, the main conclusion of the researchers is presented: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author’s tone in the 2nd paragraph is descriptive, and the author does not provide her own opinion.

2nd paragraph summary: The author presents one possible explanation for anonymous altruistic behavior: having studied brain activity, researchers concluded that hormones that cause positive sensations, i.e., biological factors, play an important role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

The 3rd paragraph begins with the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

In this case, by using “while X, Y” in S9, the author connects the 2nd paragraph to the 3rd paragraph; in the “while X” part of S9, the author concedes that the argument presented in the 2nd paragraph “may … lead to some useful insights,” and then, in the Y part of S9, the author presents the topic of the 3rd paragraph, the “more compelling perspective” of Matthieu Ricard. (Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.)

S10 and S11 provide details about Ricard’s perspective and the types of evidence he uses to support his points.

Finally, in S12, the author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.” Notice that, in judging Ricard’s conclusion “persuasive,” the author is once again stating an opinion of her own.

Also, we can see that there is clear contrast because Ricard’s conclusion and that of the researchers discussed in the second paragraph. S12 says that Ricard’s “persuasive case” is that serving others can be “genuinely selfless,” whereas S8 says that the researchers discussed in the second paragraph contend that such behavior can be “seemingly selfless.”

3rd paragraph summary: The author presents a second explanation that she believes is more compelling than the “biological argument” in the 2nd paragraph: Ricard’s perspective is that the motivation for serving others can be genuinely selfless and that this behavior can be cultivated through specific practices.

Passage summary: The author poses a question about why people engage in anonymous altruistic behavior, presents a biological explanation based on a brain imaging study, and then presents a second explanation that the author prefers.

In general, RC passages do not extensively use challenging vocabulary. At the same time, as we read, we’ll need to ensure that we properly understand any words we come across.

When we encounter words we know well, we’ll naturally understand their meanings. So, we won’t need to put forth a conscious effort to understand them.

More complex words require a bit more effort. Although we don’t have time to consciously translate every word as we read a passage, when we encounter more complex words, we can aid our understanding of the passage by translating these words into simpler words. We can make this process fast. For example, if we encountered the word “frugal,” it would take only a second to reframe the idea of being “frugal” as being thrifty, economical, or careful with money.

In addition, when we are answering questions about specific details, we must pay close conscious attention to each word in the answer choices to see whether its meaning matches a meaning conveyed by passage.

Often, if we don’t know the exact meaning of a word, we can gain enough understanding from the context to be able to answer the questions. So, don’t let unfamiliar words intimidate you — focus on precisely what the question is asking, and find support for the correct answer in the passage.

Note: To answer the questions correctly, we don’t need to know the technical vocabulary: functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), mesolimbic pathway, neurotransmitter, dopamine, hypothalamus, oxytocin, hormones, empathy-altruism hypothesis. We can understand the meaning of each sentence from the surrounding language and the definitions that are provided in the passage. (For example, the passage defines the mesolimbic pathway: “the mesolimbic pathway, the reward center of the brain that is responsible for releasing the neurotransmitter associated with pleasure.”)
analysis_tabs.sentence-simplification:
When we are preparing for RC, it’s essential to deconstruct and analyze each sentence we encounter in a passage. As you practice, perform this sentence-by-sentence analysis and simplification on your own before you go through the analysis we provide.

S1: Notice causation marker “because.”

Simplification: People are often kind to not only relatives but also others, because this kindness is often returned.

S2: Notice the contrast marker “however.” This sentence introduces a different type of kind behavior, “anonymous altruistic behavior,” which is “perplexing to scientists,” meaning that it is harder to explain.

Note: we may not be sure what the meaning of “anonymous altruistic behavior” is at first, but we are basically given a definition in the next sentence. In general, we can look to the surrounding context if we don’t understand something.

Simplification: A different type of kind behavior, anonymous altruistic behavior, has been puzzling for scientists to explain and an important topic for other scholars.

S3: Notice the rhetorical question marker (question mark). Generally, when the author asks a question, this question connects strongly to the primary purpose of the passage, and the author follows by answering this question.

The question posed, “What would motivate individuals to carry out anonymous deeds …?” is asking about the reason/cause/explanation for these anonymous deeds. So, we can expect the rest of the passage to discuss possible explanations.

This sentence also helps the reader to understand the full meaning of S2. It defines what is meant by the “anonymous altruistic behavior” introduced in S2: “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” It also gives us a sense of why this behavior is “perplexing” to scientists. Unlike the kind behavior described in S1 that is often reciprocated (returned), anonymous altruistic behavior is not reciprocated.

Simplification: Why do people perform anonymous acts of kindness that cannot be reciprocated?

S4: Notice the look above marker “SUCH acts.” We must understand what is above that “such acts” refers to. Notice that “acts” has the same meaning as “deeds” in S3, so we can see that “such acts” refers to “anonymous deeds in the service of others who have no ability to return the favor.” As we noted in our analysis of S3, these “anonymous deeds” are also called “anonymous altruistic behavior” in S2.

As we might expect, S4 introduces a possible explanation to answer the rhetorical question in S3.

Note: if we don’t know what “neural” means, we can see from S5 that it’s related to brain activity: “researchers used functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to analyze the activity in various areas of the brain.”

Simplification: A certain study sought to find an explanation, related to brain activity, for anonymous altruistic behavior.

S5: Provides details about the design of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: Using fMRI, the researchers analyzed the subjects’ brain activity while the subjects decided whether to keep money or make a donation.

S6: Provides details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Simplification: The reward center of the brain that releases dopamine, which is associated with pleasure, became active when a donation was made.

S7: Adds more details about the findings of the study introduced in S4.

Notice the additional point marker “furthermore.” S7 is very similar to S6, but discusses a different brain region and hormone.

Simplification: The region of the brain that produces oxytocin, which is associated with cooperation and trust, also became active when a donation was made.

S8: Notice the opinion marker “contended.” Also, notice that the word “so” connects a premise and a conclusion, in the structure “Premise, so Conclusion”

This sentence presents what the researchers have concluded from the findings of their study presented in the previous sentences.

Simplification: Since dopamine and oxytocin cause positive sensations, the scientists argue that biological factors play a role in anonymous altruistic behavior.

S9: Introduces a “compelling,” i.e., persuasive, alternative to the “biological argument” discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

Notice the contrast marker “while.” The structure “while X, Y” generally signals that Y is an important point, and the X part acknowledges or concedes a potential counterpoint to the important point in the Y part.

Notice that the wording “THIS biological argument” is a look above marker and refers to the biological argument directly above in S8: “these scientists contended that biological factors play an important role in seemingly selfless behavior.”

Notice that the author does express an opinion here, the opinion that Ricard’s perspective is “more compelling” than the biological argument.

Simplification: There is another perspective, that of Matthieu Ricard, which is more compelling than the biological argument discussed in the 2nd paragraph.

S10: Provides details about Ricard’s perspective.

Notice that “but” is a contrast marker in the structure “Ricard acknowledges that X, but he demonstrates that Y.” Ricard’s perspective is that anonymous altruism does not always come naturally, but it can be cultivated.

Also notice the contrast marker, “In contrast to X, Y.” Ricard acknowledges that kindness towards friends and family can come more naturally (instinctively, innately) to people than anonymous altruism does.

S11: Discusses a type of evidence that Ricard uses, cognitive science research.

Simplification: To support his perspective, Ricard also cites cognitive science research, such as Batson’s empathy-altruism hypothesis.

S12: The author summarizes Ricard’s conclusion about altruism and judges it “persuasive.”

Notice that the author repeats her positive judgment of Ricard’s perspective: S9 says that it’s “compelling,” and S12 says that it’s “persuasive.”

Notice the qualifier marker (non-extreme), “CAN be genuinely selfless.” It’s important to notice how strong an argument is, so that we can avoid Stretch traps.
